questions.alt: Question 1. The expression “actions speak louder than words” means that people are often judged more by what they do than what they say. Which sentence from the story best supports this idea? “Little Shaq had been asking for a kitten for as long as he could remember.” A (paragraph 5) B “We lost, but the rec center is having a really cool event next week.” (paragraph 9) “He wasn't good at remembering to take out the trash, and Mom did have Cc to tell him every morning to make his bed” (paragraph 16) “In all the excitement about the pet fair, Little Shaq had forgotten to tell his D P » parents the big news.” (paragraph 18)

explanation.text: Little Shaq says he is responsible, but his actions show that he forgets the trash and needs daily reminders to make his bed. This contrast supports judging responsibility by what someone does, not what someone says.

questions.alt: Question 2. Which detail from paragraph 7 best supports the idea that Little Shaq is nervous? A The food sits untouched. B The meatballs make a mess. C His mom feels unhappy. D His father catches his eye.

explanation.text: Little Shaq's favorite spaghetti and meatballs remain untouched because he is worried about asking for a kitten. Being too nervous to eat food he loves is the clearest supporting detail.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does “glanced” mean as it is used in paragraph 17? A __ looked for something B__ reached over C picked an object up D _ turned away

explanation.text: Little Shaq quickly looks around the room for an idea and notices the instruction sheet in his backpack. In this context, “glanced” means looked briefly for something.

questions.alt: Question 4. How do the details in paragraphs 18 through 21 support a theme of the story? A ___ They show that pet fairs are big events. B _ They show that hard work can be rewarded. C They show that instructions should be followed. D They show that families should talk to each other.

explanation.text: Little Shaq offers to complete the many Star of the Week jobs and care for Flopsy to prove he can handle a kitten. His parents then agree to consider his request, showing that responsible work may be rewarded.

questions.alt: Question 5. What does paragraph 24 show about Little Shaq? A Heis thankful because his parents are laughing at a joke he told. B__Heis pleased because his brother and sister agree with him. C = Heis excited because his brother and sister are proud of him. D He is happy because his parents have agreed to think about his question.

explanation.text: Little Shaq smiles and calls his parents' response “a start” because, although they have not promised a kitten, their agreement to think about it makes him happy that his request is now possible.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A Little Shaq will take care of Flopsy. B Barry hasa fish named Louie. C__ Tater makes a mess at dinner. D Malia is ina school play.

explanation.text: Caring for the class rabbit is Little Shaq's main chance to prove he can accept pet responsibilities and persuade his parents. Therefore, his plan to care for Flopsy is essential to a summary.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which sentence from the passage best shows a cause-and-effect relationship? “Under the roots of an old pine tree, there is scuffling and snuffling” A (paragraph 1) “Porcupette backs down the tree and wanders over to the nearby stream.” B (paragraph 6) “Beavers are the biggest rodents in North America, but porcupines aren't Cc far behind” (paragraph 7) “The mountain lion slinks away, batting at his mouth where sharp quills D » dangle.” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The mountain lion tries to attack Porcupette but receives sharp quills in its mouth as a result. Its slinking away in pain therefore shows an effect caused by Porcupette's defense.

questions.alt: Question 14. Why is the picture important to understanding paragraph 2? A __ It compares a baby porcupine’ size to its mother. B__It suggests that porcupines should fear big animals. C __Itincludes where porcupines go to find food at night. D__It shows what a baby porcupine'’s body looks like.

explanation.text: Paragraph 2 describes Porcupette waddling on short, sturdy legs while calm and confident. The picture therefore supplies the visual details of a baby porcupine's legs, shape, fur, and quills.

questions.alt: Question 15. Read this sentence from paragraph 12. Soon she is fast asleep, dreaming of more adventures in her prickly porcupine world. What does the phrase “prickly porcupine world” mean as it is used in this sentence? A anangry world B a world full of excitement Cc a scary world D _ aworld full of plants

explanation.text: Porcupette has spent the night climbing, swimming, finding food, and escaping a mountain lion, then dreams of still more adventures. Therefore, her “prickly porcupine world” is full of excitement.

questions.alt: Question 16. What does the author most likely think about Porcupette? A __ Porcupette needs help from her mother. B ___ Porcupette should be more careful. C__~Porcupette is able to survive on her own. D___ Porcupette is safe with other animals.

explanation.text: Without waking her mother, Porcupette finds food, climbs, swims, warns an owl, defeats a mountain lion, and finds shelter for sleep. These successful actions show that she can survive independently.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which sentence best states a central idea of the passage? A __ Porcupines are awake at night and sleep during the day. B___Porcupines drink their mother’s milk when they are babies. C__~Porcupines use their quills to stay safe from enemies. D__ Porcupines weigh more than baby black bears at birth.

explanation.text: Porcupette raises her quills to warn away an owl and later drives a mountain lion off with quills stuck in its mouth. Because both dangers are stopped this way, quills keeping porcupines safe is a central idea.

questions.alt: Question 18. 1g How does the author mainly organize the information in the passage? A __ by telling Porcupette’s problem and the solution B by telling, in order, how Porcupette spends her night C__ by comparing Porcupette’s nights to her days D by describing how some other animals affect Porcupette

explanation.text: The passage follows one night in sequence: Porcupette leaves her den, eats and climbs, swims and explores, escapes a predator, then sleeps as morning arrives. Therefore, time order is the main organization.

questions.alt: Question 19. Sometimes different animals can peacefully share the same community. How does the passage support this idea? A __ by describing how Porcupette is not afraid of raccoons at night B _ byexplaining how the owl causes Porcupette to raise her quills C__byshowing how Porcupette senses the odor of a mountain lion D by telling how the mountain lion is unable to attack Porcupette

explanation.text: A raccoon passes Porcupette on its way to fish, and she remains calm, confident, and unbothered. Their peaceful encounter shows that different animals can share the same nighttime habitat.

questions.alt: Question 20. 9 Which quote best supports a central idea of the story? A _ “This field trip to the zoo might be our last!” (paragraph 2) B “ ‘Remember when the new principal came?’ asked Jamie.” (paragraph 15) C_ “‘Thope so; said Jamie. “We have to save the zoo: ” (paragraph 22) “J think the city should use the money to make the library bigger” D (paragraph 25)

explanation.text: Jamie repeatedly organizes others and worries whether their campaign will work because she believes they must save the zoo. Her statement “We have to save the zoo” therefore expresses the story's central goal.

questions.alt: Question 21. How does Jamie support her claim that she and her friends can help save the zoo, even if they are not old enough to vote? A __ byraising money B__ by making posters and fliers C __ byspeaking at the town meeting D __ byexplaining what is wrong with the city

explanation.text: Jamie cannot vote, so she organizes classmates to make signs, dozens of posters, and fliers displayed around town. Spreading those messages is how the children persuade eligible voters.

questions.alt: Question 22. In paragraph 31, what does “Jamie’s heart sank” mean? A _ Jamie felt pain in her chest. B _ Jamie felt excited she could vote. C Jamie was sad the zoo lost the vote. D _ Jamie was tired of reading the newspaper.

explanation.text: Jamie reads that the zoo lost, immediately worries about the animals, and wishes the class had campaigned longer. Those disappointed thoughts show that “her heart sank” means she became sad.

questions.alt: Question 23. Paragraph 32 shows that Jamie's mother believes A __ the campaign to save the zoo was a bad idea B___ the zoo animals will be fine at the preserve C__ the city will make the library bigger D __ the newspaper should only be read by adults

explanation.text: Jamie's mother points out that the animals will move to the nearby Heritage Wildlife Preserve and have plenty of space. This reassurance shows her belief that they will be safe and well there.

questions.alt: Question 24. How does Jamie change from the beginning of the story to the end of the story? First, she is excited to save the zoo by campaigning. After, she is thankful the animals will go to a wildlife preserve. First, she is disappointed that the zoo might be closing. After, she is sad B that the animals will be so far away. First, she is sad that she cannot vote to save the zoo. After, she is angry at Cc her city for not voting the way she wanted. First, she is happy that her friend wants to help her. After, she is worried D that she did not do enough for the zoo.

explanation.text: At first Jamie eagerly creates a campaign to keep the zoo open. Although the vote fails, she accepts that the animals “won” because they will receive more space at a preserve, showing gratitude for the good outcome.

questions.alt: Question 26. How does the author use paragraphs 5 and 10 to develop Vikram? A __ by describing how he is more interested in taking pictures than in a shell by describing how he is a good brother who enjoys spending time with B family C __ by explaining why he and his brother argue all the time by explaining the different kinds of activities he enjoys doing while on D vacation

explanation.text: Vikram stays at the cottage to take pictures and later explains that he was photographing the beach while dismissing the shell as boring. Together, these details show more interest in photography than in Sabeel's treasure.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which sentence best supports a theme of the story? “Back at the cottage, Sabeel ran to his room to admire the shell.” A (paragraph 7) “This time, Sabeel tucked the shell under his pillow, where no one could B see it? (paragraph 11) Cc ‘Thave to return a gift” (paragraph 25) D “With the next wave, he was gone.’ (paragraph 26)

explanation.text: Sabeel loves the shell but realizes it is Sandy's home, not a possession to keep, and says he must “return a gift.” His choice supports a theme of placing a living creature's needs above one's own wants.

questions.alt: Question 28. 2g What claim does Sabeel make in paragraph 9? A _ that Vikram has stolen the shell B that Vikram does not like him C _ that he has lost the shell D _ that he wants to go to the arcade

explanation.text: After finding the shell under Vikram's bed, Sabeel immediately declares, “Vikram took it.” He therefore makes the claim that his brother stole the shell, even though Vikram denies it.

questions.alt: Question 29. What do the details in paragraph 22 show about the hermit crab? 29 A It needs water. B It senses danger. C It likes the name Sandy. D It wants to live at Sabeel’s house.

explanation.text: When Sabeel picks up the hermit crab, its legs and eyes withdraw inside the shell. Pulling its exposed body into its protective covering shows that the crab senses possible danger.

questions.alt: Question 30. What does the word “washed” mean as it is used in paragraph 25? 30 A cleaned with soap B felt worn out C became equal to something D carried by flowing water

explanation.text: Sabeel remembers the shell arriving from the ocean with a wave. Because moving seawater brought it onto shore, “washed up” means carried by flowing water.

questions.alt: Question 31. How does knowing the shell is a hermit crab change Dadi’s thoughts on what Sabeel should do? First, Dadi tells Sabeel the shell is a gift from the sea. After, Dadi thinks A Sabeel needs to return the crab to its home. First, Dadi thinks the shell is very beautiful. After, Dadi thinks Sabeel B should buy a new shell at the boardwalk. First, Dadi wants Sabeel to share his shell with his siblings. After, Dadi Cc thinks Sabeel should treasure his gift on his own. First, Dadi likes the pink inside of the shell. After, Dadi thinks Sabeel D should find a shell the same color as the crab.

explanation.text: Dadi first calls the empty-looking shell a gift from the sea. Once it is known to house a crab, she reminds Sabeel that Sandy already has a home, therefore guiding him to return the living animal to the ocean.

questions.alt: Question 1. What claim does Mr. Wheeler make in paragraph 1? A The girls can win the game if they have a plan. B__ The girls will easily win the game. C the girls should work harder during practice. D The girls are too tired to play well.

explanation.text: With the score tied, Mr. Wheeler says the team “can do this” if they get their tactics right. Since tactics are plans, his claim is that a good plan can lead the girls to victory.

questions.alt: Question 2. Which sentence from the story best supports a theme about believing in yourself? A “The vital thing is not to concede a goal!” (paragraph 4) “Besides, you have to remember, in this game it’s what goes on inside your B head that matters.” (paragraph 6) “ “Mega talent—a rising soccer superstar; she wrote beside Tyra’s name.” Cc (paragraph 15) “Alicia fed the ball back to Tyra, jumped the sprawling defender, and ran D free” (paragraph 21)

explanation.text: Tyra says success depends on what happens inside the players' heads, then urges them to think about winning rather than losing. This statement best supports believing in oneself.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does the reader learn about Tyra in paragraphs 6 through 8? 3 A Tyra knows the team needs more practice. B Tyra is a strong leader for her team. C Tyra likes to talk to the team a lot. D Tyra feels angry with her team.

explanation.text: Tyra speaks up for the coach, commands the team's full attention, and gives an energetic message about attitude, effort, possession, and desire. These actions reveal a captain who leads and motivates others.

questions.alt: Question 4. __ Read this sentence from paragraph 7 of the story. The whole team turned to her, hanging on every word. The phrase “hanging on every word” shows that the team is A __ Starting to doubt their ability to win the game B___ ready to end the game so they can relax C listening carefully to what Tyra is saying D ___ not understanding what Tyra plans to do

explanation.text: Every player turns toward Tyra and treats each word as important while she gives her motivational talk. Therefore, “hanging on every word” means listening very carefully.

questions.alt: Question 5. Where does the reader first learn about a change that leads to the team’s win? 5 A paragraph 13 B paragraph 17 C paragraph 18 D paragraph 20

explanation.text: Paragraph 18 begins the decisive change: Natalie wins the ball deep in Fernbridge's half, gets past an attacker, and chooses Tyra for the pass. That new possession therefore starts the scoring play that breaks the deadlock.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? 6 Mr. Wheeler tells the Fernbridge team to not let the other team score a A goal. Fernbridge must make it through the next forty-five minutes to win the B game. C Natalie gets the ball away from the other team and passes it to Tyra. D Tyra makes the decision to shoot the ball and scores a goal for her team.

explanation.text: The championship remains tied until Tyra chooses to shoot and scores the game's only goal. Because that decision resolves the central conflict and wins the cup, it is essential to a summary.

questions.alt: Question 13. What do the details in paragraphs 1 and 8 reveal about Spider Woman? 13 A She knows what others need. B She knows about many things. C She is an encouraging person. D She is a very careful person.

explanation.text: Spider Woman is overjoyed that Little Owl wants to learn, patiently demonstrates the weave, and later smiles and praises the tight, strong work. These supportive responses reveal an encouraging mother.

questions.alt: Question 14. _ Itis said that it is important to be prepared in case of a problem. Which sentence best shows how this idea is shown in the story? “Tell them to catch my brothers and hunt the animals of the forest now, A __ before they return to the Great Spirit in the hard time to come.” (paragraph 4) “The next morning Little Owl woke to hear her mother calling, ‘Little Owl, go fetch some water? ” (paragraph 5) “She slipped the shell into her pocket, picked up the water jar, and walked Cc back to the house? (paragraph 7) “She expected to find broken bits of eggshell; instead, her eyes fell upon a D handful of dark, purple-red kernels of corn” (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: The fish warns Little Owl's people to catch food before the water dries and the animals disappear during the hard time. Acting before the predicted shortage directly demonstrates preparation for a problem.

questions.alt: Question 15. How are paragraph 4 and paragraph 10 related? A __ by telling how the first dream causes Little Owl to have another dream B__ by using dialogue to make a point about Little Owl’s people C__ bycomparing how Little Owl feels after each of her dreams D _ by telling how Little Owl can solve a problem by warning her people

explanation.text: Both the fish and corn warn that water will disappear and instruct Little Owl to preserve food and tell her people. Together, the dreams explain how she can help solve the coming drought by warning them.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which detail from the story best supports the fish’s claim that Little Owl is a good 16 daughter? A Little Owl eats only a little cake and berries before going to bed. B Little Owl gets out of bed when her mother tells her to go get water. C Little Owl weaves a basket and her mother checks the work. D Little Owl goes to the marsh to gather twigs for basket-making.

explanation.text: When Spider Woman calls, Little Owl gets out of bed, takes the jar, and walks to the stream as directed. Her prompt obedience in completing a family chore supports the fish's praise of her as a good daughter.

questions.alt: Question 17. What does the reader learn about Little Owl in paragraph 6? 17 A She forgets what she is doing easily. B She notices the things around her. C She enjoys collecting things for herself. D She dislikes doing chores in the morning.

explanation.text: While filling the jar, Little Owl spots a tiny pearly object in the muddy bank and pries out the fish-shaped shell. Noticing this small detail shows that she pays close attention to her surroundings.

questions.alt: Question 18. 1g Read this sentence from paragraph 7. Suddenly her dream came rushing back to her, and she lost her grip on the handle of the jar. What does the phrase “rushing back to her” mean as used in the sentence? A __ Little Owl remembers events from her dream. B The dream causes Little Owl to feel upset. C The jar of water is too heavy for Little Owl. D Little Owl must hurry home with the water.

explanation.text: Seeing the fish-shaped shell suddenly makes Little Owl recall the fish and its message from the previous night. Thus, the dream “rushing back to her” means its events quickly return to her memory.

questions.alt: Question 19. How do Little Owl’s actions in the story support a theme of listening to nature? 19 A She follows directions to weave a good basket. B She works hard collecting water for her family. C She gathers things from the marsh to share. D She plans to tell someone about her dreams.

explanation.text: Little Owl understands messages spoken through the fish, water, corn, wind, and rain, and she decides she must tell someone. Acting on those natural warnings supports a theme of listening to nature.

questions.alt: Question 20. What does “out of the question” mean as used in paragraph 1? 20 A a confusing action B an important goal C extremely interesting D completely impossible

explanation.text: In 1783 no person had ever traveled through the sky, and people had no reason to believe anyone could. Therefore, saying flight was “out of the question” means it seemed completely impossible.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which sentence from the article best expresses a claim the author makes? 21 “A lot of good science starts with simply paying attention and thinking A about what you’ve seen.” (paragraph 2) “If you’ve ever watched bits of ash rising with the smoke from a fire, you’ve B seen it too.” (paragraph 3) “And the king of France wouldn’t allow it until one more experiment was C made.” (paragraph 6) “D’Arlandes leaned out, staring down at the crowd as they stared up at D him.” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The author claims good science can begin with noticing and thinking about ordinary events; Joseph's observation of rising hot air then leads to balloons. That broad statement therefore expresses the article's claim.

questions.alt: Question 22. Read this sentence from paragraph 9. 22 He ran a science museum and invented an early type of gas mask to protect workers in sewers. What does the word “ran” mean as it is used in the sentence? A moved at a fast speed B was a candidate for a position C was in charge of something D passed quickly in a direction

explanation.text: Rozier operated a science museum as its leader, just as someone might run a business or organization. In this context, “ran” therefore means was in charge of something.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? 23 A Étienne was a responsible and successful student when he was younger. B Joseph experienced failure both in school and at business. C Joseph and Étienne discovered that bags filled with hot air will rise up. D Joseph and Étienne chose to avoid riding in their own balloon.

explanation.text: Joseph and Etienne's discovery that hot air lifts cloth bags leads directly to larger balloons, public demonstrations, animal tests, and human flight. This foundational breakthrough therefore belongs in a summary.

questions.alt: Question 24. Based on the article, how does making a deal with D’Arlandes help Rozier? 24 A D’Arlandes convinces the king to let them fly in a balloon. B D’Arlandes gathers a large crowd to watch the balloon flight. C D’Arlandes is skilled at using straw to keep a fire going. D D’Arlandes is familiar with the streets and buildings in the city.

explanation.text: Rozier lacks access to the king, so he promises the nobleman d'Arlandes a place on the flight in return for help. D'Arlandes then persuades the king to permit their balloon trip.

questions.alt: Question 26. Read this sentence from paragraph 1. Roofs covered in plants, or “green roofs,’ are sprouting up all over, from schools to city skyscrapers. What does the word “sprouting” suggest? A __ Rooftop gardens are good for all types of buildings. B._ More green rooftops are being built than ever before. C__ Buildings with green roofs can be easily seen. D Plants grow better when they are on a roof.

explanation.text: Green roofs are described as appearing “all over,” from individual schools to tall city buildings. Comparing this spread to plants sprouting suggests that more green rooftops are being built than before.

questions.alt: Question 27. Many people believe you can turn trash into treasure. Which detail from the article 27 best supports this idea? “Architects are finding all sorts of new ways to build buildings that are A easier on the environment.” (paragraph 2) “Builders have found many creative ways to re-use old materials.” B (paragraph 10) “When it rains, the soil and plants soak up rainwater, which means less C water runs off into drains.” (paragraph 12) “City planners like green buildings because they save money.” D (paragraph 13)

explanation.text: Builders transform discarded newspaper, glass, plastic, ceramics, bottles, and even an old factory into useful parts of new green spaces. The statement about creatively reusing old materials therefore captures turning trash into treasure.

questions.alt: Question 28. How is paragraph 4 organized? 28 A by describing in order the steps for making a wall B by stating an idea about thick walls and giving examples C by sharing a reason for thick walls and the effects they have D by comparing and contrasting different kinds of walls

explanation.text: Paragraph 4 explains that insulation in thick walls blocks heat from moving, which keeps warmth inside in winter and outside in summer and lowers energy use. It is organized as a reason followed by effects.

questions.alt: Question 29. Based on the section “Sun Power,” how do big windows make a building better for 29 the environment? A Windows can be covered by shades. B Windows can be made from recycled glass. C Windows are placed where solar panels could be. D Windows allow in light and heat.

explanation.text: Large windows provide natural daylight instead of electric lighting and let winter sunlight warm indoor space and slate floors. By supplying both light and heat, they reduce a building's energy use.

questions.alt: Question 30. How does the section “A Better Home for All” support a central idea of the article? 30 A by describing how green roofs on buildings save energy B by explaining how soil helps plants in different weather conditions C by stating that roofs that are dark become very hot from the sun D by sharing that green buildings help city planners save money

explanation.text: The section explains that planted roofs stay cooler in summer, insulate in winter, absorb rain, clean air, and support wildlife. These benefits develop the central idea that green roofs save energy and other resources.

questions.alt: Question 31. The diagram supports the information in the article by showing that A __ wind power causes flags to move B__ buildings can be built to help the environment C__aschool bus can be powered by the sun D _ solar panels change sunlight into electricity

explanation.text: Arrows in the diagram trace light waves from the sun to panels, then show electricity traveling through wires into the school. This sequence visually explains that solar panels convert sunlight into usable electricity.

questions.alt: Question 1. What is the main way paragraphs 8 through 11 develop the story? 1 A by introducing Edie’s mother B by suggesting a possible conflict C by introducing Edie’s character D by describing the drive to school

explanation.text: These paragraphs first show Edie casually eating, sneaking coffee, joking that her mother is unprepared, and walking toward school without concern. The specific behaviors introduce her relaxed, independent character.

questions.alt: Question 2. Which detail from the story best supports the narrator’s claim that she feels different from others 2 in school? “I don’t know what to say to that, so we are silent as we make our way up the stairs . . .” A (paragraph 15) “Like we don’t belong in certain areas just because no one was expecting us.” B (paragraph 15) C “A new kid in school is always exciting around here . . .” (paragraph 16) D “But the looks are curious more than anything else.” (paragraph 16)

explanation.text: Alberta says staring makes her feel that she does not belong in certain places simply because people did not expect to see a Black person. That statement directly supports her sense of being different.

questions.alt: Question 3. In paragraphs 12 through 17, how does the setting affect the plot? 3 A by showing why Edie feels confident B by introducing the characters Edie and Alberta C by describing why Alberta is nervous D by demonstrating why Edie and Alberta are friends

explanation.text: At the busy school entrance, students stare at Edie while Alberta remembers past attention and begins feeling familiar jitters. The public school setting therefore brings out Alberta's nervousness.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which theme is best supported in paragraphs 16 and 17? A Unfamiliar situations are often difficult for people. B Communication between friends is sometimes silent. C Friendship helps people during difficult times. D___ Some people appear confident wherever they are.

explanation.text: Although Edie is new and attracts many looks, she ignores the attention and “breezes” inside as though she owns the school. Her response supports the theme that some people appear confident anywhere.

questions.alt: Question 5. Read this sentence from paragraph 17. 5 My stomach flutters and I feel those jitters settling in, right on time. Which statement best describes the narrator’s feelings in this sentence? A She is used to feeling uneasy when going to school. B She is concerned about how the students will treat Edie. C She is worried about having to attend a new school. D She is upset that Edie is receiving most of the attention.

explanation.text: Alberta begins by saying she usually has first-day jitters and previously developed a stomachache before school. When the familiar flutter returns “right on time,” it shows she is used to school-related uneasiness.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? 6 A “Normally I’d ride my bike, but Ms. Whitman offered to drive us . . .” (paragraph 2) B “It sounds like an entire cabinet of dishes just fell . . .” (paragraph 5) C “Edie chews and swallows the last of her doughnut . . .” (paragraph 14) D “How is the new girl more comfortable than I am . . .” (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: The story's central contrast is that Edie, the watched new student, enters confidently while longtime student Alberta develops jitters. Alberta's question about that difference therefore captures the main development for a summary.

questions.alt: Question 7. The narrator mainly helps the reader understand Edie by describing A__ Edie’s response to the students who look at her B_ whya pair of jeans is important to Edie C__Edie’s decision to wear combat boots at school D__ how Edie takes sips of her mother’s coffee

explanation.text: The narrator notes that Edie acts as if she does not notice anyone staring and confidently walks through the doors. Describing her response to attention most clearly reveals Edie's self-assurance.

questions.alt: Question 15. In paragraph 8, why does the author refer to spores as being like “mushroom seeds”? 15 A to explain that spores can grow into mushrooms B to emphasize the small size of most spores C to describe how spores are moved by wind D to explain that mushrooms grow underground

explanation.text: Spores fall from mushrooms, travel away, and can grow into new fungi when they land in a suitable place. Comparing them to seeds therefore explains their role in producing new growth.

questions.alt: Question 16. Read this claim from paragraph 2. ..- Mueller knows that mushrooms are here-today, gone-tomorrow treasures. How do the details in paragraph 9 support this claim? A__ by referring to the time mushrooms spend underground B__ by describing how mushrooms swell with water C_ _ byreferring to some mushrooms that grow through cement D __ by describing what happens after mushrooms release their spores

explanation.text: Paragraph 9 says mushrooms quickly release spores and may disappear again only a few days after emerging. This short life above ground supports Mueller's description of them as “here-today, gone-tomorrow.”

questions.alt: Question 17. Which detail best represents a central idea of the article? A “But fungi are all around, even when we don't see them.” (paragraph 6) B “‘Asingle strand is too small to see.” (paragraph 7) C “The color will help him identify the mushroom.” (paragraph 8) D “Unlike plant seeds, spores aren't packed with their own food.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The article explains fungi's many forms, hidden threads, temporary mushrooms, decomposition, and underground partnerships. The broad statement that fungi surround us even unseen therefore represents a central idea.

questions.alt: Question 18. Read this sentence from paragraph 12. “We wouldn't even be able to walk around the Earth because of all the dead trees.” What purpose does this sentence serve in the article? A _ tohighlight an important role that fungi play B___ to explain why fungi burst out of the ground C___ to describe how fungi spread over large areas D to indicate a typical place where fungi are found

explanation.text: Mueller imagines dead trees piling miles high without fungi because decomposers break wood down and release its nutrients. This dramatic consequence highlights fungi's essential cleanup role.

questions.alt: Question 19. In the section titled “How Fungi Function,’ the author best develops the idea that fungi and 19 plants help each other by A __ describing the way fungi filter water in the soil B__ showing how fungi spores help fruit to develop C describing how fungi roots create food for insects D__ showing how fungi and trees share their food

explanation.text: Fungal threads pass water and minerals from soil to plants, while plants return some of the sugar they make. This exchange of different forms of food therefore develops their mutually helpful partnership.

questions.alt: Question 20. As used in paragraph 14, what does the phrase “like a living internet” suggest about hyphae and tree roots? A Hyphae help provide trees with minerals. B Hyphae and trees are connected in a vast system. C __ Trees need hyphae to grow to larger sizes. D __ Trees and hyphae require each other to survive.

explanation.text: Many fungi connect with many trees underground, sending resources through intertwined roots and hyphae. Comparing this system to a living internet emphasizes its vast network of connections.

questions.alt: Question 21. In the article, what do both the author and the scientist agree is the main importance of fungi? 21 A their method of reproducing by releasing millions of spores B the number of forms they take as well as the chitin in their cell walls C their designation as a life form that is separate from plants and animals D their role as decomposers as well as their partnerships with other life forms

explanation.text: The author explains fungi breaking down dead matter and trading resources with living plants, while Mueller stresses that forests would be buried without them. Both therefore emphasize decomposition and partnerships with other life.

questions.alt: Question 22. Which detail from the article best supports the claim in paragraph 2 that members of Venturing Crew 115 enjoy going to places that are difficult to reach? “After the holidays, the Venturers get to work on their sleds. Those snowmobiles aren't A going to take care of themselves, after all.” (paragraph 8) “The crew conducts training exercises on a snow-covered airfield. It’s nice and flat and B __ wide open—perfect for beginners and those who need to brush up on their skills.” (paragraph 9) “They start by hauling the snowmobiles and camping gear as far as they can into the c wilderness until the road ends.’ (paragraph 10) “The crew spends several weeks taking classes on proper winter clothing and how to stay warm in the cold environment” (paragraph 16)

explanation.text: The crew drives trucks as far into the wilderness as the road allows, then switches to snowmobiles to travel several miles farther off-road. This journey directly shows their enjoyment of hard-to-reach places.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which statement best describes how the word choice in paragraphs 4 and 5 reflects the tone of the whole article? The explanation that the Venturers go “to the same spot” in paragraph 4 reflects the A comfortable tone of the article. The description of the natural “thick, high brush” in paragraph 4 reflects the peaceful B tone of the article. c The explanation that there is “12 feet of snow” in paragraph 5 reflects the anxious tone of the article. D The description of the Venturers’ wild campsite as a “paradise” in paragraph 5 reflects the positive tone of the article.

explanation.text: Despite twelve feet of snow in a spot unusable during summer, the author calls the winter campsite “practically paradise.” That enthusiastic description therefore reflects the positive tone used throughout.

questions.alt: Question 24. How are the details in paragraph 12 connected to the details in paragraph 14? A __ byshowing the first and last steps in the process of building the Venturers’ shelter B__ by describing the problem with shelter building and the solution the Venturers found C__ bycontrasting different methods the Venturers have used to build their shelter D _ byexplaining how the Venturers’ process of building a shelter has changed over time

explanation.text: Paragraph 12 begins construction by marking the fire circle and laying a wood platform. Paragraph 14 presents the result twelve hours later: a deep, warm, wind-protected ice shelter. Thus, they show the first and last stages.

questions.alt: Question 25. Which detail from the article most likely reveals the author’s opinion about the Venturers’ campsite? “When they get to their spot—about 5,000 feet above sea level—they begin setting up A camp,’ (paragraph 11) “As the fire burns, the platform drops deeper and deeper into the snow, at the rate of B about 1 foot per hour? (paragraph 12) Cc “It’s like a miniature coliseum, and it’s the perfect winter shelter—protected from the wind and warmed by the fire” (paragraph 14) “They are usually able to get back to the same spot the very next weekend for another D three nights” ree nights.’ (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: The author compares the completed campsite to a miniature coliseum and calls it “the perfect winter shelter.” This approving, evaluative language most clearly reveals the author's favorable opinion.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which sentence best states a central idea of the article? A __ Places where it is difficult to camp often make excellent spots for snowmobiling. B Venturing Crew 115 has to work hard to enjoy being at Snowcamp. C Snowmobiling can sometimes be dangerous for people with a lack of training. D Participating in Snowcamp requires special clothing to protect against the cold.

explanation.text: Crew members raise maintenance money, repair snowmobiles three times weekly, train, haul equipment, build the shelter for hours, and study cold-weather safety. These efforts support the central idea that Snowcamp fun requires hard work.

questions.alt: Question 27. Washington State law requires that all people aged 12 through 16 must pass a Snowmobile Safety Course before they drive a snowmobile. How does this idea connect with the details in the article? A __ Itexplains that Snowcamp is attended by relatively few people because of the strict rules. B It shows that the Venturers do the same amount of work as other people to learn snowmobiling. c It supports the idea that people in Washington State take pride in its snowmobiling opportunities. D__ It supports the idea that snowmobiling can be dangerous and requires practice and skill.

explanation.text: Requiring young operators to pass a safety course matches the crew's practice sessions, equipment checks, and weeks of winter classes. Both indicate that snowmobiling carries risks and requires learned skill.

questions.alt: Question 29. Paragraphs 1 and 3 contribute to the story by 29 A foreshadowing events that will happen later B providing details that develop the main character C explaining the problem that the characters will solve D describing events that happened at an earlier time

explanation.text: Paragraph 1 establishes Erin's advanced web-design skill, and paragraph 3 shows her discovering that she is a capable leader whom older students respect. These details primarily develop the main character.

questions.alt: Question 30. The word “modestly” in paragraph 4 suggests that the narrator A does not want to point out her own talents B does not feel that Tyler is being honest C__ gets teased by the others in her group D gets help from her family on her project

explanation.text: When Tyler praises Erin's knowledge, she credits her mother's profession instead of claiming her own talent. That understated response shows she does not want to draw attention to her abilities.

questions.alt: Question 31. What does the phrase “going live” mean as it is used in paragraph 8 of the story? 31 A making the content available to all B announcing the project to the school C requesting feedback from others D accessing the new computer system

explanation.text: The club initially asks only selected students for sample messages, but after the Intranet goes “live,” the entire school can access it and respond. Therefore, the phrase means making content available to all.

questions.alt: Question 32. What is the narrator’s opinion about the club’s name? 32 A It should be decided by the leader. B It should be a secret to students. C It should be more exciting. D It should be easier to understand.

explanation.text: Erin directly calls “Intranet Club” boring and starts a discussion of alternatives such as Webbies and I-Club. Her desire for a different name shows that she wants something more exciting.

questions.alt: Question 33. How are the narrator and Rosie similar? 33 A __ They both get annoyed at the computers the Intranet Club uses. B They are both interested in telling the school about the Intranet Club. C__ They both take their responsibilities in the Intranet Club seriously. D They are both worried that the Intranet Club interferes with their sports.

explanation.text: Erin carefully leads her group and teaches web design, while Rosie angrily works through a login problem because her letters must be finished by week's end. Both take their assigned club duties seriously.

questions.alt: Question 34. Which sentence best states a theme of the story? 34 A Working together can make people feel closer. B Completing a task can cause a feeling of pride. C Asking for help can be difficult. D Trying something new can be frightening.

explanation.text: Students of different grades collaborate on pages, seek one another's help, praise skills, joke about names, and spend break together. Their growing ease as teammates supports the theme that shared work can bring people closer.

questions.alt: Question 35. This story takes place at a time years ago when computers were not common in classrooms and very few students were taught programming skills. How does this information help develop an important idea about the characters in the story? A Students in the Intranet Club are all patient people who work well with others because they have to share the few computers available to them. Students in the Intranet Club are all exceptional because they try an activity that is new, B difficult, and unknown by other students at their school. c Students in the Intranet Club are all very mature because they choose to work with computers knowing that it will help them in their future careers. D Students in the Intranet Club are persuasive people because they are able to convince their schools to try new technology before it is widely accepted.

explanation.text: At a time when classroom computers and programming instruction were rare, these students learn HTML and build a schoolwide private website. Choosing and mastering such unfamiliar, difficult work therefore marks them as exceptional.

questions.alt: Question 1. In paragraphs 3 through 5, how does the narrator’s opinion affect the description of the theater? A The narrator believes the theater is confusing and is glad to have a guided tour. B__The narrator believes the theater is boring, so she considers sliding down the banister. C The narrator believes the theater is unwelcoming because children are forbidden. D The narrator believes the theater is grand, so she describes its structures and elegance.

explanation.text: The narrator calls the staircase the widest and fanciest imaginable and notices polished wood, gold trim, rich carpet, and elegance. These admiring details show that she considers the theater grand.

questions.alt: Question 2. As used in paragraph 11, what does the word “awed” suggest about Aunt Sally? 2 A She is distracted by the activity in the theater. B She is amazed by the magnificence of the place. C She is bothered by the patterns on the wallpaper. D She is embarrassed by the comments of the narrator.

explanation.text: Aunt Sally becomes so absorbed in the theater's splendor, crystal chandelier, and colored reflections that she does not hear the narrator or nearby laughter. Therefore, “awed” means amazed by magnificence.

questions.alt: Question 3. What do the details in paragraphs 10 through 12 reveal about the narrator? A __ She is from a small town that is less busy than San Francisco. B___ She wishes she had dressed more nicely for the theater. C __ She often visits the city of San Francisco. D She admires all the objects in the theater.

explanation.text: The narrator blurts out that the theater's 2,400 seats exceed the population of her whole town, and the guide contrasts that with San Francisco's 400,000 residents. These numbers reveal her small-town background.

questions.alt: Question 4. What is the meaning of the word “dispatched” as it is used in paragraph 14? 4 A made to leave the theater B ordered to speak quietly C asked to join the performance D told to listen carefully

explanation.text: The guide orders guests to remain silent and unnoticed like flies on a wall, warning that anyone attracting attention will be promptly “dispatched.” In context, this means made to leave the theater.

questions.alt: Question 5. Music can be a powerful force for some people. How do the details in paragraphs 15 through 18 5 support this idea in the story? A by showing the audience’s reaction to Caruso’s appearance B by telling where Caruso stands during the performance C by describing the narrator’s reaction to Caruso’s singing D by highlighting how Caruso walks to the center of the stage

explanation.text: Caruso's voice captures the narrator, makes her skin tingle and heart race, causes her to listen with her whole body, and finally takes her breath away. These intense reactions show music's power over her.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail represents a turning point in the direction of the story? A The cashier gives pieces of paper to the narrator, Aunt Sally, and Mr. Steinberg. B__ The theater guide leads the guests to the foot of a wide, fancy staircase. C Mr. Caruso walks onto the stage and begins to sing. D Mr Steinberg leads the narrator and Aunt Sally to their seats.

explanation.text: The story shifts from touring and admiring the building to its emotional climax when Caruso enters and sings. His performance transforms the narrator's experience and therefore marks the turning point.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A The narrator and Aunt Sally attend an opera rehearsal with Mr. Steinberg. B The narrator wonders if Aunt Sally also wants to slide down the banister. C Aunt Sally tells the narrator to think about past guests of the opera house. D_ Aunt Sally and Mr. Steinberg sit on either side of the narrator in the balcony.

explanation.text: The central event is the narrator attending a rare Caruso rehearsal with Aunt Sally and Mr. Steinberg and being overwhelmed by the performance. Therefore, that basic who-and-what detail is essential to a summary.

questions.alt: Question 15. Read this sentence from paragraph 5. When a baby elephant first arrives at the orphanage nursery, one of the biggest problems it faces is grief. What does the use of the word “grief” in the article show about baby elephants? A They have strong emotions. B They are unable to be cared for by humans. C They have difficulty bonding with caretakers. D___ They must learn to drink from a bottle.

explanation.text: A separated elephant can become so deeply sad that it grows ill or dies, requiring keepers to serve as loving substitute family. Calling this sorrow “grief” shows that baby elephants have powerful emotions.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which sentence best supports the author's indication that raising baby elephants is a great responsibility? “They've been rescuing orphan elephants and successfully reintroducing them to the A wild for more than 25 years.’ (paragraph 2) “When a baby elephant first arrives at the orphanage nursery, one of the biggest problems it faces is grief” (paragraph 5) “They spend every hour—waking and sleeping—with the little orphans, forming a Cc lifelong friendship.” (paragraph 5) D “Sometimes they have to teach a baby how to drink from a bottle” (paragraph 6)

explanation.text: Keepers remain with each orphan every hour, both waking and sleeping, while forming a lifelong relationship. This constant, long-term commitment most strongly supports the great responsibility involved.

questions.alt: Question 17. Read this sentence from paragraph 5. 17 Elephants are highly intelligent, loving family members. Which detail from the article best supports this claim? “. . . healthy elephants can live 70 years or longer, and their development is remarkably A similar to ours.” (paragraph 3) “. . . a baby elephant will stand under its mother and rest its trunk on her body as it B nurses with its mouth.” (paragraph 6) “. . . it’s ready to move from the nursery to Tsavo National Park, where it will eventually C be released into the wild elephant community.” (paragraph 9) “. . . previously released orphans welcome and befriend the newcomers and help teach D them proper elephant behavior.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: Previously released orphans return to welcome newcomers, form friendships, and teach them how elephants behave. These caring social actions directly support the claim that elephants are intelligent, loving family members.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which sentence from the article best shows that orphaned elephants often form long-term relationships with human keepers? “It has to be taught how to behave and communicate—even how to use its trunk, which A babies sometimes trip over while walking” (paragraph 4) “As they get more comfortable, the babies begin to wind their trunks around the keepers B instead.” (paragraph 6) c “Not only is the mud fun to play in, but the babies learn that a nice coating of gooey mud can cool them off on a hot day’ (paragraph 8) D “Tt never forgets its human family, though, and will often visit, helping to welcome the newest orphans.” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: Even after joining a wild herd, an elephant never forgets its human family and often returns to visit and welcome new babies. Continuing the relationship after release best demonstrates a lasting bond.

questions.alt: Question 19. How does paragraph 11 provide support for a central idea of the article? 19 A by identifying the age that is appropriate for orphan elephants to be released B by describing the way older elephants will welcome new orphans into a herd C by explaining why elephants are returned to the same stockade each night D by showing that baby elephants make strong connections when cared for

explanation.text: Paragraph 11 explains that every orphan leaves at its own pace, joins a new elephant family, and still returns to its human keepers. This lasting contact supports the central idea that loving care creates strong connections.

questions.alt: Question 20. In the article, what is the main way the author develops the role of the caretakers? 20 by comparing the way elephants are treated in different parts of the world to the way they A are treated in Kenya by providing examples of the activities that orphaned elephants experience while living B at the Wildlife Trust by describing how orphan elephants are moved from the Wildlife Trust to Tsavo National C Park when they are a year old by explaining how the Wildlife Trust started its mission of saving orphaned elephants D several years before

explanation.text: The author therefore develops caretakers' roles through concrete nursery and park activities: feeding bottles, supplying blankets, leading mud baths, providing shelter, teaching foods, and guiding social adjustment.

questions.alt: Question 21. According to a well-known science professor, “Empathy is the ability to share and understand the 21 feelings of another.” How does the author develop this idea in the article? A by describing the process that orphaned elephants must go through to find a new herd B by including detailed descriptions of the activities of baby elephants at a national park by describing how caretakers imitate elephant behaviors to help baby elephants C experience love D by including information about the daily physical needs of rescued orphaned elephants

explanation.text: Keepers recreate a mother's loving care by offering blankets to cuddle, sheltering babies with parasols, applying protective mud with a shovel, and staying beside them. These imitations show empathy for the orphans' feelings.

questions.alt: Question 22. How is John Muir first introduced in paragraph 1 of the article? 22 A with a story about his life B with a listing of his accomplishments C with an example that shows the impact of his work D with a comparison between him and other conservationists

explanation.text: The article first notes that four million people enjoy Yosemite because one man fought for decades to preserve it. This present-day result introduces Muir through a clear example of his work's impact.

questions.alt: Question 23. What is the main way paragraphs 2 and 3 contribute to the organization of the article? 23 A by providing biographical information about Muir B by giving examples of what inspired Muir to study nature C by sharing stories that come from Muir’s writings D by highlighting Muir’s opinions about the importance of nature

explanation.text: Paragraphs 2 and 3 trace Muir's birth, immigration, farm work, love of learning, university studies, travels, factory job, and eye injury. These chronological life details therefore provide biographical background.

questions.alt: Question 24. Read this sentence from paragraph 5. Often, he carried “only a tin cup, a handful of tea, a loaf of bread” and a journal. What can the reader conclude about Muir from this sentence? A He was often poorly prepared for his trips. B _He usually preferred to be by himself. C He chose to live as simply as possible. D He made sacrifices to study nature.

explanation.text: Muir enters the mountains carrying only a cup, tea, bread, and a journal rather than extensive supplies or comforts. This deliberately short list indicates that he chose a very simple way of living.

questions.alt: Question 25. In paragraph 6, what is the main effect of the author’s word choice? 25 A to emphasize the number of glaciers found in Yosemite B to indicate the importance of the lectures Muir gave about Yosemite C to highlight the great size of some natural features in Yosemite D to illustrate the popularity of the articles Muir wrote about Yosemite

explanation.text: Words such as “majestic” waterfalls, “massive” granite cliffs, and “giant” sequoias repeatedly stress enormous scale. The author's word choice therefore highlights the great size of Yosemite's features.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which detail best expresses a central idea of the article? 26 A “His love of nature and his writings inspired others to take action . . .” (paragraph 1) “. . . he became restless and decided to explore the world and study nature . . .” B (paragraph 3) C “From there, he headed eastward to see the wilderness . . .” (paragraph 4) “. . . newspaper articles gave him a large audience interested in learning . . .” D (paragraph 6)

explanation.text: Muir's love and published observations build a public audience, inspire supporters, create parks and the Sierra Club, and leave a continuing legacy. The statement that his passion moved others to protect nature therefore unifies the article.

questions.alt: Question 27. How did Muir’s experience at a carriage factory most affect his life? 27 A It gave him a reason to leave college and explore the world. B It taught him about the hardships associated with certain jobs. C It showed him the importance of choosing to live in different places. D It made him think carefully about how he wanted to spend his time.

explanation.text: A factory accident temporarily blinds Muir and makes him fear never seeing nature again. Once his sight returns, he leaves factory work and devotes himself to exploring nature, showing that the scare changed how he chose to spend life.

questions.alt: Question 29. Read this sentence from paragraph 7. 29 Pretty soon Audie Brayburn was considered an Honorary Swamp Critter. Which important idea does the author develop in this sentence? A Audie has not told many people that he went to the swamp. B Audie seems to belong in his surroundings in the swamp. C Audie becomes interested in other animals in the swamp. D Audie does not remember how to get out of the swamp.

explanation.text: The swamp animals first hide, then recognize that Audie carries no weapons, enjoy his music, and accept his presence. Naming him an “Honorary Swamp Critter” therefore means he seems to belong among them.

questions.alt: Question 30. The author’s word choice in paragraph 9 affects the story by A __ introducing an anxious tone B__ introducing an excited tone C creating a playful tone D creating an impatient tone

explanation.text: Audie hears the unmistakable call he has awaited, races after it, and feels his heart beat in the repeated rhythm “kaPOW kaPOW.” This quick, emphatic language therefore creates an excited tone.

questions.alt: Question 31. In paragraph 11, the phrase “keeping the beautiful bird within earshot” suggests that 31 A Audie needs to hear the woodpecker to locate it B Audie hopes that hunters will leave the woodpecker alone C Audie wants to avoid frightening the woodpecker D Audie thinks that the song of the woodpecker is pleasant

explanation.text: Audie follows the call deeper into the swamp, pausing and cocking his ears whenever needed. Keeping the bird “within earshot” means he must continue hearing it in order to locate it.

questions.alt: Question 32. How does paragraph 12 relate to paragraph 16? 32 A Paragraph 12 introduces a new setting that is further described in paragraph 16. B Paragraph 12 describes a decision that the main character regrets in paragraph 16. C Paragraph 12 mentions an event that is described in a flashback in paragraph 16. D Paragraph 12 foreshadows an event that begins to occur in paragraph 16.

explanation.text: Paragraph 12 warns that the forest's unnatural silence occurs immediately before a major storm. In paragraph 16, rain begins just after Audie stores his photograph, so the earlier stillness foreshadows the storm.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which sentence best shows the rising action in the story? “From the time he was fifteen until he turned twenty, he worked for a bakery in southeast A Houston, (paragraph 2) “He had never seen so many old trees, including dead trees that were still standing, B perfect trees for woodpecker nests.” (paragraph 5) C “Only one creature on the entire planet made that sound, only one” (paragraph 10) D “Then he waved the photo in the air until it dried, and slipped it into the ammo can” (paragraph 16)

explanation.text: After background about Audie's lifelong dream and swamp visit, the unique ivory-bill call finally appears and launches his urgent pursuit. This new complication therefore begins the rising action toward finding and photographing the bird.

questions.alt: Question 34. Which statement best expresses a theme of the story? A __ Being alone in nature can relieve stress. B__ Trying a new activity is often rewarding. C Certain locations are potentially dangerous. D___ Some people are driven by their interests.

explanation.text: Audie works five years for a car, travels to the swamp for a childhood dream, and follows the bird despite heat, hunger, thirst, and danger. His persistence supports the theme that deep interests can drive people's choices.

questions.alt: Question 35. How does the author mainly convey Audie’s character in the story? 35 A by allowing the reader to understand the reasons for his tools B by describing his choices and actions to the reader C by helping the reader understand some of his childhood interests D by sharing his thoughts and concerns with the reader

explanation.text: The author shows Audie saving money, selecting equipment, patiently camping, earning the animals' trust, pursuing the call, and ignoring warning signs. These choices and actions primarily reveal his determined character.
