questions.alt: Question 1. How do paragraphs 1 and 2 develop the character of the narrator? A __ They emphasize the narrator's enthusiasm for learning from Grandmother Five Baskets. B They highlight how much the narrator's life has changed since her time with Grandmother Five Baskets. C__ They suggest that the narrator and Grandmother Five Baskets rely on each other. D They reveal how similar the narrator and Grandmother Five Baskets are.

explanation.text: The narrator's questions are "insistent," and she immediately runs to gather the shawl and basket when the lessons begin. These eager actions show her enthusiasm for learning from Grandmother Five Baskets.

questions.alt: Question 2. What is the importance of paragraph 8 in the story? 2 A It describes the steps of basketmaking. B It reveals the traits that make a good basketmaker. It gives information about the lessons that Grandmother Five Baskets wants the narrator C to learn. D It provides details about the history between Grandmother Five Baskets and the narrator.

explanation.text: Grandmother Five Baskets warns that the narrator will face tests, learn more than basketmaking, encounter personal problems, and ultimately need to help herself. This guidance explains the deeper life lessons she intends the narrator to learn.

questions.alt: Question 3. Which important idea does the author develop in paragraph 11? 3 A Basketmaking can be a useful skill to have. B People choose to learn new things at different ages. C It is a good idea to accept help when it is offered. D Young people should show respect to those who are older.

explanation.text: Most students begin the basket lessons at age twelve or thirteen, but women age twenty or older sometimes study as well. This range shows that people choose to learn new things at different ages.

questions.alt: Question 4. Some people say that the best way to gain a skill is by participating rather than simply hearing 4 about it or watching someone else. Which detail from the story best shows how this idea is represented? “She told me what I should look for, as we walked along the rutted red road toward the A stand of loblolly pine trees . . .” (paragraph 3) “Anna, this is a good time to gather the pine straw, since there was a heavy rain several B nights ago.” (paragraph 4) “Listening to all the talk among my womenfolk from the time I was small made me eager C for my chance to begin . . .” (paragraph 12) “I learned to rinse it well, and dry it carefully on old window screens laid on rocks in the D sun.” (paragraph 13)

explanation.text: Rather than merely listening to family stories, the narrator personally rinses the pine straw and dries it carefully so it will not mold. This hands-on work demonstrates how participating develops an actual skill.

questions.alt: Question 5. In paragraph 15, what does the phrase “a little lopsided, but mine” suggest about the narrator? 5 A She is proud of what she has created. B She is concerned about her basket’s appearance. C She understands the lessons Grandmother Five Baskets teaches. D She questions Grandmother Five Baskets’ basketmaking process.

explanation.text: The narrator recognizes that her first basket is imperfect, yet stresses that it belongs to her and feels tired but very pleased. Her acceptance of its flaws reveals pride in what she has made.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail most clearly shows that Grandmother Five Baskets is confident in the narrator’s 6 abilities? “Grandmother Five Baskets moved slowly through the woods, stopping now and again, A each time calling me back to look at one plant or another . . .” (paragraph 6) “Little one, you will find yourself tested now that you have decided to learn these B lessons.” (paragraph 8) “During the next few days, Grandmother Five Baskets showed me how to clean the pine C straw we had collected . . .” (paragraph 13) “Do not judge this first basket too harshly, for your skills will improve with each one you D complete.” (paragraph 16)

explanation.text: Grandmother Five Baskets tells the narrator not to judge the first attempt harshly because her skill will improve with every completed basket. That prediction most clearly shows confidence in the narrator's ability to grow.

questions.alt: Question 7. The author develops the character of Grandmother Five Baskets by showing A __ the steps of her basketmaking process B what she thinks about how the narrator makes a basket C__ howshe guides the narrator D__howshe feels about her role in the community

explanation.text: Grandmother Five Baskets identifies useful plants, explains the coming tests, demonstrates each preparation step, and encourages the narrator after her first basket. These repeated acts of instruction develop her character as a patient guide.

questions.alt: Question 15. How do the events in the section “A Happy Accident” contribute to the development of a central 15 idea of the article? A by describing what made Perkin want to aim for a higher income B by showing how Perkin began focusing on a new use for his discovery C by telling how Perkin learned about the importance of fashion to the public D by detailing when Perkin realized he was more a businessman than a scientist

explanation.text: Perkin's attempts to make quinine fail, but one messy chemical stains everything purple, leading him to recognize its value as a fabric dye. This shift from medicine to color shows how he begins focusing on a new use for an accidental discovery.

questions.alt: Question 16. Read this sentence from paragraph 7. These dyes could vary in shade (so you wouldn't always know what exact color they would turn cloth), and they could fade or rub off. How does this sentence best help the reader understand Perkin’s actions? A __ It shows that Perkin began to notice and follow cultural trends. B__It shows that Perkin saw an opportunity to work with his family. C__ It shows that Perkin identified a problem that needed a solution. D__It shows that Perkin recognized a way to impress the royal class.

explanation.text: Natural dyes produced uncertain shades and could fade or rub off, making reliable fabric coloring difficult. These limitations show that Perkin identifies a problem his consistent artificial dye can solve.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which part of the article best reflects the author’s opinion of the topic? 17 A the title “Miraculous Mauve” B the heading “Young Science, Young Scientist” C the details about quinine in paragraph 4 D the information about using natural materials as dyes in paragraph 7

explanation.text: Calling the color "miraculous" gives Perkin's accidental dye a strongly positive, almost extraordinary quality. That evaluative word most clearly shows the author's admiring opinion.

questions.alt: Question 18. As used in paragraph 9, what does the phrase “mauve mania” suggest? A __ People became obsessed with wearing the color. B The dye required an investment of a large sum of money. C_ The dye caused illness because of its chemical content. D__ People became angry that only royalty could wear the color.

explanation.text: After Empress Eugénie and Queen Victoria wear mauve, newspapers feature it and people adopt mauve dresses, hats, and bowties. This widespread excitement indicates that "mauve mania" means people became obsessed with wearing the color.

questions.alt: Question 19. Prominent or famous people often influence the culture in which they live. How do the details in paragraphs 9 and 10 develop this idea? A __ by describing the details of the royal fashions B___ by mentioning the building of a new factory C__bysuggesting that dye-making quickly became very profitable D _ byillustrating why people from all classes wanted to use the new color

explanation.text: The empress and queen make mauve fashionable, after which magazines publicize it and ordinary men and women want mauve clothing. Because Perkin's dye is affordable, their influence spreads the color beyond royalty to every social class.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? 20 “. . . a teenager named William Henry Perkin found himself captivated by this fascinating A science.” (paragraph 3) “. . . Young Perkin realized that this chemical would make a great dye for fabrics.” B (paragraph 6) “Perkin himself kept inventing, making more new colors, including Britannia Violet and C Perkin’s Green . . .” (paragraph 11) “Today, the Perkin Medal, named in his honor, is presented each year to a scientist in the D United States . . .” (paragraph 14)

explanation.text: Perkin's realization that a failed medical chemical could become the first artificial fabric dye is the discovery that drives the article's fashion, industrial, and scientific consequences. It belongs in a summary because it captures the central turning point rather than a supporting detail.

questions.alt: Question 21. How does the author develop the discovery of a purple dye in the article? 21 A by telling stories about moments in Perkin’s life B by describing the many benefits of the color mauve C by sharing the series of steps that led to a new color D by naming people who helped Perkin’s efforts along the way

explanation.text: The article traces Perkin's failed quinine experiments, the unexpected purple stain, his recognition of its use, and the work with his brother to perfect and name the dye. This sequence of steps explains how the new color is discovered and developed.

questions.alt: Question 22. As used in paragraph 2, what does the word “irritated” mean? 22 A injured B distracted C annoyed D offended

explanation.text: Moving the bees' homes makes them uncertain, aggressive, and likely to sting as they search for familiar hives. These reactions show that "irritated" means annoyed.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which idea best expresses a theme of paragraph 2? 23 A Animals react negatively when they encounter humans. B Familiar surroundings can bring calm to living creatures. C Humans have a positive influence when they enter nature. D People can have a negative effect on animal happiness.

explanation.text: The unfamiliar summer hives make the bees confused and combative, but recognition of their old home's odor brings them back together, fanning with joy. This contrast supports the theme that familiar surroundings can calm living creatures.

questions.alt: Question 24. What is the definition of “good-tempered” as used in paragraph 3? 24 A having a pleasant nature B being organized and ready C experiencing strong emotions D showing anger through actions

explanation.text: Because the bees are gathering plenty of honey, they are "good-tempered" and can be handled easily. Their cooperative behavior indicates that the phrase means having a pleasant nature.

questions.alt: Question 25. How do Alice’s actions in paragraphs 12 through 14 affect the resolution of the story? 25 A She shares her opinion about the situation with Carl and Bob. B She works with Carl and Bob to execute a plan for the two colonies. C She allows Carl and Bob the opportunity to learn from the experience. D She ensures that the bees are unable to sting Carl and Bob.

explanation.text: Alice agrees with Carl's proposal, applies smoke to both colonies, and coordinates with the brothers as Bob stacks the queenless hive over the weak one. This teamwork shows how her actions execute the plan, creating one viable colony and resolving both groups' problems.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which sentence shows a change in the direction of the story? A “They failed to recognize them? (paragraph 2) “Alice gently blew a little smoke across the entrance, driving in the guards; then she blew B a stronger puff” (paragraph 5) C “Here’s something wrong!” (paragraph 10) “Carl uncovered the weak colony again, while Bob pried off the bottom from the D queenless one.” (paragraph 14)

explanation.text: Before Alice's exclamation, the group is conducting a planned inspection of ordinary hive conditions. "Here's something wrong!" reveals an unexpected crisis and shifts the plot toward diagnosing and saving the queenless colony.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which detail best supports Alice’s claim in paragraph 16? A __ “Instead of a crowded mass of bees, only a little cluster showed . . ” (paragraph 8) B “The queen must have perished in the winter” (paragraph 11) C “... alittle disturbance as the bees from the two colonies mixed” (paragraph 15) D “Then all was quiet; a contented hum arose within.” (paragraph 15)

explanation.text: Alice claims the two colonies can thrive together even though neither would succeed alone. The quiet, contented hum after the bees combine shows that the union has produced a stable, successful colony.

questions.alt: Question 29. Which detail from paragraph 1 best develops Chengli’s claim that the journey is long and dull? A “Three weeks on the road” B “Hour after hour, it was all the same” C “..sometimes a farmer could be seen walking behind his ox...” D “The caravan did not at these villages . ..”

explanation.text: The phrase "Hour after hour, it was all the same" combines repeated time with unchanging scenery and movement. It most directly supports Chengli's claim that the journey feels both long and dull.

questions.alt: Question 30. What effect does the phrase “and now this” in paragraph 3 have on the tone of the story? A __Itcreates a fearful tone. B__It creates a surprised tone. C__ It develops a determined tone. D__It develops a hopeless tone.

explanation.text: Chengli has already listed endless walking and hard labor when he adds "and now this" before describing still more rain. The phrase suggests that the new hardship is one burden too many, developing a hopeless tone.

questions.alt: Question 31. Which theme is developed in paragraphs 1 through 6? A__Itis difficult to be patient. B Hard work is sometimes a test of endurance. C Nature is beautiful. D People can find joy in bad situations.

explanation.text: Chengli continues despite three weeks of endless walking, aching legs and arms, burning hands, heavy loads, mud, and steady rain. These physical demands support the theme that hard work can test a person's endurance.

questions.alt: Question 32. What do the details in paragraph 9 reveal about Chengli? 32 A He desires better working conditions. B He prefers the company of animals over people. C He cares about the well-being of the animals. D He is eager to impress the people with whom he works.

explanation.text: Chengli makes the camels comfortable, checks every animal for injuries, and repairs each damaged sack, harness, or rope before allowing himself to eat. This careful routine reveals genuine concern for the animals' well-being.

questions.alt: Question 33. What are the different ways Chengli and Fourth Brother view their roles in the caravan in 33 paragraphs 6 through 10? A Chengli feels ignored, while Fourth Brother feels undervalued. Chengli is assigned to the animals he prefers, while Fourth Brother is assigned to B creatures he dislikes. C Chengli believes he has been rewarded, while Fourth Brother feels he has been punished. D Chengli shows care for the animals, while Fourth Brother shows disregard for them.

explanation.text: Chengli learns to calm the camels and takes satisfaction in keeping them healthy, whereas Fourth Brother calls his donkeys lazy and resents caring for them. This contrast shows Chengli's care and Fourth Brother's disregard for their animals.

questions.alt: Question 34. What character trait is best illustrated by Chengli’s thoughts and actions in paragraphs 9 and 10? 34 A an ability to adapt B shyness C playfulness D a desire for freedom

explanation.text: Although a camel initially spits at him, Chengli learns within a week how to keep the animals calm and master their detailed care. His adjustment to unfamiliar animals and difficult caravan work demonstrates an ability to adapt.

questions.alt: Question 35. President Barack Obama said in his farewell address in 2017, “If you're walking down the right path, and you're willing to keep walking, eventually you'll make progress.” Which detail best shows how this idea is represented in the story? “He moved back along the line of camels assigned to his care, checking each load until he A came to Fourth Brother, who was checking on his own assigned animals, the donkeys” (paragraph 6) “As the day wore on, however, the rain slackened and finally stopped. Master chose a B resting spot on well-drained land, and the call came down the line to settle for the night” (paragraph 8) “The warmth of early spring made their thick, wooly fur begin to loosen and fall out, but when Chengli first tried to brush it, the camel turned and aimed a mouthful of stinking Cc spit at him. Within the first week, however, hed learned how to keep the animals calm” (paragraph 9) “Uncle Tao often made his way to the end of the line to see that Chengli was learning the D finer points of caring for the camels, quietly teaching the boy whatever he needed to understand” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: Chengli's first attempt to brush a camel ends with it spitting at him, but he continues working until he learns how to calm the animals. His persistence and improved skill show how continuing on the right path eventually produces progress.

questions.alt: Question 36. Read this sentence from paragraph 3. Areas of the United States with especially colorful fall displays attract thousands of leaf peepers. Which phrase helps the reader to understand the meaning of “leaf peepers” as used in the sentence? A “These tourists flock to the region . ..” (paragraph 1) B “Being in the Northeast during autumn . . ” (paragraph 2) C ‘Lee studies leaf color ..” (paragraph 3) D_ = “The chemical chlorophyll, which gives leaves their green color . .” (paragraph 4)

explanation.text: Paragraph 1 identifies people who flock to New England to view changing leaves as tourists. That description explains that "leaf peepers" are visitors who come to look at colorful fall foliage.

questions.alt: Question 37. Which statement best expresses the author's belief in paragraph 6? A What people enjoy now will change over time. B__ Scientists should avoid trying to make predictions. C__ There is much for scientists to learn now and in the future. D The behavior of trees is too complex for people to fully comprehend.

explanation.text: The author lists unanswered questions about why species and neighboring trees turn different colors and how global warming will alter forests. These uncertainties support the belief that scientists still have much to learn now and in the future.

questions.alt: Question 38. How does the section “Food factory” contribute to the article’s organization? A __ Itexplains the cause of leaf color change in some plants. B___ It provides a counterargument in the debate as to why leaves change color. C__Itintroduces the idea that color change may be harmful to trees. D __ It presents a problem that scientists are attempting to solve through experimentation.

explanation.text: The section explains that shorter, colder days break down green chlorophyll, revealing yellow and orange carotenoids, while some plants newly produce red anthocyanins. These chemical causes explain the color changes in different leaves.

questions.alt: Question 39. What does the phrase “work like a sunscreen” mean as it is used in paragraph 13? A Certain chemicals cause tree leaves to change color when the seasons change. B The same chemical can be used by people and plants for sun protection. C Leaves are drying up from the heat of the sun when they turn red. D The color red may shield plants from environmental damage.

explanation.text: Anthocyanins turn leaves red when chlorophyll loss leaves them vulnerable to harsh sunlight, reducing sun damage and allowing continued nutrient absorption. This protective effect explains that red color may shield plants from environmental harm like sunscreen shields skin.

questions.alt: Question 40. What is the relationship between the author’s ideas in paragraphs 20 and 21? A The author cites ongoing studies, then proposes reader action. B The author details current research, then challenges its results. C__ The author introduces new ideas, then summarizes the supporting evidence. D The author recalls old disagreements, then invites the reader to make a judgment.

explanation.text: Paragraph 20 describes the additional species and conditions scientists are studying to settle debates about red leaves. Paragraph 21 then invites readers to conduct their own observations and experiments, so ongoing research is followed by proposed reader action.

questions.alt: Question 41. Which claim is most strongly supported with evidence in the article? 41 A People enjoy viewing the changes in leaf color in the fall. B Red leaf color may have different benefits for plants. C Red leaf color discourages insects from eating the leaves. D Humans may affect how leaf color changes in the future.

explanation.text: Studies suggest red anthocyanins protect leaves from sunlight, may improve cold survival, and may discourage insects, although that last explanation is debated. This range of evidence most strongly supports the broader claim that red color can provide different benefits to plants.

questions.alt: Question 42. How do the references to scientists David Lee and Bill Hoch support a central idea of the article? A __ Bothscientists agree that leaf color change is a beautiful sight. B__ Eachscientist rejects the other’s ideas about leaf color change. C___ These scientists contribute different ideas to the study of leaf color change. D These scientists want to create new species of plants to study leaf color change.

explanation.text: Hoch investigates sunlight protection and cold survival, while Lee considers insect behavior and researches redness in tropical young leaves. Their distinct questions and interpretations show how both scientists contribute different ideas to the study of leaf color.

exams.title: New York Grade 8 ELA - 2026 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2026 New York State Grade 8 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2026 NYS Grade 8 English Language Arts Test Released Questions

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 1-7. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 15-21. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 22-27. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 29-35. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 36-42. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. Based on paragraph 1, what does the word “obscured” mean? 1 A denied entry to B raised high above C existed far away from D limited the visibility of

explanation.text: The tangled vegetation lets the narrator see only about fifty feet before everything disappears behind it. This context shows that "obscured" means limited the visibility of.

questions.alt: Question 2. What idea do paragraphs 7 through 9 mainly contribute to the story? A The narrator can find things that will help him. B__ The Mississippi River is slowly losing water. C__ The narrator knows his geography well. D The United States offers opportunities for travel.

explanation.text: The river carries a steady stream of discarded objects, and useful items eventually pass by or wash ashore. This explains how the narrator can find supplies that help him live on the island.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does the phrase “keep your eyes peeled” mean as used in paragraph 9? 3 A to gaze longingly B to view positively C to look carefully D to glance casually

explanation.text: Useful objects may pass in the river or wash ashore only if the narrator notices them. In this context, "keep your eyes peeled" means watch or look carefully.

questions.alt: Question 4. The details in paragraphs 14 through 16 help convey a theme of the story by showing how the A feels at home on the island B___ seems out of touch with nature C feels lonely on the island D__longs to return home

explanation.text: The narrator furnishes his shelter with salvaged drawers, a cooler, books, a chair, and decorations, then expands it into rooms and adds a mailbox for a "homey feeling." These choices show that he is making himself feel at home on the island.

questions.alt: Question 5. The idiom “One man’s trash is another man’s treasure” means that what seems like waste to one person may be valuable to another. Which sentence from the story supports this idea? “It was slow going, but using my hands, I fashioned a lean-to from willow whips— A slender, limber branches torn from the trees that grew along the river” (paragraph 5) “I found a set of canisters, a bookshelf, a wicker wastebasket, a brass antelope, and a B chipped yellow flowerpot.” (paragraph 14) c “When you're the only person for miles around, Fitch is as good a name as any.” (paragraph 17) D “A soft clink-clink sound from my bedroom told me that the thief had found the money.” (paragraph 27)

explanation.text: Canisters, a discarded bookshelf, a wastebasket, an ornament, and even a chipped flowerpot become useful furnishings and decorations in the narrator's home. His reuse of objects others threw away supports the idea that one person's trash can become another person's treasure.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail shows a change in the direction of the story? 6 A “All and all, I assured myself, this is a pretty nice place to be.” (paragraph 10) “I dragged a chest of drawers to my hideout, and a picnic cooler that still contained ice.” B (paragraph 14) C “Enough’s enough! I said to myself.” (paragraph 21) D “His masked eyes glowed like smoldering coals.” (paragraph 31)

explanation.text: Before paragraph 21, the narrator peacefully builds a home and inventories mysteriously missing objects. His declaration that he has had enough shows the plot shifting toward setting traps and confronting the thief.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which element does the author use to develop the narrator’s viewpoint in the story? 7 A conflict between the characters B flashback to the narrator’s past C dialogue between the characters D description of the narrator’s thoughts

explanation.text: The story repeatedly gives the narrator's private reactions, such as deciding the island is pleasant, naming the heron, dismissing the mailbox name, and panicking during the confrontation. These descriptions reveal and develop his humorous, resourceful viewpoint.

questions.alt: Question 15. In paragraph 5, the phrase “spirit of competitiveness” refers to 15 A an expectation that her plans will succeed B an interest in working cooperatively C a desire to be the best she could D a belief that patience is important

explanation.text: Roxie does not seek to outperform her coworkers; instead, she wants to improve beyond her own previous ability. This self-directed comparison shows that her "spirit of competitiveness" is a desire to become the best she can be.

questions.alt: Question 16. According to paragraphs 6 and 7, Roxie can best be described as a person who 16 A cared about her work and was constantly learning B appreciated the support of other scientists at the museum C decided to devote her life to preparing and studying birds D preferred taxidermy to her job of preparing specimens

explanation.text: Roxie treats every specimen as a treasured work of art and develops deep bird knowledge by confronting challenges and considering possibilities. These details show both care for her work and a commitment to continual learning.

questions.alt: Question 17. Advancements in one field of science often lead to advancements in other scientific fields. Which detail from the article best supports this idea? “To find the subtle differences in the families’ feathers, Roxie carefully examined them A and drew precise pen-and-ink line drawings . .” (paragraph 9) “Examining the downy portion of the feather under the microscope, Roxie discovered B little branches, called barbs.” (paragraph 10) c “Her findings assisted engineers in building safer aircraft by designing jet engines and windscreens more resilient to bird strikes” (paragraph 11) D “The key to what had killed the owl was Roxie’s discovery that the magpie had eaten bugs that had been sprayed with an insecticide . .” (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: Roxie's biological research on identifying birds by microscopic feather structures helps engineers create jet engines and windscreens that withstand bird strikes. This transfer from feather science to aircraft design demonstrates one scientific field advancing another.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which detail from the article best expresses a central idea of always gaining more knowledge? 18 A “Over time, Roxie’s research improved aviation safety.” (paragraph 11) “As an expert in her field, Roxie assisted the Fish and Wildlife Department in discovering B what killed birds of prey.” (paragraph 12) “Roxie never wrote a book on her feather science because she felt she’d never finished C learning all there was to learn.” (paragraph 13) “Only by using Roxie’s methods of examining microscopic barbules in the sample were D the researchers able to learn that a black vulture had fed on the deer. . . .” (paragraph 14)

explanation.text: Roxie refuses to write a final book because she believes she has not finished learning everything feather science can teach. That belief most directly expresses the central idea that knowledge is something people continually gain.

questions.alt: Question 19. What is the best way paragraph 12 connects to paragraph 14? A __ by describing how Roxie’s research was used to solve a mystery B___ by comparing the feeding habits of different types of animals C __ bysuggesting that DNA analysis is superior to Roxie’s method D _ byexplaining that aircraft are frequently struck by birds during flights

explanation.text: Paragraph 12 traces how Roxie identifies feathers through a three-animal food chain to solve an owl poisoning. Paragraph 14 presents a later laboratory using her microscopic method to resolve a puzzling deer DNA result, so both describe her research solving mysteries.

questions.alt: Question 20. What does paragraph 14 reveal about the author's viewpoint? The author wants to persuade readers to visit a museum to see bird samples. P Pp The author wants to demonstrate that Roxie’s feather research is still important today. P' Y- The author wants to inform readers about the danger birds face from airplanes. 8 Pp. The author wants to prove that Roxie’s method of analysis is better than other kinds of D analysis.

explanation.text: The modern Smithsonian lab handles thousands of bird strikes and still uses Roxie's barbule method when DNA gives a confusing answer. This current success reveals the author's view that her research remains important today.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which claim by the author is most strongly supported with evidence throughout the article? 21 A Roxie wished to increase her understanding of the natural world. B Roxie thought the work she was doing with feathers was important. C Roxie believed that scientists should be receptive to new information. D Roxie wanted students to keep studying despite natural limits to knowledge.

explanation.text: From childhood observation through museum study, microscopic investigation, daily discoveries, and lifelong mentoring, Roxie continually seeks to learn more about living things. This repeated evidence most strongly supports the claim that she wanted to deepen her understanding of the natural world.

questions.alt: Question 22. Paragraphs 1 through 6 most develop Torix’s viewpoint by 22 A showing how he analyzes a problem and devises a solution B revealing what he has learned by observing the legate C demonstrating how he interacts with other characters D presenting the close family relationships in his life

explanation.text: Torix reflects that the legate is not cruel like the soldiers, notices his infected leg, and gathers honey, wool, and bandages based on his grandfather's healing practice. This sequence shows him analyzing the problem and devising a treatment that can also create a connection.

questions.alt: Question 23. How does Gaius’s attitude toward Torix change in paragraphs 5 through 7? A __ It shifts from annoyed to appreciative. B__It shifts from anxious to relaxed. C__Itshifts from distracted to thoughtful. D __Itshifts from critical to proud.

explanation.text: Gaius initially scowls and asks about the bag with growing irritation. Once Torix explains his helpful purpose, the legate's face softens and he thanks the boy sincerely, showing a shift from annoyance to appreciation.

questions.alt: Question 24. Read this sentence from paragraph 9. The legate’s face bore the soft expression of a young boy being cared for by a doting mother. What does the phrase “bore the soft expression” suggest about the legate? A _He wishes to be reunited with his mother. B __ He becomes uninterested rather quickly. C He appears to be relaxed and pleased. D = _ He has a carefree and childlike personality.

explanation.text: The comparison to a young boy receiving a doting mother's care follows Torix's gentle cleaning and bandaging of the wound. The legate's softened face therefore suggests that he feels relaxed and pleased by the attention.

questions.alt: Question 25. What change in plot occurs in paragraph 12? 25 A Gaius buys goods from the local shops, which increases sales for the owners. B Gaius’s leg wound heals, so the Roman doctor does not return to the fort. C Gaius makes visits to see Torix and Grandfather, and becomes friendly with them. Gaius has the cobbler make him new boots, because the old ones were ruined during his D injury.

explanation.text: What begins as necessary wound treatment becomes three months of daily visits, shared tea, neighborhood conversations, and adventure stories. This progression shows the plot changing as Gaius, Torix, and Grandfather become friends.

questions.alt: Question 26. What is the reason Torix wants to build a relationship with Gaius? 26 A Torix thinks he can gain valuable experience in caring for Gaius’s wounds. B Torix believes Gaius may solve his problem with the soldiers. C Torix wants to encourage Gaius to spend money on Dere Street. D Torix hopes Gaius will ask him to care for the three-legged dog, Tribus.

explanation.text: Torix remembers his grandfather's advice about finding an antidote to thousands of enemies and notices that the powerful legate disapproves of his tormentors. This reasoning shows why Torix hopes befriending Gaius will solve his problem with the soldiers.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which sentence best states a theme of the story? A __ Pets provide trustworthy companionship. B___ Big problems often require complex solutions. C Adults can learn important lessons from children. D Friendship can be a very powerful tool.

explanation.text: Torix's friendship with one legate changes how thousands of Roman soldiers treat him, replacing teasing with respectful help. This transformation supports the theme that friendship can be an exceptionally powerful tool.

questions.alt: Question 29. The description in paragraphs 1 and 2 of the difference between Greta’s and other people's attitude about the fog shows that A __ Greta behaves in ways that cause problems for her parents B Greta does not care that the fog hinders people's work routines C __ Greta is attracted to the fog in a special and mysterious way D __ Greta is not concerned with other people’s opinions of her

explanation.text: Villagers accept but dislike the fog because it disrupts farming and fishing, whereas even its first traces make Greta's eyes sparkle and draw her outdoors. Her intense delight shows that the fog attracts her in a special, mysterious way.

questions.alt: Question 30. The author’s word choice in paragraph 3 creates a tone of A__ boredom, because Greta does not foresee anything interesting happening B__ peacefulness, because the pasture is quiet and daily life is predictable C__ suspense, because there is a hint of something unexpected to come D contrast, because Greta realizes she is different from other children

explanation.text: Greta begins to feel that she is searching for something hidden in the fog, and the narrator hints that one particular trip changed everything. These hints suggest an unexpected discovery is coming, creating suspense.

questions.alt: Question 31. What idea does paragraph 5 most contribute to the story? 31 A Greta’s attachment to the area and its history B the isolation of the land where Greta lives C the beauty of the landscape where Greta lives D Greta’s curiosity about historical activities

explanation.text: Greta knows every stone and curve of the Old Post Road, values its romantic quality, and connects it with the route traveled by her ancestors. These details chiefly develop her attachment to both the place and its history.

questions.alt: Question 32. In paragraph 8, Greta’s reaction when she finds Rosie suggests that 32 A she is amused by Rosie’s independent spirit B she is happy to have found Rosie at all in the fog C she is surprised at how far from home Rosie had wandered D she is pleased that the search for Rosie brought her to this place

explanation.text: Rosie has wandered far from the road but looks completely innocent rather than guilty when Greta finds her. Greta's laughter suggests affectionate amusement at the cow's stubborn, independent behavior.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which detail best supports a theme in the story? “The men who fished dreaded it for it either kept them on shore altogether and cut down A __ their meager earnings, or it made their hours on the sea more dangerous than ever” (paragraph 1) “Years before, a number of villages had dotted the shore on either side. Now, only a few B were left and those were dwindling in size . .” (paragraph 4) “Greta knew every stone, every curve of it for miles up over the high pastures and then c down again toward the sea.” (paragraph 5) “Fog had always seemed to her like the magic spell in the old fairy tales—a spell that D caught you up and kept you as safe...” (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: Greta imagines the fog as a fairy-tale spell that encloses her safely rather than as a danger. This perspective supports the theme that openness to mystery and wonder can reveal extraordinary possibilities in familiar places.

questions.alt: Question 34. Read this sentence from paragraph 12. She caught her breath and stared. This sentence suggests that Greta is A __ frustrated by Rosie's behavior B__ stunned by the vision before her C__ worried about what she might find D__ exhausted by her journey to find Rosie

explanation.text: Greta stops breathing normally and can only stare at the outline of a house where no house should exist. This frozen reaction suggests that the vision has stunned her.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which detail from the story best foreshadows Greta’s discovery? 35 A “. . . she began to sense that she was looking for something within the fog.” (paragraph 3) B “And then there was the day when old Rosie was particularly stubborn.” (paragraph 6) “Rosie isn’t at the bars with the other cows. . . . You’d best go look for her . . .” C (paragraph 7) “It was the most exciting thing that had ever happened to her in her whole life.” D (paragraph 13)

explanation.text: Before any house appears, Greta senses that she has been searching for something inside the fog. That unexplained expectation suggests a discovery will occur, foreshadowing the mysterious building she later sees.

questions.alt: Question 36. What does the phrase “paving the way,” as used in paragraph 2, show about Seager? 36 A She wants to be the first to discover new forms of life. B She is trying to find out what is in the atmosphere of hot planets. C She is creating opportunities for future exploration. D She wants to learn more about exoplanets and their atmospheres.

explanation.text: Seager's discoveries about hot-planet atmospheres lead to new detection technology and methods for studying rocky, Earth-like worlds. Thus, "paving the way" shows that her work creates opportunities for future exploration.

questions.alt: Question 37. How does the information in paragraphs 1 through 3 contribute to the understanding of the rest 37 of the article? A It explains the type of work Seager does as an astrophysicist. B It identifies the different teams that Seager works with. C It indicates that there may be life outside of our planet. D It explains how to identify a planet outside our solar system.

explanation.text: The opening identifies Seager as an astrophysicist who studies exoplanet light, atmospheres, possible signs of life, and several space missions. This overview explains the work later discussed in the interview's questions about skills, daily research, detection methods, and Venus.

questions.alt: Question 38. In paragraph 5, what is the meaning of the word “adversity”? 38 A results from actions B difficulties in life C large work assignments D opponents of an individual

explanation.text: Seager describes failed projects and adult bullies as challenges an astrophysicist must learn to face. These examples indicate that "adversity" means difficulties in life.

questions.alt: Question 39. Read this sentence from paragraph 5. 39 Sports are helpful that way, because you can fail there—your skill and commitment have a consequence. What does this sentence reveal about Seager’s beliefs? A Commitment to a project is necessary. B Making mistakes in an activity is upsetting. C Consequences keep individuals from trying. D Failure is an essential part of learning.

explanation.text: Seager says children need opportunities to fail and that sports make the effects of skill and commitment visible. This view reveals her belief that experiencing failure is an essential way to learn and improve.

questions.alt: Question 40. Which idea best expresses a central idea of paragraph 6? 40 A Success can be found by completing goals gradually. B People usually find that it is easier to pay attention to many little tasks. C People with big ideas can finish projects more quickly. D Completing a large goal is a good way to find success.

explanation.text: Seager advises dividing a huge idea into the smallest possible parts, like concentrating on each step rather than the entire marathon. Feeling progress through those steps supports the central idea that gradual completion leads to success.

questions.alt: Question 41. Which detail best shows how scientists determine if there could be life on an exoplanet? 41 “We can basically look for what life does here. It uses chemistry to store and release A energy.” (paragraph 11) “Signs of life won’t necessarily indicate if it is microbes, plants, or intelligent life.” B (paragraph 12) “For that I have a core team that has different branches focused on different things . . .” C (paragraph 13) “We have partnered up and are helping choose their instruments. It would be a small D rocket . . .” (paragraph 13)

explanation.text: Scientists begin with what Earth life does: it uses chemistry to store and release energy, producing gases in the process. Detecting comparable chemical byproducts in an exoplanet atmosphere can therefore suggest that life could exist there.

questions.alt: Question 42. In the article “In Defense of Curiosity,” Eleanor Roosevelt wrote, “In its simplest form, curiosity 42 will help you to an all-around education.” How does Sara Seager represent this idea? Seager is a professor who spends part of each day teaching and training students to be A scientists. Seager spends most of her time studying and researching in order to learn about B exoplanets. Seager’s team uses different techniques and scientific methods to study exoplanets and C atmospheres. Seager’s unique thinking and interests have driven her to learn about many different D topics.

explanation.text: Seager's curiosity and creative questions lead her across math, programming, telescope data, computer modeling, exoplanet detection, atmospheric chemistry, biology, instrumentation, and Venus missions. This breadth demonstrates how unique interests and thinking can produce an all-around education in many topics.

questions.alt: Question 1. In paragraph 7, what does Sylvie mean when she says, “Sounds more stellar, doesn’t 1 it?” A She wants people to think the event will be amazing. B She wants people to be sure art is part of the event. C She wants people to bring their own paint to the event. D She wants people to know the event will help animals.

explanation.text: Sylvie changes the event's name to “Paint Out” and calls it “stellar” because she wants the event to sound exciting and special. In this sentence, “stellar” means amazing.

questions.alt: Question 2. What do the details in paragraphs 2 and 9 show about Sylvie? 2 A She likes playing on the swings. B She cares about animals. C She knows a lot about the town. D She is a talented artist.

explanation.text: Sylvie says they should do something special for animals and proposes donating the painting money to an animal shelter. These ideas show that she cares about animals.

questions.alt: Question 3. Based on paragraph 14, why does Sammy make a confused face in paragraph 15? 3 A He is surprised that Sylvie thinks it is getting late. B He is disappointed that Sylvie was ready before him. C He is worried his parents did not pack everything. D He is not sure why they need a booth for the dog.

explanation.text: Sylvie says it is getting late even though it is only 6:15 in the morning. Sammy's confused face shows that he is surprised she thinks such an early time is late.

questions.alt: Question 4. “Preparation is the key to success” is a common saying that means when you plan 4 for an event, it is more likely to go well. Which paragraph best shows how this idea is demonstrated in the story? A paragraph 1 B paragraph 3 C paragraph 11 D paragraph 15

explanation.text: Paragraph 11 lists the work Sylvie and Sammy complete before the event: getting permission, contacting the shelter, and inviting people. It demonstrates the saying because this preparation helps make their Paint Out successful.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which detail best expresses a central idea of the story? A “‘Hmm: Sammy tilted his head. ‘I have a few ideas too? ” (paragraph 3) “Then we can sell the paintings. We can donate the money to the Sea View B Animal Shelter” (paragraph 9) “Sylvie looked around. A balloon arch spelled ‘Happy Make a Difference c Day’ from streetlight to streetlight” (paragraph 19) “Sylvie nodded. She squeezed in a few warm-up stretches and reached for D i » er megaphone.” (paragraph 26)

explanation.text: The students plan to sell paintings and donate the money to the animal shelter. This detail expresses the central idea because their project uses art to make a difference for animals.

questions.alt: Question 6. By the end of the story, how does Sylvie most likely feel about the Paint Out? 6 A She is relieved that the planning and work are finished. B She is upset because she did most of the work. C She is concerned that they did not raise enough money. D She is proud that the event was a huge success.

explanation.text: Many people arrive, canvases fill with colorful pet pictures, and the Paint Out is soon in full swing. Because the event works so well, Sylvie most likely feels proud of its success.

questions.alt: Question 19. How does the graphic support the information in paragraph 2? 19 A by showing what raccoons look like B by explaining that raccoons have good hearing C by explaining that raccoons can see at night D by showing where some raccoons live

explanation.text: Paragraph 2 describes a raccoon's body, face mask, paws, and tail. The graphic supports those details because it lets readers see what a raccoon looks like.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which sentence best explains why raccoons mostly come out at night? 20 A Raccoons find their food when it is dark. B Raccoons spend most of their time alone. C Raccoons hide during the day to keep safe. D Raccoons have black masks and tail rings.

explanation.text: Paragraph 7 says raccoons hide during the day to stay safe from predators and people. This explains why they are active and look for food mostly at night.

questions.alt: Question 21. In which section would the reader find information about what raccoons do during 21 the winter? A “Flexible Fingers” B “Eating Almost Anything” C “Night and Day” D “Many Different Dens”

explanation.text: The “Many Different Dens” section explains that raccoons spend much of winter in their dens but may leave to find food. Therefore, that section contains the winter information.

questions.alt: Question 22. Animals learn to do things a certain way because of their habitat. Which sentence from the passage best connects to this idea? “Raccoon paws have five fingers, just like a person’s hands do” A (paragraph 2) B “Exactly what a raccoon eats depends on where it lives.” (paragraph 4) “They use the many nerves on the bottoms of their paws to feel their food.” Cc (paragraph 5) “In this home range, they have many hiding places that they use as dens.” D (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The passage says exactly what a raccoon eats depends on where it lives. This directly connects habitat to behavior because the available surroundings affect the animal's food choices.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which detail from the passage best shows what the author thinks about raccoons? A __ ‘Raccoons are very smart and quick.” (paragraph 3) B “Raccoons living near people also eat pet food . .” (paragraph 4) C “Raccoons often dip their food in water . .” (paragraph 5) D “Raccoons can sometimes spread diseases to people.” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: Calling raccoons “very smart and quick” gives the author's positive judgment of them. This detail best shows the author's view because the other choices simply report behaviors or risks.

questions.alt: Question 26. Why is the information in paragraph 3 important to the story? 26 A It shows the cause of a problem in the story. B It gives details about the setting. C It gives a hint about how the story will end. D It introduces a new character.

explanation.text: Marta's legs hurry toward the cookhouse and knock over her table. This action causes the problem because her homework papers then blow out the window.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which meaning of the word “spread” is used in paragraph 5? 27 A scattered around B grew slowly C became known D shared freely

explanation.text: The wind carries Marta's papers into many places across the village. In this context, “spread” means scattered around.

questions.alt: Question 28. 2g Which detail from the story best tells why Marta begins crying? A __ “It went to Marta’s stomach . .” (paragraph 2) B “They were in such a hurry to run to the cookhouse that they knocked over Marta’ table . .” (paragraph 3) “But the dogs, too, were chasing. Leaping into the air, they barked to each Cc » other. A game!” (paragraph 7) D “Ruined homework! Broken glasses!” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: Marta's homework has been ruined and her glasses have broken, and she thinks of both losses as she fills with grief. These problems explain why she begins to cry.

questions.alt: Question 29. Which detail from the story best tells what Grandmother believes? 29 “Grandmother’s big moon tortillas were the best in the world!” A (paragraph 1) “When we have a problem we must choose what we will be.” B (paragraph 16) C “When the eagle is high up, it sees how small the earth is.” (paragraph 19) “Grandmother wound some tape around the arm of the glasses.” D (paragraph 20)

explanation.text: Grandmother says that when people have a problem, they must choose what they will be. This statement best tells her belief because the rest of her lesson explains different ways to face problems.

questions.alt: Question 30. What does Marta most likely think about Grandmother’s healing song? 30 A It reminds her of a story. B It helps her make a choice. C It makes her want to dance. D It teaches her about animals.

explanation.text: After hearing the healing song and story, Marta decides to face her problem like the eagle. This shows that Grandmother's song helps her make a choice.

questions.alt: Question 31. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A Marta’s stomach makes loud noises. B The tortillas are in the cookhouse. C The tortillas bubble and turn brown. D Marta decides to be like an eagle.

explanation.text: Marta's decision to be like an eagle shows the lesson she learns about viewing her problem differently and then trying again. This belongs in a summary because it is the story's main resolution.

questions.alt: Question 1. What does the word “muffles” mean in paragraph 3? 1 A changes to a slow speed B moves to a new stage C causes things to get darker D makes things difficult to notice

explanation.text: As a person drifts toward sleep, the brain "muffles" signals by ignoring information from muscles, senses, and the outside world. This context shows that the word means makes those signals difficult to notice.

questions.alt: Question 2. What is a central idea of the section “All Through the Night . . ”? A _ Falling asleep takes less than thirty minutes for most people. B __ Odd feelings while falling asleep are normal. C__ Time passes differently while falling asleep. D Falling asleep is an experience that involves the whole body.

explanation.text: The section explains changes in brain signals, awareness, heart rate, breathing, eye closure, hearing, and the sense of time. These combined changes support the central idea that falling asleep involves the entire body, not only the brain.

questions.alt: Question 3. Which detail best expresses a claim the author makes about sleep? 3 “Sleep is so useful that all animals do it. And without it, waking life would A be impossible.” (paragraph 2) “As you slip into sleep, your heart slows down and you breathe more B slowly.” (paragraph 4) “During deep sleep, long, slow waves of electrical impulses move through C the brain.” (paragraph 7) “In the next stage of sleep, your muscles go completely slack. Your body D doesn’t move at all.” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The statement that every animal sleeps and waking life would be impossible without sleep makes a broad, arguable claim about its necessity. This scope shows why it expresses the author's claim, while the other choices describe specific bodily events.

questions.alt: Question 4. _ Based on the article, how does REM sleep help people? A __ The eyes move back and forth while they are closed. B__ The brain links experiences and memories. C People can begin having strange dreams. D__ People sleep more deeply when it is almost morning.

explanation.text: During REM sleep, the brain connects new experiences with old memories and combines practiced actions into muscle memory. These links explain how REM helps people build knowledge and learn skills.

questions.alt: Question 5. How does the author organize the information in paragraph 14? 5 A by stating a problem with sleep and then presenting a solution B by sharing a cause-and-effect situation when sleep is interrupted C by explaining the steps to learning a new game D by comparing how long people dream at night

explanation.text: The test changes which sleep stage is interrupted: one group loses REM sleep while the other is awakened during non-REM sleep. The next day's improved game performance among those who received REM sleep shows the effect of preserving that stage.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which sentence best describes how the chart adds to the information in the article? 6 A It explains how sleep helps keep the body healthy. B It shares the steps to fall asleep easily. C It offers facts about how the body reacts to sleep. D It compares being awake to being asleep.

explanation.text: The chart lists physical responses at each sleep stage, including changes in eyelids, muscles, breathing, heartbeat, temperature, and eye movement. These facts extend the article by showing how the body reacts throughout sleep.

questions.alt: Question 19. How does the author organize the information in paragraphs 5 through 10? 19 A by comparing each item that Dustin pulls out of the wallet B by giving the reasons why Dustin must search for the wallet’s owner C by listing the order of events that lead to Dustin finding the wallet D by showing how the clues cause Dustin to find the wallet’s owner

explanation.text: The note and photograph identify a father with twin boys, the driver's license supplies Salvador's name and distant address, and the credit card provides a phone number. Each clue causes the next step that eventually allows Dustin and his mother to contact the owner.

questions.alt: Question 20. 29 What do the details in paragraph 13 reveal about Salvador? A __ His belongings often get lost on the train. B __ He enjoys visiting the city where Dustin lives. C__sHeis grateful for Dustin’s act of kindness. D His family is very important to him.

explanation.text: Salvador thanks Dustin, praises him as a fine young man, explains how worried he was, and invites Dustin's family to Rocket Land. These warm words and generous invitation reveal his gratitude for Dustin's kindness.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which statement best explains why Dustin claims, “This has been the best adventure ever!” in paragraph 17? A ___ Dustin's actions lead to an invitation to Rocket Land with Salvador’s family. B Marisa is proud and makes Dustin's favorite cake to celebrate. Dustin has made the day pass quickly by searching for the owner of the C wallet. D__ Salvador’s wallet has many clues to help Dustin solve the mystery.

explanation.text: Returning the wallet leads Salvador to invite Dustin and his mother on a special Rocket Land outing with Carlos and Hugo. This unexpected new friendship and trip explain why Dustin calls the experience his best adventure.

questions.alt: Question 22. What does the word “approval” suggest as used in paragraph 25? A __ Everyone is ready to leave for Rocket Land. B _ Dustin is allowed to sit with Hugo and Carlos. C The boys may eat the snacks Emerita has baked. D The blue van will take everyone to Rocket Land.

explanation.text: Dustin announces that he wants to sit in back with Hugo and Carlos, and both mothers nod their "approval." Their nods indicate permission, so the word suggests he is allowed to sit there.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which sentence best states a theme of the story? A the best mysteries are solved using teamwork. B People in different cities can have a lot in common. C Helping others can bring surprising rewards. D Pets are important for bringing families together.

explanation.text: Dustin's honest effort to return a stranger's wallet brings him Salvador's thanks, new friends, and an invitation to Rocket Land. This surprising outcome supports the theme that helping others can produce unexpected rewards.

questions.alt: Question 26. How is paragraph 3 organized in the story? 26 A by making comparisons between two characters B by telling the solution to a problem C by giving the answer to a question D by describing events in the order they happen

explanation.text: Paragraph 3 repeatedly compares Rosa with Miss Sabines: their long hair, round cheeks, large brown eyes, and sisterlike bond resemble one another. These side-by-side similarities show that the paragraph is organized by comparison.

questions.alt: Question 27. Read this sentence from paragraph 6. 27 I rattled her courtyard fence and called, “Are you there?” What does the word “rattled” mean as used in this sentence? A made someone feel excited B noticed a door or a gate C walked around to wait for someone D made a noise by moving something

explanation.text: Rosa physically moves the courtyard fence while calling out, causing it to make a sound that attracts attention. In this context, "rattled" means made noise by moving something.

questions.alt: Question 28. What do Miss Sabines’ actions in paragraph 8 reveal most about her? 28 A She cares about the narrator. B She thinks the narrator is funny. C She is a very curious person. D She likes when people visit her.

explanation.text: Miss Sabines studies Rosa's expression, immediately notices that she looks troubled, and invites her to share what is wrong. This attentive response reveals that she cares about Rosa's feelings.

questions.alt: Question 29. Which sentence from the story best describes the setting? “Even so, I enjoyed making the steep but quick (ten-minute) climb to A Miss Sabines’ house.” (paragraph 2) “It has a big garden, with beautiful desert plants and herbs growing everywhere, and a courtyard that has tiles painted bright colors, with B sculptures, pinwheels, wind chimes, and lawn ornaments scattered throughout” (paragraph 5) “The pleasant voice came floating across the courtyard walls, putting me c instantly at ease” (paragraph 7) “Miss Sabines showed me some of her new plantings, we played with her pets, and I leafed through a collection of photographs by Ansel Adams, D whom Miss Sabines calls a ‘poet of Southwestern landscapes: ” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The quotation describes the house's desert garden, herbs, colorful courtyard tiles, sculptures, pinwheels, wind chimes, and lawn ornaments. These concrete visual details most fully show what the story's setting looks like.

questions.alt: Question 30. Sometimes a place can be special to a person. Which detail best shows how this idea is developed in the story? “The sun was turning the Sangre de Cristo Mountains a rosy gold color.” A (paragraph 1) “Speaking of daydreams, I often fantasize about living in a house like B Miss Sabines. ” (paragraph 5) “We passed the rest of the afternoon puttering around the house.” c (paragraph 11) “We're having a party tonight, and I’ve been gone for hours!’ ” D (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: Rosa says she often imagines living at Miss Sabines' house and would trade even a mansion for that small home. Her daydream demonstrates how personally special the place is to her.

questions.alt: Question 31. Which sentence best states a theme of the story? 31 A It is important for people to not waste their time. B Hard work always pays off in the end. C Friends can help us feel better when we are upset. D It is important to respect your elders.

explanation.text: Rosa seeks Miss Sabines because the house and friendship have always comforted her troubles, and the older woman's voice immediately puts her at ease. Their afternoon together and Rosa's thanks for the advice support the theme that friends can help when someone is upset.

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 8-14. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 22-26. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 29-35. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 8. Based on information in the article, what can the reader conclude is the most likely reason Yo-Yo 8 was able to play a public recital when he was only five years old? A Yo-Yo realized that he enjoyed performing for an audience’s reaction. B Yo-Yo was ready to perform because of his father’s teaching. C Yo-Yo had finally found an instrument that he enjoyed playing. D Yo-Yo knew he would perform the music well because of his excellent memory.

explanation.text: Dr. Ma teaches Yo-Yo difficult Bach music in short, manageable lessons and requires each measure to be memorized and practiced perfectly. That method enables the five-year-old to master three suites and shows why he is prepared for a public recital.

questions.alt: Question 9. Which statement best describes the relationship between Yo-Yo Ma and his parents? A __ They support Yo-Yo’s early desire to become a cello player. B They all enjoy listening to different types of music. C__ They hope Yo-Yo’s choice of instrument will be the piano. They are all talented enough to become famous musicians. D Y S

explanation.text: Yo-Yo's musical parents surround him with singing, instruments, and recordings, arrange early lessons, notice his interests, and provide a small cello when he asks for a large instrument. These actions demonstrate their support for his early desire to play cello.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which detail from the article best shows how Yo-Yo connects with people when he performs? “Yo-Yo Ma has been playing the cello since he was a young boy. He gave his first concert A atthe age of five.” (paragraph 1) “Yo-Yo Ma rocks his cello from side to side. He draws the bow across its strings.” B (paragraph 2) c “He throws his head back. His eyes close, his glasses reflecting the bright lights of the stage.” (paragraph 2) “The audience grows quiet. Soon they are drawn into a story he tells using his cello” D (paragraph 3)

explanation.text: When Yo-Yo begins playing, the audience becomes quiet and is soon drawn into a story communicated through his cello. This shared imaginative experience best shows how his performance connects with listeners.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which detail from paragraph 6 best supports the meaning of the phrase “as natural as breathing” 11 in the same paragraph? A “surrounded by music” B “trained as an opera singer” C “played piano and violin” D “records of classical music”

explanation.text: The phrase "surrounded by music" shows that singing, playing, and listening were constantly present in Yo-Yo's family life. That constant presence best supports the comparison of music to something as natural and ever-present as breathing.

questions.alt: Question 12. Why are paragraphs 6 and 7 important to the article? A They suggest that Yo-Yo’s musical talents come naturally to him. B___ They show how Yo-Yos parents greatly influence his passion for music. C __ They explain why Yo-Yo has more interest in the cello than the violin. D They describe how Yo-Yo quickly learns to play because of his teacher.

explanation.text: Paragraphs 6 and 7 describe his mother's singing, father's violin playing and teaching, sister's instruments, family recordings, and lessons arranged from age three. These details show how Yo-Yo's parents create the musical environment that shapes his passion.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which central idea is supported by details in paragraphs 12 and 13? 13 A Family members are necessary for success. B Learning something new can prove challenging. C Small problems rarely cause people to feel stress. D Breaking large tasks into smaller parts helps learners.

explanation.text: Dr. Ma divides a long Bach suite into two-measure lessons, which Yo-Yo masters one at a time before combining them. This method supports the central idea that breaking a large, complex task into smaller parts makes learning easier and less stressful.

questions.alt: Question 14. Read this claim from paragraph 15. 14 Dr. Ma said Yo-Yo played “like a shining star.” Which phrase from paragraph 15 best supports this claim? A “had a good memory” B “concentrated better than most” C “could play three Bach suites by heart” D “gave his first public recital at the University of Paris”

explanation.text: By age five, Yo-Yo can perform three difficult Bach suites entirely from memory. This unusually advanced achievement best supports his father's comparison of his playing to a shining star.

questions.alt: Question 22. Which statement best expresses First Woman’s relationship with the First People? 22 A She hopes her people will not become discouraged. B She is focused on bringing new types of light to her people. C She believes her people should be happy with the light they have. D She is frustrated that her efforts are not appreciated by her people.

explanation.text: Whenever the First People reject an inadequate source, First Woman tries another method, from glowing creatures and Fire Mountain to carved Sun and Moon wheels and stars. Her repeated actions show that she is focused on bringing her people new and better forms of light.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which phrase best supports the meaning of “illuminate” as used in paragraph 1? A __ “too far away to bring heat” (paragraph 1) B “They would give more light” (paragraph 3) C “They flicker on and off” (paragraph 4) D “the people brought her a slab of quartz” (paragraph 6)

explanation.text: The First People want the dim world to be brighter, so the small creatures are sent out to "give more light." That phrase directly indicates that "illuminate" means light up.

questions.alt: Question 24. How does the narrator mainly develop the character of First Woman? 24 A by describing her actions B by referring to her visit to Fire Mountain C by sharing her thoughts D by showing her working with First Man

explanation.text: First Woman's character emerges through what she does: she sends light-giving creatures, consults Fire Man, organizes the carving of wheels, and turns leftover stone into stars. These actions reveal her persistence and creativity more fully than any single visit or partnership.

questions.alt: Question 25. Which sentence best represents the turning point in the story? 25 “Eventually, First Woman had an idea for a different way to send light to Earth.” A (paragraph 6) “On the rim, they placed bird feathers—cardinal, lark, and eagle—to fly it through the B sky and spread the rays of heat and light in the four directions.” (paragraph 7) “At that moment, two ancient, wise men stepped forward and offered to help.” C (paragraph 11) “Once the Sun and the Moon were following their paths through the sky, the people D returned to where First Woman had carved the wheels.” (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: After several failed attempts to satisfy the people, First Woman conceives an entirely different way to send light by carving two stone wheels. This idea marks the turning point because it begins the successful creation of the Sun, Moon, and eventually stars.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which detail best expresses a theme of being determined? 26 “So First Woman sent Glowworm, Fox Fire, Lightning Beetle, and Firefly to the four A corners.” (paragraph 3) B “She consulted Fire Man on his glowing mountain.” (paragraph 4) C “It took a lot of hard work and a lot of help . . .” (paragraph 6) “. . . they worked with their flint knives, chisels, and stone hammers to shape the stars.” D (paragraph 13)

explanation.text: Carving the hardest available quartz into two wheels requires extensive work and help, yet First Woman and the people complete it. Their persistence through that difficult labor shows determination.

questions.alt: Question 29. What is the meaning of the word “bask” in paragraph 1? 29 A to enjoy B to hide C to ignore D to swim

explanation.text: The turtles sit safely on a log in the sunlight, protected by their shells rather than hiding or swimming away. In this peaceful context, "bask" means enjoy the warmth of the sun.

questions.alt: Question 30. Paragraph 10 is important to the article because it 30 A describes why a biologist “loves to look at turtles” B questions why turtles are rarely “all the same shape” C suggests that some turtles have round “dome shells” D states that various shells are better for “different turtles”

explanation.text: Paragraph 10 introduces round, flat, smooth, and bumpy shells, then states that each shape suits the way a particular turtle lives. This statement is important because it establishes the organizing idea for the specific turtle adaptations that follow.

questions.alt: Question 31. According to scientists, turtles have existed on this planet for more than 220 million years. How 31 does the author support this idea in the article? A by explaining that turtles belong to “the same family as snakes and lizards” (paragraph 3) B by referring to a “fossil and other clues” that show how shells have changed (paragraph 4) by explaining that it is possible to “see from the ridges how much the turtle has grown” C (paragraph 7) by referring to the “flat shape and leathery shell” that help alligator snapping turtles swim D quickly (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The author cites a 220-million-year-old turtle ancestor and explains that its fossil and other evidence show a partially developed shell. This physical evidence supports scientists' conclusion about turtles' ancient history and shell evolution.

questions.alt: Question 32. Which phrase from paragraph 14 best supports the meaning of the word “camouflage” in the 32 same paragraph? A “a lighter color on their plastron” B “looking down into the water” C “blends in with the dark water” D “predators swimming below”

explanation.text: A Blanding's turtle's dark upper shell "blends in with the dark water," making it difficult for predators above to distinguish. This concealment explains the meaning of "camouflage."

questions.alt: Question 33. According to information in the section “What Shape Is Your Shell?”, how do some turtles catch 33 their prey? A by making holes in the sand B by remaining still and using their tongues C by pulling their legs into their shells D by having brown shells and yellow on their bellies

explanation.text: Alligator snapping turtles remain motionless and use a tongue that resembles a wiggling worm to lure fish close to their mouths. This description shows that stillness and tongue movement help them catch prey.

questions.alt: Question 34. The diagram helps the reader better understand the article by showing A __ the location of different parts of a turtle’s shell B__ the size of the scutes found on a turtle’s shell C__ how the bridges connect the shell to the plastron D how the turtle is able to avoid different predators

explanation.text: The diagram labels and points to the scutes, carapace, bridge, and plastron on the turtle's body. These pointers show where the different shell parts described in the article are located.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? 35 A Turtle muscles attach to shells in the same way human muscles attach to bones. B Different parts of the turtle shell work together to form a protective barrier. C Keratin can be found in both turtle shells and human hair and nails. D Scutes protect the turtle’s bones and provide color to the shell.

explanation.text: The carapace covers the back, the plastron protects the belly, and bridges connect the two at the sides to create a strong defense. This cooperation among shell parts captures the article's broad explanation of how the shell forms a protective barrier.
