questions.alt: Question 8. How does the author introduce the idea that the Purple code finally began to be understood? 8 A by indicating that many mathematicians were working on the messages B by explaining how a person noticed something interesting about the symbols C by indicating that the messages were created by a special machine D by explaining how a system was used to obtain signals from other countries

explanation.text: After more than a year of confusing symbols, Grotjan suddenly notices that some appear at a regular interval and shows the pattern to her team. Their immediate celebration introduces the breakthrough by explaining the interesting relationship she detects.

questions.alt: Question 9. What is the purpose for including the information in paragraphs 5 through 7 in the article? 9 A to illustrate the relationship between different countries B to indicate a common naming method for codes C to indicate the importance of understanding secret messages D to illustrate the reasons why a global conflict occurred

explanation.text: The paragraphs place Purple within World War II, Japan's alliance with Germany and Italy, and worsening U.S.–Japan relations. This dangerous international context explains why reading secret Japanese communications was important.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which claim by the author is best supported by evidence in the article? A “Many cryptanalysts were people who studied how languages worked” (paragraph 4) “... Grotjan was one of just a few women working at the highest levels of code breaking” B (paragraph 5) “The United States had not entered the war, but relations with Japan were becoming C more and more strained” (paragraph 7) “... the United States used a complex system to intercept the communications and break D » the codes” (paragraph 7)

explanation.text: Paragraphs 8 through 10 detail a complex chain: intercepting radio messages, sending them to Washington, solving changing symbols through guesses, constructing a matching machine, and translating the result. This extensive evidence best supports the claim that the United States used a complex system to intercept and break codes.

questions.alt: Question 11. What does the information in paragraphs 8 and 9 mainly illustrate? 11 A how coded messages were created using a special machine B why understanding coded messages was a difficult process C why cryptanalysts made guesses about coded messages D how coded messages were translated into a different language

explanation.text: Each symbol's meaning changes as the machine advances, causing one symbol to represent different letters, so analysts must repeatedly guess and test likely phrases. These shifting rules and repeated checks illustrate why decoding the messages was difficult.

questions.alt: Question 12. How does paragraph 11 strengthen the author’s central claim? 12 A by explaining the value of decoded messages to the U.S. government B by referring to the places where coded messages originated C by describing how U.S. agencies built a cipher machine D by indicating the name given to messages obtained from cipher machines

explanation.text: Paragraph 11 explains that the reconstructed machine lets officials read Japan's secret diplomatic messages and learn its wartime actions and plans. Sending that information to leaders such as President Roosevelt demonstrates the decoded messages' value to the government.

questions.alt: Question 13. What is the purpose of the illustration in the article? 13 A to show a device used to read coded messages B to explain how messages were coded C to show an example of a coded message D to explain why some messages were coded

explanation.text: The illustration depicts a cipher machine similar to the one the article describes and labels it as such. It therefore helps readers visualize the device used to read coded messages.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which detail from the article best reveals the importance of Grotjan’s discovery? “Their cries of “That’s it! ‘Whoopee!’ ‘Hooray!’ broke the silence of the room, which was A usually as quiet as a library.’ (paragraph 3) “Each symbol stood for a letter, but the letter it stood for changed as the machine moved B forward.” (paragraph 9) “... the SIS and U.S. Navy built a Purple cipher machine just like the decoding machines c used in Japanese embassies.” (paragraph 11) D “,.. Magic contributed enormously to the defeat of the enemy, greatly shortened the war, and saved many thousands of lives.” (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: A government committee credits the decoded "Magic" messages with helping defeat the enemy, shortening the war, and saving thousands of lives. Those enormous outcomes most clearly demonstrate the importance of Grotjan's discovery.

questions.alt: Question 22. What important idea do the authors develop in paragraph 3? A___ Classrooms should continue to be updated. B Some modern schools have a connection to the past. C Older buildings often lacked necessary conveniences. D —_ Methods of teaching can change over the years.

explanation.text: The authors say a one-room schoolhouse classroom probably resembled a modern classroom, even though it used a raised desk, wood stove, and outdoor bathroom. This comparison shows that present-day schools retain some connection to those of the past.

questions.alt: Question 23. Read this sentence from paragraph 4. In Honeoye Falls, New York, there is a one-room schoolhouse where kids today can experience what it was like to be students in the late 19th century. Why is this detail important to the article? A __ Ithighlights the general popularity of historical attractions. B__It indicates that students are often interested in the history of education. C__It suggests the importance of preserving historical buildings. D It shows that a connection can be made with students from another time.

explanation.text: Modern children at Honeoye Falls wear nineteenth-century clothing and learn for a week as students did more than a century earlier. This direct reenactment connects today's students with the experience of children from another time.

questions.alt: Question 24. Traditions allow people to both honor the past and celebrate the present. Which detail from paragraph 6 best represents this idea? A “There are more than 500 varieties of lilacs and more than 1,200 lilac bushes . . ” B “It was beautifully landscaped with trees and shrubs. .” C_ “The park’s collection of lilacs originally started with 20 varieties in 1892” D “Since 1898, Rochester has held a Lilac Festival every May.”

explanation.text: Rochester has held a Lilac Festival every May since 1898, continuing a celebration rooted in Highland Park's historic lilac collection. The recurring modern event shows how a tradition can honor the past while celebrating in the present.

questions.alt: Question 25. In paragraph 9, what is the main way the authors indicate the importance of the Montauk Point 25 Lighthouse? A by referring to its status as the oldest lighthouse in the state B by describing how the lighthouse still provides assistance to ships C by explaining that the lighthouse now uses an automatic beacon D by mentioning the house attached to the lighthouse where families lived

explanation.text: The Montauk Point Lighthouse has guided many kinds of ships for more than two hundred years and continues performing that service today. This ongoing help to ships most clearly indicates its lasting importance.

questions.alt: Question 26. How do the authors develop the idea of “legacy” in the article? 26 A by explaining the history that inspired traditions B by providing a chronological sequence of events C by contrasting various celebrations in the state D by referring to the dates when festivals began

explanation.text: Each section explains a historical place or development and then shows how people preserve, reenact, celebrate, or learn from it today. Connecting the original history with its continuing tradition develops the article's idea of a legacy.

questions.alt: Question 29. Read this claim from paragraph 2. 29 Every lichen is a tiny team . . . Which detail from the article best supports this claim? A “lichen on a tree looks like a gray-green, mossy stain” (paragraph 1) B “the loose leaves or decaying logs from which they grew” (paragraph 9) C “the spores of a fungus and another that looked like an alga” (paragraph 10) D “makes food for both itself and the fungus” (paragraph 13)

explanation.text: In a lichen, the algae or bacteria makes food for itself and its fungal partner, while the fungus supplies minerals and shelter. This mutual exchange best supports the claim that a lichen functions as a team.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read the sentence from paragraph 4. “Plenty of people can draw,” he said, “but you . . . have observation.” Which detail from the article best supports this claim? A __ Theauthor highlights Potter’s ability to grow fungi in her kitchen. B The author refers to Potter’s attention to detail in her drawings. C__ The author explains that Potter enjoyed drawing from nature. D__ The author provides evidence that Potter contributed to science.

explanation.text: Potter paints fungi in their exact natural settings, labels features that distinguish species, and sketches sprouting spores every six hours under magnification. This careful attention to small changes and identifying details supports the claim that she possessed exceptional observation.

questions.alt: Question 31. What is the most important way Charles McIntosh influenced Potter’s career? 31 A He provided Potter with different types of fungi to study. B He was familiar with the scientific names of fungi. C He was interested in the fungi paintings Potter made. D He enjoyed collecting fungi while he worked.

explanation.text: McIntosh sends Potter unusual fungal specimens to paint, giving her direct access to varied study material that advances her art and investigation. His supply of specimens therefore has the most important practical influence on her work.

questions.alt: Question 32. In paragraph 8, what does the phrase “the fungus starred the ground” mean? A The fungi were bright. B The fungi were tall. C The fungi were hidden. D The fungi were plentiful.

explanation.text: Potter reports seeing fungi apparently by the thousands and finding more than twenty kinds within minutes. This abundance explains that the fungus "starred the ground" by appearing plentifully across it.

questions.alt: Question 33. What do the details in paragraph 9 most reveal about Beatrix Potter? A __ She was curious about the world. B She was a famous artist. C__ She enjoyed outdoor adventures. D She thought nature should be left alone.

explanation.text: Potter records natural settings, distinguishes species, experiments with growing fungi, magnifies spores, and sketches their changes at regular intervals. These persistent investigations reveal her curiosity about how the natural world works.

questions.alt: Question 34. Which statement best describes how Potter responded when people doubted her findings? 34 A She argued with botanists until they agreed to listen to her. B She decided to write more papers for scientists about lichens. C She put her drawings of mushrooms on display for others to see. D She chose to spend more of her time working on stories for children.

explanation.text: Potter's lichen paper and meetings receive little scientific attention, while her children's stories are becoming popular. She responds by focusing on writing and illustrating books, which leads to her fame as a children's author.

questions.alt: Question 35. Read the sentence from paragraph 10. 35 Potter also looked at lichens, which scientists grouped with the fungi. Which statement best represents the meaning of this sentence? A Scientists believed lichens and fungi were related. B Scientists had shown little interest in examining lichens. C Scientists wondered if lichens could live separately from algae. D Scientists had discovered algae makes food for itself and its partner.

explanation.text: To group lichens with fungi means scientists placed them in the same scientific category. This classification indicates that scientists believed lichens and fungi were related.

questions.alt: Question 1. Which idea would be most important to include in a summary of the story? 1 “Today began our adventure at the Columbian Exposition, the largest world’s fair in A history!” (paragraph 1) B “Father insisted on staying late, footsore and dusty as we were.” (paragraph 3) “Father decreed that we spend all day in the Manufactures and Liberal Arts Building, by C far the biggest of the Great Buildings.” (paragraph 4) “At dusk, we watched the lights come on again like thousands of candles lit all at once.” D (paragraph 5)

explanation.text: Emma's weeklong account centers on exploring the enormous Columbian Exposition and its exhibits, inventions, cultures, and amusements. Therefore, beginning the adventure at history's largest world's fair is essential to a summary.

questions.alt: Question 2. Read this sentence from paragraph 5. The lights’ reflections were magical—like being surrounded by floating fireflies. What is the effect of the author’s word choice in the sentence? A __Itcreates a tone of surprise. B__It creates a tone of adventure. C It creates a tone of wonderment. D__It creates a tone of confusion.

explanation.text: Calling the reflections “magical” and comparing the many lights around the gondola to floating fireflies emphasizes their extraordinary beauty. This imagery therefore creates wonderment.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does the word “replica” mean as it is used in paragraph 7? 3 A a building B an exit C a copy D an animal

explanation.text: The constructed Mammoth Cave seems so realistic that Emma feels she has entered the actual cavern. Because it imitates a real place, a “replica” is a copy.

questions.alt: Question 4. Read this sentence from paragraph 8. The Ferris Wheel began operating today, but Father said we can’t ride it until we’ve toured all the important exhibits. What does this sentence suggest about the father’s attitude? A He values education more than amusement. B _-He dislikes amusement attractions like the Ferris Wheel. C_—_He is unconvinced of the safety of the Ferris Wheel. Ds He thinks the attractions will be less crowded later in the week.

explanation.text: Father postpones the exciting Ferris Wheel until the family has toured every exhibit he considers important. This condition shows that he gives educational experiences priority over amusement.

questions.alt: Question 5. The events from Friday, June 23 cause a change in the narrator's attitude from A fascinated to weary, because of all the walking the family has done B interested to excited, because the amusements are fun C pleased to uncertain, because of the foreign surroundings D _ tired to energized, because the buildings are unusual

explanation.text: Emma has been interested in exhibits all week, but Friday brings the long-awaited, “thrilling” Ferris Wheel and a Midway like a world tour. These fun amusements therefore raise her interest into excitement.

questions.alt: Question 6. Journaling can be a helpful way to keep track of past events and memories. Which sentence best 6 supports this idea? A “Today we moved on to Transportation, the second-largest building.” (paragraph 6) “I was more impressed by the sparkly diamonds from the Cape Colony and lovely Aztec B turquoise from Mexico.” (paragraph 7) C “We agree it’s the best of all!” (paragraph 13) D “I’d never remember everything without it!” (paragraph 14)

explanation.text: Emma says she is grateful for the journal because she could never remember everything about the crowded week without it. Her statement directly shows how journaling preserves events and memories.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which of the following sentences supports the narrator’s claim that her trip is an adventure? 7 A She will learn about new advancements in technology. B She will have a unique opportunity to experience things from all around the world. C She will spend an entire week with her family on a vacation. D She will record her experiences in a journal that was given to her.

explanation.text: Emma encounters inventions, art, transportation, foods, plants, and people representing many regions and countries, then calls the Midway a world tour. That rare chance to experience the wider world supports her claim of adventure.

questions.alt: Question 22. How is the strength of spider silk mostly introduced in paragraph 1? 22 A through a story about the author’s interest in popular movies B through a comparison to a fictional character’s superhero abilities C through details of the material’s use in everyday objects D through reference to the myths surrounding the material’s benefits

explanation.text: The paragraph uses Spider-Man's feats—swinging from buildings and stopping vehicles—then says real silk could stop a 747. This comparison to superhero abilities introduces just how strong the material is.

questions.alt: Question 23. What does the phrase “on a smaller scale,’ as used in paragraph 2, suggest about spider silk? A __ Spider silk could be used to perform tasks requiring precision. B The simpler uses for spider silk require limited skill. C___ Some uses for spider silk are less important than others. D Spider silk is most useful for large-scale projects.

explanation.text: After large ropes and aircraft wires, the author turns to thread for stitching the brain, eye, or spinal cord and repairing tendons. These delicate jobs show that “on a smaller scale” means precise, fine work.

questions.alt: Question 24. For the project at the American Museum of Natural History described in paragraph 3, to what 24 does the phrase “reel in the silk” refer? A the effort it took to collect such a great amount of silk B the length of time it took the spiders to produce the silk C how difficult it was to find the necessary spiders D how important it was to hire dedicated workers

explanation.text: Seventy people spent years collecting enough spiders, and twelve more workers had to draw out their silk for one shawl. Thus, “reel in the silk” refers to the labor required to gather that great quantity.

questions.alt: Question 25. The details in paragraph 7 mainly contribute to the article by showing A __ the differences between production of silk by silkworms and spiders B the impressive design of silkworm cocoons C__ the metamorphosis of silkworms into moths D _ the reason that silkworms are a good alternative to spiders

explanation.text: Domesticated silkworms produce hundreds of offspring, and each caterpillar spins a cocoon from more than a thousand yards of continuous thread. This abundant, already-spun output explains why they are promising spider alternatives.

questions.alt: Question 26. How does the author present the challenge of raising spiders domestically? 26 A through descriptions of how spiders find food together B through comparisons of the behavior of male and female spiders C through situations that show why spiders cannot be kept together D through explanations about why female spiders are special

explanation.text: The author describes females attacking intruders, males stealing food, and any group of spiders eventually becoming “one big spider.” These situations show that spiders cannot be housed together for domestic production.

questions.alt: Question 29. What do the details in paragraphs 1 and 2 reveal about Subhash? 29 A He is quiet and considerate. B He is nervous and excitable. C He feels passionate about nature. D He feels lonely when at home.

explanation.text: Subhash quietly keeps his mother company, helps his father tend flowers, waits for noisy games to end, and enjoys being alone. These actions reveal a calm child who considers others rather than demanding attention.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read these sentences from paragraph 5. When they were old enough, when they were permitted to leave the house, they were told not to lose sight of one another. Together they wandered down the winding lanes of the enclave, behind the ponds and across the lowland, to the playing field where they sometimes met up with other boys. What is the main way this change in setting affects Subhash and Udayan? A__ They get into trouble when they wander too far from home. B__They realize they like the quieter areas of their community. C_ They enjoy exploring new territory together. D They prefer being with other children when exploring.

explanation.text: Once permitted beyond the house, the brothers roam winding neighborhood lanes, ponds, fields, and eventually the larger city side by side. This setting change therefore gives them new territory to explore together.

questions.alt: Question 31. In paragraph 8, Subhash and Udayan respond differently to the same situation. What does this show the reader about the characters? A ___ The boys’ parents expect different behavior from them. B__The boys demonstrate different personalities. C__ The boys’ parents give them different responsibilities. D__The boys display different levels of maturity.

explanation.text: At the film studio, Udayan boldly asks actresses for autographs while Subhash tries to make himself nearly invisible. Their opposite reactions to the same opportunity demonstrate contrasting personalities.

questions.alt: Question 32. As used in paragraph 9, what does the word “indistinguishable” show about the brothers? A One brother is considered as intelligent as the other. B They are both tired of playing the game of chess. C One brother acts the same way as the other. D__ They both sound the same when talking.

explanation.text: Both boys answer when either name is called, and listeners sometimes cannot tell who responded because their voices are nearly identical. Therefore, “indistinguishable” shows that they sound the same.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which statement best reflects the idea of growing up? “Eventually they were allowed to leave the enclave, and to enter the greater city.” A (paragraph 6) “Sitting over the chessboard they were mirror images: one leg bent, the other splayed out, B chins propped on their knees.” (paragraph 9) c “It became his mission to obey them, given that it wasn’t possible to surprise or impress them. That was what Udayan did.” (paragraph 11) “Subhash had listened. He had watched through the window, he had not gone out.” D (paragraph 14)

explanation.text: Being old enough brings permission to leave their enclave, travel independently on trams and buses, and enter the greater city. That expanding independence therefore most clearly reflects growing up.

questions.alt: Question 34. What difference between Subhash and Udayan is revealed by the information in paragraph 16? 34 A Udayan pays more attention to detail, while Subhash is unobservant. B Udayan has a more creative personality, while Subhash is carefree. C Subhash dislikes getting dirty, while Udayan likes creating a mess. D Subhash acts as his parents want, while Udayan acts as he wants.

explanation.text: Subhash follows the mason's warning and remains inside, but Udayan runs onto the plank as soon as their mother turns away. The contrast shows Subhash obeying his parents while Udayan follows his own impulse.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which statement best represents the importance of paragraphs 17 and 18 to the story? The boys’ father continues to be upset about the footprints in the cement, and he A complains to visitors. The boys’ father understands that the footprints represent an experience in the journey of B growing up. Subhash realizes that the footprints represent a division between himself and Udayan, c which he accepts. D Subhash feels guilty that he did not Udayan from making the footprints in the cement.

explanation.text: Their father refuses to erase Udayan's footprints because he believes a son's steps should remain. When the marks become a treasured family story, they represent an imperfect but meaningful experience in growing up.

questions.alt: Question 36. What is the definition of “pioneered” as it is used in paragraph 1? 36 A traveled around the world B trained extensively C danced professionally D developed something new

explanation.text: The women introduced original techniques that changed dance and influenced later generations. In this context, “pioneered” means they developed something new rather than merely training or performing.

questions.alt: Question 37. What does the reader learn about Katherine Dunham from paragraph 5? 37 A She struggles with a career in anthropology. B She blends cultural influences into modern dance. C She wants dance to return to traditional methods. D She leaves dancing in order to become an actor.

explanation.text: Dunham studies Caribbean ritual dances, brings African forms to the stage, explores Harlem dance, and later teaches a technique combining ballet with African and Caribbean traditions. These details show her blending cultures into modern dance.

questions.alt: Question 38. Based on paragraph 5, what does the word “signature” suggest about Katherine Dunham’s dance 38 L’Ag’Ya? A It is a dance that is performed in a musical. B It is a dance that is identified as Dunham’s. C It is a dance that requires Dunham’s style of dress. D It is a dance that includes cultural references.

explanation.text: L'Ag'Ya is introduced as Dunham's first distinctive creation, based on research she uniquely brought from Martinique. Calling it a “signature” dance therefore marks it as closely identified with her.

questions.alt: Question 39. Which sentence best supports a central idea of the article? “They laid the foundation for generations of dancers and choreographers to come.” A (paragraph 2) B “I wanted it to be fraught with inner meaning, with excitement and surge.” (paragraph 3) “Katherine Dunham started dancing in her late teens, but she didn’t think it would turn c into a career” (paragraph 4) D “Primus worked to make African dance an accepted and important art form among American audiences.” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The article traces women who introduced techniques, schools, companies, and cultural forms that changed dance beyond their own careers. Saying Duncan and Graham laid a foundation for later generations therefore expresses that central legacy.

questions.alt: Question 40. Which sentence best supports the claim made by the author in paragraph 1? “Graham's techniques are still taught today, and her approach to movement has inspired many young dancers to make their careers on the stage.” (paragraph 3) “Dunham spent 18 months in Jamaica, Martinique, Trinidad, and Haiti, gaining the trust B of her subjects so she could learn and perform their ritual dances.’ (paragraph 4) “She received a scholarship to the New Dance Group, which offered dance lessons to professionals and children.” (paragraph 7) “The all-female group was known for avant-garde performances minimalistic in both D movement and stage design.” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Paragraph 1 claims these women created long-lasting change. Graham's technique still being taught and inspiring young dancers supplies direct evidence that her innovations continue to influence the field.

questions.alt: Question 41. How are the accomplishments of Martha Graham represented in the article? 41 A Graham expands the changes to dance that Katherine Dunham starts. B Graham influences people’s understanding of dance. C Graham believes dancers should be trained similarly to athletes. D Graham emphasizes the need for dancers to have freedom.

explanation.text: Graham challenges accepted definitions of dance, values meaningful movement over conventional beauty, and creates a technique still taught today. These accomplishments show that she changed how people understand the art form.

questions.alt: Question 42. What is the primary structure the author uses to organize information in the article? 42 A cause and effect B problem and solution C chronological sequence D compare and contrast

explanation.text: Within each dancer's section, the author follows dated milestones from training and early work through companies, major performances, and later influence. This repeated time-ordered pattern therefore makes chronological sequence primary.

questions.alt: Question 1. What do the details in paragraph 3 reveal about SwiftBot? A He has been taught to perform certain actions. B He is learning to use his voice to communicate. C He has been able to play basketball since he was created. D He is able to teach other robots how to play basketball.

explanation.text: The narrator and Yolanda spent hours programming and adjusting SwiftBot until he could obey “play offense,” catch the ball, and dribble with control. These details reveal actions the robot has been taught.

questions.alt: Question 2. Read this sentence from paragraph 4. I couldn't wait to see what he'd do next. How does this sentence develop the narrator's viewpoint? A It shows that the narrator is interested in creating a team of basketball-playing robots. B It shows that the narrator believes SwiftBot is unable to perform at the expected level. C It shows that the narrator is unaware of what SwiftBot is programmed to do. D It shows that the narrator does not know which action SwiftBot will perform.

explanation.text: From the triple-threat stance SwiftBot can either dribble or shoot, and the narrator eagerly waits to learn which option he will choose. The sentence therefore shows uncertainty about the robot's next action.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does the phrase "eyes wide" as used in paragraph 6 reveal about the narrator? A He is impressed by SwiftBot's ability. B He is concerned the ball will hit him. C He is confused by SwiftBot's action. D He is disappointed he is unable to the ball.

explanation.text: SwiftBot jumps beyond the narrator's reach and calculates a perfect, rimless shot. Watching this achievement with “eyes wide” shows that the narrator is impressed by the robot's ability.

questions.alt: Question 4. In paragraph 9, why does the narrator say, “I put a bit of backspin on the ball as it left my fingers, wishing for the hundredth time that I could engineer a way for SwiftBot to do the same”? A __ The narrator would like to give SwiftBot as many human-like abilities as possible. B The narrator regrets the limited effort that originally went into SwiftBot’s design. C The narrator realizes that SwiftBot requires many more hours of programming. D The narrator is questioning SwiftBot’s ability to compete with humans.

explanation.text: Backspin is a subtle human movement that SwiftBot's mechanical fingers and tactile sensors cannot yet copy. The narrator's repeated wish to engineer it shows his desire to add more human-like abilities.

questions.alt: Question 5. As used in paragraph 11, what does the word “tracked” suggest about the narrator? 5 A He believes he will win the game. B He is paying close attention. C He has doubts about his basketball skills. D He is clumsy in his movements.

explanation.text: After releasing the layup, the narrator follows the ball with his eyes to see whether it reaches the target and basket. In this context, “tracked” therefore suggests close visual attention.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which sentence reveals the difficulty the narrator has in teaching SwiftBot to play a sport? “Inside his lightweight, carbon-fiber exterior was an array of integrated circuits, servos, optical sensors, wires, and batteries.” (paragraph 1) “He dribbled the ball against the floor, keeping it close to his body for better control” B (paragraph 3) C “I was tired of hammering out the dents in his outer casing” (paragraph 7) D “His arms dropped to his sides and he came to a halt.” (paragraph 14)

explanation.text: SwiftBot repeatedly fell while learning to jump and dented his casing badly enough that the narrator had to hammer it back into shape. That repeated repair is concrete evidence of the difficulty involved in teaching the sport.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which sentence best supports a theme of machines having advantages over humans? “His silver-metallic body was about my height, roughly five feet ten inches, and A mimicked your basic humanoid design: two arms, two legs, one head, and so on.” (paragraph 1) “He landed easily back onto the floor, the springs in his feet doubling as shock B absorbers.” (paragraph 7) “Leather smacked against a carbon-fiber palm as SwiftBot batted the ball back at me” c (paragraph 10) D ‘“SwiftBot didn’t have to worry about getting dehydrated, but I did” (paragraph 15)

explanation.text: After the game, the human narrator is exhausted, breathing hard, and urgently drinking water, while SwiftBot neither tires nor becomes dehydrated. This direct contrast shows a machine's physical advantage.

questions.alt: Question 22. Which sentence states a central idea of the section “The Mother of Invention”? A Corvids have a special way of thinking that helps them survive. B Animals that are scavengers help to clean our ecosystems. C__ Ravens identify food then learn how to get it. D —_ Corvids can survive among humans all around the world.

explanation.text: The section explains that corvids must identify food, patiently solve access problems, and adapt their strategies to wolves, cities, and trash bins. These examples support the central idea that special intelligence helps them survive.

questions.alt: Question 23. What does the phrase “behavioral flexibility” mean as it is used in paragraph 6? 23 A ability to travel to different places B ability to learn different ways of moving C ability to solve new problems D ability to squeeze into small spaces

explanation.text: The phrase appears beside learning, creativity, and adapting when familiar food or nesting conditions disappear. Therefore, “behavioral flexibility” means changing behavior to solve new problems.

questions.alt: Question 24. Which statement represents an important distinction the author makes between corvids and blue 24 whales in the section “Big Brains”? A Corvids are more intelligent than blue whales because they are very large birds. Blue whales should be smarter than they are because they are so much larger than B corvids. A blue whale’s brain, which can weigh over 16 pounds, is much larger than a corvid’s C brain. When comparing brain size to body size, a corvid’s brain-to-body ratio is greater than a D blue whale’s.

explanation.text: A blue whale has a much heavier brain, but it is less than 0.01 percent of its huge body; a corvid's brain is about 2.4 percent of its body. Thus, the corvid has the greater brain-to-body ratio.

questions.alt: Question 25. Which sentence from the article best supports the claim in paragraph 2? “As scavengers, they help clean ecosystems by consuming what others don't want” A (paragraph 3) “The raven has to think, be patient, and wait for an opportunity to sneak in and steal B food from the hungry wolves.” (paragraph 4) c “Animal intelligence largely depends on a rule that scientists call the ‘brain-to-body ratio: ” (paragraph 8) D “The same rule of convergent evolution can be applied to the similarities between the human brain and the corvid brain” (paragraph 13)

explanation.text: Paragraph 2 claims corvids creatively solve complex problems. The raven's need to think, wait, and safely steal food from feeding wolves therefore provides a specific example of that problem-solving intelligence.

questions.alt: Question 26. “Necessity is the mother of invention” is a very old proverb. It means that when we need 26 something, we use our intelligence to invent ways to get it. Which detail from the article shows that this can apply to corvids? “A scavenger must first identify a food source and then problem-solve to figure out how A to obtain that food—without getting into trouble.” (paragraph 4) “. . . animals with larger brains compared to the rest of their bodies are generally more B intelligent.” (paragraph 8) “But animals from both groups seem to approach problems in the same manner and C remember information in much the same ways.” (paragraph 13) “. . . understanding the corvid brain may actually tell us about the intelligence of alien life D forms.” (paragraph 14)

explanation.text: A scavenging corvid must first recognize needed food and then invent a safe way to reach it without confronting predators. That detail directly demonstrates intelligence producing a solution when necessity arises.

questions.alt: Question 29. What effect does the phrase “daring us to scale it” in paragraph 1 have on the story? 29 A It suggests that the new land poses a great challenge for the immigrants. B It suggests that the narrator will successfully adjust to the unfamiliar environment. It suggests that the immigrants will have to physically climb beyond where the train can C reach. D It suggests that there will be fighting between the immigrants and the other inhabitants.

explanation.text: The immigrants face a sheer green mountain wall, heavy humidity, and a slow climb with all their burdens. Saying the landscape “dar[es] us to scale it” therefore presents the new country as a formidable challenge.

questions.alt: Question 30. Which sentence best shows that Ichiro comes to believe that moving to the new land will result in a positive change to his life? “T felt an indescribable disgust and joy all at once urging me to join the boy in his chase, A __ scurrying after the slippery pig and running toward that great wall of chickens.” (paragraph 8) “I looked up at cages teetering above to see the face of the pig’s young owner, a mixture of B relief and gratitude and comedy glowing in his features.” (paragraph 8) “The boy spoke in a language I could not understand, and yet, I was sure from that c moment on that I would soon understand everything” (paragraph 9) “Over the years, I have thought from time to time that I had caught a glimpse of that very D boy again, but of course, it would never be the same boy.’ (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Although Ichiro cannot understand the Brazilian boy's language, their shared chase creates connection, and he becomes certain he will soon understand everything. That confidence shows hope that life in Brazil will change positively.

questions.alt: Question 31. How do the actions of the piglet’s young owner in paragraphs 8 and 9 affect the plot? A __ They cause the narrator to believe that his purpose in this new land is to help others. B They cause the narrator to have to choose between his old culture and a new one in Brazil. C They make the narrator feel hopeful that he will be able to adjust in a new country. D ihey make the narrator feel disappointed with the new surroundings when he arrives in Brazil.

explanation.text: The boy's relief, gratitude, and humor after Ichiro catches the piglet create a friendly exchange despite their language difference. That successful connection therefore makes Ichiro hopeful that he can adjust to Brazil.

questions.alt: Question 32. What is the effect of the author’s use of the verbs “stumbled,” “clutched,” and “lumbered” in paragraph 3? A ___ These verbs show that the family is moving with difficulty from the ship to the land. B___ These verbs suggest how much baggage the family has brought along on the trip. C___ These verbs demonstrate the variety of reactions the family members have to Brazil. D__These verbs indicate the excitement the family feels at arriving at their destination.

explanation.text: Ichiro “stumbled” while carrying a bundle, the children “clutched” one another, and Father “lumbered” under heavy bags. These verbs therefore emphasize the family's difficult movement off the ship.

questions.alt: Question 33. Some researchers believe that humans are more likely to remember pleasant and positive experiences than negative ones. Which statement best reflects this idea in the story? A Ichiro remembers not feeling seasick while on the boat, but he becomes nauseous when the train stops at the marketplace in Brazil. B Ichiro has a stronger emotional reaction to helping a stranger catch a piglet than to the difficulties he has when arriving in Brazil. Cc Ichiro can more accurately describe the experience of seeing the vendors in the marketplace than he can the experience of helping the boy catch a piglet. D Ichiro is very affected by the young boy that he meets upon leaving the train, and so he makes an intentional effort to meet with the boy several times over the years.

explanation.text: Because the journey brings humidity, rot, nausea, and struggle while old Ichiro most vividly recalls the joy of helping the boy and every feature of his face, his lasting positive reaction outweighs the discomforts.

questions.alt: Question 34. How do Ichiro and his father see their arrival in Brazil differently? A __ Ichiro does not understand why they left Japan, while his father knows of their reasons. Ichiro sees the need to help others, while his father is focused on achieving his own B goals. Ichiro is focused on the immediate difficulties, while his father sees Brazil as better than c expected. D Ichiro is eager to explore Brazil, while his father struggles with adjusting to the new country.

explanation.text: Ichiro concentrates on the heat, rotten odor, nausea, and other immediate hardships. His father instead smiles and says Brazil is “beyond my imagination,” therefore indicating that the country exceeds what he expected.

questions.alt: Question 35. How does the final paragraph contribute to a central idea in the story? It reveals that seeing someone after a long time apart causes both people to remember A the good times they shared together. It shows that the pleasant memories of significant life events often become stronger than B the negative details. c It demonstrates how older people often realize that past struggles were worth the difficulty when they result in new friendships. It illustrates that memories can fade unless efforts are made to stay connected with those D who shared those experiences.

explanation.text: Decades later, Ichiro still recalls the Brazilian boy's face in rich, affectionate detail, while the earlier heat, sickness, and fear no longer dominate his memory. The ending therefore shows a positive milestone outlasting its hardships.

questions.alt: Question 36. What is the meaning of the word “intact” as used in paragraph 3? 36 A perfectly preserved B completely finished C in a shady place D in a delicate condition

explanation.text: The cliff overhang sheltered the dwelling from weather, allowing it to remain complete hundreds of years after its abandonment. In this context, “intact” means perfectly preserved.

questions.alt: Question 37. How do the events in paragraphs 1 and 2 relate to the information in paragraph 7? 37 A Paragraph 7 gives historical context for the discovery in paragraphs 1 and 2. B Paragraph 7 describes the ancestors of the cowboys in paragraphs 1 and 2. C Paragraph 7 provides additional details about the land in paragraphs 1 and 2. D Paragraph 7 shows a different culture than that in paragraphs 1 and 2.

explanation.text: The opening describes the cowboys' 1888 discovery of an ancient settlement. Paragraph 7 then explains that thousands of Ancestral Puebloans had built a thriving community there for seven centuries, supplying its historical context.

questions.alt: Question 38. As used in paragraph 7, what does the word “adept” suggest about the Ancestral Puebloans? A They think architecture is more important than farming. B They have a range of skills necessary for their survival. C Farming the land takes up a majority of their time. D Being physically strong is one of their most valuable traits.

explanation.text: The Ancestral Puebloans are master builders, skilled craftspeople, successful dryland farmers, and traders. Calling them “adept” therefore emphasizes their broad set of survival skills.

questions.alt: Question 39. How do the details in paragraphs 8 through 10 help develop a central idea of the article? A __ They describe the progress made by the Ancestral Puebloans in architecture. B__ They illustrate how the unpredictable weather affected the ability to build homes. C__ They explain why the Ancestral Puebloans were forced to leave their territory. D They show how little effort was put into the early housing structures.

explanation.text: The article moves from simple pit houses to multistory adobe homes and finally to sophisticated masonry dwellings built into cliffs. This progression shows the Ancestral Puebloans' architectural advancement.

questions.alt: Question 40. Which sentence best shows the author’s viewpoint? 40 “Wetherill and Mason had discovered a large cliff dwelling that had been abandoned A hundreds of years ago by ancient Pueblo people.” (paragraph 3) “Builders filled in the spaces where the stone bricks fit together with smaller stones, mud, B and wood pieces.” (paragraph 4) “For about 700 years, Mesa Verde was a thriving community of many thousands of C Native American people now referred to as Ancestral Puebloans.” (paragraph 7) “We do know that these people settled the area that is now the home of the modern D Pueblo tribes, including the Hopi, Zuni, Acoma, and Laguna.” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: Calling Mesa Verde a “thriving community” therefore conveys the author's positive interpretation of its long, populous history. The other statements mainly report construction details or locations without that evaluative language.

questions.alt: Question 41. Which detail best expresses a central idea of the section “A National Park”? 41 “Over the next 15 months, the Wetherill family found and explored 182 different cliff A dwellings in Mesa Verde.” (paragraph 14) B “Many looters and treasure seekers began visiting the area, too.” (paragraph 15) “. . . the U.S. government would own, protect, and preserve Mesa Verde and all of its C treasures.” (paragraph 16) “. . . more than 600,000 visitors enjoy the amazing sites at Mesa Verde National Park each D year.” (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: The section traces threats from looters to the creation of a national park whose purpose is government protection and preservation of Mesa Verde. That protective mission is therefore the section's central idea.

questions.alt: Question 42. How does the author develop a connection between the cowboys and the Pueblo cliff dwelling? 42 A by recounting the cowboys’ journey in order to introduce the setting B by detailing the path to the site to show the difficulty of getting there C by describing the weather to emphasize the unpredictability of the environment D by explaining the cowboys’ skills to compare them to the ancient people

explanation.text: The author follows the cowboys as they track cattle, look across the canyon, circle to the other side, and descend toward the dwellings. Recounting this journey therefore introduces both the Mesa Verde landscape and the discovery.

exams.title: New York Grade 3 ELA - 2024 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2024 New York State Grade 3 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2024 NYS Grade 3 English Language Arts Test Released Questions

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 13-18. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 19-23. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 26-31. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 13. The phrase “change before your very eyes” in paragraph 8 suggests that clouds A __ canonly be seen up close B can look strange to others C can make it difficult for you to see D can become different while you watch

explanation.text: The next paragraph explains that clouds can change their shapes, sizes, and colors. Therefore, seeing a cloud "change before your very eyes" means it becomes different while you are watching it.

questions.alt: Question 14. What claim does the author make about cumulus clouds? A They look like bright cotton balls in the sky. B They form only when it is windy for many hours. C They show that the weather might soon change. D___ They look pink when they form at sunset.

explanation.text: Paragraph 20 asks whether the reader has seen bright cotton balls in the sky and then identifies them as cumulus clouds. This comparison supports the claim that cumulus clouds look like bright cotton balls.

questions.alt: Question 15. In which section of the passage can the reader find information about how clouds 15 are made? A “Tiny, Floating Drops” B “Changing Clouds” C “Types of Clouds” D “Study the Clouds”

explanation.text: The section "Tiny, Floating Drops" explains that clouds form when small water drops and pieces of ice gather in the sky. Because it describes the materials and process, that section tells how clouds are made.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which sentence from the passage shows a cause-and-effect relationship? 16 A “Clouds can change shapes and sizes.” (paragraph 9) “Other clouds float in the middle, and some move up and down.” B (paragraph 14) C “As the day warms up, fog clears away.” (paragraph 17) “Scientists study clouds to tell what kind of day you’ll have tomorrow.” D (paragraph 28)

explanation.text: Paragraph 17 connects a change in temperature with a change in fog. The day warming is the cause, and the fog clearing away is its effect.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which detail best shows how the author feels about clouds? 17 A “Some clouds travel as fast as race cars!” (paragraph 11) B “You can barely see the road ahead of you!” (paragraph 15) C “. . . cloudy nights are a little bit warmer than clear . . .” (paragraph 29) D “. . . you are sure to find many wonderful shapes . . .” (paragraph 31)

explanation.text: Calling cloud shapes "wonderful" is a positive judgment rather than a neutral fact about speed, temperature, or visibility. That word best reveals the author's admiration and enjoyment of clouds.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the passage? 18 A Some clouds can look like shapes of animals. B Clouds form when drops of water come together. C Clouds can appear on warm days and on cool days. D Birds fly through clouds that are low in the sky.

explanation.text: The passage broadly explains what clouds are, how they change, where they appear, and what types exist. The fact that clouds form from gathered water drops is essential to a summary because it explains their basic origin.

questions.alt: Question 19. Which event would be most important to include in a summary of the story? 19 A A man takes care of the lighthouse and uses a small key to start its light. B A boat greets the lighthouse in the day but the lighthouse does not answer. A terrible storm comes in the night and the bridge reminds the lighthouse C why it is needed. A group of workers come to the Hudson River and begin digging and D building along the shore.

explanation.text: The bridge's larger light makes the lighthouse believe it is no longer useful, but during the storm the bridge reminds it to guide the boats. This event belongs in a summary because it creates and resolves the lighthouse's central problem.

questions.alt: Question 20. How are the details in paragraphs 1 through 6 important to the story? A. __ the paragraphs explain the main problem to be solved in the story. B__ The paragraphs introduce the setting and the main character of the story. C__ The paragraphs show why later events will come to happen in the story. D The paragraphs hint at how the characters will change over time in the story.

explanation.text: The opening places the little red lighthouse on the Hudson River near New York City and describes the boats passing it. These paragraphs are important because they introduce both the setting and the story's main character before the conflict begins.

questions.alt: Question 21. How does the building of the great gray bridge affect the little red lighthouse? A The bridge makes the lighthouse feel like it is not useful anymore. B__ The bridge makes the lighthouse feel happy to have help with its job. C The bridge shows the lighthouse new ways to help the people of the city. D The bridge blocks out the light of the lighthouse so it is not seen by boats.

explanation.text: The new bridge towers above the lighthouse and shines a much larger light. The lighthouse feels useless because it compares its small beam with the bridge's powerful one and assumes it is no longer needed.

questions.alt: Question 22. ~The great gray bridge believes that the little red lighthouse A can help it send lights into the air B _ should be moved to a different place C__ is too small to be helpful in an emergency D __ must continue to do its job for the boats

explanation.text: The bridge explains that its own light guides airplanes, while the lighthouse remains responsible for the river. Its urgent request for the lighthouse to shine shows that the smaller structure must continue guiding boats.

questions.alt: Question 23. Lighthouses are important because they guide boats. They can help keep boats safe when weather is bad or water is dangerous. Which detail best shows how this idea connects to the events in the story? “All the way from Lake Tear of the Clouds, high up in the mountains, came A the Hudson River. (paragraph 3) B “Now the boats on the river talked to the little red lighthouse as they passed.” (paragraph 4) “He took off the thick white cap that let it sleep by day. He turned on the Cc gas with a funny small black key.’ (paragraph 7) D “The boats saw the light and were safe. The boats saw it, and they kept to the channel” (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: Paragraph 12 directly connects the lighthouse's beam with the boats' safety and their ability to remain in the deep channel. This detail clearly shows how a lighthouse guides boats away from danger.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which sentence best shows a theme of the story? A __ Goals can be reached through hard work. B _ Good things happen to people who tell the truth. C__ Trusting what others do can be rewarding. D The happiest moments in life are those spent with family.

explanation.text: Molly doubts Sam's tart apples and tries to remove them, but she lets the family finish the sauce and discovers that mixing apple types improves it. The result supports the theme because trusting what others contribute brings an unexpected reward.

questions.alt: Question 27. What does the word “muses” mean as it is used in paragraph 2? 27 A feels strange B becomes amazed C thinks deeply D feels upset

explanation.text: Molly is considering what ingredient might improve the family's applesauce when she "muses." In this context, the word means she thinks deeply about the problem.

questions.alt: Question 28. In paragraph 3 of the story, whose thoughts are being shown? 28 A Sam’s B Molly’s C Dad’s D Mom’s

explanation.text: The narration follows Molly's mistaken feeling that a bobcat has grabbed her and then her recognition of messy little Sam. Those perceptions belong to Molly, so paragraph 3 is showing her thoughts.

questions.alt: Question 29. Read these sentences from paragraph 20. 29 Molly screws up her mouth. She sticks out her tongue to taste just a little. Which sentence best explains why Molly acts this way? A She is certain that the applesauce will win the blue ribbon. B She thinks that the applesauce will taste bad. C She is angry at Sam for putting his apples in the applesauce. D She wants to try the applesauce before Sam tries it.

explanation.text: Molly knows that Sam added tiny, sour apples against her wishes, so she tastes the finished sauce very cautiously. Her tightened mouth and barely extended tongue show that she expects it to taste bad.

questions.alt: Question 30. Which quote from the story best shows that Molly has learned a lesson? A “Molly sighs. She hopes Sam will stay out of the way.” (paragraph 10) ““They’re beauties; Mom says, and Molly smiles with pride” B (paragraph 12) C_. “Then she gobbles the whole bite. “Wow! ” (paragraph 20) D = “We may even have a chance at that blue ribbon this year” (paragraph 21)

explanation.text: Molly first objects to the tart apples, but after tasting the mixed sauce she eagerly eats the whole bite and exclaims "Wow!" Her surprised approval shows that she has learned different apples can improve the recipe.

questions.alt: Question 31. Which sentence best shows how Molly’s feelings about Sam change in the story? 31 A “It’s Sam, her messy little cousin who lives next door.” (paragraph 3) B “ ‘Go pick apples over there,’ Molly snaps.” (paragraph 6) “ ‘No!’ Molly cries, reaching into the pot to pull out Sam’s apples.” C (paragraph 14) “Who would have known that you had the missing ingredient?” D (paragraph 22)

explanation.text: At first Molly calls Sam messy, sends him away, and tries to remove his apples from the pot. Her later question credits him with the missing ingredient, which shows that her annoyance has changed to appreciation.

exams.title: New York Grade 4 ELA - 2024 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2024 New York State Grade 4 English Language Arts Test.

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 1-6. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 19-23. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 26-31. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. What does the word “banks” mean as it is used in paragraph 1? 1 A angles in a road B rows of things that are like each other C sloping pieces of land D places where people work with money

explanation.text: The Egyptians arrive at a desert along the Nile River's banks and step off their boats onto land. In this setting, “banks” means the sloping pieces of land beside the river.

questions.alt: Question 2. What does the word “task” mean as it is used in paragraph 3? 2 A a way of moving something heavy B a job that needs to be done C a plan for making something happen D a reason to go to a new place

explanation.text: The workers are cutting, pulling, and placing giant stones to build Khufu's tomb, which the article calls their most important work. Therefore, the mighty “task” is a job that needs to be done.

questions.alt: Question 3. Which sentence best explains what happens in the section “A Towering Tomb”? A Workers drag stones up a ramp to place in a pyramid. B _ The king decides that he wants the tallest pyramid. C Workers come from different places to build a pyramid. D The king realizes that he needs more space in the pyramid.

explanation.text: The section explains that Khufu wanted more than a strong tomb: he ordered the tallest tomb ever built, reaching 481 feet. That decision is the section's main point.

questions.alt: Question 4. How does the illustration help the reader understand the information in 4 paragraph 6? A It shows how the parts of the pyramid are connected. B It shows why the pyramid needs to be so large. C It shows that there is an underground part of the pyramid. D It shows that there is an area in the pyramid for the queen.

explanation.text: Paragraph 6 names rooms, hallways, and air shafts needed inside the pyramid. The cutaway diagram places and links all those parts, so readers can understand how the pyramid's interior is connected.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which sentence from the article best supports the idea that people came from all 5 over the country to work for the pharaoh? “Others were pulling huge stones up the slanted sides of a building.” A (paragraph 2) B “Building the Great Pyramid took lots of planning.” (paragraph 6) “No one knows how the workers got the stones to the top of the pyramid.” C (paragraph 8) “Workers came to Giza from every part of Egypt to help build Khufu’s D tomb.” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: The statement that workers came to Giza “from every part of Egypt” directly identifies both their destination and their many places of origin. It therefore best supports the idea of nationwide participation.

questions.alt: Question 6. What is a main idea of the article? 6 A People were scared when they came to the banks of the Nile River. B Egyptians had the important job of building a tomb for the king. C People thought the king was a sky god who came down to Earth. D Egyptians spent three or four months each year working on the pyramid.

explanation.text: The article explains why Khufu ordered the Great Pyramid and how thousands of Egyptians planned, cut, transported, and placed its stones. These details support the main idea that building the king's tomb was an important national job.

questions.alt: Question 19. The phrase “play it cool” in paragraph 10 suggests that Abigail Iris A___ does not want to make any noise B does not want to feel foolish Cc does not want to seem too eager D___ does not want to look impolite

explanation.text: Although Abigail badly wants the kitten, she stays quiet because her brother says appearing calm can work better when asking for something. Thus, “play it cool” means not seeming too eager.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? “They are already above the popcorn I always get when we're done A shopping, and they would move right up to number one . . ” (paragraph 2) “The cat lady puts down the book she’s reading and reaches into a cage and B hands my mother the cat I picked for her” (paragraph 5) “All I see in there is a big stuffed teddy bear, and I’m thinking the cat- C _ rescue lady might need to wear better glasses when she goes out...” (paragraph 6) “He crawls up on my chest and buries his nose with the orange spot on it D into my neck, and then he’s very still . ..” (paragraph 13)

explanation.text: Spot crawls onto Abigail's chest, nestles into her neck, and becomes completely still, establishing their immediate bond. Therefore, that central connection is more important than shopping, popcorn, or the rescue worker's book.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which detail from the story shows how Abigail Iris’s mother feels about her 21 daughter’s request to have a pet? “ ‘Well, I don’t know,’ she says, smiling. ‘You have to be very gentle.’ ” A (paragraph 12) B “ ‘What a baby,’ she says about my kitten.” (paragraph 15) C “ ‘He’s very sweet,’ my mother says.” (paragraph 17) “See what he thinks about a cat now that you all are older. Maybe it’s time.” D (paragraph 19)

explanation.text: Mother's suggestion that they ask Dad, along with “maybe it's time,” shows that she is reconsidering the old refusal and may support getting a cat now that the children are older.

questions.alt: Question 22. What does the lady at the cat rescue most likely believe about Abigail Iris? 22 A Abigail Iris is too young to care for a cat. B Abigail Iris would be a good owner for Spot. C Abigail Iris does not understand cats’ needs. D Abigail Iris should get the older gray cat.

explanation.text: The rescue worker lets Abigail hold Spot after warning her to be gentle, then points out that the kitten likes her and does not try to escape. Those observations suggest she believes Abigail could be a good owner.

questions.alt: Question 23. Some people believe that pets can encourage responsibility. How do Abigail Iris’s 23 actions in the story support this belief? Abigail Iris and Mom think that a pet should be loved by a whole family so A Mom asks Dad what he thinks. Abigail Iris spends more time with her mom by going to the market as part B of her plan to get a cat. Abigail Iris clearly finds Spot to be cute and charming even though her C parents do not want a cat. Abigail Iris says she is willing to do new chores and work hard because she D loves Spot immediately.

explanation.text: Abigail promises to feed Spot, change his litter box, and spend her own allowance on toys. Her willingness to accept new chores and costs shows how caring for a pet can encourage responsibility.

questions.alt: Question 26. What is a central idea of paragraphs 2 through 4? A Changes in climate are affecting the production of chocolate. B Chocolate comes from a narrow area near the equator. C Some cacao trees grow where tropical rainforests are located. D__ The chocolate being made today is of lower quality than in the past.

explanation.text: Changed weather in the Cocoa Belt causes cacao trees to grow fewer pods and beans of lower quality while pests and disease spread. These effects therefore develop the central idea that climate change is reducing chocolate production.

questions.alt: Question 27. According to paragraphs 7 and 8, how are weather stations helping to save chocolate? A __ They help trees survive during times of little rain. B _ They provide farmers advice on treating tree diseases. C They give information about planting and caring for trees. D__They make rules for farmers to follow for each season.

explanation.text: Weather stations provide current information that tells farmers when to plant and fertilize, replacing old seasonal rules that no longer always work. They therefore guide the planting and care of cacao trees.

questions.alt: Question 28. What does the phrase “a sweet deal” mean as used in paragraph 9? 28 A dangerous sign B new beginning C difficult challenge D good result

explanation.text: The proposed fixes could produce more cacao on less land, protect rainforests, preserve chocolate, and benefit the planet. Because all those outcomes are positive, “a sweet deal” means a good result.

questions.alt: Question 29. Howis the section “Making Candy” organized? The author gives a detailed explanation about the similarities between A cacao and cocoa. B The author describes the steps used to make chocolate from growing cacao pods to preparing a chocolate bar for sale. The author provides detailed answers to frequently asked questions about Cc . how chocolate is grown and harvested. The author shows how chocolate makers are using new processes to Ds. . improve the flavor of their product.

explanation.text: The numbered section follows chocolate production in order: growing and picking pods, removing, fermenting, and drying beans, then roasting and blending them into candy. Therefore, it presents a step-by-step process.

questions.alt: Question 30. How does the photograph after paragraph 14 best support an idea developed in the article? A __ It explains why machines are not useful for harvesting pods. B__It shows how the seeds or beans are separated from the pods. C It gives an example of how climate change has damaged the pods. D__It shows why scientists want to develop trees that produce more pods.

explanation.text: The photograph shows an opened cacao pod with individual beans visible inside it. That image directly illustrates the nearby step explaining how farmers separate the beans from their pods.

questions.alt: Question 31. Many people enjoy eating chocolate. Which quote best supports this idea? “Last year, Americans spent more than $22 billion on chocolate” A (paragraph 1) B “You need more of it to make chocolate taste, well, chocolatey.” (paragraph 3) “Then the liquid chocolate is poured into molds and prepared for sale.” Cc (paragraph 17) D = “This is the moment you've been waiting for” (paragraph 18)

explanation.text: Spending more than $22 billion on chocolate in a single year demonstrates enormous demand for the treat. That amount is strong evidence that many Americans enjoy eating chocolate.

exams.title: New York Grade 5 ELA - 2024 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2024 New York State Grade 5 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2024 NYS Grade 5 English Language Arts Test Released Questions
