Official passage for Questions 29-35. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. Which detail from paragraph 3 best helps the reader understand the meaning of the phrase “well 1 turned-out”? A sound of drumming hooves B pranced down the ramp C his shabby winter coat D feverishly he pawed the ground

explanation.text: Tom remembers Chancey as a sleek, well-presented horse but now sees his shabby winter coat. That contrast shows that “well turned-out” refers to a neat appearance, which the shabby coat lacks.

questions.alt: Question 2. How do paragraphs 3 and 11 develop an idea in the story? A __ They show how Tom changes his mind about Chancey. B They explain the cause and effect of Chancey’s arrival. C__ They reveal why Tom wanted to train Chancey himself. D__ They compare how Chancey looked at different times.

explanation.text: Paragraph 3 presents Chancey on arrival with a shabby, foamy winter coat, while paragraph 11 recalls his glossy coat and rippling muscles the previous season. The paragraphs therefore compare his appearance at two times.

questions.alt: Question 3. Paragraphs 14 and 18 are important to understanding Chancey because they show that he 3 A can be a calm and friendly animal B is confused about his new home C will be easy to train and care for D understands that Tom has to leave

explanation.text: Chancey settles, gently searches Tom's pockets, accepts a mint, and later calmly eats while Tom leaves. These behaviors reveal that beneath his nervous arrival he can be relaxed and friendly.

questions.alt: Question 4. Tom and Nick are similar in the story because they both 4 A are eager to clip Chancey and make him look his best B learn about Chancey from the man who drove him C know how to make Chancey feel appreciated D show confidence in the effort to help Chancey

explanation.text: Tom insists clipping will make Chancey look fine, and Nick calmly says the neglected coat will not be much of a problem. Both boys therefore express confidence that effort and care can help the horse.

questions.alt: Question 5. How does paragraph 19 help the reader understand the story? 5 It explains the reason why Tom spends time at the stable by suggesting that his mother A does not like horses. It reveals more about Tom’s character by describing how important spending time at the B stable is to him. It shows a resolution to Tom’s problem by showing that he only leaves the stable once C Chancey is comfortable. It tells the effect of Chancey coming to the stable by explaining that Tom no longer wants D to spend time at home.

explanation.text: Tom obeys the required lunch visit mainly to stop his mother from complaining about how much time he spends at Sandy Lane. This reason reveals that being at the stable matters greatly to him.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which sentence best expresses a theme of the story? 6 A Patience and hard work teach responsibility. B Giving advice to others in unexpected situations is not always helpful. C Situations that seem difficult at first may be better than they appear. D Being kind to others helps them realize their mistakes.

explanation.text: Chancey first appears shabby, frantic, and difficult, disappointing Tom. Once treated gently, however, he settles, nuzzles Tom, and accepts food, therefore supporting the theme that a hard situation may be better than it appears.

questions.alt: Question 7. Scientists who have studied horses believe that they are able to understand the moods of the people around them. How does this idea best connect to the details in the story? A ___ The workers at the stable are frightened by how poorly Chancey behaves. B Chancey is relaxed and comfortable when Tom brushes him and speaks nicely to him. C_— Chancey is nervous and upset during his trip to the stable in a trailer. D_ Nick is confident Chancey will behave better once the horse has time to be alone.

explanation.text: Tom approaches slowly, speaks gently, offers a mint, and brushes Chancey; the horse then settles and nuzzles him instead of jumping nervously. This response connects the horse's calm mood to Tom's reassuring behavior.

questions.alt: Question 22. How do the details in paragraph 2 support a central idea of the article? A They highlight the idea that certain animals in a city may be helpful to people. B__They show that the city is large enough to support many different kinds of birds. C They explain how different species use city spaces like they do in a natural environment. D__ They compare the homes of animals living in a city with their shelters in the wild.

explanation.text: Paragraph 2 shows birds nesting on skyscrapers and bridges, butterflies migrating through streets, and bats living in a church belfry. These examples explain how species use built city spaces as habitat.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which detail from the article best explains why wildlife can survive in urban areas? “A frog darts from a drainpipe to snatch the dragonfly. From the rooftops, a hawk swoops A d » lown to grab the frog” (paragraph 1) “Wild animals need food, water, space to hunt or hide, and a place to raise young. A city B offers all of these.” (paragraph 1) “These insects can live for two months without food, and haven't changed much since scurrying around with dinosaurs millions of years ago.’ (paragraph 3) “These marvelous little wrigglers gobble up dead leaves, food scraps, and garbage, and turn them into rich soil.” (paragraph 3)

explanation.text: The article lists the essentials wild animals need—food, water, hunting or hiding space, and places to raise young—then states that a city supplies all of them. This directly explains why wildlife survives there.

questions.alt: Question 24. What is the meaning of the word “interacts” as it is used in paragraph 4 of the article? 24 A understands well B remains hidden C changes places D comes close

explanation.text: Biologists and officials want wildlife to remain healthy and encounter humans safely in shared city spaces. In this context, “interacts” means comes close to or has contact with.

questions.alt: Question 25. Scientists have found that ants eat a great deal of the food thrown away by people. How does this 25 idea mainly connect to the details in the article? A Like birds, ants will likely be able to find safe places to live in cities. B Like earthworms and bats, ants are good additions to a city environment. C Like cockroaches, ants will likely survive in a city even during very difficult times. D Like butterflies and bears, ants are only attracted to city spaces during certain seasons.

explanation.text: Because ants remove discarded food, much as earthworms turn scraps into useful soil and bats consume huge numbers of insects, their helpful cleanup and control roles make all three good additions to a city.

questions.alt: Question 26. What is the main claim the author makes about people and wildlife? 26 A People should consider city spaces and rural spaces as homes for wildlife. B People should take time to learn about the wildlife in the places where they live. C People should work with local groups to protect wildlife in their neighborhoods. D People should be prepared to encounter dangerous animals in urban environments.

explanation.text: The author calls cities natural habitat, gives examples of many urban species, and urges readers to look for wildlife in any big city. These points support treating both urban and rural spaces as wildlife homes.

questions.alt: Question 29. Which sentence best describes the narrator’s point of view? A __ The narrator shares Larry’s thoughts, showing how important lobster fishing is to him. B The narrator shares Larry’s dialogue, showing why he believes his lobsters can be sold. C __ The narrator shares the thoughts of Larry’s father, showing how carefully he prepares. D The narrator shares the feelings of Larry’s father, showing his pride about the lobsters.

explanation.text: The narrator reports Larry's private thoughts as he lies awake comparing kinds of fishing and decides to become a lobsterman. Access to those thoughts shows how important lobster fishing is to Larry.

questions.alt: Question 30. How do paragraphs 1 and 5 mainly contribute to the structure of the story? 30 A by telling the reader the causes of later events B by giving the reader a broader understanding of the setting C by describing to the reader the different types of fishing D by entertaining the reader with descriptions of animal behavior

explanation.text: Paragraph 1 locates Larry in a Maine harbor filled with fishing boats, while paragraph 5 expands the coast to beaches, rocky islands, and seals. Together, the descriptions build a broader understanding of the seaside setting.

questions.alt: Question 31. Read this sentence from paragraph 8. “There must be lobsters down there, for sure,” Larry says. Which detail from the story best supports the reason Larry feels certain about the lobsters? A __ the suggestion in paragraph 2 that Larry looks for lobsters each spring B__ the reference in paragraph 6 to Larry steering the lobster boat by himself C__ the claim in paragraph 6 about the types of places where lobsters usually hide D _ the indication in paragraph 9 about why the lobster trap buoys are important

explanation.text: Paragraph 6 explains that lobsters hide in cool water among seaweed and rocks. The thick kelp in the cove matches that habitat, so Larry has a clear reason to expect lobsters there.

questions.alt: Question 32. Read this excerpt from paragraph 9. 32 . . . the yellow buoy with black and white stripes floats on top to mark where the trap is. According to this excerpt, buoys are used to A attract lobsters to the traps B show locations where lobsters may be C help lobster fishers find their traps D allow lobster fishers to find their way home

explanation.text: After a trap sinks and disappears among the kelp, its brightly striped buoy remains floating on the surface to mark the spot. Therefore, the buoy helps fishers locate and retrieve their trap.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which detail best supports a theme of the story? A “But winter storms are hard on boats and traps . .” (paragraph 2) B “Every lobsterman uses different colors so that he can tell his buoys . .” (paragraph 3) C “They pass rocky islands where seals bask in the sun . .” (paragraph 5) D “All day long they drop the traps over the side of the boat . .” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: Larry and his father repeatedly return for more traps and work all day dropping them one by one before going home. That sustained effort best supports a theme that hard work is necessary for a successful catch.

questions.alt: Question 34. Paragraphs 13 and 14 suggest that Larry 34 A prefers eating food he catches himself B enjoys relaxing while on a boat C fishes with his father to earn money D feels proud of his ability to catch lobsters

explanation.text: Larry wants his family to eat the first three lobsters caught in his own trap, then lies awake planning to become a lobsterman. His wish to celebrate and continue the work shows pride in his new ability.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? 35 A the description of what Larry and his father wear when they go out fishing on the boat B the description of what Larry and his father see on the beaches while they are on the boat the description of the kelp in the cove that Larry and his father see while fishing on the C boat the description of Larry and his father setting traps then returning to shore all day in the D boat

explanation.text: Setting traps in many locations, making repeated trips for more, and returning to shore after a full day describe the central work Larry does with his father. Therefore, those actions matter more to a summary than clothing or scenery.

exams.title: New York Grade 6 ELA - 2024 Released Questions

Official passage for Questions 22-26. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 29-35. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 15. What is the meaning of the word “parched” as it is used in paragraph 4? 15 A difficult B dry C dangerous D dusty

explanation.text: Mali elephants live at the Sahara's edge and must search almost constantly for lifesaving water. Since their environment lacks water, “parched” means extremely dry.

questions.alt: Question 16. Read this sentence from paragraph 8. Their orange-and-black wings color the sky like an explosion of confetti. What is the author’s main purpose for using this sentence? A __ to describe a feeling of excitement while watching butterflies B__ to indicate the direction the butterflies will go as they travel C__ todescribe the large number of butterflies taking flight D__ to indicate the distance the butterflies will need to travel

explanation.text: Millions of monarchs have covered nearly every inch of the trees before rising together. Comparing their colored wings to an explosion of confetti therefore helps readers picture the enormous number filling the sky.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which sentence from the article best supports the author’s central claim? “The sun—its rise and fall over a day and the whirling of the Earth around it for a year— A powers these events.” (paragraph 2) “Like the region’s other hoofed animals—wildebeests and gazelles—zebras must stay on B the move for fresh grass and water.” (paragraph 16) “Sometimes, herds will come together by the thousands to find better feeding grounds.” c (paragraph 17) “The timing of their travels is driven by rainfall, which fuels the growth of new grass and D » refills water holes. (paragraph 19)

explanation.text: The article presents migrations tied to daily sunlight, yearly seasons, warmth, rainfall, and food growth. The broad claim that the sun powers these events therefore unifies the many animal examples.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which detail from the article represents the author’s belief? A “They are some of nature's most thrilling dramas.” (paragraph 2) “,.. forgetting the location of a lifesaving water hole would be a deadly mistake” B (paragraph 3) C_ “.. these jellyfish undertake a major journey every single day.’ (paragraph 12) D “Human babies can take a year or more to take their first steps.” (paragraph 21)

explanation.text: Calling migrations “some of nature's most thrilling dramas” therefore expresses the author's personal judgment about how exciting they are. The other choices report conditions or behaviors that can be verified.

questions.alt: Question 19. According to the article, zebras and wildebeests are similar because both animals A _ walk right after birth B migrate for the same reasons C__ live in small groups D __ eat the same type of grass

explanation.text: Zebras follow rainfall to fresh grass and water, and wildebeests follow seasonal rains to greener grasslands and water. These parallel needs show that both species migrate for the same reasons.

questions.alt: Question 20. The main way the different “Motive to Move” sections help the reader understand migration is by A __ explaining why animals search for watering holes B __ referring to areas where there is a lack of grass C_— explaining why animals travel to different places D _ referring to seasons that change from dry to rainy

explanation.text: Each “Motive to Move” section identifies the need driving travel, such as water, warmer temperatures, sunlight, or fresh grass. Together, the sections explain why different animals journey to new places.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which phrase from the article best helps the reader understand the claim in paragraph 22 that 21 “being in a rush is a way of life” for wildebeests? A the reference to the wildebeest being “a favorite snack” (paragraph 22) B the indication that wildebeests follow “the seasonal rains” (paragraph 22) C the explanation that plant eaters like the wildebeest “stay on the move” (paragraph 23) D the claim that wildebeests would “starve if they didn’t migrate” (paragraph 23)

explanation.text: The article calls wildebeests a “favorite snack” for lions, leopards, crocodiles, and hyenas and says even newborns must move within minutes. That immediate danger explains why rushing is a way of life.

questions.alt: Question 22. According to the article, what was the main effect of Franklin’s study of static electricity? 22 A the ability to generate static electricity to study it B the understanding that static electricity resembled lightning C the realization that lightning was larger than static electricity D the creation of a tool to prevent lightning from harming people

explanation.text: Franklin's experiments led him to recognize lightning as electrical energy; he then used that knowledge to invent a rod that carries strikes harmlessly into the ground. Thus, his study produced a tool that protects people and buildings.

questions.alt: Question 23. Working with electricity can sometimes be dangerous. Which detail in the article best supports this idea? A __ the claim that sharks “can sense electric signals” (paragraph 2) B___ the statement that “Lightning often hit tall buildings” (paragraph 8) the claim that a battery uses “metal and chemicals to create electrical energy” c (paragraph 10) the statement that many objects used for light “were smelly and caused fires” D (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: Lightning, a powerful form of electricity, often struck tall buildings, started deadly fires, and even killed bell ringers. The detail about strikes on buildings therefore supports the danger of working around electricity.

questions.alt: Question 24. In paragraph 14, how does the author demonstrate how the demand for electricity “caught on”? 24 A by highlighting important scientists who studied electricity B by including dates and descriptions of major discoveries C by suggesting that people began to appreciate and use electricity D by indicating that certain inventions helped people remain safe

explanation.text: Tesla's method let stations deliver electricity hundreds of miles to thousands of people; demand then grew until people could hardly imagine life without it. This expansion shows that society increasingly appreciated and used electricity.

questions.alt: Question 25. What is most likely the author’s opinion about electricity? 25 A Electricity should continue to be studied by scientists and inventors. B Electricity was made popular as the result of the electric motor. C Electricity was not useful until energy could travel longer distances. D Electricity is an important and essential part of people’s lives.

explanation.text: The article begins with electricity powering lights, music, television, phones, and even body signals, then ends by saying most people cannot imagine life without it. These statements reveal the author's view that electricity is essential.

questions.alt: Question 26. How does the author organize information to develop a central idea in the article? 26 A by contrasting the different scientists who studied electricity B by providing details about the development of electricity in chronological order C by explaining how electricity helped solve a variety of problems D by using a cause and effect structure to highlight the significance of electricity

explanation.text: The history moves from Thales 2,600 years ago to Guericke, Franklin, Volta, Faraday, Edison, and Tesla in dated sequence. This chronological organization shows how discoveries gradually developed modern electricity.

questions.alt: Question 29. In paragraph 2, the author uses the phrase “in a foreign tongue” to indicate that Mate 29 A only responds to the commands of Charlie B dislikes being patted by the townspeople C does not want to his walk with Charlie D does not care what the townspeople say

explanation.text: Charlie trained Mate with shipboard words instead of ordinary commands, so the townspeople's everyday language sounds unfamiliar to the dog. The “foreign tongue” comparison indicates that he responds only to Charlie's command system.

questions.alt: Question 30. Which detail signals a change in the direction of the story? 30 the indication that Mate listened “with his head cocked to one side and one ear raised” A (paragraph 2) the statement that “Mate would bark loudly, acting as the Fast and Able’s foghorn” B (paragraph 3) C the indication that “Charlie fell to the bottom of the boat unconscious” (paragraph 4) the statement that “Mate leaped into the cold water and dog-paddled to the beach” D (paragraph 5)

explanation.text: Before Charlie is struck, the passage describes a peaceful afternoon sail. His fall into the boat unconscious therefore creates sudden danger and changes the story into Mate's effort to find help.

questions.alt: Question 31. Read this sentence from paragraph 5. Mate whimpered and frantically licked his master’s face, but the old seaman did not move. The words “whimpered” and “frantically” affect the tone of the story by A __ Creating a sense of sorrow B__ establishing a feeling of hopelessness C creating a sense of anxiety D _ establishing a feeling of confusion

explanation.text: Mate's whimpering and frantic attempts to wake an unresponsive Charlie show urgent fear for his master. Those emotionally charged words therefore create an anxious tone.

questions.alt: Question 32. How does paragraph 8 best help advance the plot of the story? 32 A by showing that Jake knows Charlie’s dog B by demonstrating how Mate responds to Jake’s voice C by having Jake realize Charlie may be in trouble D by illustrating the trust Jake inspires in Mate

explanation.text: Jake recognizes Mate, remembers the sailor commands, and sees from the dog's response that Charlie may be in trouble. This realization advances the plot by turning the confused crowd into rescuers who follow Mate.

questions.alt: Question 33. What do the details in paragraphs 9 through 11 mainly reveal about Mate? 33 A He is popular in the town. B He cares deeply about his master. C He knows how to save lives. D He responds quickly to commands.

explanation.text: Mate leads people to the beach, swims back to the drifting boat, and keeps licking and attending to Charlie until he wakes. These determined rescue actions reveal deep concern for his master.

questions.alt: Question 34. Which detail best shows how the author indicates the close friendship of Charlie and Mate to the reader? “Sailing in each other’s company provided a perfect afternoon for Charlie and Mate,’ A (paragraph 3) “Mate either slept on the sun-warmed deck or joined Charlie in the cockpit, B (paragraph 3) C_ “The cold seawater flew off Mate’s furry coat and splashed over Charlie,” (paragraph 10) D “Charlie softly whispered, ‘Shipshape!’ to Mate, then a little louder, ‘Hardtack!’ ” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The statement that sailing in one another's company makes a perfect afternoon directly describes the joy Charlie and Mate take in being together. It therefore best demonstrates their close friendship.

questions.alt: Question 35. A common saying is that “Dogs are man’s best friend.” Which detail from the story best supports 35 this idea? A Mate responds to words Charlie teaches him. B Mate walks with Charlie when they are in town. C Mate sleeps in the boat when Charlie is sailing. D Mate leaves the boat to get help for Charlie.

explanation.text: Mate leaves the familiar boat, swims through cold water, and searches town for help when Charlie is unconscious. Risking himself and taking action to save Charlie most strongly demonstrates a best friend's loyalty.

exams.title: New York Grade 7 ELA - 2024 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2024 New York State Grade 7 English Language Arts Test.

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 15-21. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 36-42. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 15. Which statement best expresses a central idea of paragraph 2? 15 A Sticks and rudder pedals were used in both cockpits of the airplane. B People learned how to fly airplanes by observing the actions of the pilot. C Metal tailskids would create friction to slow the airplanes as they landed. D Pilots maneuvered their airplanes by using vertical sticks and rudder pedals.

explanation.text: Bessie cannot see or hear direct instructions, so she watches her duplicate controls move with the instructor's actions. This evidence shows that people learned to fly by carefully observing a pilot.

questions.alt: Question 16. What do the details in paragraph 7 most reveal about Bessie Coleman? 16 A her preference for planes made in France B her desire to share her knowledge with others C her desire to travel throughout the United States D her dissatisfaction with performing as a barnstormer

explanation.text: Bessie plans to open an aviation school where anyone interested can learn. Her goal shows that she wants to share her knowledge of flying with others.

questions.alt: Question 17. What is the main way paragraph 3 connects with paragraph 8? 17 A by referring to the altitude to which Coleman flew B by describing Coleman’s popularity with crowds C by indicating the types of planes Coleman flew D by demonstrating Coleman’s skills as a pilot

explanation.text: Paragraph 3 describes Bessie's precise final flight test, including an exact landing with the engine off. That preparation connects to paragraph 8 because she later demonstrates those piloting skills before a public crowd.

questions.alt: Question 18. How do paragraphs 8 and 9 develop a central idea in the article? 18 by referring to the thousands of people watching the first female African-American A aviator B by indicating that Coleman was prohibited from flying the plane without another pilot by illustrating how the first female African-American pilot flew in loops while tilting the C plane D by describing how Coleman wanted to fly a type of plane with which she was unfamiliar

explanation.text: Three thousand spectators watch Bessie make the first public flight by a Black woman in the United States, and she then flies solo. These historic achievements are the paragraphs' central focus because both paragraphs emphasize her pioneering public flights.

questions.alt: Question 19. The tone of paragraph 9 can best be described as A ___ anxious, due to the dangerous jump of the stuntman B___ tense, because of the height to which the airplane flew C__ delighted, due to the reaction of the crowd to the stuntman D enthusiastic, because people were excited for the opportunity to fly

explanation.text: The crowd goes wild after Bessie carries the stuntman, and passengers eagerly line up for rides. These celebratory reactions create a delighted tone because they express the audience's excitement and approval.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which statement best describes why Coleman goes to flight school in France? A __ She knows there are more aircraft manufacturers in France. B__ She is denied the opportunity to train as a pilot in the United States. C__ She believes flying is more popular in France than in the United States. D She knows she will be able to learn by observing her instructors in France.

explanation.text: Bessie goes to France because flight schools in the United States refuse to train her. France gives her the opportunity to earn a pilot's license that she was denied at home.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which sentence would be most important to include in a summary of the article? 21 A Coleman first takes flying lessons in a twenty-seven-foot biplane. B Coleman claims flying in France is as popular as driving cars in America. C Coleman is described by newspapers as a careful but skilled pilot. D Coleman is the first African-American woman to perform a solo flight.

explanation.text: Bessie's first public solo flight as a Black woman is the passage's major historical achievement. A summary should emphasize that event because the plane and audience are only supporting details.

questions.alt: Question 22. Read this phrase from paragraph 8. 22 . . . and as for shoes, they imprisoned freedom-loving feet accustomed to the feel of warm earth. How does the use of personification in this phrase mainly add to the reader’s understanding of the narrator? A by emphasizing the narrator’s preference for being outside B by illustrating how the narrator feels about school C by showing how the narrator is different than Little Man D by highlighting the narrator’s desire to help Papa

explanation.text: The shoes are said to imprison feet that are accustomed to the warm earth. This personification emphasizes Cassie's preference for being barefoot because she associates the outdoors with freedom.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? 23 “Always meticulously neat, six-year-old Little Man never allowed dirt or tears or stains to A mar anything he owned.” (paragraph 1) “If I hadn’t known the cause of it, I could have forgotten very easily that he was, at twelve, B bigger than I . . .” (paragraph 5) “An ancient oak tree on the slope, visible even now, was the official dividing mark C between Logan land and the beginning of a dense forest.” (paragraph 10) “. . . for the past three years there had not been enough money from the cotton to pay D both and live on too.” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The family's cotton income does not cover the mortgage, taxes, and other expenses, so Papa must work away from home. That financial struggle is the paragraph's main point.

questions.alt: Question 24. How does the narrator mainly develop each brother’s viewpoint? 24 A by indicating their feelings about school B through descriptions of their personalities C by indicating her disagreements with them D through descriptions of their appearances

explanation.text: Cassie describes Little Man as extremely neat, Stacey as sour, and Christopher-John as sensitive and cheerful. These contrasting descriptions reveal each sibling's distinct personality and viewpoint.

questions.alt: Question 25. Siblings may be very different but still completely understand each other. How do the details in the story best support this idea? A __ The narrator describes the traits of each brother as they walk to school. B The narrator and her brothers understand the importance of the land to their family. C The narrator realizes why her oldest brother is feeling unhappy. D The narrator and her brothers respect their father’s decision to work in other states.

explanation.text: Cassie recognizes each sibling's different habits and feelings, even explaining why Stacey is in a bad mood. Her accurate descriptions show that the siblings differ but understand one another.

questions.alt: Question 26. What do the details in the story reveal about Papa? 26 A He prefers working on the railroad to growing cotton. B He was not surprised when the price of cotton changed. C He is determined to keep the land his father purchased. D He was not able to talk to his other children about the land.

explanation.text: Papa works away from home to help pay the mortgage and taxes, and he promises that the family will not have to live on someone else's land. His actions and words show his determination to keep their land.

questions.alt: Question 29. Read these sentences from paragraph 6. 29 “You decide too quickly. Such snap judgments make you unworthy. You are dismissed.” Which important idea does the author develop in these sentences? A It is best to lead by example. B Make sure to tell the truth when you speak. C Try to have all of the information before making a choice. D Go with your first instinct when making a statement.

explanation.text: Lee objects before learning the complete rules and is immediately dismissed for making a snap judgment. His mistake shows why a wise decision requires having all the available information first.

questions.alt: Question 30. What does paragraph 8 reveal about Persephone? 30 A She struggles at her school. B She becomes frustrated easily. C She wants to have more choices. D She thinks carefully before acting.

explanation.text: Persephone studies what each student's choice might mean and realizes that the apparently easy winning answer may be a trap. This careful evaluation shows that she thinks deeply before deciding.

questions.alt: Question 31. What is the best definition of “settle” as it is used in paragraph 8? 31 A to agree to something less B to separate materials C to become quiet D to find a place to stay

explanation.text: Laura deliberately chooses blades even though the proctor has already revealed stone. Because she accepts a losing position instead of seeking victory, “settle” means to agree to less than one wants.

questions.alt: Question 32. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? “,.. shed received twelve years of strict training and guidance in history and philosophy A and psychology . .” (paragraph 1) B “The students who select the wisest answer will become Deciders.” (paragraph 2) C ‘Lee's face flushed, and his lips trembled. He ran out of the room . ..” (paragraph 6) D “The Proctor sat at a red table. Behind him there was an exit.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The examination asks students to select the wisest response, and those who succeed become Deciders. This is the best summary because it captures both the test's purpose and its importance to the students.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which sentence from the story best describes Persephone’s perspective about the RPS exam? 33 A “Persephone’s mind raced and she felt a flush across her skin.” (paragraph 3) B “Persephone thought about protesting, but something held her back.” (paragraph 4) C “Persephone knew it couldn’t be that easy.” (paragraph 8) D “Persephone watched them go into the room one by one.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: Persephone “knew it couldn't be that easy” when the obvious winning choice appeared. That thought reveals her skeptical, analytical perspective on the examination.

questions.alt: Question 34. What does the word “equality” mean as it is used in paragraph 12? 34 A agreement B fairness C intelligence D tolerance

explanation.text: Persephone explains that matching stone with stone places both players on an even field, where neither destroys nor bows to the other. Therefore, equality represents fairness.

questions.alt: Question 35. How does Persephone change from the beginning to the end of the story? 35 A At first she is afraid of the test, but then she succeeds at it. B At first she is uncertain about the test, but then she understands it. C At first she is suspicious of the test, but then she cares about it. D At first she is angry at the test, but then she finds it amusing.

explanation.text: At first Persephone is unsure whether the examination is chance or a trick. By the end, she understands the choices' meanings, explains the wisdom of equality, and passes.

questions.alt: Question 36. According to paragraph 3, the calls of prairie dogs are “distinctive” because they 36 A are understood by other animals B tend to be short C convey important information D are unique sounds

explanation.text: Each prairie-dog warning has sound differences that people can barely hear, yet the calls change with the kind of danger. Therefore, “distinctive” means having a unique sound.

questions.alt: Question 37. Which claim by the author is most strongly supported with evidence? “Prairie dogs got their name because people thought their calls sounded a little like a A aoe’ » og’s bark” (paragraph 1) “It's hard for humans to hear the differences between the calls, though, because they only B last about half a second . . ” (paragraph 3) “The prairie dogs watched regular dogs the same way they watched coyotes, but not as c intensely.’ (paragraph 7) “Slobodchikoff also thinks prairie dogs pay attention to the order of the sounds ..” D (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The recorded calls consistently put “chitter” before “chatter” and never reverse them. That repeated order supports the claim that prairie dogs pay attention to the sequence of sounds.

questions.alt: Question 38. Read this sentence from paragraph 9. 38 At first Slobodchikoff couldn’t believe it. Which statement provides the best support for this claim? Prairie dogs have almost the same response to coyotes as they do to the regular dogs A they encounter. Prairie dogs have similar reactions to airborne predators, although they have a variety of B responses to land animals. Prairie dogs have unique responses to people wearing different colors as well as to C various types of dogs. Prairie dogs have alarm calls they use to warn their colonies, although their calls provoke D little response.

explanation.text: Prairie dogs make different calls for different shirt colors and even for different types of dogs. Such precise distinctions are surprising evidence of how complex their communication may be.

questions.alt: Question 39. How does paragraph 5 relate to paragraph 10? 39 A by explaining that prairie dog alarms appear to contain specific details B by indicating the similarity of prairie dog alarm calls about airborne predators C by explaining that prairie dogs may make a continuous sound when they communicate D by indicating that prairie dog calls distinguish between land animals and birds

explanation.text: Paragraph 5 introduces small call differences connected to particular dangers, while paragraph 10 explains that those calls can contain detailed information. The later paragraph develops the meaning of the earlier observation.

questions.alt: Question 40. Which statement best represents a central idea of the article? “Actually, maybe they should’ve been called prairie watchdogs because they bark to alert A the colony ..” (paragraph 1) “After all, prairie dogs are just rodents, like mice and rats, and aren't supposed to be that B smart? (paragraph 9) “Although the calls are a single sound, or at least a continuous one, they seem to carry a lot of information” (paragraph 10) “Besides studying alarm calls, he has also recorded the little noises, the chitter- chattering .. ” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: Researchers find that one continuous prairie-dog alarm call can identify a threat and describe specific features such as color or type. This evidence supports the idea that a single call carries a great deal of information.

questions.alt: Question 41. Read this phrase from paragraph 11. They chitter-chatter, but they don’t chatter-chitter. What does this phrase suggest about prairie dogs? A___ The noises prairie dogs make to each other consist of predictable patterns. B_ Prairie dogs are unable to reverse the order of the sounds they make to each other. C__ The noises prairie dogs make to each other are less important than their alarm calls. D Prairie dogs usually ignore the sounds that are unrelated to the presence of predators.

explanation.text: The repeated “chitter-chatter” order suggests that prairie-dog calls follow predictable patterns. The fact that the animals never use the reverse order supports this conclusion.

questions.alt: Question 42. The main reason spectrograms are important to Slobodchikoff’s research is because they A ___ Suggest to researchers that prairie dogs respond to different clothes B help researchers analyze prairie dog alarm calls from several miles away C __ enable researchers to hear slowed-down prairie dog alarm calls D___ allow researchers to compare many prairie dog alarm calls

explanation.text: Spectrograms turn recorded calls into pictures that researchers can slow down and examine. They are important because researchers can compare many alarm calls and notice differences people cannot easily hear.

questions.alt: Question 15. What tone does the phrase “I studied it obsessively” in paragraph 1 establish in the article? 15 A It creates curiosity by encouraging readers to consider becoming beekeepers. B It creates interest by showing the passion the author has for beekeeping. C It creates seriousness by showing readers how compelling beekeeping can become. D It creates suspense by suggesting how long the process is to become a beekeeper.

explanation.text: The word “obsessively” suggests that the author studies the catalog with unusually intense attention. This establishes interest because his effort reveals a strong passion for beginning beekeeping.

questions.alt: Question 16. Paragraphs 2 through 4 develop the idea presented in paragraph 1 by 16 A explaining the reasons why the author finds beekeeping to be so complicated B detailing a range of things about beekeeping that attracted the author to the hobby C describing the steps the author followed to select the equipment for and build a beehive D summarizing the experience that led the author to become a successful beekeeper

explanation.text: After discovering the catalog, the author orders a beginner's kit, assembles and paints the hive, and selects a bee supplier. This sequence develops paragraph 1 because it describes how he chose equipment and built his hive.

questions.alt: Question 17. Paragraph 7 contributes to a central idea of the article by demonstrating that 17 A bees prefer the climate of southern states B beekeeping requires learned knowledge and skills C bees need several days to adjust to a new hive D beekeeping is easier in areas that are hot and dry

explanation.text: The author's first colony dies because he does not yet know to protect the bees from cold rain by replacing the lid. His costly mistake demonstrates that beekeeping requires knowledge and skills learned through experience.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which sentence from the article best reveals the author’s enthusiasm for beekeeping as a hobby? “I wanted my hives to have a bit more style, and I had a can left over from painting the outside of my house.” (paragraph 3) “Once I received the bees I proceeded to install them into my freshly painted hive” B (paragraph 6) “Once I achieved that level of success and tasted the result, the honey, all of the setbacks and lessons were worth it” (paragraph 9) “T have never been too successful, but I have always harvested enough honey for myself and my friends” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The author says that tasting his honey made all the setbacks and lessons worthwhile. This most clearly reveals enthusiasm because he values the result enough to persist through years of failure.

questions.alt: Question 19. In the author’s first two years of beekeeping, he was unable to 19 A get the bees to colonize his hive B maintain a positive attitude C keep the bees alive all year D harvest any honey

explanation.text: The first colony dies before producing honey, and the second lives through the year but produces “not a drop.” Therefore, the author is unable to harvest any honey during either of his first two years.

questions.alt: Question 20. How does the author’s attitude toward beekeeping change over the course of the article? It shifts from excitement about the arrival of the beehive to defeat because of the dead A bees. B__It varies from confusion about how to proceed to contentment with the new hobby. C__Itshifts from eagerness to set up the apiary to embarrassment for his failures. D It varies from strong interest in the new project to satisfaction with his accomplishments.

explanation.text: The author begins with intense interest, eagerly ordering supplies and styling his hive. His attitude becomes satisfaction because, after learning from failures, he harvests enough honey for himself and his friends.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which statement best expresses a central idea of the article? 21 A Beekeeping is challenging, yet it yields rewards. B Few farmers are able to become successful beekeepers. C Beekeeping requires the purchase of special, expensive equipment. D Some farmers specialize in growing and selling bee colonies.

explanation.text: The author's colonies fail at first and require years of practice, yet the eventual honey makes his setbacks worthwhile. These contrasting results support the central idea that beekeeping is challenging but rewarding.

questions.alt: Question 22. Read this sentence from paragraph 7. 22 Dawn showed a mean gray sea heading up into leaden gray clouds and a wind that moaned through the stays and rigging. What tone does this sentence create in the excerpt? A a tone of threat and danger B a tone of distrust and sadness C a tone of curiosity and confusion D a tone of reflection and longing

explanation.text: Words such as “mean,” “leaden,” and “moaned” make the sea and sky seem hostile and ominous. This imagery creates a tone of threat and danger because it signals the approaching storm's power.

questions.alt: Question 23. In paragraph 9, what is the effect of the phrase “slamming, rising, heeling, skidding, slamming 23 down again”? A It imitates the disorder of David’s thoughts during the storm. B It shows the persistent force of the storm on David and the Frog. C It emphasizes the repetitive nature of the waters along the Baja coast. D It illustrates the conditions in which the Frog was intended to sail.

explanation.text: The rapid series of motion words repeats “slamming” and gives no pause between the boat's movements. This rhythm shows the storm's persistent force battering David and the Frog.

questions.alt: Question 24. Which detail best shows David's attitude toward his situation? “The storm might be days getting to him, might not come at all, and he didn’t have much A food and fishing wasn’t going that well” (paragraph 2) “,.. he brought her around, up into the wind until she was tacking northwest and B making a solid six knots.’ (paragraph 4) “More than once he was knocked off his feet by a wall of water . . . but he never let go of c the helm, rose and took it again and again . . ” (paragraph 8) “Tt was then that he saw the ship—a small, older ship, coming out of the dusk, aiming D almost at him but slightly off his bow, running with the wind and sea” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: Even after a wall of water repeatedly knocks David off his feet, he never releases the helm and keeps rising to steer. This detail best shows his determined attitude toward surviving the storm.

questions.alt: Question 25. Which detail from the excerpt best supports the idea that David is an experienced sailor? 25 “He thought of turning and going back into the bay to ride it out but decided against it.” A (paragraph 2) “. . . he worked the helm, let her ease up . . . kept the speed between five and six knots, did B not run from the storm but into it, used it, rolled with it, absorbed it.” (paragraph 7) “. . . at one point he snarled, growled at the wind and sea—the helm in his gut, his arms C aching, his legs on fire . . .” (paragraph 9) “He came about and let the Frog sail closer, came up into the wind and stopped about D thirty yards away, rising and settling on the waves and swells.” (paragraph 14)

explanation.text: David adjusts the helm and the Frog's angle while deliberately maintaining a speed of five to six knots in the storm. Those controlled, technical actions demonstrate the judgment of an experienced sailor.

questions.alt: Question 26. How does paragraph 16 contribute to a theme of the excerpt? 26 A by revealing that David’s family and friends are people who tend to think negatively B by including the name of David’s hometown to show where his trip began C by emphasizing that David has persevered and survived despite the dangerous storm D by including details about the location of the young man as he spoke to David

explanation.text: The sailors identify David as the missing boy who was far beyond the search area and had been presumed dead. Their reaction emphasizes his perseverance because he has survived the storm and an extraordinary distance at sea.

questions.alt: Question 29. Which idea would be most important to include in a summary of the article? “The Serengeti is about the size of Vermont, so the scientists could not study the entire A area. Instead, they surveyed three regions . .” (paragraph 5) “The scientists looked at four possible causes: lions, parasites, illegal hunters (called B » poachers), and poor food supply.’ (paragraph 6) “To find out if lions had been killing more giraffes in recent years, the team looked at calf c survival rates and long-term records . . ” (paragraph 7) D “When the food supply is short, the environment supports fewer giraffes and the females have fewer calves” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The investigation of lions, parasites, poachers, and food supply organizes the article's explanation of the population decline. This idea belongs in a summary because the following sections test each possible cause.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read this quotation from paragraph 11. “The Serengeti is one of Earth’s natural wonders,” she says. What is the effect of Dr. Strauss’s word choice in this quotation? A __ It indicates that Dr. Strauss considers this area a marvel. B__It suggests that Dr. Strauss wants to study the ecology of the area. C__ It shows that Dr. Strauss wants to strengthen the reputation of Tanzania. D___It demonstrates that Dr. Strauss considers the giraffe population level critical to the area.

explanation.text: Calling the Serengeti one of Earth's “natural wonders” compares it to something extraordinary and worthy of admiration. This word choice indicates that Dr. Strauss considers the area a marvel.

questions.alt: Question 31. What is the meaning of “rebound” as used in paragraph 12? 31 A return to a location B recover from a challenge C replace with something new D reveal a problem

explanation.text: The researchers hope their education program and resulting changes will reverse the giraffe population's decline. In this context, “rebound” means to recover from that challenge.

questions.alt: Question 32. Which sentence states a central idea of the article? 32 A Researchers are determining the reasons for changes in the giraffe population. B Environmentalists are distributing books to increase understanding of giraffes. C Adult giraffes are better equipped than calves to handle threats in the wild. D Giraffes are more challenging to study than other wild animals in Africa.

explanation.text: The article follows researchers as they investigate four explanations for the Serengeti's shrinking giraffe population and identify two likely causes. Therefore, determining the reasons for population change is its central idea.

questions.alt: Question 33. How does the author emphasize the significance of a food source to the Serengeti giraffe 33 population? A by describing the wide variety of foods the giraffes can choose from B by describing positive changes in the environment where the giraffes live C by explaining that female giraffes need plenty of food to sustain reproduction D by explaining that the giraffes have recently adapted to new diets

explanation.text: The author explains that plentiful food helps females remain healthy and produce many calves, while shortages lead to fewer births. This link emphasizes that food is essential to sustaining giraffe reproduction.

questions.alt: Question 34. What benefit does Dr. Strauss’s research team offer to Tanzanians and the future of the giraffes? 34 A a chance to invent new methods for counting and keeping track of giraffe populations B a way to analyze nutrition sources for giraffes in challenging environments C a solution for keeping poachers out of protected giraffe environments D a new viewpoint on how to support endangered giraffe populations

explanation.text: The research identifies poaching and poor food supply as likely causes of the decline, then uses education to help Tanzanians respond. This offers a new evidence-based viewpoint on supporting the giraffe population.

questions.alt: Question 35. Protecting Earth’s plants and animals is important to keep the environment balanced and healthy. 35 How do the details in the article support this idea? A by describing how scientific study influences diversity in the environment B by suggesting that one change can impact other parts of the environment C by arguing that the environment must be free of human influences to improve D by explaining how the environment will continue to naturally maintain animal variety

explanation.text: The growth of trees giraffes do not eat reduces available food, which contributes to fewer calves, while poaching reduces adult numbers. These effects show that a change in one part of an environment can affect other parts.

questions.alt: Question 36. How do the details in paragraphs 1 through 3 help develop a central idea in the article? A They explain the events that strengthened the American army. B They establish the situation that made it easier to assist the war effort. C They explain the importance of religion to some people during the war. D They establish the reason the British located their headquarters in Philadelphia.

explanation.text: The British meet near the Darraghs and speak freely because they mistakenly believe the Quaker family is neutral. Since Lydia secretly supports the Americans, that situation makes it easier for her to assist the war effort.

questions.alt: Question 37. As used in paragraph 6, what does the phrase “sounds from the room pulled Lydia from her bed” 37 suggest about Lydia? A She senses the men must be discussing something very important. B She hopes the officers will meeting at her home in Philadelphia. C She worries that one of the buttonmold messages has been discovered. D She wonders if the officers are beginning to question her neutrality as a Quaker.

explanation.text: Lydia has been ordered to stay in bed during a top-secret meeting, yet the muffled sounds compel her to listen. Her reaction suggests that she senses the officers are discussing something important.

questions.alt: Question 38. What important idea does the author develop in paragraphs 5 through 7? 38 A Lydia’s dedication to her family B Lydia’s willingness to risk her safety C Lydia’s tendency to do what she is asked D Lydia’s attitude about sharing her home

explanation.text: Lydia leaves her bed and tiptoes toward a secret meeting despite the officers' orders. Eavesdropping could expose her as a spy, so these actions develop her willingness to risk her safety.

questions.alt: Question 39. What is a motivation for Lydia’s actions in paragraphs 12 and 13? 39 A She wants to visit her son at White Marsh. B She believes Captain Craig knows her son. C She receives a message from her son. D She worries for her son’s life.

explanation.text: Lydia knows the planned attack threatens the American soldiers, including her son Charles. Concern for his life therefore helps motivate her journey to warn Captain Craig.

questions.alt: Question 40. What is the effect of the phrase “In a soft, hushed voice” as used in paragraph 13? A The reader realizes that Lydia's trip has taken a toll on her physically. B The reader is reminded of the dangers at every point in a mission such as this. C__ The reader is informed about how women were expected to address leaders in the army. D The reader understands that Lydia has doubts as to whether she should have made the trip.

explanation.text: Lydia speaks softly and quietly because the warning must remain secret; discovery could lead to a charge of treason and death. The phrase reminds readers that danger surrounds every stage of her mission.

questions.alt: Question 41. Which quotation most directly causes the events in paragraph 16? 41 A “When the officers arrived, Lydia let them in and went to bed.” (paragraph 6) B “She recognized him as a friend, Captain Charles Craig.” (paragraph 12) “When the British arrived at White Marsh, General Howe found the American troops C armed with cannons.” (paragraph 14) “Sternly, he asked if any of the others in her family were awake on the night of the D officer’s last meeting.” (paragraph 18)

explanation.text: General Howe finds the American troops already armed with cannons, proving that someone warned them. That discovery leads to the later investigation because the British want to learn how their plan was exposed.

questions.alt: Question 42. How does the event in paragraph 20 relate to the details in paragraph 2? 42 A It shows that the British continue to hold a mistaken belief about the Darraghs. B It presents evidence that the British will lose against the American troops. C It suggests that the British believe they can learn something from Lydia Darragh. D It provides support for the idea that the British still plan to attack White Marsh.

explanation.text: Paragraph 2 explains that the British speak freely because they believe the Quaker Darraghs are neutral. Even after the warning reaches Washington, the officer leaves without suspecting Lydia, so that mistaken belief remains.

Official passage for Questions 26-31. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. Which theme is best supported by paragraphs 10 through 13 in the story? A__ Change can sometimes take people by surprise. B___ Brothers often enjoy doing the same things. C Family activities can be fun for everyone. D Every family has its own way of doing things.

explanation.text: Trent is the younger brother, yet his new red height mark is above Troy's for the first time. Troy's disbelief and Trent's delight show that this unexpected change takes both brothers by surprise.

questions.alt: Question 2. What do the details in paragraphs 3 and 14 show about Trent? 2 A Trent is not worried about his brother’s feelings until Troy is upset. B Trent would rather be measured later when he has had more time to grow. C Trent would rather spend time on his birthday having fun by himself. Trent is not interested in his height until he learns that he is taller than D Troy.

explanation.text: At first Trent wants to work on his aquarium and asks to postpone being measured. Once Mother says his mark is higher than Troy's, he squeals, checks the marks, and celebrates, showing that the news creates his interest.

questions.alt: Question 3. In paragraph 18, what does it mean when the narrator says, “I wanted to lay this matter to rest. . 2”? A He wants to know the truth. B_He is tired after many activities. C He needs time to get ready for bed. D = _-He is worried that his father is busy.

explanation.text: Troy volunteers to stand back-to-back with Trent because he wants to settle the height question as soon as possible. Thus, “lay this matter to rest” means find out the truth and end the debate.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which detail from the story shows the best evidence for the claim that Trent is taller than Troy? A “... Trent trotted to the doorway and stood up against the jamb” (paragraph 5) B “The red mark was indeed a little higher than the blue.” (paragraph 13) Cc “Trent and I turned and stepped backwards towards each other” (paragraph 20) D “...hands rested on each of our heads, keeping us in place.” (paragraph 22)

explanation.text: The colored marks record each boy's measured height on the same doorjamb. Because Trent's red mark is visibly above Troy's blue one, this detail supplies the clearest direct evidence that Trent is taller.

questions.alt: Question 5. How is the information in paragraph 37 important to the story? 5 A It compares the feelings of two main characters. B It explains the cause and the effect of a problem. C It tells why the setting is important to the events. D It explains how the conflict in the story is settled.

explanation.text: In the mirror, Troy sees the height difference himself and calls the reflected evidence undeniable. That confirmation settles the conflict by proving that Trent really is taller.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail would be best to include in a summary of the story? 6 A Trent is excited about his new fish. B Trent’s mark on the door is higher than Troy’s. C Troy is tired of being measured on his birthday. D Troy’s hair is different than Trent’s.

explanation.text: The discovery that Trent's door mark is higher than Troy's begins the brothers' argument and leads to every later comparison. Because it captures the story's central event, it belongs in a summary.

questions.alt: Question 19. What does Rupa most likely think about saris at the beginning of the story? 19 She thinks that grandmothers wear saris but that young women should A not. B She thinks that saris are pretty but would not want to wear one every day. She thinks that saris are only worn on special days and are not good for C every day. She thinks that people wear saris because they are helpful but not D comfortable.

explanation.text: Rupa tells Dadima that her saris are beautiful but immediately asks whether wearing them every day becomes tiring. This question suggests she likes their appearance but would not want to wear one daily herself.

questions.alt: Question 20. How does Dadima best support her claim that she never gets tired of wearing saris? 20 A She shows that many of her saris are very beautiful. B She gives examples of things a sari can be used for. C She explains that her own grandmother wore saris. D She is able to dress her granddaughters in her own saris.

explanation.text: Dadima demonstrates that a sari can serve as a fan, a pouch for shells, an umbrella, and even a hiding place. These varied practical uses support her claim that she can always do something with it.

questions.alt: Question 21. Why does Dadima show the girls some of her saris? 21 She wants to teach her granddaughters how to wrap a sari to wear it A correctly. She wants to show her granddaughters how to choose a sari that will look B good. She wants to share special memories from her life with her C granddaughters. She wants to give her granddaughters a chance to play dress up with pretty D clothes.

explanation.text: Dadima explains that one sari was her first, one was worn when she traveled from India to America, and one was her wedding sari. Showing them lets her share memories of important moments with her granddaughters.

questions.alt: Question 22. In paragraph 29, the phrase “unfold and unfold and unfold some more” suggests 22 that A the girls are joking B the sari is tied in knots C the girls are confused D the sari is very large

explanation.text: The repeated word “unfold” shows that the girls must keep opening more and more material before reaching the end. That continued action emphasizes how large and long the sari is.

questions.alt: Question 23. Sometimes people have customs of wearing special clothing for certain occasions. 23 Which detail from the story best connects to this idea? Dadima shows the girls the saris that she wore on important days in her A life. B Dadima helps the girls put on saris so they can look more like her. C Dadima explains how saris are helpful and can be used to stay comfortable. Dadima tells the girls that her own grandmother’s grandmother also wore D saris.

explanation.text: Dadima's three special saris are connected to important occasions: receiving her first sari, moving from India to America, and getting married. Those examples directly link clothing customs with meaningful life events.

questions.alt: Question 26. What do the details in paragraphs 7 and 12 show about the baby-sitter? A The baby-sitter has a good idea. B_ The baby-sitter enjoys sports. C the baby-sitter knows Billy very well. D The baby-sitter has many hobbies.

explanation.text: The baby-sitter realizes that calming Billy will require a new kind of expertise, then uses the sports trophies as inspiration for the “funderstorm” game. Linking Billy's interests to the storm shows a good idea taking shape.

questions.alt: Question 27. How does paragraph 6 connect to paragraph 16 in the story? A The paragraphs show the cause and effect of the storm. B The paragraphs reveal how the narrator changes over time. C The paragraphs compare the characters’ feelings. D The paragraphs show a problem and the solution.

explanation.text: In paragraph 6, Billy reveals the problem that thunderstorms frighten him. By paragraph 16, the baby-sitter has invented “funderstorm,” a game that distracts and calms him, so the later paragraph provides the solution.

questions.alt: Question 28. Read this sentence from paragraph 21 of the story. 28 It also rattles his nerves. What is the meaning of the phrase “rattles his nerves”? A Billy is jumping around. B Billy has hurt himself. C Billy feels frightened. D Billy is laughing hard.

explanation.text: The thunder rattles the windows, and Billy immediately starts to dive back under the bed. His attempt to hide shows that the sound “rattles his nerves” by making him feel frightened.

questions.alt: Question 29. How does Billy feel about the baby-sitter’s ideas for what to do during the 29 thunderstorm? A Billy likes the ideas so much that he wants him to come back again. B Billy thinks that the ideas are not as much fun as other games. C Billy believes that the ideas are so silly that he jokes about them later. D Billy is confused about the ideas and asks his parents to explain them.

explanation.text: By the end, Billy says he cannot wait for the next storm and gives the baby-sitter a knowing look; the narrator therefore expects another call whenever storm clouds appear. These details show how much Billy likes the games.

questions.alt: Question 30. From whose point of view is the story told? 30 A Billy’s B the baby-sitter’s C Mrs. Clarkson’s D an unknown character’s

explanation.text: The narrator uses “I” while describing how the Clarksons hired the sitter, what the sitter thinks, and how the games are invented. Therefore, the baby-sitter is the character telling the story.

questions.alt: Question 31. Which statement best shows a central idea of the story? 31 A Doing other things can make scary events easier. B Hiding when you are afraid can make you feel better. C Making new friends is easy when you make up games together. D Practicing any skill is important when you want to improve.

explanation.text: Billy begins under the bed, frightened by thunder, but timed games turn each flash and boom into a challenge until his worried look disappears. This change shows that focusing on other activities can make a scary event easier.

exams.title: New York Grade 4 ELA - 2023 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2023 New York State Grade 4 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2023 NYS Grade 4 English Language Arts Test Released Questions

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 1-6. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 19-23. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 26-31. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. What does the word “quest” mean as used in paragraph 4? 1 A difficult assignment B determined search C favorite activity D careful planning

explanation.text: Daisy has been persistently looking for the perfect word but is tired because she still has not found it. This context shows that her "quest" is a determined search.

questions.alt: Question 2. Which sentence best states a theme of paragraph 7? A __ Giving someone a gift is just as much fun as receiving a gift. B Buying a gift from a store is easier than making a gift. C Cards with messages inside create wonderful memories. D There are many ways to show someone that you care.

explanation.text: The children give Miss Goldner many different presents, including purchased items, a handmade frame, and cards with personal messages. This variety supports the theme that people can show their care in many ways.

questions.alt: Question 3. How does paragraph 14 relate to paragraphs 19 through 22? 3 A Paragraph 14 explains the setting in paragraphs 19 through 22. Paragraph 14 describes the cause for the events in paragraphs 19 B through 22. Paragraph 14 introduces a character developed in paragraphs 19 C through 22. D Paragraph 14 gives a problem that is solved in paragraphs 19 through 22.

explanation.text: Paragraph 14 describes why "giggle" is enjoyable to say and hear. When Miss Goldner and the students try the word in paragraphs 19 through 22, its sound makes them smile and laugh, showing that Daisy's description causes the response.

questions.alt: Question 4. In paragraph 18, why does Daisy ask Miss Goldner to try saying the word? 4 A She wants Miss Goldner to pronounce the word for the class. B She wants to make sure Miss Goldner heard the word correctly. C She wants everyone to see how much Miss Goldner loves the word. D She wants Miss Goldner to see how fun the word is to say and hear.

explanation.text: Daisy presents the word with a flourish and asks Miss Goldner to experience it rather than simply accept the gift. The smiles and laughter that follow demonstrate that Daisy wants her teacher to discover how fun "giggle" is to say and hear.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which detail best shows how Samantha changes from the beginning to the end of the story? A Samantha walks home with Daisy and Emma. B Samantha says that Daisy brought the best gift. C = Samantha worries that Mrs. Joseph will be upset. D Samantha thinks of different words to describe the day.

explanation.text: Samantha initially calls Daisy's idea ridiculous and denies that a perfect word exists. By the end she says Daisy's word was the best gift, which reveals her change from scorn to appreciation.

questions.alt: Question 6. What point is the author making in the story? 6 A Words can be powerful. B Learning can be fun. C Learning is easier with friends. D Words should be spoken loudly.

explanation.text: One word spreads smiles and laughter through the entire class, draws in the principal, and even changes Samantha's opinion. These effects show the author's point that words can have considerable power.

questions.alt: Question 19. What is the meaning of the word “breakthrough” as it is used in paragraph 2 of the 19 article? A funny story B strange event C difficult challenge D important change

explanation.text: Before David Greybeard enters camp, the chimpanzees always flee whenever Goodall approaches. His brave visit therefore marks an important change that allows her research relationship with the animals to begin improving.

questions.alt: Question 20. What is a central idea of paragraph 4? 20 A David Greybeard had fun looking through Goodall’s campsite. B Goodall’s actions helped her build trust with David Greybeard. C David Greybeard spent most of each day looking for and eating food. D Goodall was so still and quiet that David Greybeard was unaware of her.

explanation.text: Goodall repeatedly leaves bananas for David Greybeard and lets him visit without frightening him. His eventual willingness to take a banana from her hand shows that her patient actions build his trust.

questions.alt: Question 21. How does paragraph 5 support a central idea of the article? 21 By explaining that Goodall’s work became easier over time, it shows that A repeating her tasks eventually made her an expert. By telling that she was able to come close to the chimps, it shows that B Goodall’s thoughtful and patient study of the animals was a success. By demonstrating how Goodall was able to become friendly with David C Greybeard, it shows that Goodall learned how to communicate with chimps. By detailing the different steps in the process of her work, it shows that D Goodall did the same work as other scientists but got better results.

explanation.text: David Greybeard begins approaching Goodall in the forest, and other chimpanzees copy his confidence and let her come closer. This success demonstrates that her thoughtful, patient effort to earn the animals' trust improves her study.

questions.alt: Question 22. Which detail from the article shows how Goodall affected future researchers? 22 “According to Goodall’s camp cook, the chimpanzee had spent about an A hour eating fruit from one of the oil palms that grew nearby.” (paragraph 1) “It was the summer of 1961. For a year, Goodall had been studying the B chimps living in Gombe Stream Reserve near Lake Tanganyika in Central Africa.” (paragraph 2) “Goodall’s career began as a childhood wish to see African animals in their C natural environments.” (paragraph 6) “She also opened the door for other women to become ethnologists, D scientists who study animal behavior.” (paragraph 6)

explanation.text: The article says Goodall "opened the door" for other women to become scientists who study animal behavior. This statement directly shows that her career created future opportunities for women researchers.

questions.alt: Question 23. How does the structure of the article help the reader understand the events? 23 By explaining the sequence of events over time, it shows how each small A step was important to Goodall’s work. By comparing Goodall’s thoughts about her work at different times, it B shows how she changed her research over time. By providing a description of the camp, it shows why Goodall’s work was C special and important. By listing the problems and solutions of studying chimpanzees, it shows D why Goodall’s work changed ideas in science.

explanation.text: The article moves from David Greybeard's first camp visit through repeated feedings, growing trust, access to other chimpanzees, and Goodall's later discoveries. This chronological structure shows how each small development contributes to the success of her work.

questions.alt: Question 26. How does the illustration support the information in paragraph 2? 26 A It shows parts of the bird that are usually eaten. B It shows why the furcula has an unusual name. C It shows why all birds have wishbones. D It shows where the furcula is on the body.

explanation.text: The labeled turkey diagram points to the wishbone in the upper chest, between the neck and breast bone. This visual location supports paragraph 2's description of where the furcula sits in a bird's body.

questions.alt: Question 27. Read this sentence from paragraph 1. But one of the most popular—pulling the wishbone—dates back centuries. Which sentence from the article best supports this claim? A “The winner is the person who ends up with the bigger piece” (paragraph 3) “The practice of making a wish on a bird’s furcula dates back to the ancient B___Etruscans, a people who lived in what is today northern Italy.” (paragraph 4) Cc “Passersby would pick it up, stroke it for good luck, and make a wish” (paragraph 4) “Today a wishbone tug-of-war is as much a part of Thanksgiving as is gobbling down turkey smothered in gravy’ (paragraph 6)

explanation.text: The article traces wish-making on a bird's furcula back to the ancient Etruscans in northern Italy. That ancient origin directly supports the claim that pulling a wishbone dates back centuries.

questions.alt: Question 28. How are the details in paragraphs 4 through 6 organized? 28 A by presenting descriptions of some points B by presenting a problem and a solution C by comparing and contrasting events D by listing events in the order they happened

explanation.text: The account begins with the ancient Etruscans, then follows the ritual to the Romans, the English, settlers in the New World, and modern Thanksgiving. This progression shows that the details are organized in the order the tradition developed over time.

questions.alt: Question 29. How does the information in the section “Through the Ages” support a central idea of the article? A __ Itshows that today turkey and gravy are eaten for Thanksgiving. B__It shows that some people believed birds had unusual powers. C It shows that there were many wild turkeys in the New World. D _ Itshows that many people share the history of the wishbone.

explanation.text: The wishbone ritual passes from the Etruscans to Romans, then to the English and settlers in the New World, changing along the way. This history shows that many groups of people share in creating and continuing the tradition.

questions.alt: Question 30. Which sentence best explains how to win at tug-of-war with a turkey wishbone? “At Thanksgiving, the tradition is for two people to hold one end of the bone, make a wish, and then tug” (paragraph 3) B “But there are a few tips that may help you get the upper hand.” (paragraph 7) Cc “The more pressure you can apply to your side of the wishbone, the better chance you have . .” (paragraph 8) D “Whether you end up with the larger or the smaller piece of the furcula is ultimately a matter of luck” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Paragraph 8 explains that greater pressure on one's side of the bone improves the chance of receiving the larger piece. This cause-and-effect advice most directly tells a reader how to increase the odds of winning.

questions.alt: Question 31. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A _ Breaking a wishbone is just one of many Thanksgiving traditions. B All birds have a wishbone that helps to support the birds as they fly. Cc Making wishes with a wishbone is a tradition that began thousands of years ago. D There was once a shortage of chickens in Rome because the wishbone tradition was so popular.

explanation.text: The article chiefly traces wish-making from the ancient Etruscans through later cultures to today's Thanksgiving tug. The tradition's thousands-year history belongs in a summary because it captures the article's broad focus rather than a minor detail.
