questions.alt: Question 1. How does Miss Delicious’s message in paragraph 11 affect Brianna? 1 A It teaches her the value of leadership. B It inspires her to become class president. C It makes her want to have a career as a chef. D It changes her mind about taking on responsibilities.

explanation.text: Miss Delicious credits her fifth-grade presidency with teaching the leadership and responsibility that later enabled her success. Brianna therefore decides that following her hero's path requires becoming class president herself.

questions.alt: Question 2. What is the meaning of “obvious” as the narrator uses it in paragraph 16 of the story? 2 A easy to see and understand B interesting to other people C supported by friends and family D something everyone expects to happen

explanation.text: Brianna has laid out a direct connection between Miss Delicious's fifth-grade presidency and later success, then says she must win the same office. Calling this conclusion "obvious" means she thinks it is easy to see and understand.

questions.alt: Question 3. Which evidence best supports Brianna’s claim in paragraph 4 about making good things happen? A “Think about what you want, decide how you plan to get it, then write it down...” (paragraph 6) B “... She didn't think any of her success would have been possible had it not been for the skills ..” (paragraph 9) Cc “... the election wasn't just for each fifth-grade class to have its own president” (paragraph 19) D = “And I could just see me making my acceptance speech . . ” (paragraph 23)

explanation.text: Aunt Tina's advice to decide what one wants, plan how to obtain it, and write the steps down turns a wish into action. This planning process directly supports Brianna's claim that people must make good things happen themselves.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which theme is supported by details in paragraphs 20 through 23? A Family helps each other overcome challenges. B___Leaders need to be able to communicate their ideas. C Success is often better than people think it will be. D Reaching one goal can give you the confidence to set others.

explanation.text: Brianna remembers achieving her free-throw goal by studying technique and following a written plan. That earlier success shows why she has confidence to pursue the larger goal of becoming president of the whole fifth grade.

questions.alt: Question 5. How are paragraphs 24 through 26 important to the story? A They give background information about the main character in the story. B They introduce a second problem that Brianna faces in the story. C They provide a resolution to the main conflict in the story. D They reveal the effect of Brianna’s careful planning in the story.

explanation.text: Brianna is already adjusting to the expanded schoolwide election when Jasmine arrives and nominates herself. This new, determined competitor introduces a second obstacle to Brianna's plan rather than resolving the election conflict.

questions.alt: Question 6. How is Jasmine Moon most likely similar to Brianna in the story? 6 Jasmine Moon wears her hair in a way that covers her face, showing that she is most A likely shy and quiet like Brianna. Jasmine Moon gets along with her teacher quickly when she arrives at school, showing B that she is most likely kind and friendly like Brianna. Jasmine Moon is new to the school and decides to run for class president, showing that C she is most likely confident and determined like Brianna. Jasmine Moon is interested in being active and participating in social events, showing D that she is most likely eager and curious like Brianna.

explanation.text: Although Jasmine is new to the school, she promptly nominates herself in an unfamiliar election. That bold decision suggests that she shares Brianna's confidence and determination to pursue a goal.

questions.alt: Question 7. How does Brianna’s point of view influence how events in the story are described? 7 A Brianna talks in detail about her aunt and her idol, making her seem wise and curious. B Brianna talks in detail about the work she does, making her seem lonely and quiet. C Brianna does everything for herself, making her aims in the story seem selfish and smug. D Brianna is sure of herself, making her aims in the story seem reasonable and achievable.

explanation.text: Brianna presents her declaration, detailed preparation, past success, and future acceptance speech with strong confidence. Her first-person certainty shows why winning the election seems to her like a reasonable, achievable step toward a larger dream.

questions.alt: Question 22. What is the best meaning of the word “values” as it is used in paragraph 4 of the article? 22 A appreciates B enjoys C expects D collects

explanation.text: Commodity money differs by location according to what local people "valued," such as barley where it was a major food source. This context indicates that the word means appreciated or considered important.

questions.alt: Question 23. What is the main way the phrase “a prickly situation” in paragraph 6 helps the reader understand the article? A __ by showing that some commodities were often difficult to find B by highlighting the idea that objects often had different values C by providing a reason why a common money system was necessary D by indicating that some commodities were rarely useful to all people

explanation.text: A pile of fishhooks could literally prick the person trying to carry it, illustrating one practical flaw of commodity money. Such storage and transportation problems explain why people needed a more convenient common money system.

questions.alt: Question 24. How do the details in paragraphs 5 through 8 help develop a central idea of the article? A __ by comparing money in ancient cultures with the money that people use today B__ by showing how different people tried to develop effective systems of money C _byexplaining why different objects were often considered to be valuable D by describing how the process of making coins has changed very little over time

explanation.text: The article moves from locally valued salt, shells, and other commodities to the flaws of those objects and then to standardized metal coins. These examples show how different societies kept developing more effective systems of money.

questions.alt: Question 25. Read this sentence from paragraph 3 of the article. 25 Ancient people needed something with a value everyone agreed on— money. Which detail from the article describes the most important feature of money? A “A deal was final when both people agreed that it was a fair trade.” (paragraph 1) B “Everyone valued barley, so the grain was used for trade.” (paragraph 4) C “It had to be valuable to everyone, easy to carry, and hard to destroy.” (paragraph 7) “Heavy strings of coins could now be exchanged for a single sheet of paper.” D (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: For money to solve the problems of commodity trade, it needed universal value, portability, and durability. The statement listing all three requirements therefore describes money's most important features rather than one culture's example.

questions.alt: Question 26. What is most likely the author’s opinion about bartering? 26 A Bartering is useful in some situations and will likely continue in the future. B Bartering is still an option when people live in smaller communities. C Bartering is now mainly used by children as a way to exchange food. D Bartering will likely increase as people use less physical money.

explanation.text: The author notes that modern people still barter goods and services and gives familiar examples such as trading snacks or baseball cards. The closing question about whether bartering will ever stop suggests that it remains useful in some situations and will probably continue.

questions.alt: Question 29. What is the main way paragraph 1 connects with paragraph 4? A They both show how Benny appreciates nature. B_ They both describe events that Benny hopes for in the future. C They both show the artistic skills that will help Benny achieve his goal. D They both reveal why Benny wants to win the flag design contest.

explanation.text: In paragraph 1, Benny imagines becoming a strong adult who catches salmon, while paragraph 4 imagines his flag in parades and on his future fishing boat. Both paragraphs therefore describe events he hopes will occur later in his life.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read this sentence from paragraph 2. The blue sky was a roof that covered Benny’s Alaska at night. Which word best describes the tone in this sentence? A anxious B scheerful C peaceful D serious

explanation.text: The blue sky gently covering Alaska like a roof creates an image of shelter and calm beneath the stars. This soothing comparison suggests a peaceful tone.

questions.alt: Question 31. Which meaning of the word “grand” is used in paragraph 4 of the story? 31 A exciting B expensive C decorative D large

explanation.text: Benny imagines how "grand" it would feel to see his flag displayed in a parade or on a ship. His eager hopes show that the word means exciting or wonderful, not physically large.

questions.alt: Question 32. Why are paragraphs 9 through 12 important to the story? A They show how Benny enjoys playing winter sports with his classmates. B They describe how Benny appreciates the flowers that arrive in the spring. C They suggest that the contest is less important to the teacher and to other students. D They refer to the time between the start of the contest and the day the winner is announced.

explanation.text: These paragraphs move through a month of skating, the end of winter, melting snow, and the arrival of spring before the teacher gathers the class. The seasonal progression shows the time passing between the contest's beginning and the announcement of its winner.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which sentence best expresses a central idea of the story? 33 A Benny believes his physical strength will help him find success as he grows up. B Benny observes his surroundings quite closely and finds them to be meaningful. C Benny is surprised that winning the contest makes him feel proud and confident. D Benny wonders if other students with greater talent deserved to win the contest.

explanation.text: Benny closely observes the Big Dipper, North Star, blue sky, forget-me-nots, and Great Bear, giving each feature a meaning connected to Alaska. Those meaningful observations become the symbols and colors of his winning flag.

questions.alt: Question 34. Which detail from the story best shows how Benny is different than other students? 34 A the suggestion that Benny would be “strong himself” (paragraph 1) B the statement that Benny desires to “win the contest” (paragraph 4) C the description of Benny sitting “quietly, thinking and thinking” (paragraph 5) D the indication that Benny believes other designs are “better than his” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The other children talk, laugh, and hold up designs, but Benny sits quietly and thinks deeply about what he loves most about Alaska. This contrast shows that his thoughtful silence distinguishes him from the group.

questions.alt: Question 35. How is the setting most important to the story? A The fishing village where Benny lives makes him eager to become a fisherman. B_ The school that Benny attends encourages him to participate in the flag contest. C The different seasons allow Benny to play different games outside with his classmates. D The scenery that Benny pays attention to inspires him to make a special flag design.

explanation.text: Alaska's stars, blue night sky, forget-me-nots, and Great Bear supply the exact shapes, color, and symbols in Benny's design. The setting is therefore essential because the scenery he studies inspires his special flag.

questions.alt: Question 1. Which statement best supports a central claim of the story? 1 A Carrots are necessary for wild horses to perform the tasks they are given. B Treats given to some wild horses may result in rude and aggressive behavior. C Wild horses can learn to trust people if they are treated with patience and kindness. D People should avoid naming wild horses until they learn the behavior of the animals.

explanation.text: The narrator spends weeks quietly familiarizing Ranger with her voice and movement, then uses carrots as gentle rewards until he accepts people. His growing trust supports the claim that patience and kindness can help a wild horse overcome fear of humans.

questions.alt: Question 2. Read this phrase from paragraph 5. ... hooves kicking puffs of dust and tail streaming behind him like a banner. The author most likely uses this phrase to A highlight Ranger's speed B_ show Ranger's refusal to be tamed C __ illustrate Ranger’s desire for freedom D _ indicate Ranger's distance from the corral

explanation.text: Ranger's pounding hooves kick up dust while his tail streams behind him like a banner as he gallops. These energetic images show that he is moving at great speed.

questions.alt: Question 3. Paragraphs 5 and 6 fit in the overall structure of the story by 3 A expanding the setting B introducing the conflict C foreshadowing a resolution D describing a relationship

explanation.text: Ranger suddenly gallops into view outside the supposedly safe corral, prompting the narrator to demand how he escaped. This event shows the central conflict beginning: she must manage a powerful horse that can jump the fence at will.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which quotation best supports a theme of the story? A “It was dusk by the time we hauled him home, so I tossed him some hay, showed him where the water tank was, and then left him to settle in” (paragraph 2) B “If hed managed to get out of his safe corral, how on earth could I catch him again?” (paragraph 4) Cc “Ranger galloped into view along the edge of the cornfield, hooves kicking puffs of dust and tail streaming behind him like a banner? (paragraph 5) D “T spent the next few weeks letting him get used to the sound of my voice, the rhythm of my movements.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: Instead of rushing Ranger, the narrator spends weeks letting him adjust to her voice and movements while she quietly proves she is not a threat. This sustained patience best supports the story's theme that trust develops through gentle, consistent effort.

questions.alt: Question 5. What is the most likely meaning of the word “skeptical” as used in paragraph 10? 5 A afraid of B cautious about C unaware of D worried about

explanation.text: Ranger refuses unfamiliar treats and even bucks away at the sound of a carrot breaking because wild mustangs distrust new flavors. This behavior indicates that he is cautious about unfamiliar food.

questions.alt: Question 6. What do the details in paragraphs 11 through 14 indicate about Ranger? 6 A He is ready to begin his training. B His curiosity is overcoming his lack of trust. C He is excited to try different foods. D His desire for companionship is growing.

explanation.text: Ranger watches Ghost eat a carrot, edges closer to investigate, and hesitates before taking his own bite. His decision to approach and taste it shows that curiosity is beginning to overcome his distrust.

questions.alt: Question 7. How does the author mainly develop the narrator’s point of view in the story? 7 A by sharing the narrator’s thoughts about Ranger B by showing how the narrator tries to make Ranger feel at home C by describing the narrator’s reaction when Ranger escapes D by indicating why the narrator believes Ranger will like carrots

explanation.text: The narrator shares her private expectations, panic, guesses about Ranger's motives, and interpretations of his expressions throughout the story. These thoughts reveal and develop her changing view of the horse from dangerous escape artist to trusting partner.

questions.alt: Question 22. Hummingbirds have eyes on the sides of their heads, allowing them to see frontwards and 22 sideways. Which phrase from paragraph 10 best suggests why this feature is useful for hummingbirds? A “most at home in the air” B “they can also go backward” C “move from flower to flower” D “they can hover in midair”

explanation.text: Hummingbirds must navigate precisely while repeatedly traveling among blossoms to feed. Being able to see both forward and sideways supports that movement because the birds can track nearby flowers and their path at the same time.

questions.alt: Question 23. The section “Where in the World” mainly contributes to the reader’s understanding of the article by A letting the reader know where hummingbirds are often found B indicating why some hummingbirds prefer cooler climates C explaining to the reader how hummingbirds interact with people D emphasizing that some hummingbirds often travel alone

explanation.text: The section identifies the Americas as hummingbirds' range and lists habitats from jungles and Alaskan regions to coasts, deserts, city parks, and gardens. These geographic details mainly explain where the birds can be found.

questions.alt: Question 24. Which detail from the article does the information in the table support? A _ “For their size, they produce more feathers than any other bird” (paragraph 3) B “The birds raise their families in the wilderness, but also in busy city parks and backyard gardens.” (paragraph 5) Cc “It normally feeds every 10 minutes—sometimes more often—downing about 60 meals a day!” (paragraph 7) D “A hummingbird feeds mostly on nectar—the sweet liquid formed inside flowers.” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: The table directs readers to countryside nests, neighborhood flower beds and feeders, and parks in New York City. These varied locations support the statement that hummingbirds raise families in wilderness areas, city parks, and backyard gardens.

questions.alt: Question 25. Read this detail from paragraph 12 of the article. A hummer can make sudden stops—even on a narrow perch—with grace.... This detail suggests that hammingbirds are A skilled at flying B unsure when landing C easily observed D rarely distracted

explanation.text: A hummingbird traveling quickly can still stop suddenly and land gracefully on a narrow perch. That combination of speed, control, and precision demonstrates that the bird is highly skilled at flying.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which detail from the article would be most important to include in a summary? “Stand between a hummingbird and the sun—with the light to your back—and you'll witness the flash as the bird faces the sun.” (paragraph 3) B “Hummingbirds live where flowers bloom—but only in the western half of the world?” (paragraph 4) Cc “Ruby-throated and rufous hummers fly more than 3200 kilometres (2000 miles) from their nesting sites in the north to their winter homes in the south.’ (paragraph 6) D “They're surprisingly good right away, but they have to work to improve their landing skills.” (paragraph 13)

explanation.text: The article surveys where hummingbirds live, feed, migrate, and fly, so their range is an important broad fact. The statement that they live wherever flowers bloom in the western half of the world belongs in a summary, while the other choices are narrower examples.

questions.alt: Question 29. Read this sentence from paragraph 4. 29 To us, the kitchen was a mysterious place. What is the main way the author develops this idea? A by contrasting the restaurant kitchen with the kitchen in his home B by referring to the sizes of the sink and refrigerator in the restaurant kitchen C by describing objects in the restaurant kitchen with which he was unfamiliar D by explaining the purpose of the raised planks of wood in the restaurant kitchen

explanation.text: The author first calls the restaurant kitchen mysterious, then contrasts his simple home stove, refrigerator, sink, and table with a bustling space full of gigantic woks and specialized equipment. This contrast shows the restaurant kitchen's unfamiliar, impressive quality.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read this sentence from paragraph 9. Life in a Chinese restaurant gave us access to some strange snacks, most of which we grew to like. Which statement best represents the meaning of this sentence? A __ Theauthor learns to enjoy unfamiliar food available in the restaurant. B_ The author ignores the restaurant food he finds unpleasant. C The author prefers restaurant snacks he has had before. D The author looks for different snacks in the restaurant.

explanation.text: The restaurant gives the children unfamiliar foods such as preserved plums, whose intense taste they learn to appreciate, along with pickled scallions and sugar cane. These examples show the author gradually enjoying strange snacks available through restaurant life.

questions.alt: Question 31. Many restaurant owners must be creative to be successful. Which quotation from the article best 31 supports this idea? “. . . planks of wood, raised off the ground an inch or two, served to give his legs and feet A some spring . . .” (paragraph 4) B “. . . now and then, he’d make a batch of Jah-Don . . .” (paragraph 7) C “If he tasted a candy he liked, he’d try to duplicate it . . .” (paragraph 8) “Sometimes, Dad would get a sugar cane or two at the produce market . . .” D (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: Whenever the father tastes a candy he likes, he attempts to reproduce it for the restaurant, even developing his own rock candy. This willingness to invent a version of a product demonstrates the creativity useful to a restaurant owner.

questions.alt: Question 32. How does the author best help the reader understand his father’s point of view? A by describing his father striding “the length of the four woks” (paragraph 4) B by describing his father taking out “several rods” of pork (paragraph 5) C _ by claiming that “There was nothing he wouldn't try: (paragraph 8) D by claiming he offered “a sugar cane or two” as a snack (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The broad statement that the father would try anything is supported by his experiments with pastries and homemade candy, including an unsuccessful rock candy. It reveals his adventurous, inventive approach more fully than a single physical action does.

questions.alt: Question 33. What is the meaning of the phrase “to spin stories” in paragraph 11? 33 A to tell lies B to invent humorous tales C to warn others D to create feelings of excitement

explanation.text: Gim Bok invents the playful warning that swallowing an apple seed will make a tree grow inside Shirley's stomach. This impossible, teasing tale shows that "spin stories" means create humorous stories.

questions.alt: Question 34. In the article, the main way the author represents his childhood experiences is by describing 34 A the size of the kitchen in the restaurant B the various types of food he ate at the restaurant C the different people who worked at the restaurant D the attempts of his father to make candy in the restaurant

explanation.text: The memories repeatedly center on eating restaurant foods: barbecued pork, cream-puff-like treats, experimental candy, preserved plums, scallions, and sugar cane. This evidence shows that the range of foods is the main way the author represents his childhood experience.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which claim by the author is most strongly supported by evidence in the article? A “The rice room . . . was the generic name for an area in the back of our father’s restaurant.” (paragraph 1) B “From the time of my birth . .. the cafe at 710 Webster Street was my home away from home.’ (paragraph 2) Cc “,. nothing rivaled the satisfaction of working the plum around, getting down to the plum seed” (paragraph 9) D “,..a tall, spindly man with rimless glasses and thinning hair who liked to spin stories to us.’ (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The author spent his first ten years around the restaurant, sometimes treated it as his actual home, and recalls its kitchen, food, family routines, and workers in rich detail. This extensive evidence most strongly supports his claim that the cafe was his home away from home.

Official passage for Questions 36-42. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 15. What do the details in paragraphs 5 through 9 mainly reveal about Kareem? 15 A He wishes to visit more museums. B He prefers listening to jazz music. C He enjoys playing competitive games. D He desires to learn more about his heritage.

explanation.text: Kareem and Norbert research their ethnic origins, trade facts about Filipino and African achievements, and visit the Metropolitan Museum to learn more. These activities reveal Kareem's desire to understand his heritage.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which quotation best represents the idea that the opinion of classmates can have a major effect on teenagers? A “Size implies maturity. Yes, I was the size of an adult (actually, bigger than most!), but I was still just a kid” (paragraph 1) “As a card-carrying Good Boy, I wanted to meet adults’ expectations and get their praise, B__ but as a Regular Kid, I wanted to be like my peers and get their friendship. . .” (paragraph 1) Cc “Weekends, I hung out with my only close friend left in the projects, Norbert.” (paragraph 5) D “And back and forth wed go, trying to one-up the other in a cultural heritage version of rock, paper, scissors.” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Kareem contrasts his wish to satisfy adults with his wish to be like his peers and earn their friendship. That tension directly shows how classmates' opinions and acceptance can strongly affect a teenager.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which statement best describes the importance of Norbert in Kareem’s life? 17 “We would shut ourselves in my room to play chess and talk about our plans for the A future.” (paragraph 5) “. . . both of us had started to take an interest in finding out more about our ethnic B origins.” (paragraph 6) “And back and forth we’d go, trying to one-up the other in a cultural heritage version of C rock, paper, scissors.” (paragraph 9) “. . . I had a friend in whom I could confide any thought or feeling, knowing he would D never make fun of me.” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Norbert is the one friend Kareem trusts with any thought or feeling because he knows Norbert will not ridicule him. This emotional safety best explains why the friendship is especially important.

questions.alt: Question 18. In paragraph 10, what does Kareem mean when he says, “I didn’t have an aggressive nature”? 18 A He believes he needs to change the way he plays basketball. B He understands he may not be a strong player because of his age. C He discovers the other players know more ways to score in basketball. D He realizes he needs to practice more if he wants to improve as a player.

explanation.text: Kareem describes his play as politely passive while successful players compete roughly and use their bodies with authority. Saying he lacks an aggressive nature means he knows he must change his playing style and become tougher.

questions.alt: Question 19. What does paragraph 14 show about the relationship between Kareem and Coach Donahue? 19 A Kareem and Coach Donahue understand the importance of studying. B Kareem is beginning to understand and appreciate Coach Donahue’s personality. C Kareem expected Coach Donahue’s decision to have him play varsity. D Kareem is worried that Coach Donahue is disappointed by his performance as a player.

explanation.text: Coach Donahue acts businesslike but lets a slight grin reveal that he enjoys Kareem's stunned reaction to the varsity uniform. Kareem notices the humor, which shows he is beginning to understand and appreciate the coach's personality.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which quotation best expresses a central idea in the article? A “The scary thing about being so tall when you're so young is that people automatically treat you as if youre older” (paragraph 1) “My refuge from the explosive turmoil outside, and the implosive turmoil I had started to feel inside, was basketball.” (paragraph 4) Cc “We even took the subway to the Metropolitan Museum of Art to find out more about Filipino and African cultures” (paragraph 9) D “T hurried away on a mission to tell anyone who would listen that I would be playing varsity that afternoon” (paragraph 15)

explanation.text: Basketball gives Kareem an escape from both public unrest and his own inner turmoil, even though mastering the sport requires hard work. That relationship is central because the article shows basketball shaping how he copes and grows.

questions.alt: Question 21. How is Kareem and Norbert'’s friendship developed in the article? A The authors describe the various interests that Kareem and Norbert have. B The authors use cause and effect to describe how Kareem and Norbert became friends. Cc The authors present a problem Kareem and Norbert encounter and describe how they solve it. D The authors compare and contrast the friendship of Kareem and Norbert to the friendships of other students.

explanation.text: The authors show Kareem and Norbert playing chess, discussing their futures, listening to jazz, researching their cultures, and visiting a museum. These varied shared interests are the main way their friendship is developed.

questions.alt: Question 22. How do lines 1 through 8 develop the speaker’s point of view? 22 A by revealing why the speaker dislikes being around people B by explaining why the speaker moved away from the sea C by showing how the speaker knows the reason for feeling discontented D by suggesting the speaker is happiest when moving from place to place

explanation.text: The speaker searches her heart and identifies the source of her sorrow: city life has exhausted her, while she longs for the sea's wind, spray, and surf. This self-examination shows that she understands why she feels discontented.

questions.alt: Question 23. How do lines 1 through 16 (the first two stanzas) contribute to the structure of the poem? A They introduce the conflict the speaker feels about her opportunities in different places. B_ They contrast the speaker’s experiences and feelings about living in different places. C They compare phases in the speaker’s life when she wanted to be in a different place. D They show the causes and effects of the speaker deciding to live in a different place.

explanation.text: The first stanza describes the speaker's present weariness in the city and desire for the sea. The second creates a contrast because it recalls past coastal freedom while emphasizing her current sense of being trapped beneath buildings.

questions.alt: Question 24. The repetition of the phrase “once again” in lines 19, 23, 25, and 27 affects the meaning of the poem by A indicating the main places the speaker wishes to revisit B emphasizing how strongly the speaker wishes to return to the sea C __ illustrating some of the speaker’s most cherished memories D revealing the speaker’s concern about some features of the sea

explanation.text: Each "once again" introduces another sensory detail the speaker yearns to experience, including the harbor sights, gulls, tide, and fog bell. The repetition emphasizes her longing because every remembered sensation points toward returning to the sea.

questions.alt: Question 25. Which lines best support a theme of the poem? A “Searching my heart for its true sorrow, / This is the thing I find to be: / That I am weary of words and people, / Sick of the city, wanting the sea;” (lines 1-4) B “If I could hear the green piles groaning / Under the windy, wooden piers, / See once again the bobbing barrels, / And the black sticks that fence the weirs;” (lines 17-20) Cc “If I could see the weedy mussels / Crusting the wrecked and rotting hulls, / Hear once again the hungry crying / Overhead, of the wheeling gulls.” (lines 21-24) D “Feel once again the shanty straining / Under the turning of the tide, / Fear once again the rising freshet, / Dread the bell in the fog outside; (lines 25-28)

explanation.text: The opening lines state both the speaker's sorrow and its cause: she is tired of the city and longs for the sea. They best support the theme because the rest of the poem expands that homesickness through coastal memories.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which phrase best states the speaker’s problem in the poem? A “Wanting the loud sound and the soft sound” (line 7) B “Crusting the wrecked and rotting hulls, (line 22) C “Fear once again the rising freshet; (line 27) D = “Iam too long away from the water;” (line 35)

explanation.text: The speaker says she has been away from the water too long and immediately adds that she needs water nearby. This phrase states the central problem that explains her unhappiness and longing throughout the poem.

questions.alt: Question 29. How does paragraph 4 develop a theme in the story? 29 A by identifying the mother’s opinion about Haydn B by describing the crates where the mother keeps her music C by showing the value of music in the mother’s life D by explaining the importance of the concert for the mother

explanation.text: When the mother is upset or stressed, she turns to Haydn at the piano for comfort, although her busy present life leaves little time to play. This history develops the theme by showing music's lasting emotional value in her life.

questions.alt: Question 30. Which quotation shows a change in the direction of the story? A “...she landed in Takoma Park, at Ahimsa House.” (paragraph 2) B “But he loved hearing stories about the people who lived there: (paragraph 3) C = “When Josh was five, he discovered the cello” (paragraph 4) D “The next day, Mom signed him up for cello lessons . . ” (paragraph 5)

explanation.text: The opening focuses on family food and life at Ahimsa House, but "When Josh was five, he discovered the cello" shifts the story toward his relationship with music. The direction changes because that discovery prepares for the cello's role in the ending.

questions.alt: Question 31. In paragraph 6, how does the narrator’s question to Josh affect the resolution of the story? 31 A by giving Josh an opportunity to express his feelings B by letting the narrator share a memory with her brother C by revealing how Josh has grown in his ability to play the cello D by allowing the narrator to make dinner while her brother is occupied

explanation.text: Asking Josh to play cooking music brightens him after entering the empty house and leads him to perform from memory. The invitation helps resolve the sadness because his cello becomes a way to express feelings he cannot put into words.

questions.alt: Question 32. In paragraph 8, the word “wilted” most likely means that Josh is 32 A confused B frustrated C sad D tired

explanation.text: Josh droops when he enters the empty house but brightens as soon as the narrator suggests playing his cello. This contrast shows that "wilted" describes sadness rather than tiredness or confusion.

questions.alt: Question 33. People often use food as a way to create a sense of community. Which phrase from the story best supports this idea? A “Everyone took turns cooking” (paragraph 1) B = “Cottage Cheese Contraption, invented by an old woman” (paragraph 2) C = “when Mom served chai lattes at the Sunflower Café” (paragraph 3) D “fried it all into a delicious hot mush, while Josh ‘talked’ to me” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: At Ahimsa House, everyone participates by taking a turn preparing meals for the group. That shared responsibility best supports the idea that food can create community because cooking brings all members into a common activity.

questions.alt: Question 34. In paragraph 9, why is the word “talked” in quotation marks? 34 A Josh does not wish to have a discussion with the narrator. B Josh communicates with the narrator by using the cello as his voice. C Josh only pretends to communicate with the narrator. D Josh does not want the narrator to share his feelings with their mother.

explanation.text: Josh does not speak aloud while he plays, yet the narrator understands the music as feelings he cannot express in words. The quotation marks show that he uses the cello as his voice.

questions.alt: Question 35. How does the author best develop the narrator’s point of view in the story? 35 A by sharing the narrator’s memories B by describing the narrator’s opinions C through the narrator’s reactions to events D through the narrator’s dialogue

explanation.text: The narrator recalls Ahimsa House, her mother's piano playing, Josh's first encounter with a cello, and her father's old request for cooking music. These memories develop her point of view because they explain why she knows music can comfort Josh now.

questions.alt: Question 36. Which word best defines the phrase “family myth” in paragraph 1? 36 A fantasy B background C promise D knowledge

explanation.text: Liz and the rest of her family dismiss the Lincoln story because they do not believe the grandfather's claim is true. In this context, "family myth" means a family fantasy or tale assumed to be invented.

questions.alt: Question 37. What does the word “footnote” mean as it is used in paragraph 2? 37 A a reference to an idea B a small but important role C an accomplishment of a goal D an explanation or definition of an event

explanation.text: The grandfather says Grace gives their ordinary family a "footnote in history" through her exchange with Lincoln. The expression means a small but meaningful part in a much larger historical story.

questions.alt: Question 38. How does paragraph 3 contribute to the organization of the article? 38 A It introduces the distrust that existed in Liz Bedell’s family. B It foreshadows that Liz Bedell and her family will be surprised by the truth. C It establishes the position Liz Bedell’s grandfather had in the family. D It shows how little Liz Bedell knew about her past.

explanation.text: The blunt statement that no family member believed the grandfather establishes their certainty that his story was false. It foreshadows surprise because the displayed letters later prove that he had told the truth.

questions.alt: Question 39. How does paragraph 10 contribute to the development of a central idea in the article? 39 A by demonstrating Liz Bedell’s experience in different states B by identifying important people that Liz Bedell knows C by revealing the effect of Liz Bedell’s grandfather’s influence on her D by showing how Liz Bedell chose to honor her great-great aunt’s legacy

explanation.text: Liz's grandfather encourages her political ambitions by using Grace as proof that one person can make a difference. Paragraph 10 shows the effect of that influence through Liz's political-science degree, campaign work, and congressional job.

questions.alt: Question 40. In paragraph 12, Grace Bedell supports her argument that Lincoln should grow a beard. Which 40 statement does not support the argument? A “I have got 4 brother’s and part of them will vote for you any way . . .” “. . . if you will let your whiskers grow I will try and get the rest of them to vote for B you . . .” C “. . . you would look a great deal better for your face is so thin . . .” D “All the ladies like whiskers and they would tease their husband’s to vote for you . . .”

explanation.text: The fact that some brothers already planned to vote for Lincoln says nothing about how a beard would help him. The other statements support Grace's argument by claiming whiskers would improve his appearance or attract additional votes.

questions.alt: Question 41. In paragraph 15, what does the word “flustered” suggest? 4401 A Grace Bedell became shy around the large crowd. B Meeting Abraham Lincoln was worrisome for Grace Bedell. C Grace Bedell lost interest in the candidate by the time of the meeting. D Receiving a kiss from Abraham Lincoln made Grace Bedell nervous.

explanation.text: After Lincoln calls Grace forward and kisses her cheek, she runs away while still carrying the flowers she meant to give him. Her disorganized reaction shows that the unexpected personal attention made her nervous and overwhelmed.

questions.alt: Question 42. Which quotation best reveals the author's purpose for writing the article? A “... she dismissed as ‘family myth’ a story her grandfather often told about his Aunt Grace Bedell” (paragraph 1) B “Her grandfather encouraged her to pursue a lifelong interest in politics.” (paragraph 8) Cc “As to the whiskers, having never worn any, do you not think people would call it a piece of silly affectation . . . ?” (paragraph 14) D “He explained that this Lincoln exhibition was the first time, to his knowledge, that the two letters had been displayed together.” (paragraph 16)

explanation.text: The article centers on Liz's discovery that a family story she had dismissed as myth was documented history. The opening quotation best reveals that purpose because it establishes the disbelief that the Lincoln letters later overturn.

exams.title: New York Grade 8 ELA - 2023 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2023 New York State Grade 8 English Language Arts Test.

questions.alt: Question 1. Which statement best expresses a theme in paragraphs 6 through 8? A __ People should take time to recognize the work of others. B Thinking ahead and making plans is important. C Crying is sometimes a good way to get attention. D People can have anxiety when meeting someone new.

explanation.text: Margaret arrives without an appointment after Moskowitz has apparently left and realizes that an earlier train or telephone call could have prevented the problem. Her regret supports the theme that planning ahead is important.

questions.alt: Question 2. Which quotation best supports a central idea of the story? 2 A “I asked the letter-writer to recommend someone qualified . . .” (paragraph 4) “During the Easter vacation I booked a cheap room and took the train to New York City B . . .” (paragraph 5) “I apologize, sir, but I was told to talk to you and to show you some photographs.” C (paragraph 12) “Mr. Moskowitz, I have to know if you think I have the ability to become a professional D in this field.” (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: Margaret's central uncertainty is whether she should choose safe museum work or risk pursuing architectural photography. Her question to the expert expresses the central idea because she needs an honest judgment of her ability before choosing her future.

questions.alt: Question 3. How does the dialogue in paragraphs 19 and 20 propel events in the story? 3 A It suggests that Mr. Moskowitz requires help judging the narrator’s work. B It shows that Mr. Moskowitz is impressed by the narrator’s picture. C It indicates that Mr. Moskowitz reluctantly agrees to speak with the narrator. D It reveals that Mr. Moskowitz will likely help the narrator in her career.

explanation.text: After seeing one photograph, Moskowitz cancels the waiting elevator, asks Margaret to follow him, and summons other architects to the conference room. Those commands propel the story because they show that her picture has impressed him enough to stop and evaluate her work.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which quotation shows a change in the direction of the story? A “The operator rang his office; no answer. ‘Sorry, but it looks like you're out of luck, Miss White; she said.” (paragraph 7) B “T didn't hesitate. ‘Oh, Mr. Moskowitz!’ I called out. Just a moment, sir, please! I'd like to speak to you. ” (paragraph 10) Cc “‘Never mind, Chester; Mr. Moskowitz told the operator. “We don’t need you now: ” (paragraph 19) D “For the next hour they asked me questions about my age . . . my education, and my experience. (paragraph 21)

explanation.text: Until paragraph 19, Moskowitz is determined to catch his train and refuses to look at the portfolio. Telling the elevator operator they no longer need him reverses that direction because Moskowitz now chooses to stay and examine the photographs.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which event from the story most affects the narrator’s feelings about her future career? 5 A She receives payment for her pictures published in the Cornell Alumni News. B Several graduates of the department of architecture praise her work. C She is invited to a job interview at the American Museum of Natural History. D Several experts confirm that she has a talent for architectural photography.

explanation.text: Praise from alumni encourages Margaret, but she still doubts whether it reflects genuine professional ability. The architects' assurance most affects her because their expert judgment resolves the doubt about choosing photography.

questions.alt: Question 6. What effect do the shifting attitudes of the switchboard operator and Mr. Moskowitz have on the story? A __ They create a mood of suspense. Bs They introduce confusion to the plot. C They build a tone of disappointment. D They reinforce the central conflict.

explanation.text: The switchboard operator first tells Margaret she is out of luck but then helps identify Moskowitz; he initially refuses her and then stops after seeing one image. These uncertain reversals create suspense about whether Margaret will receive the expert judgment she needs.

questions.alt: Question 7. The saying “Do not put all your eggs in one basket” means that a person should not depend on one idea or possibility for success. Which of the narrator’s actions in the story represents this saying? A _ She submits a photo-essay for her journalism course assignment. B_ She agrees to publish her photographs in the Cornell Alumni News. C She applies for a job at the American Museum of Natural History. D She fails to make an appointment with Mr. Moskowitz.

explanation.text: Margaret pursues expert advice about photography but also applies to the museum as a safer employment option. Keeping that second career possibility means she does not depend on only one route to success.

questions.alt: Question 22. The word choice in paragraph 2 affects the tone of the story by A__ developing a sense of appreciation for the setting B__ indicating a possible direction for the plot C creating a sense of anxiety for the main character D _ suggesting a possible resolution for the conflict

explanation.text: Paragraph 2 describes Miyax's trembling hands, quickened heartbeat, desperate lack of food, and uncertainty that the wolves will help her. The tone becomes anxious because this urgent language leaves her survival in doubt.

questions.alt: Question 23. What do the details in paragraphs 3 and 4 reveal about Miyax? 23 A She has enjoyed learning about the habitats of different wild animals. B She believes her father would be able to develop a friendship with the wolf. C She has developed an understanding of nature and animal behaviors. D She plans to share new animal signals that she learns with her family.

explanation.text: Miyax knows that animals use signals, has successfully imitated squirrels, and studies the black wolf's role as the pack's wise leader. These details reveal a developed understanding of nature and animal behavior.

questions.alt: Question 24. Which detail supports a theme of the story? A “She had been lost without food for many sleeps on the North Slope of Alaska” (paragraph 2) B “Patience with the ways of nature had been instilled in her by her father.” (paragraph 6) Cc “Then his eyes sped to each of the three adult wolves that made up his pack and finally to the five pups...” (paragraph 7) D “Not a tree grew anywhere to break the monotony of the gold-green plain . .” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: Although Miyax urgently needs food, she remains still rather than frightening the wolf because her father taught her patience with nature. This detail supports a theme about using learned patience and respect when facing a difficult problem.

questions.alt: Question 25. Which quotation best reveals Miyax’s attitude toward Amaroq? A “The pack looked to him when the wind carried strange scents or the birds cried nervously.” (paragraph 4) B “True, she moved slowly and quietly, so as not to alarm him; yet she did wish he would see the kindness in her eyes.” (paragraph 5) Cc “The great wolf’s eyes softened at the sight of the little wolves, then quickly hardened into brittle yellow jewels as he scanned the flat tundra” (paragraph 7) D “Miyax tensed and listened, too. Did he hear some brewing storm, some approaching enemy?” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Miyax moves slowly so she will not alarm Amaroq and hopes he will recognize the kindness in her eyes. Her careful behavior and wish for trust reveal a friendly, respectful attitude toward him.

questions.alt: Question 26. How does the author’s use of language in paragraph 10 contribute to the tone of the story? 26 A by giving a name to the leader of the wolf pack B by illustrating how the land has inspired Miyax to not give up C by using geographical terms to describe the setting D by reinforcing Miyax’s senses of danger and hope

explanation.text: Miyax remembers the terrifying realization that she is lost in an endless "ocean of grass," but her heart then leaps when Amaroq finally looks at her. The language combines danger and hope because fear returns just before the wolf's attention gives her a possible chance.

questions.alt: Question 29. How does the author’s description of Aleks’s plan in paragraph 1 impact the tone of the story? 29 A It creates irony because everything happens the way Aleks thinks it will. B It creates confidence that Aleks will succeed because he knows what he must do. C It creates suspense as the reader wonders if something will go wrong for Aleks. D It creates confusion because not everything happens the way Aleks wants it to.

explanation.text: Aleks's escape requires destroying his record, reaching the stables unnoticed, getting Quicksilver, and finding an unknown way out before dinner ends. The many risky steps create suspense because readers cannot know which part may fail.

questions.alt: Question 30. What important idea does the author develop in paragraphs 1 and 2? A The Tellus army is poorly prepared for action. B_ Aleks has a plan that is based on the army’s routines. C Aleks is unsure about which office has his army enlistment papers. D The Tellus army guards will soon return to the barracks from dinner.

explanation.text: Aleks chooses the dinner shift because it leaves the barracks with only a minimal guard, and he knows he must leave before dinner ends. These details develop the idea that his plan depends on predictable army routines.

questions.alt: Question 31. Which sentence states a theme of paragraph 3? 31 A Overconfidence can lead to carelessness. B Pride in one’s kingdom is essential to military success. C Pain can prevent a person from reaching one’s goal. D Trust is difficult to establish and maintain.

explanation.text: Shulga's office door and filing drawers are unlocked because he is too cocky to imagine anyone searching them. His overconfidence creates the careless security weakness that allows Aleks to enter and investigate.

questions.alt: Question 32. How does Aleks’s attitude change in paragraph 5? A _Itshifts from exhausted to curious. B It shifts from panicked to confident. C _Itshifts from distracted to focused. D _Itshifts from annoyed to surprised.

explanation.text: Aleks begins the paragraph growling in frustration as repeated searches fail. His attitude turns to surprise when a tilted drawer reveals an unexpected false bottom.

questions.alt: Question 33. The saying “Don't let anything you” is an encouragement to pursue and achieve a goal despite obstacles. Which quotation from the story best reflects this idea? A “After that... he would find a way out. There had to be one somewhere” (paragraph 1) B “Luckily, the door was unlocked and the room empty; Shulga had obviously gone straight from the time-out room to dinner.’ (paragraph 3) Cc “Aleks glanced at the clock; he knew he shouldn't, but hed always been the curious type” (paragraph 6) D “A quick glance through the glass panel showed the corridor to be empty, so Aleks slipped from the room.” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Even while injured and uncertain of the route, Aleks insists that he will find a way out because one must exist. That determination best reflects pursuing a goal despite obstacles.

questions.alt: Question 34. Read these sentences from paragraph 12. 34 ‘We need to be quiet, boy. No getting excited.’ What do these sentences reveal about Aleks? A He is aware that dangers may still await him. B He is questioning his decision to leave the army. C He is unfamiliar with how this horse will react. D He is reconsidering his plan of escape.

explanation.text: Aleks asks Quicksilver to remain quiet and not become excited so that no one discovers their departure. His caution reveals that he understands the escape is still in progress and further dangers may await them.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which sentence would be most important to include in a summary of the story? 35 A Aleks notices that Shulga’s office is decorated the same as Antova’s office. B Aleks finds an Anglyan journal in a secret compartment in Shulga’s office. C Aleks fears someone is coming when he hears a door slam in the building. D Aleks slows his pace to avoid suspicion as he approaches the stables.

explanation.text: The Anglyan journal is concealed beneath a false drawer bottom in the office of an enemy officer, and Aleks risks taking it with him. This unusual secret discovery is important to a summary because it introduces a major new mystery, unlike incidental office or travel details.

questions.alt: Question 36. Why does the author use the phrase “my finned fishy friends” in paragraph 2? 36 A to highlight the serious issues facing ocean life B to make readers feel a connection to the article C to establish the importance of SoFi’s fin technology D to help readers understand the ocean environment

explanation.text: The author first places readers in the imagined role of an ocean fish and playfully calls them "my finned fishy friends." The phrase builds connection because its direct, humorous address invites readers into the topic.

questions.alt: Question 37. Read this sentence from paragraph 7. 37 She’s not quite fish flesh, but she’s not a typical marine robot either. Which detail about SoFi best supports the idea expressed in this sentence? A SoFi may contribute to the protection of endangered marine life. B SoFi can swim at speeds that are similar to some fish. C Fish are not scared by SoFi and sometimes swim with her. D Divers issue commands to SoFi using a simple controller.

explanation.text: SoFi is artificial, yet real fish do not flee from her and sometimes swim beside her. Their response best supports the idea that she behaves enough like a fish to avoid seeming like a typical intrusive robot.

questions.alt: Question 38. Which detail from the article best shows the author’s point of view about SoFi? A “Robotic fish like her could be essential to understanding and protecting marine life in danger of disappearing . ..” (paragraph 5) B “This foot-and-a-half long robot mimics a real fish” (paragraph 6) Cc “I was amazed at how well it was working, how well I was able to get this tail to beat back and forth or swim left and right . .” (paragraph 10) D “And the fish can't be as big as a submarine—unless we wanted to build a whale” (paragraph 13)

explanation.text: Calling robotic fish "essential" for understanding and protecting threatened marine life is an evaluation of their value. This language most clearly reveals the author's positive view of SoFi's potential.

questions.alt: Question 39. Which statement best explains how the ideas in paragraph 8 relate to the details in paragraphs 12 39 through 14? A Engineers want to build a robot to explore marine life. B Engineers create a system to guide SoFi underwater. C Engineers focus on design requirements to achieve their goal. D Engineers develop SoFi to think and act like a small fish.

explanation.text: Paragraph 8 calls SoFi an engineering challenge, and paragraphs 12 through 14 list the requirements engineers had to solve: waterproofing, buoyancy, weight, communication, and compact equipment. Those details show them focusing on design needs to achieve the robot-fish goal.

questions.alt: Question 40. One aspect of technology that is often stressed is its negative impact on the environment. Which detail from the article presents a contrast to this idea? A “They explained how their finned robot was created, and how her first ocean swim on a coral reef outside of Fiji went.” (paragraph 5) “But without a line giving her away by connecting her to a boat .. . she doesn’t seem to B » bother or scare off real fish” (paragraph 7) c “SoFi started as a nine-inch silicon tail that wiggled with the assistance of a hydraulic pump.’ (paragraph 9) “The high-pitched signals only travel about 65 feet and are inaudible to fish, although it’s possible some whales or dolphins could hear them .. ” (paragraph 18)

explanation.text: Unlike loud, tethered marine vehicles that frighten animals, SoFi swims without a boat cable or noisy propeller and does not disturb nearby fish. This contrasts with environmental harm because the technology observes wildlife without disrupting it.

questions.alt: Question 41. In paragraph 21, Mr. Katzschmann reacts positively to the idea that a shark might eat SoFi 41 because that would A lead to further funding and research B test the strength of the underwater system C help biologists understand how to protect marine life D support the goal of studying animal interactions

explanation.text: A shark attacking SoFi would produce rare footage of a real animal interacting naturally with the robot. Katzschmann welcomes that possibility because observing animal interactions is one of the project's research goals.

questions.alt: Question 42. Which idea would be most important to include in a summary of the article? 42 A SoFi uses coded messages to communicate. B SoFi receives commands from a remote control. C SoFi is an important tool for understanding ocean life. D SoFi is designed to swim at different speeds in the ocean.

explanation.text: The article emphasizes that SoFi can observe animals without frightening them and may help biologists study behavior, ecosystems, and pollution. Her importance as a tool for understanding ocean life therefore belongs in a summary more than individual technical features do.

Official passage for Questions 7-12. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. In paragraph 3, what is mmoosstt lliikkeellyy the narrator’s point of view of Stephanie? 1 A Stephanie likes to talk with many people on the bus. B Sometimes Stephanie acts younger than she really is. C Stephanie likes to stay friends with people for a long time. D Sometimes Stephanie acts in a mean way toward a friend.

explanation.text: Rosa explains that Stephanie sometimes ignores her or treats her like a little kid when other friends are present. These actions show that Rosa sees Stephanie as sometimes behaving meanly toward a friend.

questions.alt: Question 2. What does the phrase “left out” mean as used in paragraph 7? A watched B alone C troubled D careful

explanation.text: Rosa joins Kiara while Stephanie talks with the other dancers, hoping Stephanie will notice and feel "left out." Because Rosa wants Stephanie to experience being excluded as she did, the phrase means alone.

questions.alt: Question 3. Which quotation best shows how Rosa and Stephanie usually act with each other? A “Rosa and Stephanie did everything together.” (paragraph 2) “She walked away to join Toni and Vanessa, and Rosa was left B : » standing alone.” (paragraph 5) Cc “Rosa tried not to check the mirror to see if Stephanie had noticed” (paragraph 7) D “But Rosa walked past her and slid into the empty seat next to Kiara.” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The story says Rosa and Stephanie do everything together, then lists riding the bus, eating lunch, taking dance lessons, and attending camp together. This evidence best shows their usual closeness as best friends.

questions.alt: Question 4. In paragraphs 16 through 20, the reader can see that Rosa changes from 4 A trying to avoid someone to making new friends B feeling sad about something to having a good time C wanting to upset her friend to wanting to include her D having a good time to feeling angry at someone

explanation.text: Rosa first copies Stephanie's unfriendly behavior because she wants to get even and hurt her feelings. After seeing Stephanie's sad face, Rosa feels bad and invites both girls, showing a change from exclusion to inclusion.

questions.alt: Question 5. In paragraph 21, what does the phrase “Stephanie brightened” mean? 5 A She became smarter. B She moved into the sunlight. C She changed into dressy clothes. D She became happier.

explanation.text: Stephanie has just been invited to join Rosa and Kiara at the pool when she "brightened" and says she would like that. Her positive reaction indicates that the invitation makes her happier.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which sentence bbeesstt supports the central message of the story? 6 A Stephanie makes room for Rosa to sit next to her on the bus. B Stephanie tells Rosa that she is going to the pool with someone else. C Rosa tells Stephanie that she has plans with Kiara. D Rosa asks Stephanie if she wants to go to the pool with her and Kiara.

explanation.text: Rosa discovers that excluding Stephanie does not feel good, even though she had wanted revenge. Her invitation for Stephanie to join her and Kiara supports the central message that including others is better than trying to get even.

questions.alt: Question 7. What question does paragraph 3 help to answer? 7 A How do ears take in sounds? B What do eardrums do? C How do noises travel? D What do sounds feel like?

explanation.text: Paragraph 3 asks readers to touch their throats while speaking and notice the tiny, quick shaking of their vocal cords. This hands-on example explains what the vibrations that create sound feel like.

questions.alt: Question 8. Which sentence explains why there are no sounds in outer space? A __ Sound waves cannot travel fast enough to be heard in space. B Sound waves cannot bounce off things in space. C There is no air in space for sound waves to travel through. D There are no people or animals in space to hear sound waves.

explanation.text: Paragraph 7 says outer space has no air and therefore nothing through which sound waves can move. Without a material to carry the waves, no sound is produced outside a spaceship.

questions.alt: Question 9. What happens right after sound waves cause the eardrum to vibrate? A The brain makes sense of what is heard. B Nerve hairs send signals to the brain. C Sound waves travel through the ear canal. D Some sound waves cause lower sounds.

explanation.text: After sound waves make the eardrum vibrate, special nerve hairs farther inside the ear pick up those vibrations. This sequence shows that the hairs next send signals to the brain, which later makes sense of the sounds.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which phrase from the passage best explains the meaning of “silence” as it is used in paragraph 7? A “There are no sounds” (paragraph 7) B “Because there is no air” (paragraph 7) C “sound waves bounce back” (paragraph 8) D “clap your hands in an empty room” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The author describes outer space by saying, "There are no sounds," and then calls that condition "silence." This nearby phrase directly explains that silence means an absence of sound.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which sentence best states the main idea of the passage? A Some noises are loud and others are quiet. B Sound waves do not travel through all things the same way. C Vibrations create sound waves that let us hear noises. D There is no air or sound in outer space.

explanation.text: The passage traces how vibrations create sound waves, how ears collect them, and how the brain interprets their signals. This repeated explanation supports the main idea that vibrations produce the waves that allow people to hear noises.

questions.alt: Question 12. Based on the passage, what does the author most likely think about how people hear? A Learning how people understand sounds is very interesting. B__Itis strange that all sounds do not return to where they start. C _Itis hard to find out how people make sense of sounds. D The fact that there is no sound in space is a problem.

explanation.text: The author uses lively examples, simple experiments, and inviting questions to help readers explore vibrations, ears, space, and echoes. This enthusiastic presentation suggests that the author finds the process of understanding sound very interesting.

Official passage for Questions 19-24. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. Read these sentences from paragraph 1. 1 FFaatthheerrss ccoommee iinn aallll sshhaappeess aanndd ssiizzeess.. IInn tthhee aanniimmaall kkiinnggddoomm,, ffaatthheerrss hhaavvee ssoommee ffaasscciinnaattiinngg ((aanndd ssttrraannggee!!)) wwaayyss ooff hheellppiinngg tthheeiirr yyoouunngg.. How does the author support the information in these sentences? A by explaining that large and small father animals exist in the wild B by explaining that father animals can appear unusual by showing that father animals know their babies change shape as C they grow by showing that father animals use different ways to care for D their babies

explanation.text: The article gives many examples: fish fathers use their mouths, frog fathers use their throats, penguins use their feet, and foxes hide food. These examples support the opening because they show different ways animal fathers care for their young.

questions.alt: Question 2. Which statement supports a main idea found in both paragraph 4 and paragraph 6? 2 A Father animals need help from the mother when they want to eat. B Father animals have many ways to provide food for their babies. C Father animals use their bodies to protect their babies from harm. D Father animals know that keeping the eggs warm is important.

explanation.text: A Darwin's frog keeps its babies inside its throat, while an Emperor penguin holds an egg on his feet beneath his belly skin. Both fathers therefore use parts of their bodies to protect their young from harm.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does the word “flap” mean as it is used in paragraph 6? 3 A box closing B piece of fabric C loose covering D part of a book

explanation.text: The penguin places the egg on his feet and covers it with a "flap" of thick belly skin to keep it warm. The word means a loose covering because the skin lies over and protects the egg.

questions.alt: Question 4. According to the information in paragraph 7, which statement bbeesstt explains something 4 special the father red fox does for his kits? A He watches the kits as they learn to dig for food. B He stays in the den to make sure the kits are warm. C He hunts for food and then covers it so the kits have to find it. D He comes back to the den every few hours to check on the kits.

explanation.text: The father fox brings food and then buries extra meat near the den under twigs. Hiding the meat is special because it teaches his kits to search for and find food themselves.

questions.alt: Question 5. How does the table support the information in the article? 5 A It compares father and mother animals. B It shows the number of babies a father animal cares for. C It gives more information about the sizes of father birds. D It gives more examples of how animal fathers take care of babies.

explanation.text: The table adds seahorses, owls, rheas, and jacanas and lists each father's unusual caregiving action. It supports the article by giving more examples of animal fathers protecting, feeding, or raising babies.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which statement bbeesstt summarizes the main idea of the article? 6 A Frog fathers sometimes care for a large number of babies. B Animal fathers have unusual ways of helping their young. C Animal fathers sometimes care for their young in groups. D Fox fathers need many skills to keep their kits healthy.

explanation.text: Every section describes a different animal father caring for young through an unusual behavior, from carrying babies in a throat to teaching kits to hunt. Together, these examples support the broad main idea that animal fathers have remarkable ways of helping their young.

questions.alt: Question 19. 2 paragraph 4 of the article, what is the most likely reason the author sends his work to an editor at Random House? A The author likes the illustrations in the story. B__ His second grade teacher suggests that he send the story to the editor. C The author thinks his story is one that people would like to read. D His mother helps him send the story to the editor.

explanation.text: The author says he considered the magician story funny and good enough to become a book. That belief explains why he sent it to a Random House editor: he thought other people would enjoy reading it.

questions.alt: Question 20. Read these sentences from paragraph 5. Now I tell young writers, “Keep trying. If you work hard and make your writing the best you can, you'll be published too” The author best supports these sentences by including A _ information about how he wrote stories for family members B the point about how the book he wrote in second grade was turned down C _astory about why his brother draws for his books D the fact that he and his brother sent out four books before one was accepted

explanation.text: Editors rejected the author's first three book attempts with his brother, but an editor accepted the fourth. Their repeated submissions support his advice because continued effort eventually led to publication.

questions.alt: Question 21. Read this sentence from paragraph 6. Suddenly, I was a published writer! What does “published writer” mean as it is used in this sentence? A __ someone who has original ideas when writing B someone who has written work printed to share with the public C someone who likes to retell written works by changing the events D someone who writes stories to give away as gifts

explanation.text: The author has just explained that his story appeared in the Oakland Tribune's "Kids' Corner." Calling himself a "published writer" means his written work was printed where the public could read it.

questions.alt: Question 22. How is the information in paragraph 6 organized? 22 A Details are presented in the order they happened. B An effect and its cause are mentioned. C Different events are compared and contrasted. D A problem and its solution are presented.

explanation.text: Paragraph 6 moves from the author's newspaper story in third grade to rejected books in college, a fourth accepted attempt, and publication one year later. The structure is chronological because those details appear in the order the events occurred.

questions.alt: Question 23. The purpose of the information in paragraph 7 is to show that A the author continues to enjoy seeing his work in print and on the screen B the author’s favorite part of a movie is what happens at the end C _ the author wants his writing to be in magazines and newspapers D _ the author’s work is very good when his name appears on the screen

explanation.text: The author says he still feels a thrill when holding a new book or seeing a story in print, and he celebrates his Mulan screen credit. These examples show that he continues to enjoy seeing his work published and adapted.

questions.alt: Question 24. Which sentence from the article best supports the main idea of “Sharing My Story”? A “The editor at Random House didn’t buy my story.’ (paragraph 5) B “I had my first story printed in a newspaper when I was in third grade. (paragraph 6) C = “However long it takes, there are great rewards.’ (paragraph 7) D “I never met the editor who told me to keep writing, but she was a friend to me? (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The article traces years of writing, rejection, repeated effort, and eventual publication, then encourages young writers to persist. "However long it takes, there are great rewards" best supports that main idea because it joins perseverance with its result.
