questions.alt: Question 1. Which statement best expresses a theme of the story? A itis wise to work in secret if a dream is not supported by relatives. B Working with new people can be scary, but will become easier over time. Cc Beautiful objects can bring happiness, but they are often not worth the price. D Determination and creative thinking can help people overcome challenges.

explanation.text: Sali cannot afford the expensive cloth or sew the robe, so she invents work-for-payment arrangements with both the shopkeeper and tailor. Her sustained effort and practical solutions support the theme that determination and creative thinking overcome challenges.

questions.alt: Question 2. In paragraph 1, what does the phrase “like a flame” help the reader to understand about 2 the cloth? A its size and how much it weighs B its color and how it moves C its temperature and its direction D its pattern and its use

explanation.text: The cloth is a glorious orange scrap that turns and dances in the hot wind. The comparison works because a flame is brightly colored and moves in a similar fluttering way.

questions.alt: Question 3. What do the details in paragraph 3 show about Sali? 3 A She is thoughtful and creative. B She is greedy and demanding. C She is careful and ambitious. D She is strange and secretive.

explanation.text: Sali imagines the scrap as part of a flowing dress but decides to make it a lovely headpiece for her doll. This shows creativity in seeing different possibilities and thoughtfulness in sharing the treasure with the doll.

questions.alt: Question 4. What does paragraph 13 reveal about Sali’s point of view? 4 Sali is worried that all of her work will be wasted if she cannot complete A her project. Sali is eager to learn new skills so that she can take care of herself like B a grownup. Sali is delighted to keep her secret and surprise her family with her C new dlokibani. D Sali is unsure and does not have a detailed plan to have the dlokibani made.

explanation.text: After earning the fabric, Sali realizes she cannot sew and asks herself how the robe will be made. These thoughts reveal that she is uncertain and has not yet formed a detailed plan for completing the project.

questions.alt: Question 5. What can the reader infer about the shopkeeper and the tailor? A They are clever and proud. B They are tense and distracted. C They are silly and foolish. D They are fair and generous.

explanation.text: Both adults accept Sali's offer to work rather than dismissing a child who lacks money, and each gives her what she earns. Their willingness to make a fair exchange and help her achieve the robe shows generosity as well as fairness.

questions.alt: Question 6. How do paragraphs 7 and 17 contribute to the structure of the story? A They foreshadow an important choice Sali makes later. B They explain why Sali wants a new dress. C They show the actions Sali takes to solve her problem. D they provide details about the shopkeeper and the tailor.

explanation.text: In paragraph 7, Sali offers to work for the shopkeeper to earn cloth; in paragraph 17, she offers the tailor work in exchange for sewing. These matching actions solve her problems because each trades labor for something she cannot buy.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which quotation best supports a theme of the story? A “‘Why do you stand so long at the door?’ he asked.” (paragraph 6) B “*You’ve worked well for this cloth, Sali,’ he said.” (paragraph 11) C “Sali rushed straight home with her treasure.’ (paragraph 12) D “Then he started cutting the billows of orange fabric” (paragraph 19)

explanation.text: The shopkeeper's statement connects Sali's many days of labor directly to receiving the expensive cloth. It supports the story's theme because her determination and hard work earn the reward she needs.

questions.alt: Question 8. Which sentence bbeesstt represents a conflict between Guthrie and her father? 8 A Guthrie’s father is concerned when she begins flying at a young age. B Guthrie’s father thinks free-fall parachuting is too dangerous for her. C Guthrie’s father is worried about her becoming a race car driver. D Guthrie’s father insists she has to take private parachuting lessons.

explanation.text: Janet wants to make a free-fall jump, but her father first refuses and says "Absolutely not" because he considers it dangerous. Their opposing views about whether she should parachute create the conflict.

questions.alt: Question 9. Paragraphs 10 and 11 are important to the article because they 9 A describe to the reader how Guthrie drove around Long Island B inform the reader that Guthrie joined a local sports car club C explain to the reader how Guthrie began to win auto races D tell the reader that Guthrie was helped by a driver in Connecticut

explanation.text: Guthrie first joins a local sports-car club and begins entering and winning races, then an impressed driving instructor urges her toward auto racing. This sequence explains how casual driving developed into competitive success.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which quotation best reveals the author’s point of view? A “Tt was no surprise that Janet Guthrie excelled at one of the most dangerous sports on Earth. (paragraph 1) B “... the pro taught her how to pull the rip cord that opened the chute, how to absorb the shock after landing . .” (paragraph 7) “She flew whenever she could break away from her classes at the University of Michigan” (paragraph 8) D “To get into top physical shape for Vollstedt’s test, Guthrie did exercises...” (paragraph 19)

explanation.text: Saying it was "no surprise" that Guthrie excelled in a dangerous sport is an admiring judgment based on her lifelong love of adventure. Unlike the other factual details, it directly reveals the author's view of her daring character.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which detail from the article represents Guthrie’s biggest challenge? A “Before long she taught herself how to take apart and rebuild its engine ..” (paragraph 12) B “.. finishing in some of the country’s most celebrated long-distance competitions.” (paragraph 14) Cc “..a secret test at the Ontario Motor Speedway near Los Angeles.” (paragraph 18) D “One day she lost her balance, landed hard on her left foot, and fell. .” (paragraph 19)

explanation.text: Just before a rare chance to test an Indianapolis-style car, Guthrie breaks a bone in her foot and receives a heavy cast. This injury is her biggest challenge because it threatens her ability to take the test and seize the opportunity.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which detail from the article bbeesstt represents Janet Guthrie? 12 “She had always loved adventure and daring new experiences.” A (paragraph 1) “Excited by her success, Guthrie attended a driving school in Connecticut.” B (paragraph 11) “In 1973, she won the North Atlantic Road Racing Championship.” C (paragraph 14) “Broken ankle and all, Guthrie stepped into an unfamiliar car.” D (paragraph 20)

explanation.text: From flying and parachuting to rebuilding engines and testing an unfamiliar race car with a broken ankle, Guthrie repeatedly seeks adventure. The opening statement best represents her because it describes the daring trait behind all those choices.

questions.alt: Question 13. In paragraph 20, what does Guthrie mean when she says, “It was like going to the moon”? A She thinks driving a fast car is quite exciting. B She believes racing can often be dangerous. C She thinks the speed of the car is frightening. D _ She believes racing is similar to space flight.

explanation.text: Guthrie calls the high-speed test a thrill immediately before comparing it with going to the moon. The simile refers to excitement because both experiences seem extraordinary and unlike ordinary travel.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which idea from the article does the title “ ‘Janet Guthrie: Lady in the Fast Lane’ from Profiles 14 in Sports Courage” bbeesstt support? A Guthrie was a hard worker and saved money to achieve her goals. B Guthrie was famous for participating in a race with a broken foot. C Guthrie was fearless at trying thrilling and challenging new activities. D Guthrie was determined to become a commercial pilot like her father.

explanation.text: The title emphasizes both speed and courage, qualities Guthrie shows by trying free-fall parachuting, competitive racing, and an unfamiliar championship car despite injury. These events support the idea that she fearlessly accepts thrilling new challenges.

questions.alt: Question 29. What does “brewing” mean as it is used in paragraph 1? 29 A being studied B separating C developing D being encouraged

explanation.text: "Trouble was brewing" is followed by falling temperatures, strong winds, and rapidly increasing snow. That context shows that a dangerous storm was developing.

questions.alt: Question 30. What is the meaning of the phrase “sops up” as it is used in paragraph 5? 30 A pushes back B takes in C dries off D cools down

explanation.text: The author compares air to a sponge that absorbs lake water and later wrings the moisture onto land. In that comparison, "sops up" means takes in the water.

questions.alt: Question 31. Why is wind direction an important factor in the creation of lake-effect snow? A The wind direction can increase the amount of water taken into the air. B The wind direction can decrease the temperature of the air. Cc The wind direction can cause the water in a lake to become rough. D The wind direction can turn the water in a lake into ice.

explanation.text: When wind travels across more of the lake's surface, the air has more opportunity to absorb evaporated water. Thus, wind direction can increase the amount of moisture carried into the air and later released as snow.

questions.alt: Question 32. What is the purpose of the information in paragraph 7? 32 A to explain how lake-effect snow is beneficial for an area B to provide details about how lake-effect snow is removed from streets C to compare how lake-effect snow impacts different cities near large lakes D to describe life in a place where lake-effect snow is common

explanation.text: Paragraph 7 says Buffalo residents experience massive snowstorms as a regular part of life and are well trained in snow removal. It describes local life because those details show how residents experience and handle frequent lake-effect snow.

questions.alt: Question 33. The diagram adds new information to the article by showing that A lake-effect snow forms when cold air moves over warm water B heat and moisture rise up over the lake water to help form snow C clouds increase in size as lake-effect snow forms D__huge amounts of moisture fall to the ground in the form of snow

explanation.text: The article explains that heat and moisture rise and cool before falling as snow, while the diagram also shows the clouds becoming larger during that process. The cloud-growth stage is therefore new information added by the visual.

questions.alt: Question 34. Which detail would be mmoosstt important to include in a summary of the article? 34 A People who live in areas with lake-effect snow rarely have days off. B Cold air and warm water are needed to form lake-effect snow. C Buffalo is a city that is used to dealing with lake-effect snow. D Lake Erie produces less lake-effect snow at certain times of the year.

explanation.text: The article's main purpose is to explain how lake-effect snow forms when below-freezing air passes over warmer lake water and gains moisture. That basic combination belongs in a summary because it states the phenomenon's central cause.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which sentence bbeesstt states a central idea of the article? 35 A Lake-effect snow is common in certain areas of the country. B Lake-effect snow can occur only for a short period of time each year. C Lake-effect snow requires a special set of conditions in order to occur. D Lake-effect snow can cause damage to power lines and trees.

explanation.text: Lake-effect snow forms only when several factors align, including cold air, warmer open lake water, useful wind direction, and enough temperature difference. Because the article repeatedly explains those ingredients, the need for special conditions is its central idea.

questions.alt: Question 1. How does paragraph 9 contribute to the development of a theme in the story? A __bysuggesting that Aven often observes herself B__by describing the care Aven puts into her appearance C __ by showing Aven is worried about what people think D by illustrating how Aven prepares herself for a performance

explanation.text: Aven studies herself in the mirror and wonders whether she can face other people in her dress. This develops the theme because her concern about their judgment establishes the insecurity she later overcomes.

questions.alt: Question 2. How does paragraph 13 mmaaiinnllyy develop the character of the narrator? 2 A by informing the reader that the guitar belonged to the narrator’s mother B by telling the reader that the narrator found the guitar in a storage closet C by informing the reader that the narrator’s parents were watching her by telling the reader about the type of shoes the narrator wears D while performing

explanation.text: The repaired instrument waiting onstage is not an ordinary guitar but the one that belonged to Aven's mother. This develops her character because it gives the performance a personal family connection and greater emotional meaning.

questions.alt: Question 3. What is the effect of the narrator’s repetition of the word “me” in paragraph 14? 3 A She thinks the audience is impressed with her ability to play the guitar. B She hopes people will notice the objects which belong to her mother. C She thinks it is remarkable that she is performing in front of an audience. D She hopes that people appreciate how much she practiced during the week.

explanation.text: Aven repeats "me" while noticing herself in the dress, necklace, and performance as the whole audience watches. The repetition is effective because it emphasizes her amazement at occupying the center of attention.

questions.alt: Question 4. What does the word “insignificant” mean as it is used in paragraph 15? 4 A talentless B brave C unimportant D accepted

explanation.text: Aven says she no longer feels "insignificant" and immediately contrasts that feeling with being as big as a giant cactus and shining. The contrast shows that "insignificant" means unimportant or too small to matter.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which phrase from paragraph 15 bbeesstt conveys a theme of the story? 5 A “the fireworks exploded” B “the lights of the city” C “millions of people” D “the light was in me”

explanation.text: At first Aven depends on how others may view her, but after performing she recognizes that the light may come from within herself. "The light was in me" best conveys the theme of discovering inner confidence and worth.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail bbeesstt represents how Aven changes at the end of the story? 6 A “. . . plucked at a string with one not quite steady toe.” (paragraph 13) B “. . . I saw that all their eyes were on me . . .” (paragraph 14) C “. . . arms around each other, swaying to the music.” (paragraph 14) D “I felt as big as the giant saguaro . . .” (paragraph 15)

explanation.text: Earlier, Aven's heart thumps and her toe is unsteady because she fears performing before others. Feeling "as big as the giant saguaro" shows her change into someone confident, noticeable, and strong.

questions.alt: Question 7. Read this phrase from paragraph 15. 7 II ffeelltt lliikkee II wwaass sshhiinniinngg .. .. .. Which sentence bbeesstt represents the meaning of this phrase? A Aven feels proud of who she is. B Aven is looking forward to more performances. C Aven understands she is more important than others. D Aven is relieved the audience enjoyed her performance.

explanation.text: Aven's feeling of shining comes after she performs successfully and stops feeling insignificant. The image means pride because she now recognizes her own identity, ability, and inner worth.

questions.alt: Question 8. Which statement represents a central claim of the article? A __ Environments often influence career choices. B__Bats are misunderstood creatures who need protecting. C_ Photographs can help people overcome their fears of the unknown. D Bats often travel a great distance when they migrate.

explanation.text: The article repeatedly shows bats being feared, killed, and driven from caves because people believe false ideas about them. Merlin's research, organization, and photographs all address the central claim that misunderstood bats need protection.

questions.alt: Question 9. What does the information presented in the last two lines of paragraph 3 illustrate? A People forced the bats to leave the cave. B__Bats had already migrated to another place. C People enjoyed using the cave for activities. D Bats left the cave because of the amount of garbage.

explanation.text: The once-crowded cave is empty and contains sticks, stones, rifle cartridges, and fireworks wrappers. Those human objects provide evidence that people attacked the colony and forced or killed the bats rather than the animals simply migrating.

questions.alt: Question 10. Why does the author use words like “Misguided” (paragraph 3) and “misunderstand” 10 (paragraph 4) in the article? A to suggest that bats have been the victims of myths B to emphasize why a solution to bat removal is needed C to explain why many bats travel great distances every year D to indicate that books do not always give accurate information about bats

explanation.text: The article lists false beliefs that bats are blind, fierce, diseased, and eager to bite. Calling people "misguided" and saying they "misunderstand" bats suggests that harmful treatment grows from myths rather than facts.

questions.alt: Question 11. Read this sentence from paragraph 3. 11 ““EEvveerryywwhheerree II wweenntt,, ppeeooppllee wweerree kkiilllliinngg bbaattss iinn llaarrggee nnuummbbeerrss jjuusstt oouutt ooff iiggnnoorraannccee,,”” ssaayyss MMeerrlliinn.. Which quotation from the article bbeesstt explains the cause of the “ignorance” described by Merlin? A “. . . Merlin figured out that the books were wrong.” (paragraph 1) B “Many people are afraid of bats.” (paragraph 3) C “What he found shocked him.” (paragraph 3) D “Bats fly at night and spend the day in dark places.” (paragraph 5)

explanation.text: The article explains that many people fear bats and then describes killings caused by ignorance. This fear best explains the ignorance because people act against bats before understanding that they are gentle and useful.

questions.alt: Question 12. What is the main way paragraph 5 helps develop ideas in the article? A __byexplaining the habits of bats during daytime hours B__by describing the stress bats often experience C _ by explaining why Merlin is interested in bat photographs D _ by describing how Merlin helps people better understand bats

explanation.text: Merlin replaces frightening pictures of stressed bats with natural photographs of bats flying, feeding, fishing, and caring for young. Those images help people understand bats accurately and consequently want to protect them.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which detail would be mmoosstt important to include in a summary of the article? 13 Merlin Tuttle studied bats and helped educate the public about A their importance. B Farmers told Merlin Tuttle that bats were bad for their fruit trees and crops. Merlin Tuttle earned a degree in advanced mammalogy when he was C in college. D People told Merlin Tuttle they were afraid of bats when he talked to them.

explanation.text: The article traces both halves of Merlin's work: researching bats and correcting the public's harmful misunderstandings through conservation and photography. That combined statement belongs in a summary because it captures his central scientific and educational contributions.

questions.alt: Question 14. How does the author develop an important idea in the article? 14 by describing Merlin’s discovery of gray bat migration, which shows how A ideas can change by referring to traditional photographs of bats, which make bats appear to B be threatening by describing Merlin’s effort to inform people about bats, which shows the C importance of education by referring to caves where bats live, which indicates that ideas may D sometimes be based on fear

explanation.text: Merlin creates a conservation group, writes books, and takes accurate photographs to replace fear with knowledge about bats. These efforts develop the idea that education matters because better understanding makes people willing to save the animals.

questions.alt: Question 22. What does the word “brittle” mean as it is used in paragraph 3? 22 A very valuable B cold to the touch C easily broken D hard to read

explanation.text: The old map is described as delicate and unable to withstand much more folding or unfolding. Those clues show that "brittle" means fragile and easily broken.

questions.alt: Question 23. How does Milo respond to the discovery of the map? 23 A with slight nervousness B with intense curiosity C with extreme astonishment D with mild amusement

explanation.text: Milo carefully unfolds the paper, shines light through it, identifies it as a nautical chart, and turns it repeatedly to understand the unknown waterway. His sustained investigation reveals intense curiosity about the discovery.

questions.alt: Question 24. How does the plot change in paragraphs 8 through 10? 24 A Someone arrives in search of Milo. B Milo hides the wallet in the shed. C Someone arrives in search of the wallet. D A guest departs because of the snow.

explanation.text: A bundled stranger appears, circles the pavilion, drops onto the tracks, and searches the place where Milo found the wallet. These actions change the plot because someone has arrived looking for the object Milo now holds.

questions.alt: Question 25. Read this quotation from paragraph 10. 25 SSttiillll,, wwhheenn tthhee ddaarrkk sshhaaddooww sswwuunngg iittsseellff bbaacckk uupp ooffff tthhee ttrraacckkss,, ssoommeetthhiinngg mmaaddee MMiilloo eeddggee ddeeeeppeerr iinnttoo tthhee sshheedd aanndd ttuucckk hhiimmsseellff aass ffaarr bbeehhiinndd tthhee wwiinncchh aass hhee ccoouulldd.. What effect does this quotation have on the tone of the story? A It adds to the mysterious tone. B It introduces a judging tone. C It creates a discouraged tone. D It establishes a concerned tone.

explanation.text: The person remains an unidentified "dark shadow," while Milo cannot explain the instinct that makes him hide behind the winch. This uncertainty about the stranger's identity and purpose adds to the mysterious tone.

questions.alt: Question 26. How do Milo’s actions in paragraphs 9 through 11 contribute to the development of the plot? 26 A by revealing his intention to keep the wallet B by showing that he fears the guest in the pavilion C by suggesting that he knows there are more maps D by emphasizing his preference for being alone

explanation.text: Although Milo knows he should return the wallet, he hides from its apparent owner, refolds the map, conceals the wallet in his pocket, and waits until the person leaves. These choices reveal his intention to keep it.

questions.alt: Question 27. The author mainly develops Milo’s point of view by A _ expressing Milo’s opinion about where the map leads B revealing Milo’s thoughts about his discovery and the guest Cc commenting on Milo’s decision to keep the wallet D _ explaining Milo’s unwillingness to communicate with the guest

explanation.text: The narration shares Milo's reasoning as he identifies the chart, tries to orient it, infers that the stranger wants the wallet, and debates returning it. Revealing those thoughts develops his curious but cautious point of view.

questions.alt: Question 28. Which detail would be mmoosstt important to include in a summary of the story? 28 A Milo notices that the paper has a watermark. B Milo turns the map so that the compass rose points north. C Milo sees that the guest is wearing a heavy coat. D Milo hides in the shed until the person outside leaves.

explanation.text: Milo's decision to hide while the stranger searches creates the story's central suspense and leads to his concealment of the wallet. It belongs in a summary because it is a major plot action, unlike smaller observations about the map or clothing.

questions.alt: Question 1. How does Chip change as a result of the way Mr. O’Dell teaches? 1 A He improves his memory. B He becomes confused. C He develops enthusiasm. D He loses confidence.

explanation.text: Chip once considered science a collection of boring textbook words, but Mr. O'Dell's riddles, observations, and field trips teach him a new way to look at living things. This change shows growing enthusiasm.

questions.alt: Question 2. Read this sentence from paragraph 8. 2 OOnnccee wwhheenn aa ssttuuddeenntt ppooiinntteedd oouutt aann aanntthhiillll,, MMrr.. OO’’DDeellll ppuutt hhiiss nnoossee ddoowwnn ssoo cclloossee ttoo wwaattcchh tthhaatt CChhiipp eexxppeecctteedd aann aanntt ttoo cclliimmbb rriigghhtt oonnttoo iitt aanndd mmaarrcchh uupp tthhrroouugghh tthhee ffrreecckklleess ttoo ssttaarree bbaacckk aatt hhiimm tthhrroouugghh hhiiss ggoolldd--rriimmmmeedd ggllaasssseess.. What tone does this sentence add to the story? A a curious tone B a humorous tone C an anxious tone D an observant tone

explanation.text: Chip imagines an ant marching up Mr. O'Dell's freckled nose and staring through his glasses. That playful exaggeration therefore makes the teacher's close inspection of the anthill humorous.

questions.alt: Question 3. How do the details about Mr. O’ Dell in paragraph 11 support a central idea of the story? A They show his fascination with blue skinks. B_ They show his appreciation for living animals. C They show his excitement about baby dinosaurs. D They show his passion for reading animals’ feelings.

explanation.text: Mr. O'Dell asks Chip to return the skink and explains that some creatures die outside their natural environment. His concern for its home shows appreciation for living animals, not merely fascination with one lizard.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which word is a synonym for “displace” as it is used in paragraph 14? 4 A guard B return C change D remove

explanation.text: Mr. O'Dell compares displacement to being driven far away and dumped somewhere unfamiliar. Since the creature is taken out of its home, “displace” means remove.

questions.alt: Question 5. In the story, how does the author develop Chip’s point of view of Mr. O’Dell? 5 A by mentioning that Chip is one of Mr. O’Dell’s favorite students B by showing Chip answering a tough question that Mr. O’Dell asks C by explaining why Mr. O’Dell has special field trip privileges D by describing events that make an impression on Chip

explanation.text: Chip reacts to Mr. O'Dell's water riddle, energetic science lessons, detailed field observations, and advice about the skink. Describing these memorable events reveals why Chip comes to value the teacher.

questions.alt: Question 6. How do Chip’s explorations in the outdoors influence him? A They lead to his initial interest in a variety of local animals. B They make it difficult for him to understand Luther’s point of view. C They prompt him to discover wildlife in plowed fields. D They enable him to show Luther how to handle a skink.

explanation.text: Long before class, Chip explored drainage ditches and grassy banks, developed favorite animals, and learned enough to teach Luther about local birds and turtles. Those outdoor experiences therefore sparked his first broad interest in wildlife.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of “Excerpt from The Last Wild Place”? A Mr. O'Dell treats students differently based on their interests. B Mr. O’Dell teaches science better than he teaches other subjects. C Mr. O’Dell values the purpose and habitat of every animal. D Mr. O'Dell previously taught in college and wrote a book.

explanation.text: Mr. O'Dell teaches that every animal has a purpose and belongs in its natural environment, then makes sure Chip returns the skink home. Therefore, this principle shapes Chip's lesson and is central to a summary.

questions.alt: Question 8. What is mmoosstt lliikkeellyy the author’s purpose in paragraph 1? 8 A to demonstrate Susan Butcher’s significant contribution to women’s sports B to introduce the relationship between Susan Butcher and her dogs C to explain that more men than women compete in the Iditarod D to convey the challenges of the Iditarod race

explanation.text: Paragraph 1 describes a dogsled race of more than a thousand miles across some of the planet's most difficult wilderness. These details therefore introduce the extreme challenge Butcher repeatedly faced.

questions.alt: Question 9. What do the details in paragraph 4 explain about the Iditarod? 9 A why more men than women enter the Iditarod B why Susan Butcher had to withdraw from the Iditarod C how much preparation is needed to compete in the Iditarod D how Susan Butcher selected her team of dogs for the Iditarod

explanation.text: Butcher and her dogs trained as much as sixteen hours a day to build stamina, maintain top condition, and work as a team. That demanding schedule shows how much preparation the Iditarod requires.

questions.alt: Question 10. What is the effect of the author’s use of the word “must” four times in the sentences at the 10 beginning of paragraph 5? A It emphasizes that the ideas in these sentences are critical for the race. It demonstrates that the ideas in these sentences reflect what the B musher believes. C It indicates that the ideas in these sentences are essentially the same. D It shows that the ideas in these sentences are the only important traits.

explanation.text: Repeating “must” presents trust, leadership, judgment, and even disobeying a dangerous command as requirements rather than preferences. The repetition therefore emphasizes that these qualities are critical to surviving the race.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which claim by the author is most strongly supported with evidence? A “Two pet Siberian huskies kindled her interest in dogsled racing.” (paragraph 3) B “The musher must have faith in each and every dog on her team...” (paragraph 5) C_ “.. the race was still largely the domain of men.” (paragraph 6) D = “In 1986, it was Butcher’s turn.” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: A lead dog refuses Butcher's command to turn left and saves the entire team from a collapsing snow bridge. This concrete example most strongly supports the claim that a musher must trust every dog.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which quotation bbeesstt portrays Susan Butcher’s relationship with her dogs? 12 “. . . she studied veterinary medicine at a nearby university, focusing on the A care of dogs.” (paragraph 3) “. . . both the musher of the dogsled and the entire team of dogs must be in B top physical condition.” (paragraph 4) “. . . Butcher bonded with her dogs and treated them as friends, family, and C professional athletes.” (paragraph 5) “. . . Butcher was able to pull the dogs to safety and continue the race.” D (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The description of Butcher treating her dogs as friends, family members, and professional athletes directly conveys both affection and respect. The other details show care or teamwork without defining the whole relationship.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which statement bbeesstt expresses the relationship between paragraph 3 and paragraph 8? 13 A Paragraph 3 provides a summary, and paragraph 8 lists details. B Paragraph 3 explains a cause, and paragraph 8 reveals effects. C Paragraph 3 describes goals, and paragraph 8 presents an outcome. D Paragraph 3 identifies problems, and paragraph 8 explains a solution.

explanation.text: Paragraph 3 describes Butcher's goals of becoming a professional musher, moving to Alaska, and assembling an Iditarod team. Paragraph 8 therefore presents the outcome when she wins and sets a record.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A “... Butcher was drawn as a child to the wilderness and to the animals that filled it? (paragraph 2) B “Training for the Iditarod encompasses a wide scope of activities” (paragraph 4) “The portion of trail to the left was a flimsy snow bridge that would have Cc » collapsed . ..” (paragraph 5) D “She had not only won the race, she had also set a new speed record” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: Butcher's first-place finish and new speed record are the culmination of her years of ambition, training, setbacks, and persistence. Because this is her central achievement, it is essential to a summary.

questions.alt: Question 29. What does the word “precision” mean as it is used in paragraph 3? 29 A frequency B force C accuracy D speed

explanation.text: A xylophone player must strike exactly the right bar at exactly the right moment to produce the intended note. That need for exact placement and timing shows that “precision” means accuracy.

questions.alt: Question 30. What idea do paragraphs 7 and 8 share? 30 A Stringed instruments are characterized by natural materials. B Some instruments are more popular and appealing than others. C New instruments can be created from everyday materials. D Even simple instruments can produce beautiful music.

explanation.text: Paragraph 7 tells how a metal bolt inspired a new steel-guitar technique, while paragraph 8 describes people making instruments from gourds. Both examples show musical instruments emerging from ordinary materials.

questions.alt: Question 31. What does the word “version” mean as it is used in paragraph 10? 31 A a special edition B a specific performance C a related form of an earlier object D an example from a variety of types

explanation.text: The modern oboe developed from the older zurna and serves as its present-day counterpart. In this context, a “version” is therefore a related form of an earlier object.

questions.alt: Question 32. How is paragraph 10 mmaaiinnllyy structured? 32 A by comparing and contrasting ideas B by presenting events in time order C by stating causes and effects D by detailing solutions to problems

explanation.text: The paragraph traces the zurna from its origins in Islamic regions through Arab expansion and early military use to later ceremonies and present-day folk music. This history is presented in time order.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which quotation best conveys a central idea of the article? A “Many of the instruments we know and play today, like the piano, the guitar, and the oboe, evolved from these older instruments.” (paragraph 1) B “The fact that marimba songs were well liked by Americans shows the increased exposure the public had to foreign styles of music.’ (paragraph 4) Cc “During the Great Depression, people couldn’ t afford store-bought instruments so they made their own.” (paragraph 8) D “Whistling is a way to use your vocal cords as a musical instrument” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The article repeatedly traces familiar instruments to older forms: the piano to dulcimers, the guitar to the oud and lute, and the oboe to the zurna. Therefore, the quotation about modern instruments evolving from older ones unifies those examples.

questions.alt: Question 34. What point of view is the author most likely expressing in paragraph 11? A Musical instruments should be made available to everyone. B The ability to make music is widespread. Cc Everyone should involve themselves with music. D Listening to music brings great joy.

explanation.text: The author ends by explaining that anyone without an instrument can whistle and carry a song anywhere. Therefore, this accessible example expresses the view that the ability to create music is widespread.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which sentence would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A Many musical instruments have been made by using simple available materials. B Percussion instruments make sounds when struck, shaken, or scraped. Cc Guitars are used by Hawaiian, country, rock, and bluegrass musicians. D The zurna was first used in military bands and to greet important persons.

explanation.text: Across the article, people make music with strings, gourds, animal skin, metal objects, hammers, and even their own voices. Therefore, the broad idea that instruments can use simple available materials belongs in a summary.

questions.alt: Question 8. Which detail bbeesstt reflects the narrator’s point of view of her mother and her beadwork? 8 A comparing the mother to an artist arranging paints upon a palette B describing the mother drawing a blade to trim the buckskin C mentioning the mother making moccasins for her daughter D describing the mother picking up the tiny beads one at a time

explanation.text: Comparing Mother's careful bead arrangement to an artist's palette presents her craft as creative art. Together with the narrator's proud, beaming attention, that comparison therefore reflects admiration for her mother and the work.

questions.alt: Question 9. What similarity is shared between the events in paragraph 3 and the events in paragraph 4? 9 A Both paragraphs emphasize the value of honoring family traditions. B Both paragraphs describe the ability to use tools with precision. C Both paragraphs demonstrate how teachers inspire others. D Both paragraphs highlight the joys of friendship and play.

explanation.text: In paragraph 3, Mother precisely uses an awl, thread, and tiny beads; in paragraph 4, the children use sharpened rods to pry roots and carefully gather gum drops. Both passages show skillful use of tools.

questions.alt: Question 10. The central idea of paragraph 4 is A children enjoy eating sweet things B the narrator is a leader among her friends Cc children are able to make their own fun D the setting has many natural resources

explanation.text: The children roam the hills, search for roots and drops of gum, and turn whatever they find outdoors into an afternoon's entertainment. These details show that they can create their own fun.

questions.alt: Question 11. What does the narrator’s frequent use of the word “we” in paragraphs 4 and 5 suggest? 11 A youthfulness B dependence C companionship D remembrance

explanation.text: The repeated “we” and “our” describe children roaming, gathering treats, exchanging clothing, and pretending together. That group language therefore emphasizes companionship among the narrator and her friends.

questions.alt: Question 12. What is the meaning of “discourse” as used in paragraph 6? 12 A brief remark B spoken story C response to someone D disagreement between people

explanation.text: One child tells a heroic deed while the others listen, respond, and urge the speaker onward. Because the “discourse” is the account being told aloud, the word means a spoken story.

questions.alt: Question 13. How do paragraphs 8 and 9 contrast with the paragraphs right before and after them? A They provide a look at the narrator’s imagination. B They demonstrate the narrator’s desire for independence. C They emphasize the narrator’s physical abilities. D They focus attention on the narrator’s selfish nature.

explanation.text: Because the surrounding paragraphs describe shared games while paragraphs 8 and 9 focus on the narrator's private notion that she can outrun her own shadow, the episode gives a special look at her imagination.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which sentence best expresses a central idea of the passage? A The narrator is interested in her mother’s beadwork on small moccasins. B After beadwork lessons with her mother, the narrator loves to play outdoors. C The narrator’s friends try to help her catch her shadow while playing. D When running through the fields, the narrator and her friends make lots of noise.

explanation.text: The passage moves from close beadwork instruction with Mother to the narrator's joyous release into outdoor play with friends. Therefore, that relationship between learning at home and freedom outside is broader than any single detail.

questions.alt: Question 22. What does the phrase “cold, white light” in paragraph 3 suggest about Chibi? 22 A She prefers to be outdoors during the day. B She demands fast responses from humans. C She struggles to survive outside in the winter. D She is indifferent to the feelings of humans.

explanation.text: Chibi allows people to look but slips away as soon as they try to touch her, rejecting contact “at all costs.” Comparing that rejection to cold light suggests indifference to a person's wish for affection.

questions.alt: Question 23. What is the meaning of the word “subtle” as it is used in paragraph 5? 23 A environmental B slight C powerful D rapid

explanation.text: Chibi reacts to changes in wind and light that humans cannot even detect. Since those changes are barely noticeable, “subtle” means slight rather than powerful or rapid.

questions.alt: Question 24. How does the narrator’s point of view shift in paragraphs 7 and 8? A He grows concerned the landlady will discover the cat. B He starts to think about keeping the cat. Cc He becomes worried for the safety of the cat. D He begins to feel affection toward the cat.

explanation.text: Chibi's sleeping on the sofa brings the narrator deep happiness, and he soon regards her as better than every cat he sees elsewhere. These reactions show his shift from uncertainty around cats to affection for Chibi.

questions.alt: Question 25. In paragraph 8, why does the narrator say he “had begun little by little to understand 25 cat lovers”? A He realizes that most cats are probably like Chibi. B He decides to buy a cat calendar to celebrate Chibi. C He feels a sense of contentment when Chibi visits. D He is in awe of the boy who runs around outside with Chibi.

explanation.text: When Chibi plays, rests, and sleeps in the house, her presence gives the narrator a “deep sense of happiness.” That contentment explains why he begins to understand people's love of cats.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which activity first leads Chibi to engage with the narrator and his wife? 26 A sleeping on the sofa B staying away from the landlady C wanting to play ball D being called by a nickname

explanation.text: Chibi first begins visiting on her own because she loves ball play, repeatedly entering and turning toward the garden until the couple follows. Her desire to play therefore initiates their interaction.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which quotation best expresses a central idea of the story? A “The cat’s name was Chibi, which means ‘little one.’ ” (paragraph 1) B “The cat’s manner of rejection was like cold, white light” (paragraph 3) Cc “Having played to her heart’s content, Chibi would come inside and rest for a while.” (paragraph 7) D “But, though I had started to think of her as the best cat around, she was not really our cat.” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The narrator comes to consider Chibi the best cat even while recognizing that she belongs elsewhere. That tension therefore expresses the central idea of a guest cat entering their lives without becoming their pet.

questions.alt: Question 28. Which statement would be mmoosstt important to include in a summary of the story? 28 A Chibi looks especially slim and tiny for a cat. B Chibi grows comfortable with the narrator and his wife. C Chibi avoids being touched by humans. D Chibi rarely makes a sound except for the tinkling of her bell.

explanation.text: Chibi changes from avoiding human contact to seeking play, resting on the sofa, and entering through the window whenever she wishes. Therefore, her growing comfort with the couple captures the story's main development.

questions.alt: Question 29. In paragraph 1, what does the phrase “lurches along its tracks—then takes a heart-stopping 29 plunge” suggest about the Cyclone roller coaster? A It moves at an unexpected rate of speed. B It provides a thrilling experience for riders. C It appeals to some visitors but not others. D It presents a safety hazard for riders.

explanation.text: The Cyclone lurches, suddenly plunges, and makes riders whoop and scream with excitement. Those physical and emotional reactions show that the roller coaster gives riders a thrilling experience.

questions.alt: Question 30. Why was George Ferris’s wheel considered an “engineering marvel” (paragraph 4)? A It became the most popular attraction at the exposition. B It was the fastest ride in the Midway section of the exposition. C It gave people the opportunity to see the capabilities of new technology. D It was something that other amusement park owners tried to bring to their parks.

explanation.text: Ferris's 264-foot spinning wheel became a spectacular demonstration of what the new machine age could accomplish. It was an engineering marvel because visitors could directly experience the capabilities of new technology.

questions.alt: Question 31. How did Tilyou’s visit to the World’s Columbian Exposition affect the property at 31 Coney Island? A Tilyou was motivated to build a park with amusement rides. B Tilyou brought George Ferris’s giant wheel to Coney Island. C Tilyou created multiple attractions based on different themes. D Tilyou decided to construct an electric railway to Coney Island.

explanation.text: At the exposition, Tilyou recognizes the potential of gathering rides, food, and shows in one entertainment area. That inspiration leads him to turn his Coney Island property into an enclosed amusement park.

questions.alt: Question 32. the use of quotation marks around the word “spaceship” in paragraph 6 suggests that A __ the spaceship is a form of transportation for the ride B the vehicle that the visitors ride looks like a spaceship C the park has an old spaceship converted into a ride D the spaceship resembles a similar attraction at another park

explanation.text: Visitors climb into a vehicle for an amusement called Trip to the Moon; they are not entering an actual spacecraft. The quotation marks therefore indicate a ride vehicle designed to look like a spaceship.

questions.alt: Question 33. Based on paragraphs 10 and 11, what is the author’s point of view? A Newer amusement parks offer families more fun than Coney Island. B Coney Island should be modernized in order to keep attracting visitors. C__Ithas cost too much money to restore Coney Island after recent difficulties. Y Y D Coney Island remains an exciting destination for visitors after many years.

explanation.text: Although fires, age, and Superstorm Sandy destroyed attractions, Coney Island survived and still offers new generations entertainment and thrills. These details show the author's view that it remains an exciting destination.

questions.alt: Question 34. Read this sentence from paragraph 11. And from New Jersey’s Wildwood Park to California’s Pacific Park and Florida’s ‘Walt Disney World, other amusement and theme parks have taken their cues from Coney Island. Which quotation best supports this claim? “The inspiration for amusement parks such as Coney Island sprang from an A __ exciting event in 1893—the World’s Columbian Exposition in Chicago” (paragraph 2) B “When he was unable to buy Ferris’s wheel, he had his own version built” (paragraph 6) Cc “Attractions that are so familiar today—roller coasters, water rides, fun houses, Tunnels of Love—all got their start there” (paragraph 9) D “They have grown to epic proportions in their efforts to offer a world full of fun.” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The claim says later parks followed Coney Island's example. The statement that familiar roller coasters, water rides, fun houses, and Tunnels of Love all began there provides direct evidence of that influence.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which statement expresses a central idea developed throughout the article? A The Coney Island amusement park was initially separated by different types of entertainment. B The World’s Columbian Exposition had many elements that were later used in the Coney Island amusement park. Cc The Coney Island amusement park was influenced by previous attractions and eventually impacted the modern amusement park. D The invention of the electric railway was important to the popularity of the Coney Island amusement park.

explanation.text: The exposition's Midway inspires Tilyou to develop Coney Island, and Coney Island then becomes the model for amusement parks across the country. This two-way chain of influence is therefore developed throughout the article.

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 1-6. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.
Official passage for Questions 7-12. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 13-18. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. What does the word “crouched” mean as it is used in paragraph 6? 1 A spun around B bent down C stood up D fell over

explanation.text: Riley crouches next to Craig so that he can climb onto her back for a ride. She must bend down to his level, so "crouched" means bent down.

questions.alt: Question 2. In paragraph 9, what is the most likely reason Riley stays quiet as she and Craig walk 2 home? A She is thinking about how well she played hockey. B She is upset with Craig because he hurt her back. C She is thinking about how to get skates for Craig. D She is tired from skating in the hockey game.

explanation.text: Craig has just said that he needs skates in order to fly, and Riley says nothing on the walk home. A few days later she brings him her old skates, which shows that she had likely been thinking about how to get skates for him.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does paragraph 12 help the reader understand about Craig? 3 A Craig is too young to learn how to skate. B Craig is very excited about learning to skate. C Craig is unable to take the skates off by himself. D Craig is worried that his sister will take the skates back.

explanation.text: Once the newspaper makes the skates fit, Craig cannot stop smiling and does not want to take them off even for the walk to the lake. These reactions show that he is very excited to learn how to skate.

questions.alt: Question 4. In paragraph 16, what does the phrase “like being a snail” help the reader to understand 4 about Craig? A He skates very slowly. B He moves in a crooked line. C He searches for a place to hide. D He looks like all the other skaters.

explanation.text: Craig inches forward behind the chair, and his blades make a scratching sound instead of his sister's quick swish. Comparing himself to a snail therefore emphasizes how slowly he is skating.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which sentence best describes how paragraph 6 relates to paragraph 23? 5 Paragraph 6 provides a problem and paragraph 23 provides a A solution. B Paragraph 6 asks a question and paragraph 23 provides an answer. C Paragraph 6 provides a cause and paragraph 23 shows an effect. D Paragraph 6 provides similarities and paragraph 23 shows differences.

explanation.text: In paragraph 6, carrying Craig makes Riley too slow, so the piggyback ride does not give him the feeling of flight. In paragraph 23, Riley and Liz solve that problem by pulling him fast across the ice on his own skates.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which sentence best describes a central message of the story? A Change is normal and an important part of life. B_ Friendships often become stronger over time. C New experiences can be exciting and wonderful. D Natural talent is more important than practice.

explanation.text: Craig's first attempt at skating is difficult, but help from Riley and Liz finally lets him race over the ice and feel as if he is flying. His joyful new experience supports the message that trying something new can be exciting and wonderful.

questions.alt: Question 7. According to paragraph 5, what happens right before thunder can be heard? A Little drops of water move around in the sky. B Dark clouds appear in the sky. C Rain droplets start to fall from the clouds. D The air spreads because of heat from lightning.

explanation.text: Paragraph 5 says lightning heats the air and causes it to expand quickly. That expanding air then creates the booming sound of thunder, so it happens immediately before thunder is heard.

questions.alt: Question 8. What is the main idea of paragraph 6? 8 A Lightning can reach from the sky to the ground. B A bolt of lightning can travel up to nine miles. C Flashes of lightning can jump from one cloud to another. D Lightning can move over large distances very quickly.

explanation.text: Paragraph 6 says lightning flashes faster than a blink and can travel miles from ground to cloud or between clouds. These details support the main idea that lightning moves very quickly across long distances.

questions.alt: Question 9. Which idea from the passage does the second illustration best support? A Lightning can be helpful for nature. B Lightning moves very quickly. C Lightning appears in different ways in the sky. D Lightning may strike before or after it rains.

explanation.text: The second illustration shows four labeled forms: forked, ribbon, heat, and sheet lightning. Those different shapes and settings support the idea that lightning can appear in several ways across the sky.

questions.alt: Question 10. What does the word “nutrient” mean as it is used in paragraph 10? 10 A a type of lightning B a supply of heat C a kind of plant D a form of food

explanation.text: The passage directly explains that nitrogen is a nutrient because it feeds plants and helps them grow. That context shows that a "nutrient" is a form of food or nourishment.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which question does the section “Fast Facts” help to answer? 11 A How does lightning help the earth? B How is lightning different in the summer? C How is electricity created in clouds? D How can someone avoid an electrical shock?

explanation.text: The "Fast Facts" section says lightning places nitrogen in the ground and air, where it feeds plants and helps them grow. It answers how lightning helps the earth because it identifies a useful effect on plants.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which sentence shows a cause and effect relationship that is stated in the passage? 12 A People see lightning before they hear thunder. B Wildfires can start when lightning touches the ground. C Heat from electricity is hotter than the surface of the sun. D Lightning bolts can hit tall trees and buildings.

explanation.text: The passage states that when lightning strikes a tree or dry grass, a wildfire can begin. This is a cause-and-effect relationship because the lightning strike causes the fire to start.

questions.alt: Question 13. What does the word “clamped” mean as it is used in paragraph 4? 13 A smelled B lowered C moved D grabbed

explanation.text: The crab closes its large claw around the bait and holds on while the narrator raises the string. In this context, "clamped" means grabbed tightly.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which detail from the story best shows the narrator’s point of view about catching crabs for supper? A “My clothes were already sticking to me, and sweat trickled down our faces.” (paragraph 7) B “This smells disgusting. How can they eat this?” (paragraph 8) Cc “... cracking the shells and pulling out the sweet white meat. We had to keep trying” (paragraph 11) “... but that crab was all claws and those mad pincers were waving all D » over the place!” (paragraph 15)

explanation.text: The narrator imagines cracking shells and eating the crabs' sweet white meat, then says, "We had to keep trying." This thought best reveals that catching crabs for supper is important and worth the effort to the narrator.

questions.alt: Question 15. Which detail from the story best shows why Paulie and the narrator keep trying to catch 15 a crab? “Crabs love stinky old fish, so our first crab didn’t take long to come.” A (paragraph 4) “ ‘Crabs are scaredy-cats,’ I said, pulling up the line to check the bait.” B (paragraph 8) C “I could tell he was trying not to cry.” (paragraph 19) D “We both thought about broccoli casserole.” (paragraph 21)

explanation.text: Paulie and the narrator both dislike broccoli casserole, which is what they expect to eat if they return without crabs. They keep trying because catching crabs will let them avoid that unwanted meal.

questions.alt: Question 16. What happens right before Paulie and the narrator reach the porch in paragraph 30? A They learn that they will have broccoli casserole for dinner. B They hand a bucket of crabs to their father. C They find out that something is moving in the bucket. D They hear their mother calling them to come home.

explanation.text: Before running to the porch, Paulie and the narrator hear scratching and other noises coming from the bait bucket. They go toward the light because they realize that something is moving inside it.

questions.alt: Question 17. How does paragraph 31 connect to the problem in paragraph 26? 17 A It shows the solution to the problem. B It gives an effect of the problem. C It explains the cause of the problem. D It gives an example of the problem.

explanation.text: In paragraph 26, the children think their failed crabbing trip means they must eat broccoli casserole. Paragraph 31 solves that problem because the spare-bait bucket unexpectedly contains a large pile of crabs.

questions.alt: Question 18. What best states the lesson of paragraphs 27 through 31? 18 A Hard work and a strong will can solve almost any problem. B Good things can happen even when all hope seems lost. C Staying calm is important when faced with a problem. D Working with and trusting others can help to finish any job.

explanation.text: The children believe their chance for a crab dinner is gone and start walking home disappointed. Their unexpected discovery of many crabs shows that something good can happen even when hope seems lost.

Official passage for Questions 13-18. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. According to the story, what does it mean when Jem says, “I am sentenced to life in space” (paragraph 6)? A _ She cannot use the video system on the station. B She must wait for her friend to arrive at the station. C She must stay on the station in order to remain healthy. D She cannot leave the station because of the magnetic storm.

explanation.text: Jem explains that low gravity left her bones too thin and her muscles too weak to survive on Earth. Therefore, being “sentenced to life in space” means she must remain on the station to stay healthy.

questions.alt: Question 2. Read this sentence from paragraph 14. The tension stops me in the doorway like a force field. What does the word “tension” mean as it is used in this sentence? A _ excitement B_ disappointment C - surprise D_ worry

explanation.text: The shuttle cannot see to dock, and lowering its shields would endanger everyone aboard. That crisis fills the command room with worry, so “tension” means worry in this sentence.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does paragraph 27 reveal about Jem’s father? A He does not think that Jem understands the problem. B He does not want Jem to be in the command room. C Hes surprised that Jem can communicate with the shuttle. D He thinks it is wrong for Jem to use the S.A.T. booth.

explanation.text: Jem's father immediately asks whether she is in touch with the shuttle and rushes her to the S.A.T. booth. His urgent question shows that he is surprised she can communicate with it.

questions.alt: Question 4. How does Jem cause the events that happen in paragraph 28? A Jem tells the crew how to get information to the shuttle. B Jem becomes upset when the shuttle cannot dock. C Jem asks if the shields on the shuttle can be lowered. D Jem leaves her lessons because the shuttle is arriving soon.

explanation.text: Jem reveals that the old S.A.T. still works and gives a technician her code. Because she shows the crew how to contact the shuttle, they can send the docking coordinates in paragraph 28.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which quotation from the story identifies the setting? A “The announcement drones on, but I don’t listen.” (paragraph 4) B “Eleven years ago, my birth on Space Station Alpha made history.’ (paragraph 5) C “They'll have to try docking by sight.” (paragraph 15) D “T give one tech my code and watch her fingers fly over the keyboard” (paragraph 28)

explanation.text: The statement that Jem was born on Space Station Alpha directly names the place where the story occurs. The other quotations describe actions but do not identify the setting.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which quotation best supports a theme of the story? A “But new studies show that older children wont be harmed by a short stay.’ (paragraph 7) B “Spending 11 years on the station has its advantages.” (paragraph 8) C “If the holo-video worked, we could talk them in.” (paragraph 21) D “Messages flash back and forth as techs feed docking coordinates to the shuttle.” (paragraph 28)

explanation.text: Jem's eleven years aboard the station gave her knowledge of its forgotten S.A.T. system. Calling those years an advantage supports the theme that an unusual experience can become a valuable strength.

questions.alt: Question 7. What does the word “conservation” mean as it is used in paragraph 6? A action B education C preparation D protection

explanation.text: The programs described just before this word are designed to save snow leopards while helping herders. Since their purpose is to protect the cats, “conservation” means protection.

questions.alt: Question 8. g How does paragraph 9 connect to paragraph 6 in the article? A by describing a method for counting snow leopards B by describing what it is like to see a snow leopard C _ by explaining why snow leopards are rarely seen by humans D _byexplaining how scientists identify individual snow leopards

explanation.text: Paragraph 6 says researchers need more reliable leopard counts. Paragraph 9 then describes motion-and-heat-sensitive camera traps, therefore providing one method for obtaining those counts.

questions.alt: Question 9. g Which idea best explains why Dr. McCarthy and his co-workers traveled to Kyrgyzstan? A “The loss of habitat has caused a food shortage.” (paragraph 4) B “But they need more reliable ways to count leopards before they will know.’ (paragraph 6) C “They saw no leopards, but they hadn't expected to.” (paragraph 7) D “Researchers compared patterns in the photos to identify cats.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: Paragraph 6 says scientists cannot judge whether conservation is working without reliable leopard counts. That need explains why McCarthy's team traveled to Kyrgyzstan to test counting methods.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which idea from the article best supports the main idea? A “The snow leopard is smaller than the tiger, the lion, and the leopard of Africa and Asia.’ (paragraph 2) B “Researchers estimate that only 3,500 to 7,500 snow leopards are alive today.” (paragraph 6) C “Each snow leopard’s spot pattern is different.” (paragraph 10) D “The cameras had taken photos of 15 different snow leopards at two study sites.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The estimate of only 3,500 to 7,500 surviving snow leopards shows how threatened the species is. This evidence supports the article's main focus on why the cats need protection.

questions.alt: Question 11. Howis the article mainly organized? A compare and contrast P B sequence of events q C question then answer D cause and effect

explanation.text: The article repeatedly links causes to results: habitat loss reduces prey, hungry leopards attack livestock, and herders then kill them. It also explains how protection programs can interrupt that chain.

questions.alt: Question 12. How does the table at the end of “Saving Snow Leopards” support the main idea of the article? A __byshowing reasons why snow leopards are struggling to survive B by listing ways to better protect snow leopards C by presenting new information about the habitat of snow leopards D by providing evidence that there are fewer snow leopards alive now than in the past

explanation.text: The table gathers threats such as illegal hunting, habitat and prey loss, killings by herders, and weak protection. These causes show why snow leopards are struggling to survive.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which detail from paragraph 1 best supports a theme of the story? A The folktale takes place long ago. B_ Kanikiya likes to wear a scarlet ribbon. C The brolgas are birds that live along the river. D Kanikiya has a talent that is recognized by others.

explanation.text: People compare Kanikiya's graceful dancing to moonlight on a running river. Their admiration shows that others recognize her special talent, an idea that matters throughout the folktale.

questions.alt: Question 14. Read this sentence from paragraph 6. They understand my sorrow, Kanikiya thought, the knowledge warming her. What does the phrase “the knowledge warming her” suggest? A afeeling of comfort B sadness and disappointment C safeeling of anger D wisdom and clear thinking

explanation.text: Kanikiya believes the brolgas' mournful dance shows that they understand her sorrow. Feeling understood warms her emotionally, so the phrase suggests comfort rather than anger or wisdom.

questions.alt: Question 15. What does the word “mournful” mean as it is used in paragraph 7? A angry B bored C _ nervous D sad

explanation.text: After Kanikiya is kept from dancing, she cannot eat or smile and feels a growing heaviness in her heart. Those details show that the “mournful” days are sad ones.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which sentence from the story best shows how a character’s actions help to develop the story? A “You must not leave the camp until the brolgas have left.” (paragraph 6) B “She wove baskets from the reeds the other children collected” (paragraph 7) Cc “Slipping out of the camp, she rushed down to the riverbank to dance with the cranes.’ (paragraph 8) “Her mother looked for her throughout the camp and, not finding her D oe” there, searched near the river” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Kanikiya's decision to slip away and dance with the cranes causes her disappearance and transformation. Because that action creates the story's climax, it most clearly develops the plot.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which statement best describes how Kanikiya changes from the beginning to the end of the story? A She realizes that she must leave her home to find happiness. B She learns that the most important thing is to obey the rules. C She discovers that the birds love her more than she loves them. D She is frightened by camp tales and then sees that they are untrue.

explanation.text: Being confined to camp makes Kanikiya weak and miserable, while dancing with the cranes promises peace. By the end, she leaves the camp and becomes a crane so she can live in happiness.

questions.alt: Question 18. 1g Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A _ Kanikiya is often seen near the river by others. B_Kanikiya’s mother tells her a story about a girl. C Kanikiya feels like she must dance. D _Kanikiya weaves baskets.

explanation.text: Kanikiya's feeling that she must dance drives the central conflict with her mother and ultimately leads her to join the cranes. Therefore, that motivation is essential to a summary of the whole story.
