questions.alt: Question 1. In paragraph 5, what does the phrase “held our breath” suggest about the narrator and Josh? A They are sneaking around the house to avoid being seen by Mother. B They are feeling thankful that Aunt Marjorie has left. C they are waiting anxiously to see what Mother will do next. D They are trying to avoid talking about The Hat because it is upsetting.

explanation.text: After Aunt Marjorie leaves, the children know Mother's thrift and fashion sense are in conflict over the hat. They “held their breath” because they anxiously awaited her decision.

questions.alt: Question 2. How does paragraph 5 contribute to the story’s structure? 2 A It foreshadows the resolution of the story. B It develops the narrator’s feelings about Mother. C It explains Mother’s problem in detail. D It gives background details that explain the story.

explanation.text: Paragraph 5 explains the story's central problem: Mother is too thrifty to discard the nearly new hat but cannot stand wearing it. This conflict motivates her later attempts to reuse it.

questions.alt: Question 3. Which quotation best supports a theme of the story? A “My mother believed in using things up.” (paragraph 1) B “We looked at each other, then scouted the house.” (paragraph 6) C “It wasn’t hard to spot.” (paragraph 6) D “Then we glanced over at the lamp” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: Mother's belief in using things up guides every attempt to find a purpose for the unwanted hat, including the successful scarecrow. The quotation therefore supports a theme of resourceful reuse.

questions.alt: Question 4. In paragraphs 8 and 9, how does the narrator’s point of view influence the description of events? A The narrator thinks the events are funny, so she describes them in a humorous way. B The narrator thinks the events are boring, so she describes them in a dull way. Cc The narrator is confused by the events, so she describes them in a mysterious way. D The narrator is happy about the events, so she describes them in a joyful way.

explanation.text: The narrator exaggerates Pete dropping “like a stone,” suggests mouth-to-beak resuscitation, and compares the glowing flowers to glaring eyes. These playful details show that she views the mishap as funny.

questions.alt: Question 5. Read this sentence from paragraph 12. 5 It was as though time stood still. Which idea does the sentence develop? A Everyone was pleased. B Nobody moved or spoke. C Nobody wanted to leave. D Everyone was late.

explanation.text: Josh has just declared that the hat is not for the birds while Mother stands with one foot raised. The pause before she laughs makes the moment feel frozen because nobody moves or speaks.

questions.alt: Question 6. What can the reader infer from the details in paragraphs 17 and 18 of the story? A Mother worries that the crows will being afraid of The Hat and changes to a new hat every June. B Mother is concerned about the crows and gives them one month each year to eat what is in the cornfield. Cc Mother spends each June enjoying time with Aunt Marjorie and does not do much work in the cornfield. D Mother removes The Hat from the cornfield in June so that Aunt Marjorie’s feelings will not be hurt.

explanation.text: The hat keeps birds away from the cornfield, but Great-aunt Marjorie, who gave Mother the hat, visits every June. Crows appearing only then implies Mother removes it so Marjorie will not see its use and feel hurt.

questions.alt: Question 7. How are the narrator and Josh alike? A They both make comments that cause their mother to have new ideas. B They both are interested in watching their mother solve her problem. C They both contribute their old items to build the scarecrow for the field. D They both joke about how their dog is more attractive than the hat.

explanation.text: Both children wait to see what Mother will do, search for the missing hat, and watch as she considers destroying it. These shared actions show their interest in how she will solve her problem.

questions.alt: Question 8. Read this sentence from paragraph 1 of the article. 8 In the days when farmers worked with ox and sled and cut the dark with lantern light, there lived a boy who loved snow more than anything in the world. How does the author’s word choice in the sentence affect the meaning of the passage? A by suggesting that the ideas in the passage are made up B by showing that the subject of the passage became famous C by suggesting that the topic of the passage is familiar D by showing that the events in the passage happened long ago

explanation.text: Oxen, sleds, and lantern light are presented as ordinary farm tools in Bentley's childhood. Those historical details therefore signal that the events occurred long before modern transportation and electric lighting.

questions.alt: Question 9. What is the meaning of the word “pelted” as it is used in paragraph 3? 9 A created B found C saved D threw

explanation.text: The children are said to have “pelted snowballs at roosting crows.” Since snowballs are objects they would toss at the birds, “pelted” means threw.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which quotation best supports a main idea of the article? 10 A “He expected to find whole flakes that were the same . . .” (paragraph 4) B “ ‘Fussing with snow is just foolishness,’ his father said.” (paragraph 6) C “Even so his first pictures were failures . . .” (paragraph 7) “ ‘Now everyone can see the great beauty in a tiny crystal,’ he said.” D (paragraph 7)

explanation.text: Bentley works for years to preserve the beauty he sees in snowflakes so other people can see it too. His statement that “now everyone can see” directly expresses that central purpose.

questions.alt: Question 11. What does the information in paragraph 9 suggest about the author’s point of view? A The author believes that Bentley could have been more careful. B The author respects Bentley’s many different interests. C The author admires Bentley’s dedication. D_ The author questions the methods Bentley used.

explanation.text: Paragraph 9 describes Bentley waiting for hours in the cold, handling fragile crystals without breathing or twitching, and trying again after failures. This care shows the author's admiration for his dedication.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which statement is true based on the information in paragraphs 6 and 11? 12 Bentley’s work with snow required expensive equipment that he was willing A to spend all his money on. Bentley was thought to be foolish throughout his life because of his interest B in snow. Bentley’s parents thought he should do something with his life other than C taking pictures of snow. Bentley became less interested in studying snow than in publishing pictures D and giving speeches.

explanation.text: Bentley's parents spend savings equal to the value of ten cows on his camera, and later he spends every penny on his pictures. Together, these details show that his work required costly equipment he willingly funded.

questions.alt: Question 13. What does the reader learn about Bentley from paragraphs 10 and 11? 13 A He was more interested in sharing his work than in making money from it. B He worked hard to develop a way of making photographs of snowflakes. C He wanted to find out if all snowflakes were different from each other. He was able to follow his interests because of the help he got from his D family.

explanation.text: Bentley gives photographs away, sells them for only a few cents, holds slide shows, and shares his findings in articles and speeches. These choices show he values sharing the work more than profit.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which sentence best describes how the article is organized? A The reasons for Willie Bentley’s experiments with snow are presented, followed by their eventual conclusions. B The events of Willie Bentley’s life and his study of snow are described as they happened over time. Cc The different problems of photographing snow are explained and then Willie Bentley’s solutions are described. The important ideas about snow in Willie Bentley’s discoveries are D ; presented, followed by details and examples.

explanation.text: The account moves from Bentley's childhood observations to his teenage experiments and then to his recognition as a snow expert. Therefore, the progression organizes his life and study in time order.

questions.alt: Question 15. How do paragraphs 1 and 23 relate to each other? A They show the change in Gregor’s life during the story. B They show what Gregor has learned in the story. C They show how MacTavish changes in the story. D They show the growth of MacTavish’s fortune during the story.

explanation.text: Paragraph 1 introduces Gregor as a poor tenant farmer unable to afford the flock he wants. By paragraph 23 he owns a huge flock and shares a fortune, so the two paragraphs frame the change in his life.

questions.alt: Question 16. What does the phrase “marched back” in paragraph 14 suggest about MacTavish? 16 A He is confused. B He is worried. C He is determined. D He is excited.

explanation.text: MacTavish has begun to leave, but he turns and “marches back” when Gregor refuses to sell, then offers to pay anything. His forceful return shows his determination to obtain the sheep.

questions.alt: Question 17. Howare Gregor and the man who sold the sheep to him similar? A They are both unskilled at selling things to people. B_ They both try to trick someone in order to make money. C They are both concerned with helping their family and neighbors. D They both believe that animals can have special qualities.

explanation.text: The seller persuades Gregor that a sickly sheep can create wealth, while Gregor spreads the same claim to lure MacTavish. Both men therefore use a trick about the sheep to make money.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which of Gregor’s actions shows how he is different from MacTavish? 18 A Gregor cheats another person. B Gregor buys a sickly sheep. C Gregor shares his wealth. D Gregor moves to a big house.

explanation.text: After becoming rich, Gregor shares his remaining fortune with families MacTavish had cheated. Therefore, his generosity distinguishes him from MacTavish, who withheld earnings and kept wealth for himself.

questions.alt: Question 19. Which sentence is true about Gregor and MacTavish? 19 MacTavish has a plan for how the sheep will make him rich, but Gregor A does not. B MacTavish wants to own big houses and many jewels, but Gregor does not. C Gregor wants to move away to another land, but MacTavish does not. D Gregor is generous with his family and his neighbors, but MacTavish is not.

explanation.text: Gregor uses his new wealth to help his mother and the poor families of the valley. MacTavish, in contrast, cheats those families and hoards houses and gems, showing that Gregor is the generous one.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which sentence expresses a theme of the story? 20 A Big loss can come from being greedy. B Family can make hard times seem easier. C Wealth may come from hard work. D Appreciating others can lead to happiness.

explanation.text: MacTavish's desire to own anything makes him trade his mansion and all its contents for a supposedly magical sheep. Because his greed costs him that fortune, the story shows that greed can bring a great loss.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A ___ Gregor goes to the market and talks to many farmers about their sheep. B _ Gregor’s mother is asleep when Gregor takes money to buy the sheep. Cc MacTavish lives at another one of his houses after he buys the sheep from Gregor. D MacTavish goes to buy Gregor’s sheep after he hears rumors about the animal.

explanation.text: Because hearing rumors about a wealth-producing sheep draws MacTavish to Gregor and begins the bargain that transforms Gregor's life, that turning point belongs in a summary more than minor details.

questions.alt: Question 22. What idea is developed in paragraphs 4 through 7? 22 A Wangari and her mother want to plant more trees. B Mugumo trees are important to people and animals. C Mugumo trees can provide shade to many people. D Wangari and her mother think education is important.

explanation.text: The mugumo provides food and a home for animals, holds the spirits of Kikuyu ancestors, and inspires Wangari's promise to protect it. These details therefore develop its importance to both people and animals.

questions.alt: Question 23. Read this sentence from paragraph 8 of the article. 23 Her mind was like a seed rooted in rich soil, ready to grow. What does the sentence help the reader to understand about Wangari? A She likes to think about plants. B She wants to keep learning. C She imagines ways to help others. D She believes in working together.

explanation.text: A seed in rich soil is prepared to keep growing, just as Wangari's mind is ready to develop through more education. The simile therefore shows that she wants to continue learning.

questions.alt: Question 24. How are the details in paragraphs 13 and 14 organized? A asa description of how animal habitats changed Basan explanation of the solution to a problem in the environment C —asacomparison of the village before and after the government sold the land D asa description of how a problem was caused in the area and its effects

explanation.text: Paragraph 13 describes companies cutting forests, and paragraph 14 traces the results: lost habitats, eroding soil, muddy rivers, and hunger. The details therefore move from an environmental cause to its effects.

questions.alt: Question 25. Paragraphs 17 and 18 explain that Wangari spread her idea by 25 A sharing it with women around the country B giving it the name Green Belt Movement C watching the land in Kenya turn green again D planting trees herself everywhere she went

explanation.text: Wangari carries seedlings and tools to villages, towns, and cities and urges mothers to plant with her. Therefore, traveling to women across Kenya is how she shares and spreads the idea nationwide.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which sentence most likely expresses Wangari’s point of view? 26 People can make the changes they want by working together with A determination. B People change their traditions and customs with each generation. C People cannot rely on the government to help them in a time of need. People in other countries do not need to work as hard on the same D problem.

explanation.text: Wangari calls on women to work together, travels throughout Kenya, and helps the Green Belt Movement plant thirty million trees. That sustained group effort shows her belief in cooperation and determination.

questions.alt: Question 27. How does the title of the article support a main idea? 27 A It describes advice Wangari followed. B It describes how Wangari solved a problem. C It explains how Wangari felt about trees. D It explains which values Wangari’s village held.

explanation.text: The “seeds of change” are the trees Wangari persuades people to plant. Those plantings restore forests, clean water, food, and habitats, so the title points to how she solves the environmental problem.

questions.alt: Question 28. Based on the information in the article, where did Wangari most likely get her idea for 28 planting trees across Kenya? A from the school she attended in the capital city B from the government of her country C from the women of the village where she grew up D from what her mother taught her as a girl

explanation.text: As a girl, Wangari's mother teaches her that the mugumo shelters and feeds many lives and must not be cut down. That early lesson connects directly to Wangari's later plan to restore Kenya by planting trees.

Official passage for Questions 22-28. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. What does paragraph 5 reveal about Paige? A _ She fears going out on the lake. B She wants to avoid her grandparents. Cc She prefers the outdoors to coming inside. D _ She wants to play with the neighborhood girls.

explanation.text: When her mother announces that the grandparents have arrived, Paige says “Rats” and returns slowly with her head down. Her disappointed reaction shows that she had wanted to avoid seeing them and going fishing.

questions.alt: Question 2. How do paragraphs 8 through 10 develop the plot of the story? A They give background information about Paige. B_ They illustrate Paige's internal conflict. C They explain why Paige admires her Grandpa. D They show how Paige and her brothers are alike.

explanation.text: Grandpa assumes Paige loves riding in the fishing boat, while Paige privately says he is wrong and that she hates it. This contrast reveals her internal conflict between her own wishes and pleasing him.

questions.alt: Question 3. Read the sentence from paragraph 14 below. Across from the kitchenette stood the bathroom, which contributed to the monster’s bad case of morning breath. What does the metaphor mean in the sentence? A __ The RV had a rotten smell. B People slept poorly inside the RV. C __ The RV wasa cramped place. D__ People made a mess inside the RV.

explanation.text: The bathroom is said to give the RV “morning breath,” comparing the vehicle to a person with bad breath. Along with the rotten food nearby, the metaphor means that the RV smells rotten.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which detail signals a change in the direction of the story? A __ Grandpa loans Paige a fishing pole. B_ Paige warns her family about the heat. Cc Grandpa and Grandma arrive in their RV. D Paige and the girls beat the boys in a water fight.

explanation.text: The grandparents' arrival in their RV interrupts Paige's water-fight victory and introduces the fishing boat she dreads. Therefore, that event redirects the story from neighborhood play to the unwanted fishing trip.

questions.alt: Question 5. How does the author most develop Grandpa's point of view in the story? A by having the narrator describe Grandpa B__bysharing Grandpa's thoughts with the reader C by including dialogue between Grandpa and the kids D__byshowing how Grandpa acts with Paige’s brothers

explanation.text: Grandpa's dialogue reveals that he believes it is never too hot to fish and that Paige loves the boat. His own words most directly develop his enthusiastic view of the outing.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A_ Paige loses a lot of fishing poles. B Grandpa owns many different hats. C Paige enjoys water-skiing and tubing. D Grandpa wants to take the kids fishing.

explanation.text: Grandpa's desire to take the children fishing launches the story's main conflict and determines the afternoon's events. Details about lost poles, hats, or Paige's favorite water sports are secondary.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which sentence best expresses the theme of the story? A People usually change as they grow older. B Sometimes people are embarrassed by family. Cc People often cherish their childhood memories. D__ Sometimes people make choices to please others.

explanation.text: Paige dreads fishing and privately hates the slow boat, yet she joins the trip because Grandpa eagerly wants to take the children. Her reluctant choice supports the theme that people sometimes act to please others.

questions.alt: Question 8. How does the author first introduce the idea of outdoor play or outdoor activity? A by inviting the reader to consider his or her own behavior B by providing data about changes in outdoor play C__bystating the opinion that outdoor play is good for health D_ by listing effects of too much time spent indoors

explanation.text: The article opens by asking readers to compare their own time outside with time spent online, gaming, or watching television. These direct questions therefore introduce the topic through the reader's behavior.

questions.alt: Question 9. How does paragraph 4 support the author's argument? A by describing the TV watching habits of children B_ by explaining reasons for the popularity of computer games C _byproviding solutions to the problems of indoor, nonmoving play D by suggesting that playing inside too much can cause children to become sick

explanation.text: Paragraph 4 links the rise in sedentary indoor activities to obesity, stress, and higher rates of physical and emotional illness. That evidence supports the argument by showing that excessive indoor play can harm health.

questions.alt: Question 10. What is the definition of “vital” as it is used in paragraph 6? A related B harmful C essential D unfamiliar

explanation.text: The author says outside light supports the immune system and helps people feel happier. Because it is necessary for those benefits, “vital” means essential.

questions.alt: Question 11. J] How does paragraph 13 develop a central claim of the article? A __ by describing how children are creative when playing outdoors B___ by emphasizing that children are physically healthier because of outdoor play C _ by explaining how children are interested in playing with friends the most D__byillustrating how children are aware of the benefits of the environment

explanation.text: Paragraph 13 explains that varied outdoor terrain challenges balance, coordination, and stamina and that enjoyable activity is easier to continue. These details support the claim that outdoor play improves physical health.

questions.alt: Question 12. How does the graph best support Richard Louv’s claim that people are “separated from nature” (paragraph 15)? A by showing that activities such as bird watching and camping are less popular than other activities B__ by showing that spending time with friends is the most popular activity c by showing that listening to music is an activity that happens as often as using electronic devices D___byshowing that many do not know how to bike, fish, or camp

explanation.text: The graph shows 84% choosing to hang out and 65.3% choosing music, movies, or devices, but only about 30% choosing wildlife or hiking activities. This gap supports the claim that people are separated from nature.

questions.alt: Question 13. How do paragraphs 16 through 18 contribute to the structure of the article? A The paragraphs describe a problem introduced earlier in the article. B The paragraphs contrast ways of accomplishing goals described in the article. C the paragraphs summarize the points made previously in the article. D The paragraphs give support to the main argument in the article.

explanation.text: These paragraphs explain that nature builds environmental awareness, can be found even in cities, and is worth choosing over another video-game session. Those points add further support for the article's pro-outdoor-play argument.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which quotation best expresses the central idea of the article? A “If youre like Casey and many other people, it’s likely that you spent more time inside four walls than outside in fresh air” (paragraph 4) B “Just being exposed to the great outdoors does wonders.” (paragraph 6) c “Tf you walk through the woods in search of cool birds and plants, you may find your mind expanding.” (paragraph 16) D “It’s not called the great outdoors for nothing.” (paragraph 18)

explanation.text: Because the article's sections describe sensory, physical, mental, and environmental benefits of going outside, the broad claim that the outdoors “does wonders” best captures them all.

questions.alt: Question 15. How do paragraphs 3 and 4 contribute to the story? A They help show Rakhee’s kindness and ease around creatures in the outdoors. B They help the reader understand Rakhee’s concern of whether her grandmother will remember her. C They provide evidence for Rakhee’s love of singing Indian songs as a child. D They give the reader insight into Rakhee’s memories of her grandmother.

explanation.text: Rakhee recalls only a vague image of her grandmother at first, then remembers their affectionate ant-song routine in detail. These paragraphs therefore give insight into her childhood memories of Muthashi.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which important idea does the author develop in paragraphs 7 through 9? A __ Rakhee has lived a very exciting life. B __Rakhee wishes she had grown up in India. Cc Rakhee has much to learn about her family’s culture. D __ Rakhee’s family is typical of Indian families.

explanation.text: Rakhee learns about relatives she has never met, the Indian custom of extended families sharing a home, and her mother's sari and bindi. These new details show how much she has to learn about her family's culture.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which statement best describes how the narrator reacts to the events in paragraphs 8 and 9? A__ She becomes more appreciative of her mother. B She is uneasy about the change in her mother. Cc She becomes confused by her mother. D She admires the change in her mother.

explanation.text: Rakhee says she “loved” the transformation, calls her mother a wondrous creature, and honestly tells her she looks beautiful. Those positive reactions show admiration rather than uneasiness or confusion.

questions.alt: Question 18. In paragraph 9, what is the meaning of the phrase “it streamed down her back in a lustrous river”? A her long hair was flowing and shiny B her long hair was damp from being in a bun Cc her long hair seemed heavier than normal D her long hair moved steadily in one direction

explanation.text: The comparison to a “lustrous river” emphasizes both movement and reflected light: Amma's released hair streams down her back and shines. The phrase therefore describes long, flowing, shiny hair.

questions.alt: Question 19. What does paragraph 9 most reveal about the narrator’s mother? A that she is more talkative when she is in India B that she follows customary traditions when she is in India Cc that she is more self-conscious when she is in India D that she visits many people when she is in India

explanation.text: Amma changes from American clothes into a sari, adds a bindi, and releases her hair because she cannot arrive home “dressed like an American.” These choices show her following Indian customs.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which statement best states a theme of the story? A Families can be surprising. B Beauty can be found in most things. C _ Traveling to new places can be tiring. D —_ New experiences can change how we see the world.

explanation.text: Rakhee's first views of the ocean, Indian dress, village scenery, a cow, and a street elephant all challenge what feels familiar to her. Her changing impressions support the theme that new experiences reshape how people see the world.

questions.alt: Question 21. How does the author most develop Rakhee’s point of view? A __ bycomparing Rakhee'’s experience to her mother’s B_ by having Rakhee describe her impressions of India C __byshowing Rakhee’s alarm during the scene with the elephant D by including Rakhee’s reaction to Amma’s changed appearance

explanation.text: As the first-person narrator, Rakhee describes what surprises, frightens, and fascinates her about India, from the landscape to the elephant. Therefore, her impressions most fully develop her point of view.

questions.alt: Question 22. How is the idea “Lightning begins inside thunderstorms” (paragraph 2) developed in the article? A__ by comparing a lightning bolt to a small spark B by describing how lightning occurs in different types of weather C _ byexplaining how air at different temperatures creates a charge in clouds D by providing examples of how much electricity is produced by electrons jumping through air

explanation.text: Updrafts carry raindrops into cold upper air while downdrafts move ice into warmer air; their collisions build electrons on the ice. This explanation shows how air at different temperatures produces a charge inside clouds.

questions.alt: Question 23. Read this quotation from paragraph 6. Between 1,500 and 2,000 thunderstorms are crackling and booming around the world at this very moment... Why does the author use the words “crackling and booming” instead of “occurring”? A to help the reader experience the speed of lightning B__ to help the reader imagine the sounds of thunderstorms C tobe clear about the electrical nature of lightning D tobe accurate about how common thunderstorms are

explanation.text: Because “crackling” and “booming” imitate the sharp and deep sounds heard during storms, those vivid words help readers imagine thunderstorms in a way the neutral word “occurring” would not.

questions.alt: Question 24. How does the illustration support the author's claims in the section “Lightning around the World”? A _ byhighlighting the size of lightning strikes in particular areas B__by providing evidence of why lightning strikes in some areas C _byillustrating which areas are more affected by lightning strikes D_ by showing the limited areas in the world where lightning strikes occur

explanation.text: The map's shading indicates how often lightning strikes in each part of the world, with darker areas receiving more. It therefore shows which regions are more affected and supports the section's geographic comparisons.

questions.alt: Question 25. How does the section “Lightning Safety” connect to the section “What is Lightning?” A “Lightning Safety” summarizes the effects of lightning described in “What is Lightning?” B “Lightning Safety” contrasts different types of lightning described in “What is Lightning?” Cc “Lightning Safety” elaborates on the cause of the powerful lightning described in “What is Lightning?” D “Lightning Safety” describes ways to avoid the powerful lightning explained in “What is Lightning?”

explanation.text: “What Is Lightning?” explains the bolt's tremendous heat and electrical power. “Lightning Safety” then gives actions for avoiding injury from that same danger, such as crouching or entering a car or building.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which sentence best expresses a central idea in the article? A Lightning strikes are only about as thick as a pencil. B__Lightning strikes are uncommon far out over the ocean. C Lightning strikes are due to unstable weather conditions. D Lightning strikes are able to travel through pipes in a building.

explanation.text: The article explains that colliding warm and cool air creates frequent storms in lightning hotspots, while places with stable temperatures receive little lightning. This contrast supports unstable weather as a central cause.

questions.alt: Question 27. a7 What does paragraph 9 suggest about the author's point of view in the article? A __ The author has great respect for lightning. B The author has difficulty understanding lightning. C The author believes that lightning can be useful. D_ The author believes that it is impossible to avoid lightning.

explanation.text: After explaining careful safety measures, the author invites protected readers to enjoy lightning's “awesome beauty” and calls it Earth's electricity. That combination of caution and awe shows great respect for its power.

questions.alt: Question 28. Which idea would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A “The farther away lightning strikes, the deeper the sound of the thunder . ..” (paragraph 5) B “Lightning zaps the remote mountain village of Kifuka, in central Africa, nearly every day.” (paragraph 6) Cc “Because of its unpredictability and power, lightning can be extremely dangerous.” (paragraph 8) D “You're safe inside the car because electricity will travel over the metal surface instead of through the interior” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: The article explains lightning's immense power, where it occurs, and how people can protect themselves. The statement that its unpredictability and power make it dangerous connects those major ideas and belongs in a summary.

questions.alt: Question 1. What is the best definition of “shimmies” as it is used in paragraph 1? A skids B stuns Cc shakes D _ sputters

explanation.text: Old Glory "shimmies" like someone dancing the jitterbug, and the truck jiggles enough to tickle the narrator's stomach. These movement details show that the word means "shakes."

questions.alt: Question 2. In paragraph 2, the phrase “in a giant tangle” suggests that the girls A are playing a game B enjoy being crowded together C feel confused about their destination D are concerned about their safety

explanation.text: The three girls squeeze together on one bench seat with their arms and legs woven around one another, yet they squeal with excitement. Their playful reaction indicates that they enjoy being crowded together rather than feeling troubled by it.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does the description of the setting in paragraph 6 reveal about the narrator? A __ She sees beauty where others may not. B She uses her imagination to rise above her surroundings. C She wants to take over her grandfather's business someday. D She appreciates McGunn’s ability to make junk seem appealing.

explanation.text: Although a junkyard may sound like an ugly trash heap, the narrator admires its rust as a pretty orange stripe against the blue sky. This contrast reveals that she notices beauty in a place other people might dismiss.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which sentence demonstrates how Grandpa Gus’s character is revealed in the setting of the story? “Old Glory inches toward the gates of McGunn’s Iron and Metal, a junkyard A that Gus knows so well, he could walk through it blindfolded and never once bump his shin on anything” (paragraph 5) B ““Got yourselves a real beauty queen there, Mick says as he points to the El Camino attached to the winch on the back of Old Glory.’ (paragraph 8) Cc “It’s a shell of what it used to be, missing its hood, and its engine, and all its doors.” (paragraph 9) D “He'll take your old grills or your rusted patio furniture or even clean out the contents of your grandparents’ shed .. .” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The description says Gus knows McGunn's junkyard so thoroughly that he could cross it blindfolded without bumping into anything. Setting his familiarity inside the vast maze of scrap shows his experience and confidence as a longtime hauler.

questions.alt: Question 5. What do the actions described in paragraph 11 reveal about the narrator? A It shows she has learned from her grandfather how to fix broken items and sell them for extra cash. B It shows she is surprised at her grandfather's strength, hauling heavy items for people. It shows she admires her grandfather because he knows his way around C ; Willow Grove. D It shows she is proud of her grandfather's ability to earn people’s respect by doing such an ordinary task.

explanation.text: The narrator calls Gus's work amazing and notices townspeople smiling, thanking him, and asking him to return. Her focus on the respect he earns through ordinary hauling reveals her pride in him.

questions.alt: Question 6. In paragraph 12, what does the phrase “the burden of useless stuff lifted off of them” suggest? A They are relieved of possessions they do not need. B They have removed a heavy load they had been carrying. Cc They have found someone who understands their problems. D They are able to get out from under something that is crushing them.

explanation.text: Once Gus removes unwanted objects, people straighten their shoulders, breathe more fully, and smile. These signs of release show that they feel relieved to be free of possessions they no longer need.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which idea best supports a theme of the story? A “Our voices sound like a whole playground as we squeal and squirm.” (paragraph 3) B “But that makes it the perfect car for Gus, who’s a trash hauler? (paragraph 10) Cc “It's amazing, I think, his ability to take something broken and worthless and turn it into a fold of green bills in his pocket.” (paragraph 11) D “And that, in my mind, is another special power that belongs to Gus” (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: Gus turns objects other people consider broken and worthless into money while also helping his neighbors. This transformation supports the theme that resourcefulness can uncover value and possibility in things others discard.

questions.alt: Question 8. Which phrase best describes how the article develops the idea presented in paragraph 2? A __ by providing counterarguments B_ by offering solutions to the problem C by discussing personal experiences D by introducing research results

explanation.text: After explaining that important brain construction happens during sleep, the article cites studies and experts on attention, mental health, immunity, weight, and brain development. These research results develop the idea by showing why sleep matters to teenage brains and bodies.

questions.alt: Question 9. The use of quotation marks around the word “relaxing” in paragraph 4 suggests that A some activities hinder true relaxation B deep sleep helps one experience true relaxation C teens do not value activities that give true relaxation D blue light helps one experience true relaxation

explanation.text: The supposedly "relaxing" activities involve screens whose blue light reduces a sleep-promoting hormone, along with online interactions that may excite or upset teens. These effects show why the quotation marks signal that the activities can interfere with genuine relaxation and sleep.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which statement best describes how the section “Hello, Night Owl!” supports a central idea of the article? A__ It describes an important cause and effect relationship. B It creates an effective comparison and contrast between ideas. C It provides evidence that disproves a popular theory. D It presents a counterargument to the initial claim.

explanation.text: The section identifies late activities, blue light, online emotions, caffeine, and a naturally delayed circadian rhythm as causes of late bedtimes. It then explains their effect—teens get less sleep—supporting the article's central concern.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which idea would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A “Screens for television, games, computers, tablets, e-readers, and cell phones give off blue light.” (paragraph 4) B “Caffeine from sodas and energy drinks makes matters worse.” (paragraph 6) c “For one thing, lack of sleep makes it harder to pay attention.” (paragraph 10) D “One study found that the more sleep people got, the more likely people were to find them attractive.” (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: The article broadly examines how insufficient sleep harms teenagers' learning, safety, health, and development. The difficulty of paying attention belongs in a summary because it is a major consequence, while the other choices are narrower supporting details.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which claim from the article is most strongly supported with evidence? A “Even without modern technology, though, teens shift their circadian rhythm,’ (paragraph 7) B “The rest of the world doesn't shift, however.” (paragraph 9) C “Lack of sleep could hurt mental health” (paragraph 12) D “Lots of issues remain for sleep researchers to explore.” (paragraph 20)

explanation.text: The claim about mental health is followed by studies finding an inverse relationship between sleep and depression or other mental illnesses. As sleep decreased, risk increased, which provides direct evidence for the claim.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which sentence from the article best shows the author's point of view? A “Blame your brain, at least in part.” (paragraph 1) B “And that’s a serious public health problem? (paragraph 9) C — “Crankiness can result, especially if you don't feel well?” (paragraph 14) D “And you can't easily change your body’s natural circadian rhythm.” (paragraph 21)

explanation.text: Calling teen sleep deprivation "a serious public health problem" is an evaluative judgment rather than a neutral fact. That wording reveals the author's view that the widespread lack of sleep deserves urgent concern.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which paragraph best summarizes a central idea from the article? A _ paragraph 1 B_sparagraph 3 C paragraph 20 D paragraph 21

explanation.text: Paragraph 20 acknowledges that researchers still have questions but states the clear conclusion of the studies: teenage brains need sleep. This conclusion shows why the paragraph best summarizes the article's central idea.

questions.alt: Question 15. What does the phrase “collect his thoughts” tell the reader about Ohkwa’ri in paragraph 1? A He is memorizing a speech. B He is expecting an argument. Cc He is hesitant to express disagreement. D He is preparing to say something important.

explanation.text: Ohkwa'ri sits quietly while his uncle gives him time to "collect his thoughts" before asking something important. The context shows that he is organizing his ideas and preparing to speak about a serious matter.

questions.alt: Question 16. How does the forest setting shape the actions that are described in paragraph 11? A The setting causes a person to move quickly. B The setting requires a person to be very careful. C The setting requires a person to be extremely quiet. D_ The setting causes a person to behave nervously.

explanation.text: A person drinking directly from a forest stream could miss an approaching enemy or dangerous animal and might reach dirtier surface water. These hazards explain why the setting requires the careful use of a cup while staying alert.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which important idea does the author develop in paragraph 13? A __ Respecting privacy is a problem in the community. B Sharing helps people in the community. C Acquiring wealth is valued in the community. D Hiding useful objects prevents loss for the community.

explanation.text: Useful objects such as the sumac blowpipe remain in plain sight so anyone in the village may take and return them. This custom demonstrates that sharing resources benefits the whole community.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which quotation best supports a central idea of the story? A “He could help his uncle finish hollowing the bowl by blowing on the coal through the thin hollow branch of a sumac.” (paragraph 5) B “Now I only have to smooth the inside and this cup will be ready to use” (paragraph 6) Cc “Tt was useful, but it was better to have something that was useful and beautiful” (paragraph 12) D “It would be a good way to learn, a good way to make myself tougher and stronger. (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: Ohkwa'ri wants to build a small lodge so he can learn to live independently and become tougher before adulthood requires it. This desire supports the story's central idea of preparing for maturity and responsibility.

questions.alt: Question 19. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A Ohkwarri helps his uncle Big Tree make a fine drinking cup. B — Ohkwarri keeps his personal things under his bed in a box. Cc Ohkwa’ri blows on the coal through a sumac branch. Ds Ohkwa’ri knows how to safely obtain water from a river.

explanation.text: Ohkwa'ri's work with Big Tree on the carefully carved drinking cup occupies much of the story and shows him learning an adult skill from his uncle. That major interaction belongs in a summary, while the box, blowpipe, and river details only support it.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which paragraph best shows a change in the direction of the plot of the story? A paragraph 6 B paragraph 7 C paragraph 12 D paragraph 15

explanation.text: In paragraph 15, Ohkwa'ri announces that it is time for him to build a lodge. This statement shows the plot changing direction from finishing and discussing the cup to his plan for learning independence.

questions.alt: Question 21. How does the author most develop Ohkwa’ri’s point of view in the story? A__ bycontrasting him with his uncle B by showing how he imitates his uncle C _byrevealing his thoughts about growing up D by describing his impatience to live on his own

explanation.text: The narration repeatedly reveals Ohkwa'ri comparing his current abilities with his uncle's and distinguishing a boy's cup from a man's. His thoughts about building a lodge to become tougher and self-reliant most directly develop his view of growing up.

questions.alt: Question 22. Paragraph 2 mainly contributes to a central idea of the article because it A __ shows that Napoleon III was an unpopular leader B_ gives the exact number of purposes for the gift C describes the loyalty of the French people D explains both reasons for the gift

explanation.text: Laboulaye says the monument would strengthen the bond between France and the United States and signal French support for liberty and equality under Napoleon III. Paragraph 2 therefore explains both reasons behind the proposed gift.

questions.alt: Question 23. Read this sentence from paragraph 3. Bartholdi wrote that the seed for the Statue of Liberty was sown at the party that night. The words “the seed for the Statue of Liberty was sown’ refer to the A timetable for building the statue B first ideas about the project C plan for funding the project D design for the statue

explanation.text: At the party, Laboulaye's opinion prompts Bartholdi to begin thinking about a monument, although actual plans come years later. The seed metaphor therefore refers to the project's earliest ideas, before a timetable, design, or funding plan existed.

questions.alt: Question 24. Read this sentence from paragraph 6. He hoped to drum up enthusiasm for the project as well as find an appealing location to display the work. The use of the phrase “drum up” shows that Bartholdi needed to A___ discover the best place for exhibiting the completed project B reduce the cost of the project C create a widespread public demand for the project D teach the public about the reason for the project

explanation.text: Bartholdi travels to the United States to learn how Americans feel and to build enthusiasm for a project that does not yet have broad support. In this context, "drum up" means create widespread public interest and demand.

questions.alt: Question 25. Why was Bedloe’s Island selected for the site of the Statue of Liberty? A The local people already knew about the history of the island. B The island was close to a populated city. C The size of the island would make the statue stand out. D The island was located in a busy harbor.

explanation.text: Bedloe's Island sits in the active New York Harbor, where the statue would attract attention and greet immigrants arriving in the New World. These advantages show that its busy harbor location made it the ideal site.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which evidence from paragraph 11 best supports the author’s claim that the fund-raising efforts for the Statue of Liberty were “creative? “Though the original goal of completing the statue for the hundredth A birthday... seemed unlikely, the group still did its best to meet that deadline.” B “Appeals for donations for the statue appeared in the French press...” C = “Banquets and balls were held in several French cities.” D “Bartholdi came up with just enough money to begin work on Lady Liberty”

explanation.text: The organizers held banquets and balls in several cities, using donated food and ballrooms so admission fees could support the statue. These unusual social events demonstrate why the fund-raising efforts are described as creative.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which event showed a change in the attitude of Americans towards the construction of the Statue of Liberty? A President Ulysses S. Grant met with Bartholdi to discuss the project. B People from the United States agreed to support the Franco-American Union, Cc Americans wanted to hear more about the design of the statue from Bartholdi. D Americans learned that the statue was to be a symbol of freedom and democracy.

explanation.text: At first, few Americans were enthusiastic or willing to contribute money. The later Franco-American Union included supporters from the United States as well as France, showing that American attitudes had shifted toward active support.

questions.alt: Question 28. Which statement best describes a major contribution of Bartholdi toward making the Statue of Liberty a reality? A He met with some of the most famous people in America. B He planned for a monument that would be extremely large. C He remained committed to the project over a long period of time. D He drew sketches to show the way the monument would look when completed.

explanation.text: Bartholdi keeps developing the monument through a war, years of planning, a trip to win American support, site selection, sketches, and fund-raising difficulties. His sustained work demonstrates that long-term commitment was his major contribution to making the statue real.

questions.alt: Question 1. Paragraphs 1 through 4 reveal that Alan is A___ discouraged after several of his ideas fail B__ overwhelmed and dismayed by his environment C adventurous and enjoying the current challenge D focused even in a life and death situation

explanation.text: Alan calmly calculates the air, battery time, distance, and physical limits of several escape plans before adapting the nitrogen cylinder. This methodical problem-solving shows that he stays focused even though a mistake could kill him.

questions.alt: Question 2. In paragraph 4, what is the effect of the simile “with tools as brittle as glass”? 2 A It affects the plot by explaining that the tools were hard to see. B It creates suspense by showing that the tools were missing some pieces. It adds to the setting by suggesting that the tools were reflecting light off the C snow. D It increases danger by emphasizing that the tools were breakable in the cold.

explanation.text: Comparing the tools to brittle glass emphasizes that Titan's extreme cold has made them likely to snap. This detail increases the danger because Alan must remove stubborn bolts without breaking the tools his escape depends on.

questions.alt: Question 3. Why does Alan use the compressed nitrogen? A The nitrogen’s energy will propel him toward the base before he runs out of air. B_ The nitrogen is used for emergencies when astronauts become stranded. C The nitrogen will stay liquid in the atmosphere. D The nitrogen’s cable can be cut and it will continue to work.

explanation.text: The high-pressure nitrogen blasts the cylinder upward and pulls Alan high into the air, giving him momentum before he opens his wings. This improvised launch allows him to glide farther and faster toward the base before his life-support resources fail.

questions.alt: Question 4. What quotation best reveals Alan’s character? A “As Alan glided toward the base, he flapped more often, watching the height-above-surface and distance-to-target displays.” (paragraph 8) B “He could see details on the surface more clearly now, because he was flying so low. (paragraph 8) Cc “Alan forced himself to stay calm. Panic would mean disaster” (paragraph 9) D “His descent rate began to slow, but the muscles in his arms burned with pain” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Alan deliberately controls his fear even as he loses altitude and his survival depends on continued flight. His recognition that panic would cause disaster most clearly reveals his self-control under extreme pressure.

questions.alt: Question 5. In paragraph 9, “appendages” refers to something that 5 A calms a person B is attached C slows things down D is lightweight

explanation.text: The "massive appendages" beat as Alan flaps and produce lift, so the phrase refers to his mechanical wings. Because an appendage extends from or is attached to something, the word fits the wings connected to his suit.

questions.alt: Question 6. The main way the point of view affects the story is by making it A formal B_ lighthearted Cc humorous D suspenseful

explanation.text: The narration closely follows Alan's calculations, physical pain, fear, equipment readings, and uncertain progress toward the base. This limited access explains why his dangerous moment-by-moment experience makes the story suspenseful.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which sentence would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A __ Acable connects space suits to a power source for spacewalks. B The power from the nitrogen blast allows Alan to travel farther and faster. C Alan soars away from his target during part of his flight. D_ The weather on Titan is cold and hazy.

explanation.text: Alan cannot survive the trip by walking or flying under his own power, so he turns the nitrogen cylinder into a rocket-like launch. Its blast gives him the extra speed and distance central to his escape, making that event essential to a summary.

questions.alt: Question 8. As used in paragraph 2, what does the phrase “fading into the background” mean? 8 A disappearing from view B losing its importance in people’s minds C moving farther and farther from the city D remaining important only to those who value nature

explanation.text: New York continues to use the Hudson as a sewer but no longer values it as the source that helped build the city. This contrast indicates that the river is losing importance in people's minds, not physically disappearing.

questions.alt: Question 9. Which claim from the article is least supported? 9 “New York didn’t seem to need the river anymore, except as a sewer. And A that’s what it became.” (paragraph 2) “Industry on the river had made some New Yorkers filthy rich.” B (paragraph 3) C “But it had just made the river filthy.” (paragraph 3) “But now there were other dreamers in the valley, with their own dreams of D wealth.” (paragraph 6)

explanation.text: The article asserts that river industries made some New Yorkers rich but gives no names, amounts, or examples to demonstrate that wealth. By contrast, specific wastes support the pollution claims and the later court case supports the environmental dreamers' actions.

questions.alt: Question 10. What is the role of paragraph 7 in the organization of the article? 10 A It compares the two groups of dreamers. B It concludes the part of the article about industry. C It introduces the part of the article about activists. D It transitions to the part of the article where change occurs.

explanation.text: Paragraph 7 predicts that industrial and environmental dreamers will meet in court. This sentence connects the history of pollution with the following conflict between Con Edison and Scenic Hudson, transitioning to the changes produced by activism.

questions.alt: Question 11. What does “unblemished” mean as used in paragraph 11? 11 A unaware B unknown C unharmed D unstable

explanation.text: Con Edison abandons its plan to carve a huge hole into Storm King Mountain, so the mountain survives without the proposed damage. In this context, "unblemished" means unharmed.

questions.alt: Question 12. How does the idea expressed in paragraph 15 relate to the article? A There are two main ways that the river can be fixed. B_ the river is able to move in two different directions. C People who benefit from the river must also protect it. D Opposing groups can each get what they want from the river.

explanation.text: The statement says the river can continue supporting people only if people care for the river in return. It therefore expresses the article's idea that those who benefit from a natural resource also have a duty to protect it.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which sentence is most important to include in a summary of the article? A One company colored the river red and yellow with excess car paint. B The Hudson River became polluted in the effort to gain wealth. C The Mahicans have a saying about taking care of the river. D The author has dreamed of returning to the Hudson River for fifty years.

explanation.text: The pursuit of industrial wealth leads people to dump sewage, chemicals, factory waste, and garbage into the Hudson until fishing collapses. This cause-and-effect relationship is central to the article and therefore belongs in its summary.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which quotation best expresses the author’s point of view in the article? 14 “In less than 300 years it had grown from a tiny Dutch outpost in the A wilderness to the business capital of the world.” (paragraph 1) “In 1963, Con Edison, New York City’s power company, proposed a plan B for constructing the largest hydroelectric pumping station ever built.” (paragraph 8) “More and more people joined the movement as they realized how much C difference each of us can make.” (paragraph 12) “It’s now my turn to help in keeping the river of dreams flowing, for all D those dreamers yet to come.” (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: The author personally accepts responsibility for helping keep the river healthy for future dreamers. This forward-looking commitment most clearly reveals his view that protecting the Hudson is a shared, continuing obligation.

questions.alt: Question 15. In paragraph 1, what does the phrase “clattering nerve center of the restaurant” suggest? 15 A messiness, chaos B energy, core C tension, anxiety D greatness, stability

explanation.text: The restaurant kitchen is full of clattering sound, bright light, motion, and constant orders, and it is the center of the family's life. Calling it a "nerve center" therefore suggests both energetic activity and a central role.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which quotation best expresses a central idea of the story? 16 “I started experimenting with being a vegetarian when I turned A fourteen . . .” (paragraph 5) B “. . . I decided early on that this is the work I want to do . . .” (paragraph 6) “It hardly seems fair that I have to walk away from all of that . . .” C (paragraph 8) D “. . . just in case I ever want to do anything else with my life.” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The narrator's early decision that restaurant work is what she wants to do guides her frustration with school and her detailed plans for becoming a chef. This statement shows her passion for a culinary career and best expresses the story's central idea.

questions.alt: Question 17. Read this sentence from paragraph 9. 17 She’s the queen of all reasons people can do anything they want in life. What does this sentence suggest about Julia Child? A Her famous kitchen became part of a cooking museum. B She was successful in a career that interested her. C She always remained calm in the kitchen. D Her cooking style created unique flavor combinations.

explanation.text: Julia Child begins cooking near age forty but goes on to create television shows and cookbooks and become important in culinary history. Her example shows that she successfully pursued a career that deeply interested her.

questions.alt: Question 18. In paragraph 11, what does the one-word statement “Somehow” reveal? 18 A It illustrates the narrator’s enthusiasm about her future plans. It shows the narrator has some questions about whether she will succeed or B not. It shows the narrator does not have every detail of her future plans figured C out just yet. It demonstrates the narrator’s lack of knowledge about how difficult her D goals are to achieve.

explanation.text: The narrator is sure she will be discovered but can describe the process only as "somehow." That vague word reveals that her goal is firm even though she has not worked out every step for reaching it.

questions.alt: Question 19. What does the statement “I know I’m going to have to pay my dues” (paragraph 11) show 19 about the narrator’s attitude toward her plans? A She thinks the price of fame might be too high. B She realizes success depends on more than setting a goal. C She is highly motivated by the idea of becoming famous. D She thinks becoming a celebrity chef requires only money.

explanation.text: To "pay my dues" means the narrator expects to put in time and effort before becoming a celebrity chef. This acknowledgment shows that she understands success will require more than simply naming her goal.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which sentence would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A When the narrator’s mother makes her do homework, the narrator rolls her eyes. B When the narrator’s mother wanted to invest in a restaurant, Pia wanted to buy the old bank building. C The narrator admires celebrity chefs. D The narrator lives in California.

explanation.text: The narrator's admiration for Julia Child and other television chefs shapes her dream of having a cooking show, books, products, and fans of her own. That aspiration is central to the story, while the other choices are minor background details.

questions.alt: Question 21. The author develops the narrator’s point of view mainly through the use of A __ internal monologue B conflict between characters Cc limited dialogue D flashback

explanation.text: Most of the story consists of the narrator's private reactions to homework, judgments about school, memories of restaurant work, and imagined future as a chef. This internal monologue reveals and develops her ambitious, confident point of view.

questions.alt: Question 22. As it relates to the information in the article, what does “With a Few Flat Tires Along the Way” 22 in the title suggest? A Women rode bicycles along rough and unpaved roads. B Women encountered resistance to their efforts. C Women had a difficult time learning to ride bicycles. D Women were unused to dealing with mechanical issues.

explanation.text: The article describes social criticism, restrictive expectations, ridicule, and fear of equality as obstacles facing women cyclists. The "Few Flat Tires" metaphor shows that women encountered resistance, rather than only literal bicycle trouble, along their path toward freedom.

questions.alt: Question 23. Read this sentence from paragraph 2. 23 “At sixteen years of age, I was enwrapped in the long skirts that impeded every footstep,” remembered Frances Willard, who in 1895 wrote a best-selling account of how she learned to ride a bicycle at age 53. Which word from the sentence best helps the reader understand the meaning of “impeded”? A enwrapped B every C footstep D remembered

explanation.text: The word "enwrapped" shows that Willard was enclosed in long skirts, which could restrict the movement of her legs. That physical constraint helps explain that "impeded" means hindered or slowed.

questions.alt: Question 24. Read this sentence from paragraph 3. 24 But the bicycle changed that. How does the sentence develop an idea? A It marks a transition. B It introduces an example. C It refines a previous statement. D It provides evidence for a claim.

explanation.text: Before this sentence, women of different classes are confined, anonymous, or absent from public life; afterward, the author describes women cycling and becoming visible. This shift shows that "But the bicycle changed that" marks the transition between those contrasting conditions.

questions.alt: Question 25. What do the details in paragraph 3 reveal about the author’s point of view? A The author thinks that clothing restrictions were less of a burden than the necessity of hard work. B The author thinks that all women were treated unfairly regardless of economic status. Cc The author thinks that poor women had more freedom of dress even though they had to work hard. D The author thinks that the contrast between two classes of women had an effect on their advancement.

explanation.text: Wealthier women face restrictive clothing and domestic confinement, while poorer women labor anonymously in mills and factories. By saying the result was the same, the author indicates that women across economic classes were treated unfairly and excluded from public prominence.

questions.alt: Question 26. What idea about women riding bicycles is emphasized in paragraphs 6 and 7? 26 A Riding bicycles offered more than just freedom of movement. B Riding bicycles was a simple way to participate in a political movement. C Riding bicycles changed women’s fashion. D Riding bicycles was a popular subject in magazines.

explanation.text: The magazines describe cycling as creating independence, confidence, delight, and a sense of new power, even comparing women riders to people who have grown wings. These effects show that bicycles offered personal and social liberation beyond the ability to travel farther.

questions.alt: Question 27. A distinction the author makes between Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Frances Willard is that 27 only A Stanton wrote in favor of women riding bicycles B Willard was actually known to ride a bicycle C Stanton was actually criticized for riding a bicycle D Willard was a true spokeswoman for the bicycle

explanation.text: The article says there is no evidence Stanton ever rode a bicycle, even though she strongly advocated for it. Willard, however, wrote a best-selling account of learning to ride at age fifty-three, so actual riding distinguishes her from Stanton.

questions.alt: Question 28. Which quotation best expresses the central idea of the article? A “Young girls could play outside, but when they matured, their freedom of movement was greatly restricted” (paragraph 2) B “Suddenly, women were leaving their homes to cycle and socialize on country roads and city streets.” (paragraph 3) Cc “Thanks to the wheel, women were starting to be seen and heard in public life” (paragraph 3) D “Tt was not a stretch for some cyclists to see the possibility of a larger role for women in the world” (paragraph 4)

explanation.text: Bicycles bring women out of their homes, make female athletes and models publicly visible, and become linked to demands for greater rights. This evidence shows why the statement that women were starting to be seen and heard best captures the article's broad transformation.

exams.title: New York Grade 3 ELA - 2019 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2019 New York State Grade 3 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2019 NYS Grade 3 English Language Arts Test Released Questions

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 1-6. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.
Official passage for Questions 7-12. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. What does the word “crouched” mean as it is used in paragraph 6? 1 A spun around B bent down C stood up D fell over

explanation.text: Riley reties her skates and moves low beside the seated Craig so he can climb onto her back. That position shows that “crouched” means bent down.

questions.alt: Question 2. In paragraph 9, what is the most likely reason Riley stays quiet as she and Craig walk 2 home? A She is thinking about how well she played hockey. B She is upset with Craig because he hurt her back. C She is thinking about how to get skates for Craig. D She is tired from skating in the hockey game.

explanation.text: Craig has just explained sadly that he needs skates in order to “fly.” Riley's later question and discovery of her old skates show that her silence comes from thinking of a way to help him get skates.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does paragraph 12 help the reader understand about Craig? 3 A Craig is too young to learn how to skate. B Craig is very excited about learning to skate. C Craig is unable to take the skates off by himself. D Craig is worried that his sister will take the skates back.

explanation.text: Craig cannot stop smiling when the skates fit and does not want to remove them even for the walk to the lake. These reactions demonstrate how excited he is to learn to skate.

questions.alt: Question 4. In paragraph 16, what does the phrase “like being a snail” help the reader to understand 4 about Craig? A He skates very slowly. B He moves in a crooked line. C He searches for a place to hide. D He looks like all the other skaters.

explanation.text: Craig inches along behind the chair while his blades scrape instead of swish like Riley's. Comparing himself to a snail therefore emphasizes how slowly he is skating.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which sentence best describes how paragraph 6 relates to paragraph 23? 5 Paragraph 6 provides a problem and paragraph 23 provides a A solution. B Paragraph 6 asks a question and paragraph 23 provides an answer. C Paragraph 6 provides a cause and paragraph 23 shows an effect. D Paragraph 6 provides similarities and paragraph 23 shows differences.

explanation.text: In paragraph 6, riding on Riley's back is too slow and wobbly to make Craig feel as though he is flying. In paragraph 23, Riley and Liz therefore solve that problem by pulling him rapidly across the ice on his own skates.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which sentence best describes a central message of the story? A Change is normal and an important part of life. B_ Friendships often become stronger over time. C New experiences can be exciting and wonderful. D Natural talent is more important than practice.

explanation.text: Craig's first skating attempts are difficult, but Riley and Liz help him move faster until he joyfully feels as though he is flying. This experience shows that trying something new can become exciting and wonderful.

questions.alt: Question 7. According to paragraph 5, what happens right before thunder can be heard? A Little drops of water move around in the sky. B Dark clouds appear in the sky. C Rain droplets start to fall from the clouds. D The air spreads because of heat from lightning.

explanation.text: Paragraph 5 explains that lightning's heat makes the air expand rapidly, creating the booming sound of thunder. Thus, the heated air spreads immediately before the thunder is heard.

questions.alt: Question 8. What is the main idea of paragraph 6? 8 A Lightning can reach from the sky to the ground. B A bolt of lightning can travel up to nine miles. C Flashes of lightning can jump from one cloud to another. D Lightning can move over large distances very quickly.

explanation.text: Paragraph 6 says lightning moves faster than a blink, can travel from the clouds to the ground and back, and may stretch for many miles. Together, these facts show it crosses large distances very quickly.

questions.alt: Question 9. Which idea from the passage does the second illustration best support? A Lightning can be helpful for nature. B Lightning moves very quickly. C Lightning appears in different ways in the sky. D Lightning may strike before or after it rains.

explanation.text: The second illustration displays forked, ribbon, heat, and sheet lightning with visibly different shapes and patterns. It therefore supports the idea that lightning appears in several ways in the sky.

questions.alt: Question 10. What does the word “nutrient” mean as it is used in paragraph 10? 10 A a type of lightning B a supply of heat C a kind of plant D a form of food

explanation.text: The next sentence explains that nitrogen feeds plants and helps them grow. Because a nutrient supplies nourishment, “nutrient” means a form of food.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which question does the section “Fast Facts” help to answer? 11 A How does lightning help the earth? B How is lightning different in the summer? C How is electricity created in clouds? D How can someone avoid an electrical shock?

explanation.text: “Fast Facts” states that lightning puts nitrogen into the ground and air, where it feeds plants and helps them grow. This information therefore answers how lightning can help the earth.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which sentence shows a cause and effect relationship that is stated in the passage? 12 A People see lightning before they hear thunder. B Wildfires can start when lightning touches the ground. C Heat from electricity is hotter than the surface of the sun. D Lightning bolts can hit tall trees and buildings.

explanation.text: The passage states that when lightning strikes a tree or dry grass, a wildfire can begin. The strike therefore causes the resulting fire.

exams.title: New York Grade 4 ELA - 2019 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2019 New York State Grade 4 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2019 NYS Grade 4 English Language Arts Test Released Questions

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 13-18. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 19-24. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 13. What does the phrase “set out” mean as it is used in paragraph 6 of the article? A _ grabbed her suitcase B began her journey C accepted work D started writing

explanation.text: The sentence says Nellie "set out" one year after requesting the trip, and the next details describe her traveling east across the Atlantic. This context shows that the phrase means she began her journey.

questions.alt: Question 14. Read this sentence from paragraph 6. Traveling east across the Atlantic, Nellie took just one bag in order to move quickly. How is this detail important to paragraph 1? A _Itshows how she is the same as the character in the book. B__It shows one way to help her reach her goal. C __ Itshows a young reporter exploring the world in real life. D _ Itshows that a young woman taking a trip alone is unusual.

explanation.text: Nellie's goal is to travel around the world in fewer than eighty days. Taking only one bag supports that goal because carrying less allows her to move quickly.

questions.alt: Question 15. How does the author organize the information in paragraphs 6 and 7? A by listing events in the order they happened B by comparing and contrasting the places Nellie visited C by showing what caused Nellie to want to take the trip D _bystating how the problem of traveling so far was solved

explanation.text: The author first describes Nellie leaving, writing during the trip, and crossing Europe and Asia, then tells of her arrival in San Francisco and return to New York. This sequence shows the events in the order they happened.

questions.alt: Question 16. What does the word “journalism” mean as it is used in paragraph 9? A writing for newspapers B traveling for women C finding adventures D discovering opinions

explanation.text: Nellie invents a new style of "journalism" by reporting what she sees, thinks, and feels to her newspaper's readers. That description indicates that journalism means writing for newspapers.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which detail from the article does the map support? A ‘Start the man, and I'll start the same day .. ” (paragraph 2) B_ “She reached New York in 4% days. (paragraph 7) C “She met her challenge!” (paragraph 8) D “She reported to her readers what she saw, thought, and felt . .” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: The map traces Nellie's completed route from the New York area through Europe and Asia, across the Pacific, and back across the United States in seventy-two days. This completed journey supports the statement that she met her challenge to circle the world in fewer than eighty days.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which sentence best states the main idea of “Around the World”? A _ Traveling around the world in a short period of time is a difficult goal. B It was unusual for a woman to travel in the past. C Geography is an important subject to study. D A woman showed she can do anything.

explanation.text: Nellie refuses limits placed on women, completes the journey faster than the proposed goal, and proves herself competent and resourceful. These details support the main idea that a woman demonstrated she could accomplish what people assumed only a man could do.

questions.alt: Question 19. What does the word “demand” mean as it is used in paragraphs 2 and 21? A _ tocorrect B to look for C to work on D to insist

explanation.text: King Tiger orders Borneo to give him food and threatens an attack if its animals refuse; later, he proposes making the same demand of the elephants. His threats show that "demand" means to insist on receiving something.

questions.alt: Question 20. What does the reader know in paragraphs 6 through 8 that the tigers do not know? A King Tiger wants to take food from the Island of Borneo. B The Island of Borneo has no tiger king. C Mouse Deer is afraid of King Tiger’s whisker. D The whisker from Mouse Deer is not from a tiger.

explanation.text: Mouse Deer's thoughts tell the reader that Borneo has no tigers at all, much less a tiger king. The visiting tigers do not know this, which explains why his promise to carry their message deceives them.

questions.alt: Question 21. Paragraph 15 supports a theme of the story by showing that Mouse Deer A __ honors the tigers B__ is clever and brave C _ is happy to be telling lies D finds his king

explanation.text: Although Mouse Deer trembles, he invents a fearsome king who sharpens his claws between mountains and welcomes a fight. His convincing story shows both the cleverness of his plan and the bravery required to face the tigers.

questions.alt: Question 22. In paragraph 17, the phrase “pranced off” shows that Mouse Deer feels A _ eager to run far away from the tigers B satisfied with how things went with the tigers C worried that the tigers will return with their king D _ surprised to see that the tigers could swim

explanation.text: Mouse Deer prances only after his trick has astonished the tigers and sent them swimming away. This lively movement reveals that he feels pleased and satisfied with how the encounter ended.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which sentence best describes how Mouse Deer causes the event in paragraph 21? A _Hesees three tigers looking at him. B He carries King Tiger’s whisker in his mouth. C He asks Porcupine for one of his quills. D He waits so the tigers will think he went far.

explanation.text: Mouse Deer borrows a large porcupine quill and presents it as a whisker from Borneo's supposedly enormous tiger king. Because the quill frightens King Tiger, that choice causes him to redirect his demands to the Island of the Elephants.

questions.alt: Question 24. What do the details in paragraph 21 suggest about King Tiger? A He wants to avoid showing that he is scared. B He believes there is more food on a different island. C He knows that it is important to plan ahead. D He thinks he is the greatest tiger in the world.

explanation.text: After staring at the enormous "whisker," King Tiger abruptly claims he has been thinking and suggests attacking a different island. This excuse reveals that he is scared of Borneo's supposed king but does not want his followers to see his fear.
