passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 8-14. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 15-21. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 29-35. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 8. Read this sentence from paragraph 1 of the article. 8 In the days when farmers worked with ox and sled and cut the dark with lantern light, there lived a boy who loved snow more than anything in the world. How does the author’s word choice in the sentence affect the meaning of the passage? A by suggesting that the ideas in the passage are made up B by showing that the subject of the passage became famous C by suggesting that the topic of the passage is familiar D by showing that the events in the passage happened long ago

explanation.text: The sentence places the story in a time when farmers used oxen and sleds and relied on lanterns to light the darkness. Those outdated tools show that the events happened long ago.

questions.alt: Question 9. What is the meaning of the word “pelted” as it is used in paragraph 3? 9 A created B found C saved D threw

explanation.text: The other children make snowballs and "pelted" them at crows while Willie studies flakes. Because snowballs are objects the children send toward the birds, the word means "threw."

questions.alt: Question 10. Which quotation best supports a main idea of the article? 10 A “He expected to find whole flakes that were the same . . .” (paragraph 4) B “ ‘Fussing with snow is just foolishness,’ his father said.” (paragraph 6) C “Even so his first pictures were failures . . .” (paragraph 7) “ ‘Now everyone can see the great beauty in a tiny crystal,’ he said.” D (paragraph 7)

explanation.text: Willie's central goal is to preserve the beauty he sees in snowflakes so other people can experience it too. His declaration that everyone can now see a tiny crystal's beauty shows that his successful photograph fulfills that goal.

questions.alt: Question 11. What does the information in paragraph 9 suggest about the author’s point of view? A The author believes that Bentley could have been more careful. B The author respects Bentley’s many different interests. C The author admires Bentley’s dedication. D_ The author questions the methods Bentley used.

explanation.text: Paragraph 9 details how Willie waits for hours in the cold, keeps the shed unheated, avoids breathing or twitching, and works before each flake melts. This careful account of his persistence reveals the author's admiration for his dedication.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which statement is true based on the information in paragraphs 6 and 11? 12 Bentley’s work with snow required expensive equipment that he was willing A to spend all his money on. Bentley was thought to be foolish throughout his life because of his interest B in snow. Bentley’s parents thought he should do something with his life other than C taking pictures of snow. Bentley became less interested in studying snow than in publishing pictures D and giving speeches.

explanation.text: Willie's camera cost his parents their savings and as much as ten cows, showing that his work required costly equipment. Later, he never becomes rich because he spends every penny on his pictures, confirming his willingness to devote all his money to the work.

questions.alt: Question 13. What does the reader learn about Bentley from paragraphs 10 and 11? 13 A He was more interested in sharing his work than in making money from it. B He worked hard to develop a way of making photographs of snowflakes. C He wanted to find out if all snowflakes were different from each other. He was able to follow his interests because of the help he got from his D family.

explanation.text: Willie gives photographs away, sells them for only a few cents, creates birthday gifts, and holds slide shows for children and adults. These choices show that sharing his discoveries mattered more to him than earning money.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which sentence best describes how the article is organized? A The reasons for Willie Bentley’s experiments with snow are presented, followed by their eventual conclusions. B The events of Willie Bentley’s life and his study of snow are described as they happened over time. Cc The different problems of photographing snow are explained and then Willie Bentley’s solutions are described. The important ideas about snow in Willie Bentley’s discoveries are D ; presented, followed by details and examples.

explanation.text: The article follows Willie from his childhood fascination with snow through years of experiments, his first successful photograph, and his later recognition as an expert. This progression shows that his life and study of snow are presented chronologically.

questions.alt: Question 15. How do paragraphs 1 and 23 relate to each other? A They show the change in Gregor’s life during the story. B They show what Gregor has learned in the story. C They show how MacTavish changes in the story. D They show the growth of MacTavish’s fortune during the story.

explanation.text: At the beginning, Gregor and his mother work hard but remain too poor to buy the flock they want. By paragraph 23, he has sold gems, bought a huge flock, and shared a fortune, so the two paragraphs show how completely his life changes.

questions.alt: Question 16. What does the phrase “marched back” in paragraph 14 suggest about MacTavish? 16 A He is confused. B He is worried. C He is determined. D He is excited.

explanation.text: MacTavish turns and "marched back" immediately after Gregor refuses to sell, then announces that he will pay anything. His forceful return and increased offer show that he is determined to obtain the sheep.

questions.alt: Question 17. Howare Gregor and the man who sold the sheep to him similar? A They are both unskilled at selling things to people. B_ They both try to trick someone in order to make money. C They are both concerned with helping their family and neighbors. D They both believe that animals can have special qualities.

explanation.text: The market seller falsely claims the sickly sheep will make its owner rich so Gregor will buy it. Gregor later spreads the same claim to attract MacTavish, which shows that both men use a trick in hopes of gaining wealth.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which of Gregor’s actions shows how he is different from MacTavish? 18 A Gregor cheats another person. B Gregor buys a sickly sheep. C Gregor shares his wealth. D Gregor moves to a big house.

explanation.text: After gaining MacTavish's fortune, Gregor shares the remaining wealth with poor families who were cheated. This generous action shows how he differs from MacTavish, who repeatedly keeps money earned by other farmers.

questions.alt: Question 19. Which sentence is true about Gregor and MacTavish? 19 MacTavish has a plan for how the sheep will make him rich, but Gregor A does not. B MacTavish wants to own big houses and many jewels, but Gregor does not. C Gregor wants to move away to another land, but MacTavish does not. D Gregor is generous with his family and his neighbors, but MacTavish is not.

explanation.text: Gregor uses his new wealth to help his mother and the other struggling families in the valley. MacTavish, by contrast, cheats those same neighbors and gives them less than they earn, demonstrating that Gregor is generous while MacTavish is not.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which sentence expresses a theme of the story? 20 A Big loss can come from being greedy. B Family can make hard times seem easier. C Wealth may come from hard work. D Appreciating others can lead to happiness.

explanation.text: MacTavish already has houses, gems, and costly clothing, yet his desire for even more wealth makes him trade an entire mansion for one sheep. His enormous loss demonstrates the story's warning that greed can carry a high price.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A ___ Gregor goes to the market and talks to many farmers about their sheep. B _ Gregor’s mother is asleep when Gregor takes money to buy the sheep. Cc MacTavish lives at another one of his houses after he buys the sheep from Gregor. D MacTavish goes to buy Gregor’s sheep after he hears rumors about the animal.

explanation.text: The rumor about a wealth-producing sheep draws greedy MacTavish to Gregor, where he agrees to exchange his mansion and possessions for the animal. This event belongs in a summary because it drives the trick that changes both men's fortunes.

questions.alt: Question 29. What idea is developed in paragraphs 4 through 7? 29 A Wangari and her mother want to plant more trees. B Mugumo trees are important to people and animals. C Mugumo trees can provide shade to many people. D Wangari and her mother think education is important.

explanation.text: The mugumo provides fruit for Wangari, geckos, and elephants, nesting places for birds, branches for monkeys, and sacred shade for Kikuyu ancestors. These details show that the tree matters to both the community and many animals.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read this sentence from paragraph 8 of the article. 30 Her mind was like a seed rooted in rich soil, ready to grow. What does the sentence help the reader to understand about Wangari? A She likes to think about plants. B She wants to keep learning. C She imagines ways to help others. D She believes in working together.

explanation.text: A seed in rich soil has the conditions to keep developing, just as Wangari's educated mind is prepared to develop further. The comparison therefore shows her desire and readiness to continue learning.

questions.alt: Question 31. How are the details in paragraphs 13 and 14 organized? A asa description of how animal habitats changed Basan explanation of the solution to a problem in the environment C —asacomparison of the village before and after the government sold the land D asa description of how a problem was caused in the area and its effects

explanation.text: The government first sells land to companies that remove native forests. The passage then traces the results—lost habitats, scarce firewood, eroded soil, muddy rivers, failed crops, and hunger—so the details move from a cause to its effects.

questions.alt: Question 32. Paragraphs 17 and 18 explain that Wangari spread her idea by 32 A sharing it with women around the country B giving it the name Green Belt Movement C watching the land in Kenya turn green again D planting trees herself everywhere she went

explanation.text: Wangari calls on other mothers to work with her, then travels among villages, towns, and cities carrying seeds, saplings, and tools. The women plant trees at each stop, which shows that she spreads the idea by sharing it with women around the country.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which sentence most likely expresses Wangari’s point of view? 33 People can make the changes they want by working together with A determination. B People change their traditions and customs with each generation. C People cannot rely on the government to help them in a time of need. People in other countries do not need to work as hard on the same D problem.

explanation.text: Wangari urges the women to work together, and their planting grows from one tree to thirty million despite the problem's enormous scale. Their success supports the view that determined cooperation enables people to create the change they seek.

questions.alt: Question 34. How does the title of the article support a main idea? 34 A It describes advice Wangari followed. B It describes how Wangari solved a problem. C It explains how Wangari felt about trees. D It explains which values Wangari’s village held.

explanation.text: The title's "seeds of change" are both the actual seeds Wangari organizes women to plant and the beginning of a much larger movement. It supports the main idea by showing how her tree-planting plan solves the environmental problems harming Kenyan families.

questions.alt: Question 35. Based on the information in the article, where did Wangari most likely get her idea for 35 planting trees across Kenya? A from the school she attended in the capital city B from the government of her country C from the women of the village where she grew up D from what her mother taught her as a girl

explanation.text: As a girl, Wangari's mother teaches her that the mugumo shelters and feeds many living things and is sacred to their people. Wangari's promise never to cut it down shows how that early lesson could inspire her later plan to restore Kenya by planting trees.

exams.title: New York Grade 6 ELA - 2019 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2019 New York State Grade 6 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2019 NYS Grade 6 English Language Arts Test Released Questions

questions.alt: Question 1. Which important idea is developed in paragraph 6? 1 A Uncle Nigel has been at the museum a long time. B Uncle Nigel is afraid he may lose his job. C Uncle Nigel has a plan to attract visitors to the museum. D Uncle Nigel is hoping to start a new job soon.

explanation.text: Uncle Nigel explains that declining attendance could cause the museum's funding to be cut, then admits that he and Aunt Delia could lose their jobs. This admission shows that the possible job loss is the important concern developed in the paragraph.

questions.alt: Question 2. What does the phrase “on a short fuse” mean as used in paragraph 8? 2 A hard to please B hard to find C easy to upset D easy to frighten

explanation.text: Uncle Nigel warns the children to behave especially well while Aunt Delia is under stress about the museum. That context indicates that being "on a short fuse" means she is easy to upset.

questions.alt: Question 3. How does paragraph 9 contribute to the structure of the story? 3 A It changes the setting of the story. B It establishes the conflict in the story. C It states the theme of the story. D It foreshadows events in the story.

explanation.text: Paragraph 9 shifts from the museum's financial trouble to Uncle Nigel's announcement that he found an artifact that should not exist. This mysterious discovery suggests future action, foreshadowing the later revelation of an Incan treasure key and the adventure it may begin.

questions.alt: Question 4. How does the language in paragraph 12 contribute to the overall tone of the story? 4 A It introduces a threatening tone. B It highlights the sorrowful tone. C It stresses the mysterious tone. D It creates a worried tone.

explanation.text: The treasure is hidden in a secret chamber, its three scattered keys contain clues, and a curse supposedly keeps fortune hunters from returning alive. These unexplained legends and dangers contribute to the story's mysterious tone.

questions.alt: Question 5. What do paragraphs 10 and 13 indicate about Addison? 5 A He is uncertain whether his uncle’s story is true. B He is curious to see what his uncle has found. C He knows that his uncle’s discovery will save the museum. D He wishes he had gone with his uncle on the dig.

explanation.text: Addison thinks of nothing better than seeing a strange relic from a distant country, then quietly asks what Uncle Nigel found in Bolivia. Both reactions indicate that curiosity about the discovery is guiding his attention.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which sentence from the story signals a turning point? A “Addison and Molly weren’t sure how to respond.’ (paragraph 7) B “J just returned from a dig in the jungles of Bolivia and found the most improbable relic” (paragraph 9) “Instead, they locked it away in a secret chamber and hid three keys across the Incan Empire.’ (paragraph 12) D “The wind howled so fiercely it could be heard even in the basement.” (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: Uncle Nigel's announcement of the improbable relic redirects the scene from worries about museum funding toward a potentially important archaeological discovery. This shift signals a turning point because it begins the treasure-key portion of the plot.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which quotation is most important to include in a summary of the story? 7 A “. . . Addison and Molly knew the wooded grounds by heart.” (paragraph 2) B “ ‘Uncle Nigel, we’re here!’ Molly called.” (paragraph 5) “As Uncle Nigel talked, Addison’s eyes darted to the shadowy corners of the C office . . .” (paragraph 11) D “I’m pretty sure it is Atahualpa’s first key.” (paragraph 19)

explanation.text: The identification of the carved stone as Atahualpa's first key is the scene's central discovery and connects directly to the legend of the hidden Incan treasure. It belongs in a summary because it captures the event likely to drive the story forward.

questions.alt: Question 8. In paragraph 4, what does the phrase “woman with the most statues in her honor” show about 8 Sacagawea? A that she was a charming person B that she had been fearless C that she is a famous person D that she died long ago

explanation.text: Having more statues in her honor than any other American woman means Sacagawea has been widely remembered and celebrated. This recognition shows that she is a famous historical figure.

questions.alt: Question 9. How is the author’s pride in her ability to read introduced in the article? 9 A through a story from her childhood B by contrasting her family with other families C through examples of the books she finished D by comparing herself with other readers

explanation.text: The author recalls earning her first library card as a child, including her fear of making a mistake and her delight when the card became hers. Her vivid memory and celebration of that moment show her pride in becoming a reader.

questions.alt: Question 10. How do the details about Sacagawea in paragraphs 5 and 6 contribute to the development of 10 the article? A by helping readers see the difficulties in Sacagawea’s life B by explaining the mystery surrounding Sacagawea’s life C by creating curiosity for readers about where Sacagawea traveled D by describing how Sacagawea played an important role in history

explanation.text: Sacagawea's presence signaled peace, she saved vital documents during a boat mishap, and her translating helped secure horses and guidance. These contributions explain how she played an important role in the expedition's historical success.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which is the best definition of “crucial” as it is used in paragraph 6? 11 A heroic B fantastic C important D urgent

explanation.text: Lewis and Clark needed Shoshone horses and guidance to cross the Rocky Mountains, so Sacagawea's interpreting was necessary to their success. In this context, "crucial" means important or essential.

questions.alt: Question 12. Based on the article, the author’s relationship to Sacagawea is developed through 12 A a comparison of the author’s life to that of Sacagawea B explanations that show how the author is similar to Sacagawea C examples of the research that the author did on the life of Sacagawea a chronological sequence of events that explained Sacagawea’s life to the D author

explanation.text: The author and her father investigate records in major libraries, reservation collections, foreign countries, cyberspace, and the National Archives. These examples of sustained research show how they trace the family line connecting her to Sacagawea.

questions.alt: Question 13. Read this sentence from paragraph 13. 13 We’re cousins of the Shoshone woman with the baby on her back who explored America by canoe, horseback, and on foot to map it for the first time. What does this sentence suggest about Sacagawea? A how long she participated in the expedition B how remarkable her accomplishment was C how familiar she was with the areas being explored D how she felt about her leadership abilities

explanation.text: Sacagawea crosses a vast, unmapped region by canoe, horseback, and on foot while carrying her baby. Listing the difficult modes of travel and her role in mapping America demonstrates how remarkable her accomplishment was.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which sentence best expresses a central idea of the article? A “Our family owned only an encyclopedia and a well-used dictionary” (paragraph 2) B “The presence of a mother and child was a sign that the expedition was a peaceful one and helped keep it safe from Indian attack.” (paragraph 5) Cc “My father said he didn’t know, but he’d wondered himself if we had a common ancestor.” (paragraph 9) D “We explored tiny libraries on American Indian reservations.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The father's uncertainty about whether the family shares an ancestor with Sacagawea creates the question that he and the author spend years investigating. This sentence expresses the central idea because their search for that family connection organizes the rest of the article.

questions.alt: Question 29. What do paragraphs 5 through 7 show about the author’s purpose for writing the article? A The author wants to convince readers that school was the only way to advance in Ancient Egypt. B The author wants to describe to readers what school was like for students in Ancient Egypt. Cc The author wants to explain to readers why students had to write on limestone in Ancient Egypt. D The author wants to entertain readers with interesting stories about life in Ancient Egypt.

explanation.text: Paragraphs 5 through 7 explain what students copied, the tools and inks they used, and the surfaces on which they practiced. Those concrete classroom details show that the author's purpose is to describe what school was like for aspiring scribes.

questions.alt: Question 30. How does the author develop ideas in paragraph 6? 30 by explaining why Ancient Egyptian brushes had to be made out of reed A plants by describing the process of making Ancient Egyptian ink from beginning B to end by presenting the problem that Ancient Egyptian ink came only in red and C black and offering a solution by comparing and contrasting Ancient Egyptian writing tools to art tools D that are familiar to modern readers

explanation.text: The author compares scribes' ink disks, water pots, and reed brushes with parts of a modern watercolor set, while also noting differences in colors and materials. This familiar comparison helps readers understand the ancient writing tools.

questions.alt: Question 31. What does the information in paragraph 8 illustrate about becoming a scribe? 31 A It can lead to a successful future. B It gives the opportunity to write on papyrus paper. C It requires memorization of hundreds of signs. D It means leaving home at a young age to attend school.

explanation.text: Paragraph 8 says a graduate of scribal school could become a doctor, priest, secretary, supervisor, or another literate professional. Those respected job possibilities illustrate that becoming a scribe could lead to a successful future.

questions.alt: Question 32. Which quotation best expresses a central idea of the article? A “Scribes were very important people in Ancient Egypt because they were almost the only ones who could read and write.’ (paragraph 2) B “Although records tell us that there were a few female scribes, most were men.” (paragraph 2) Cc “Scribal students used writing tools somewhat like the parts of a water color set you might have.” (paragraph 6) D “They wrote letters home and sent bills for work they'd done” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: The article repeatedly emphasizes the years of study, specialized writing systems, tools, and varied work that made scribes valuable. The statement that they were important because almost no one else could read or write best captures that central idea.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which quotation from the article is best supported by the photograph? A “This was a kind of shorthand script used for everyday writing.” (paragraph 4) B “Students memorized the hieroglyphic signs and practiced writing them on pieces of stone, pottery, or wood.” (paragraph 5) Cc “They wrote poetry and stories and put down words of wisdom and advice for their children” (paragraph 9) D “Parents were happy to pay the high price to send a child to scribal school.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The photograph shows rows of hieroglyphic signs carved into a hard surface. This visual evidence best supports the description of students memorizing those signs and practicing them on materials such as stone, pottery, or wood.

questions.alt: Question 34. In the father’s letter, the phrase “Mingling with the crocodiles” suggests 34 A the low pay of some types of work B the foolishness of avoiding work C the presence of danger in other jobs D the need to try hard in any job

explanation.text: The father's letter contrasts safe scribal study with a fisherman's labor on a river where crocodiles are present. "Mingling with the crocodiles" therefore suggests the physical danger found in other kinds of work.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which sentence would be most important to include in a summary of the article? 35 A Egyptians used hieratic for everyday writing. B Students made ink from red and black disks. C Scribes wrote important things on papyrus. D Children studied for years to become scribes.

explanation.text: The article's main focus is the long, demanding education required to master hieroglyphs, hieratic writing, tools, and documents before entering an important profession. The fact that children studied for years belongs in a summary because it captures this broad idea, while the other choices are narrower details.

Official passage for Questions 15-21. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 29-35. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 8. How do paragraphs 2 and 3 develop a central idea in the article? A They state that William was not able to attend school. B They show how William learned things on his own. C they explain that others did not care about William’s questions. D They give examples of the types of books William read.

explanation.text: Although William cannot attend school, he experiments with radios and a bicycle dynamo, visits the library, studies science diagrams, and looks up unfamiliar English. This shows that he continues learning independently rather than depending on formal classes.

questions.alt: Question 9. What does the phrase “pore over” mean as it is used in paragraph 3? 9 A write about B glance at C examine D copy

explanation.text: William spends time with the books' drawings and diagrams and uses a dictionary or librarian to understand difficult words. This careful study shows that "pore over" means to examine something closely.

questions.alt: Question 10. Paragraph 6 develops the author’s central claim by showing that William 10 A was distracted by so many objects in the junkyard B was resourceful in finding what he needed C was able to make use of his limited time in school D was excited to look for new projects

explanation.text: William searches discarded machinery for usable parts and transforms a tractor fan, plastic pipe, bamboo, bearings, and a piston into windmill components. These inventive substitutions show that he is resourceful despite having little money or equipment.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which sentence best describes the relationship between William and the people who doubted 11 him? A William was inspired and delighted by them. B William ignored and then convinced them. C William was concerned and worried about them. D William listened to and then got help from them.

explanation.text: William ignores children who call him crazy and stays focused on building his machine. When the windmill lights the bulb, the same community cheers, which shows that his success convinces the people who had doubted him.

questions.alt: Question 12. In paragraph 14, what does the word “marvel” suggest? A They are amazed by what William has done. B They are curious to see what will happen to the bulb. C they are worried that William’s experiment may be dangerous. D They are ready to have more lights in the house.

explanation.text: William's family gathers around the first electric light in their home after watching him build the windmill from scraps. Their reaction is one of wonder, so "marvel" suggests they are amazed by what he accomplished.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which quotation shows an effect of success on William’s life? A “Shoppers, farmers, and traders could see William’ s tower from the local market. (paragraph 10) B “From the top of the tower he explained that, by using the power of wind, his machine could create electricity” (paragraph 11) Cc “William knew this was his moment—his moment to show everyone he wasn’t crazy ...” (paragraph 12) D “The story of the boy with only an eighth-grade education who'd built ‘electric wind’ spread across the Internet.” (paragraph 16)

explanation.text: After William's windmill succeeds, news of the self-taught boy and his "electric wind" spreads on radio, in newspapers, and across the Internet. This shows an effect of success because his working invention brings him broad public recognition.

questions.alt: Question 14. Based on the information in the article, which of the following is most likely the author’s point 14 of view? A Access to formal schooling is important for success. B Science education is needed to help people flourish. C Curiosity and persistence can lead to change. D Families are the best support system.

explanation.text: William's curiosity drives him to experiment and study science, while his persistence keeps him gathering parts and building despite poverty and ridicule. This shows that curiosity and persistence can create meaningful change for a family and community.

questions.alt: Question 15. What role do paragraphs 1 through 3 mainly play in the story? A They clarify the emotions that the two architects are feeling. B_ They foreshadow the surprise at the ending. C They set the scene and explain the process for picking a new design. D They set a humorous tone by describing the two architects sitting in school desks.

explanation.text: The opening identifies the two competing architects, the school-board meeting, their covered models, and the presentation-and-vote process. These details establish the setting and explain how the new school design will be selected.

questions.alt: Question 16. In paragraph 6, “markedly” means doing something in 16 A an obvious way B a careful way C a respectful way D an enthusiastic way

explanation.text: Mr. Kowalski says that his entire concept has changed "markedly" and therefore needs new plans and extra presentation time. In this context, the word means that the change is clear and obvious.

questions.alt: Question 17. How does the word choice in paragraphs 9 and 11 affect the story? 17 A It describes how similar the design is to the Capitol building. B It shows why the design is practical for a school. C It conveys how impressive Mr. Kowalski’s design is. D It explains what classical architecture looks like.

explanation.text: Words such as "swooped," "graceful," "sunlit," and "modern" emphasize the model's dramatic beauty and innovation. This vivid description helps explain why the school board is astounded and applauds the design.

questions.alt: Question 18. How do paragraphs 4 and 5 connect to what the reader learns in paragraph 18? A They reveal that Mr. Kowalski thinks Holling’s father is a better architect than he is. B They support the idea that Mr. Kowalski is guilty of stealing Mr. Hoodhood’s plans. C They show that Mr. Kowalski hopes Holling likes his plan for the school. D They provide information about how Mr. Kowalski’s and Mr. Hoodhood’s designs are different.

explanation.text: Before presenting, Mr. Kowalski repeatedly looks at Holling and nervously clears his throat. Once Holling realizes that Meryl Lee obtained his father's design from him, those uneasy looks support the conclusion that Kowalski knows he is using stolen plans.

questions.alt: Question 19. What do paragraphs 15 through 18 reveal about Holling? 19 A He disobeys his father easily. B He did not know he was being deceived. C He is unconcerned with the opinions of others. D He is a dishonest person.

explanation.text: Holling finally understands that Meryl Lee used their Valentine's Day meeting to obtain his father's school design. His statement that he "hadn't seen at all" what she was doing reveals that he did not know he was being deceived.

questions.alt: Question 20. How does the author develop Holling’s point of view in the story? 20 A by explaining how his father sees the competition B by describing Mr. Kowalski’s plans for the school building C by describing his observations until he realizes what has happened D by explaining the architectural review process

explanation.text: The first-person narration records Holling's observations of the architects, his father's anger, and Kowalski's repeated looks before Holling understands their meaning. This progression develops his point of view by moving from puzzled observation to a painful realization.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which detail about Mr. Kowalski and Mr. Hoodhood would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A They have model buildings under sheets on the table. B They are competing to design a new school. Cc They must meet with the school board at a school. D They have to wait to hear who has been chosen.

explanation.text: Mr. Kowalski and Mr. Hoodhood are presenting rival designs for the same new junior high school. Their competition belongs in a summary because it creates the story's main situation and explains the conflict over the copied plan.

questions.alt: Question 29. How does paragraph 3 relate to the other paragraphs in the section “Unlikely in the Extreme”? 29 Paragraph 3 introduces a problem, and the other paragraphs explain the A solution to the problem. Paragraph 3 explains an idea, and the other paragraphs describe how the B idea was disproved. Paragraph 3 presents an argument, and the other paragraphs give evidence C to support the argument. Paragraph 3 sets up a comparison, and the other paragraphs give details D about the comparison.

explanation.text: Paragraph 3 explains why scientists once believed liquid water could not survive under Antarctica's extremely cold ice. The later paragraphs disprove that belief with flat-ice observations, radar data, satellite evidence, and the confirmed dimensions of Lake Vostok.

questions.alt: Question 30. What role does paragraph 8 play in the organization of the article? 30 A It shows techniques that scientists use to study remote regions. B It explains how the environment on Jupiter might support life. C It describes the activities of scientists interested in extreme environments. It introduces the connection between activities on Earth and research in D outer space.

explanation.text: Paragraph 8 shifts from hidden Antarctic lakes to Galileo's evidence of an ocean beneath the icy surface of Europa. It therefore introduces the article's connection between research on Earth's extreme environments and the search for life in outer space.

questions.alt: Question 31. How does paragraph 10 develop a central idea in the article? 31 A It explains how life in an extreme environment could be possible. It describes scientific research on the possibility of life in an extreme B environment. C It provides clues to the possible existence of life in an extreme environment. It presents questions to be answered about the possibility of life in an D extreme environment.

explanation.text: Paragraph 10 asks how life without sunlight could obtain nutrients and withstand the pressure beneath miles of ice. These unanswered questions develop the central idea by identifying what scientists must determine about possible life in an extreme environment.

questions.alt: Question 32. How did scientists address the concern described in paragraph 14? A They removed an ice core from the lake to study it. B They applied chemicals to melt ice and keep the borehole open. C they obtained a water sample with clean technology. D They found sediment at the bottom of the lake.

explanation.text: Ordinary drilling chemicals and surface microbes could pollute an isolated lake, so researchers at Lake Whillans used hot water, ultraviolet light, sterile equipment, and sterile clothing. This clean technology let them collect a water sample while addressing the contamination concern.

questions.alt: Question 33. What is the meaning of the word “contaminate” as used in paragraphs 14 and 16? 33 A color B replace C freeze D pollute

explanation.text: The chemicals and surface microbes could change the lake's water and make a sample no longer pure or reliable. In that context, "contaminate" means to pollute the isolated environment.

questions.alt: Question 34. According to the information in this article, which sentence describes how life was discovered 34 beneath Antarctica? Scientists found proof of water beneath Antarctic ice, then they drilled to A get samples of the water to look for life. Scientists saw signs of ice that may cover water on other planets, so they B drilled for water beneath Antarctic ice. Scientists knew that there were life forms in the deep parts of the ocean, so C they concluded that there was life in the water beneath Antarctic ice. Scientists found microbes on the surface of Antarctic ice, then they drilled D into the ice to look for microbes beneath the surface.

explanation.text: Radar and satellite evidence first confirmed liquid water beneath Antarctica's ice. Scientists then drilled with clean technology, tested a water sample, and found thousands of thriving microbes, which is the sequence by which the hidden life was discovered.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which sentence best shows the author’s point of view on the topic of the article? A ___ The amount of water below the ice in Antarctica is surprising. B Astronomers are excited about signs of ice and water on other planets. Cc Antarctica is one of the most important scientific environments on Earth. D_ The formation of very flat ice at the Vostok station is an important clue.

explanation.text: The author calls Antarctica's hidden wetland one of Earth's most valuable environments because it may guide the search for life elsewhere in the solar system. That evaluative claim reveals the view that Antarctica is exceptionally important to science.

exams.title: New York Grade 8 ELA - 2019 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2019 New York State Grade 8 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2019 NYS Grade 8 English Language Arts Test Released Questions

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 15-21. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 22-28. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 29-35. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 15. As used in paragraph 2, what does the phrase “fading into the background” mean? 15 A disappearing from view B losing its importance in people’s minds C moving farther and farther from the city D remaining important only to those who value nature

explanation.text: New York grows into a powerful city and begins treating the Hudson only as a sewer rather than as the source that once supported it. Thus, the river is "fading into the background" because it is losing importance in people's minds.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which claim from the article is least supported? 16 “New York didn’t seem to need the river anymore, except as a sewer. And A that’s what it became.” (paragraph 2) “Industry on the river had made some New Yorkers filthy rich.” B (paragraph 3) C “But it had just made the river filthy.” (paragraph 3) “But now there were other dreamers in the valley, with their own dreams of D wealth.” (paragraph 6)

explanation.text: The article lists extensive evidence that industry polluted the Hudson and later describes environmentalists' alternative idea of natural wealth. By contrast, it gives no facts showing how particular New Yorkers became rich, so that claim is the least supported.

questions.alt: Question 17. What is the role of paragraph 7 in the organization of the article? 17 A It compares the two groups of dreamers. B It concludes the part of the article about industry. C It introduces the part of the article about activists. D It transitions to the part of the article where change occurs.

explanation.text: Paragraphs 5 and 6 establish opposing dreams of industrial wealth and environmental richness. Paragraph 7 creates a transition because its prediction that those groups will meet in court leads into the legal battle that begins the river's recovery.

questions.alt: Question 18. What does “unblemished” mean as used in paragraph 11? 18 A unaware B unknown C unharmed D unstable

explanation.text: Con Edison's proposed project would have carved a gigantic hole into Storm King Mountain, but the company abandons the plan after the court fight. Because the mountain survives without that damage, "unblemished" means unharmed.

questions.alt: Question 19. How does the idea expressed in paragraph 15 relate to the article? A There are two main ways that the river can be fixed. B_ the river is able to move in two different directions. C People who benefit from the river must also protect it. D Opposing groups can each get what they want from the river.

explanation.text: The article shows that the Hudson supports cities, industry, wildlife, and people, yet neglect nearly destroys it. Paragraph 15 expresses the reciprocal lesson that those who benefit from the river must protect it so it can continue supporting them.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which sentence is most important to include in a summary of the article? A One company colored the river red and yellow with excess car paint. B The Hudson River became polluted in the effort to gain wealth. C The Mahicans have a saying about taking care of the river. D The author has dreamed of returning to the Hudson River for fifty years.

explanation.text: Much of the article traces how the pursuit of industrial wealth filled the Hudson with sewage, chemicals, and factory waste. That cause-and-effect relationship is essential to a summary because it explains both the river's crisis and the need for activism.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which quotation best expresses the author’s point of view in the article? 21 “In less than 300 years it had grown from a tiny Dutch outpost in the A wilderness to the business capital of the world.” (paragraph 1) “In 1963, Con Edison, New York City’s power company, proposed a plan B for constructing the largest hydroelectric pumping station ever built.” (paragraph 8) “More and more people joined the movement as they realized how much C difference each of us can make.” (paragraph 12) “It’s now my turn to help in keeping the river of dreams flowing, for all D those dreamers yet to come.” (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: The final statement is a personal commitment to continue protecting the Hudson for future generations. It best expresses the author's point of view because it directly reveals his belief in shared responsibility for the river.

questions.alt: Question 22. In paragraph 1, what does the phrase “clattering nerve center of the restaurant” suggest? 22 A messiness, chaos B energy, core C tension, anxiety D greatness, stability

explanation.text: A "nerve center" controls activity, while "clattering" evokes the busy sounds of dishes and kitchen work. The phrase suggests energy and central importance because it portrays the kitchen as the restaurant's active core.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which quotation best expresses a central idea of the story? 23 “I started experimenting with being a vegetarian when I turned A fourteen . . .” (paragraph 5) B “. . . I decided early on that this is the work I want to do . . .” (paragraph 6) “It hardly seems fair that I have to walk away from all of that . . .” C (paragraph 8) D “. . . just in case I ever want to do anything else with my life.” (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The narrator's long-term decision to make cooking her life's work drives her frustration with school and all of her future plans. That declaration therefore states the story's central idea about her strong culinary ambition.

questions.alt: Question 24. Read this sentence from paragraph 9. 24 She’s the queen of all reasons people can do anything they want in life. What does this sentence suggest about Julia Child? A Her famous kitchen became part of a cooking museum. B She was successful in a career that interested her. C She always remained calm in the kitchen. D Her cooking style created unique flavor combinations.

explanation.text: Julia Child began cooking near age forty but went on to create television shows, cookbooks, and an important place in culinary history. Her example shows the narrator that people can pursue an interesting career and become successful.

questions.alt: Question 25. In paragraph 11, what does the one-word statement “Somehow” reveal? 25 A It illustrates the narrator’s enthusiasm about her future plans. It shows the narrator has some questions about whether she will succeed or B not. It shows the narrator does not have every detail of her future plans figured C out just yet. It demonstrates the narrator’s lack of knowledge about how difficult her D goals are to achieve.

explanation.text: The narrator is certain that she will get discovered but can describe the method only as "somehow." This vague word reveals that her goal is firm even though she has not worked out every detail for achieving it.

questions.alt: Question 26. What does the statement “I know I’m going to have to pay my dues” (paragraph 11) show 26 about the narrator’s attitude toward her plans? A She thinks the price of fame might be too high. B She realizes success depends on more than setting a goal. C She is highly motivated by the idea of becoming famous. D She thinks becoming a celebrity chef requires only money.

explanation.text: To "pay my dues" means accepting the work and experience required before earning success. The phrase shows that the narrator knows becoming a celebrity chef will require more than simply choosing the goal.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which sentence would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A When the narrator’s mother makes her do homework, the narrator rolls her eyes. B When the narrator’s mother wanted to invest in a restaurant, Pia wanted to buy the old bank building. C The narrator admires celebrity chefs. D The narrator lives in California.

explanation.text: The narrator models her ambitions on Julia Child and imagines having a television show, cookbooks, products, fans, and recipes of her own. Her admiration for celebrity chefs belongs in a summary because it explains her central dream.

questions.alt: Question 28. The author develops the narrator’s point of view mainly through the use of A __ internal monologue B conflict between characters Cc limited dialogue D flashback

explanation.text: Most of the passage consists of the narrator's private reactions to homework, memories of restaurant life, and detailed plans for becoming a chef. Internal monologue develops her point of view because it gives direct access to her ambitions and impatience.

questions.alt: Question 29. As it relates to the information in the article, what does “With a Few Flat Tires Along the Way” 29 in the title suggest? A Women rode bicycles along rough and unpaved roads. B Women encountered resistance to their efforts. C Women had a difficult time learning to ride bicycles. D Women were unused to dealing with mechanical issues.

explanation.text: A flat tire interrupts forward travel, so the subtitle uses it as a metaphor for obstacles in women's progress toward freedom. The article's mockery, restrictive customs, and fear of changing gender roles show the resistance women encountered.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read this sentence from paragraph 2. 30 “At sixteen years of age, I was enwrapped in the long skirts that impeded every footstep,” remembered Frances Willard, who in 1895 wrote a best-selling account of how she learned to ride a bicycle at age 53. Which word from the sentence best helps the reader understand the meaning of “impeded”? A enwrapped B every C footstep D remembered

explanation.text: Willard says she was "enwrapped" in long skirts, meaning the fabric wrapped around her body and legs. That clue helps show that the clothing restricted, or "impeded," each step.

questions.alt: Question 31. Read this sentence from paragraph 3. 31 But the bicycle changed that. How does the sentence develop an idea? A It marks a transition. B It introduces an example. C It refines a previous statement. D It provides evidence for a claim.

explanation.text: Before this sentence, the paragraph explains that public American life appeared almost entirely male. "But the bicycle changed that" marks the transition to evidence of women cycling, socializing, racing, and gaining public visibility.

questions.alt: Question 32. What do the details in paragraph 3 reveal about the author’s point of view? A The author thinks that clothing restrictions were less of a burden than the necessity of hard work. B The author thinks that all women were treated unfairly regardless of economic status. Cc The author thinks that poor women had more freedom of dress even though they had to work hard. D The author thinks that the contrast between two classes of women had an effect on their advancement.

explanation.text: Wealthier women were confined by restrictive clothing, while poorer women worked anonymously in mills and factories; either way, public life remained male. This shared result reveals the author's view that women faced unfair limits regardless of economic status.

questions.alt: Question 33. What idea about women riding bicycles is emphasized in paragraphs 6 and 7? 33 A Riding bicycles offered more than just freedom of movement. B Riding bicycles was a simple way to participate in a political movement. C Riding bicycles changed women’s fashion. D Riding bicycles was a popular subject in magazines.

explanation.text: Paragraphs 6 and 7 describe cycling as a source of freedom, independence, new power, and personal transformation, even comparing women to figures taking flight. Riding offered more than movement because it changed how women viewed themselves and their possibilities.

questions.alt: Question 34. A distinction the author makes between Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Frances Willard is that 34 only A Stanton wrote in favor of women riding bicycles B Willard was actually known to ride a bicycle C Stanton was actually criticized for riding a bicycle D Willard was a true spokeswoman for the bicycle

explanation.text: The article says no evidence shows that Stanton ever rode a bicycle, even though she strongly advocated for it. Willard, however, wrote an account of learning to ride at age fifty-three, which distinguishes her as an actual cyclist.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which quotation best expresses the central idea of the article? A “Young girls could play outside, but when they matured, their freedom of movement was greatly restricted” (paragraph 2) B “Suddenly, women were leaving their homes to cycle and socialize on country roads and city streets.” (paragraph 3) Cc “Thanks to the wheel, women were starting to be seen and heard in public life” (paragraph 3) D “Tt was not a stretch for some cyclists to see the possibility of a larger role for women in the world” (paragraph 4)

explanation.text: The article explains how bicycles moved women beyond the home, increased their independence, and linked female cyclists with demands for greater rights. The statement that women became seen and heard in public life captures all of those connected changes.

exams.title: New York Grade 3 ELA - 2018 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2018 New York State Grade 3 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2018 NYS Grade 3 English Language Arts Test Released Questions

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 1-6. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 19-24. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. What does the word “assistant” mean as it is used in paragraph 2? A shelper B leader C neighbor D friend

explanation.text: The assistant works with Irene by showing Alex an object and naming it while Irene watches. Because this person helps Irene teach the parrot, “assistant” means helper.

questions.alt: Question 2. Which sentence from the passage shows a cause and effect relationship? A “But there is one parrot who speaks more than a hundred words and actually understands their meanings.’ (paragraph 1) B “Teaching Alex to speak words that he understands has let Irene talk to him directly.’ (paragraph 3) Cc “In this way, Irene is finding out what sorts of things Alex’s brain can do? (paragraph 3) D “He can also make comparisons, such as bigger or smaller and same or different, between objects.” (paragraph 4)

explanation.text: Teaching Alex meaningful words causes a new result: Irene can communicate with him directly. The words “has let” clearly connect the teaching with that effect.

questions.alt: Question 3. Read this sentence from paragraph 4. Alex can identify over forty kinds of objects, five different shapes, five materials, and seven colors, and he can use his knowledge to solve problems and answer questions. What is the best meaning of the word “identify” as used in this sentence? A feel B_lookat C pick up D _ recognize

explanation.text: Alex can identify many objects, shapes, materials, and colors and then use that knowledge to answer questions. In this context, “identify” means recognize what each item is.

questions.alt: Question 4. How does the photograph add to the information in the passage? A It shows one way that Irene works with Alex. B__It shows that Irene does not talk with Alex. C It shows that Alex is able to count objects. D _ It shows the few objects that Alex cannot name.

explanation.text: The photograph shows Irene beside Alex as they look at objects arranged on a tray. This image illustrates the question-and-object work described in the passage.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which part of the passage best shows how Alex feels about Irene? A paragraph 9 B paragraph 10 C paragraph 17 D paragraph 18

explanation.text: In paragraph 17, Alex climbs onto Irene's shoulder and rubs his head against her cheek. That affectionate action shows that he feels close to Irene.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail best supports the main idea of the passage? A Irene’ parrot is named Alex. B Alex can find a green object when asked. C Irene sometimes puts Alex on her shoulder. D Alex has a croaky parrot voice.

explanation.text: The passage's main idea is that Alex understands words and can use knowledge to solve problems. Finding the green object when Irene asks directly demonstrates that unusual ability.

questions.alt: Question 19. In paragraph 1, what does Stella mean when she says, “I feel like a spider in a web”? A _ Stella is untangling the strings. B Stella is confused by the strings. C Stella is surrounded by the tangled strings. D _ Stella is winding the strings around her spool.

explanation.text: Tangled kite strings drift down all around Stella after the two kites collide, so like a spider enclosed by its web, she feels surrounded by the strings.

questions.alt: Question 20. In paragraphs 6 through 8, what do you know as the reader that Robby does not know? A __ Stella wishes she could trade her kite for a jellyfish kite. B Stella has to run to make her kite fly. C Paper plate kites are easier to fly than other kites. D Paper plate kites are easier to make than shark kites.

explanation.text: Paragraph 7 reveals Stella secretly wishes she could trade her troublesome shark for the pink jellyfish kite. Robby only hears her criticize paper plates, so he does not know that wish.

questions.alt: Question 21. Read this sentence from paragraph 8. If she could catch a tiny breeze, her kite would fly. What does “catch” mean as it is used in the sentence? A _ and hold a moving object B __ bring in while hunting or fishing Cc get an illness D find and use

explanation.text: Stella hopes a small breeze will provide enough moving air to lift her kite. Therefore, “catch” means find and use the breeze rather than physically hold it.

questions.alt: Question 22. What happens after Stella trips over the shark’s fin and falls into the dirt? A Stella tries to fly her kite again. B Stella takes Robby’s paper plate home with her. C Stella’s kite points downward and refuses to fly. D _ Stella points at a kite shaped like a monster truck.

explanation.text: After Stella falls, Robby offers her his flying paper plate, and she accepts it. Therefore, the next event is Stella carrying that kite toward the car and watching it beside her window.

questions.alt: Question 23. What do the details in paragraph 17 show about Robby? A _ Heisat the park most weekends. B He isa thoughtful friend to Stella. C He is better at flying kites than Stella. D He is creative with paper plates.

explanation.text: Robby sees Stella's disappointment and offers the kite that has been working well, even though it means making another one later. This generous response shows he is a thoughtful friend.

questions.alt: Question 24. What happens because Stella cannot fly her shark kite? A Stella tries to fix her kite. B Stella notices a pink jellyfish kite. C Stella gets into the car with her mother. D _ Stella tries the paper plate kite.

explanation.text: Stella's shark repeatedly dives or refuses to rise, so she finally accepts Robby's paper plate and feels it fly steadily. Her failure with the shark directly causes her to try the simpler kite.

exams.title: New York Grade 4 ELA - 2018 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2018 New York State Grade 4 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2018 NYS Grade 4 English Language Arts Test Released Questions

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 7-12. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 19-24. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 7. What does the word “conservation” mean as it is used in paragraph 6? A action B education C preparation D protection

explanation.text: Paragraph 6 asks whether programs meant to save snow leopards are working. Because these efforts guard the animals from threats, “conservation” means protection.

questions.alt: Question 8. g How does paragraph 9 connect to paragraph 6 in the article? A _ by describing a method for counting snow leopards B by describing what it is like to see a snow leopard C _ by explaining why snow leopards are rarely seen by humans D by explaining how scientists identify individual snow leopards

explanation.text: Paragraph 6 says researchers need reliable ways to count snow leopards. Paragraph 9 therefore provides one such method by explaining how automatic camera traps photograph cats that pass nearby.

questions.alt: Question 9. g Which idea best explains why Dr. McCarthy and his co-workers traveled to Kyrgyzstan? A “The loss of habitat has caused a food shortage.” (paragraph 4) B “But they need more reliable ways to count leopards before they will know’ (paragraph 6) C “They saw no leopards, but they hadn't expected to.” (paragraph 7) D “Researchers compared patterns in the photos to identify cats.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: Researchers cannot judge whether protection programs work until they can count the animals reliably. Dr. McCarthy therefore travels to Kyrgyzstan to test better ways of estimating leopard numbers.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which idea from the article best supports the main idea? A “The snow leopard is smaller than the tiger, the lion, and the leopard of Africa and Asia.’ (paragraph 2) B “Researchers estimate that only 3,500 to 7,500 snow leopards are alive today.” (paragraph 6) C “Each snow leopard’s spot pattern is different.” (paragraph 10) D “The cameras had taken photos of 15 different snow leopards at two study sites.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The article focuses on threats to snow leopards and efforts to keep them from disappearing. The estimate of only 3,500 to 7,500 remaining cats directly demonstrates why protection is urgent.

questions.alt: Question 11. Howis the article mainly organized? A compare and contrast B sequence of events C question then answer D cause and effect

explanation.text: The article repeatedly links causes with results: habitat loss reduces prey, hungry leopards attack livestock, and herders then kill them. It also explains how protective programs can reduce those effects.

questions.alt: Question 12. How does the table at the end of “Saving Snow Leopards” support the main idea of the article? A __by showing reasons why snow leopards are struggling to survive B__ by listing ways to better protect snow leopards C _ by presenting new information about the habitat of snow leopards D by providing evidence that there are fewer snow leopards alive now than in the past

explanation.text: The table gathers threats such as illegal hunting, habitat and prey loss, killings by herders, weak protection, and limited awareness. These causes show why snow leopards are struggling to survive.

questions.alt: Question 19. Which detail from paragraph 1 best supports a theme of the story? A The folktale takes place long ago. B Kanikiya likes to wear a scarlet ribbon. C The brolgas are birds that live along the river. D Kanikiya has a talent that is recognized by others.

explanation.text: People often watch Kanikiya and compare her graceful dancing to moonlight on a running river. Their admiration therefore establishes that her special talent is visible and valued by others.

questions.alt: Question 20. Read this sentence from paragraph 6. They understand my sorrow, Kanikiya thought, the knowledge warming her. What does the phrase “the knowledge warming her” suggest? A afeeling of comfort B sadness and disappointment C afeeling of anger D__ wisdom and clear thinking

explanation.text: Kanikiya is crying when the cranes bow and perform a slow dance that seems to share her sorrow. Realizing that they understand her makes her feel less alone and gives her comfort.

questions.alt: Question 21. What does the word “mournful” mean as it is used in paragraph 7? A angry B bored Cc nervous D sad

explanation.text: The cranes perform a slow dance while Kanikiya cries, and the following paragraph describes her days as a “mournful blur” filled with heaviness. This context shows that “mournful” means sad.

questions.alt: Question 22. Which sentence from the story best shows how a character's actions help to develop the story? A “You must not leave the camp until the brolgas have left.” (paragraph 6) B “She wove baskets from the reeds the other children collected.” (paragraph 7) c “Slipping out of the camp, she rushed down to the riverbank to dance with the cranes.” (paragraph 8) “Her mother looked for her throughout the camp and, not finding her there, searched near the river” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Kanikiya's decision to slip away and dance despite her mother's command leads directly to the search and the discovery of a ribboned crane. Her action therefore drives the story toward its ending.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which statement best describes how Kanikiya changes from the beginning to the end of the story? A She realizes that she must leave her home to find happiness. B She learns that the most important thing is to obey the rules. C She discovers that the birds love her more than she loves them. D _ She is frightened by camp tales and then sees that they are untrue.

explanation.text: At first Kanikiya remains in camp even though giving up dance makes her weak and miserable. By the end she has therefore left her human home and become a crane, allowing her to find happiness through dancing.

questions.alt: Question 24. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A _ Kanikiya is often seen near the river by others. B_ Kanikiya’s mother tells her a story about a girl. C Kanikiya feels like she must dance. D _ Kanikiya weaves baskets.

explanation.text: Kanikiya's powerful need to dance causes the conflict with her community, her sadness in confinement, her escape, and her transformation. That need is therefore essential to any summary of the story.

exams.title: New York Grade 5 ELA - 2018 Released Questions

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 1-7. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 22-28. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 29-35. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. In paragraph 2, what does the sentence “They look just like fleecy rainbows” suggest about the flowers? A The flowers are colorful and fuzzy. B_ The flowers are wet and fluffy. C The flowers are striped and shaggy. D The flowers are transparent and puffy.

explanation.text: The petals are described as feathery and yellow, orange, red, purple, and blue. Comparing them with fleecy rainbows therefore suggests both a fuzzy texture and many colors.

questions.alt: Question 2. What does the word “welt” mean as used in paragraph 9? A _ itch B ) skin C - gnat D bump

explanation.text: Because the hot-pink welt appears on Alex's arm after a biting insect attacks him, it is the raised bump left on his skin by the bite.

questions.alt: Question 3. Read this sentence from paragraph 14. His smile faded as he went on. What does the sentence suggest about Professor Raglin? A Heis suffering from the bad smell. B He regrets having to study the plant. Cc He dislikes the plant he is talking about. D He is about to deliver disappointing news.

explanation.text: Professor Raglin begins with good news that the plant is not toxic, but his smile disappears before he lists its useless, unpleasant qualities and says it may be destroyed. The change therefore signals disappointing news.

questions.alt: Question 4. Read this sentence from paragraph 15. “At least they’re not poison,” Wendy said after Professor Raglin had left. What does the sentence suggest about Wendy? A __ Wendy looks for the positive side of situations. B Wendy does not like people to give her bad news. C Wendy challenges people who do not agree with her. D Wendy encourages people to learn to love the flowers.

explanation.text: Wendy has just learned that the flowers may be smelly and useless, yet she immediately finds one favorable fact: they are harmless. This response shows that she looks for a situation's positive side.

questions.alt: Question 5. How does the setting of the story affect what happens to the Woolly-Puff? A __ Alack of space causes the flower to be shipped away. B_ Aneed for compost causes the plant to be valued. C_ A problem with insects causes the flower to be kept. D A lack of pets causes the plant to be adopted.

explanation.text: New Harmony has a severe infestation of biting “naggers,” and the insects unexpectedly avoid Woolly-Puffs. Because the colony needs relief from those pests, the flowers are kept as natural bug chasers.

questions.alt: Question 6. What does the phrase “smell funny” mean as it is used in paragraph 15? A The flowers made the children laugh. B__ The scent of the flowers was unusual. C The flowers caused the children to be itchy. D The stems of the flowers were strange.

explanation.text: Professor Raglin says the flowers smell like moldy lemons, and Wendy agrees that their odor is different. Thus, “smell funny” refers to an unusual scent, not something humorous.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which statement best states a theme of the story? A _ Friends should support each other in difficult situations. B Following the rules can sometimes get you in trouble. C It may take courage to speak up when you have a good idea. D The smallest things can cause big problems.

explanation.text: Although the council has decided to remove the flowers, Wendy attends its meeting, presents her idea, and asks the members to watch the insects. Her courage to speak up therefore saves the useful plants.

questions.alt: Question 22. In paragraph 2, what does the phrase “made her want to dance along with the leaves” most suggest about Mrs. Majeska? A She is ready to move along quickly to get home. B She remembers the words to a song in her head. Cc She likes the fall weather because it causes change. D _ She is happy because she is enjoying her day.

explanation.text: Mrs. Majeska enjoys the brisk wind, dancing leaves, glorious weather, and anticipation of a favorite meal. Wanting to dance along therefore expresses how happy she feels while enjoying the day.

questions.alt: Question 23. How do paragraphs 3 and 4 foreshadow the ending of the story? A__ byemphasizing that a single glove is of little use B by emphasizing that nobody cares about a lost glove C __byshowing that Mrs. Majeska is careful when picking up the glove D by showing that Mrs. Majeska is worried about the owner of the glove

explanation.text: Mrs. Majeska feels sorry for the owner and asks what good one glove could be by itself. This early concern foreshadows her later decision to reunite the matching gloves so they remain useful together.

questions.alt: Question 24. Based on paragraphs 5 and 6, which sentence best contrasts Mrs. Majeska and the children? A Mrs. Majeska wants to clean the glove but the children do not. B_ Mrs. Majeska is sorry for the glove owner but the children are not. C Mrs. Majeska is happy to see the glove while the children are not. D_— Mrs. Majeska understands the value of the glove while the children do not.

explanation.text: The children treat the second glove as worthless debris and kick it through the dust. Mrs. Majeska recognizes it as the missing mate and understands that joining the pair restores its value.

questions.alt: Question 25. Mrs. Majeska’s actions in paragraphs 7 and 8 most contribute to the development of the central idea by showing that she A ___ iscontent that the gloves are back together B considers taking the gloves home for herself C thinks she is silly for worrying about the gloves D cleans off the gloves to make them easy to find

explanation.text: Mrs. Majeska retrieves, cleans, and carries the second glove back to its mate, then feels satisfied seeing the pair together. Her contentment demonstrates the central importance she places on restoring what belongs together.

questions.alt: Question 26. What can the reader infer about Mrs. Majeska in paragraph 9? A She is nervous that the gloves will not be found by their owner. B_ She is concerned about what the boy and the two ladies think of her. C She is scared by the boy who is riding on the bike. D She is upset by the old ladies who walk past her.

explanation.text: When a bicyclist and two women approach, Mrs. Majeska pretends to tie her shoe so they will not see her reuniting the gloves. This behavior shows concern that strangers may think she looks foolish.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which word best describes Mrs. Majeska in paragraph 10? A proud B restless C © stubborn D regretful

explanation.text: Mrs. Majeska wishes she had taken the gloves, decides to return for them, and finds them gone. Her second thoughts and failed attempt to retrieve them show that she is regretful.

questions.alt: Question 28. What does the point of view in paragraph 11 help the reader to understand? A ___anowner’s joy in finding the gloves B Mrs. Majeska’s concern that a puppy might chew the gloves C a housewife’s eagerness to put the gloves to use Ds Mrs. Majeska’s surprise that someone took the gloves

explanation.text: Readers hear Mrs. Majeska's mystified thoughts as she imagines several possible reasons the gloves disappeared. Her point of view reveals her surprise that anyone else would take them.

questions.alt: Question 29. Which sentence best describes a main idea of the article? A John Roebling and his son formed an uncomfortable working relationship. B John Roebling was an inspired engineer who designed modern bridges. C John Roebling came to the United States to build bridges. D John Roebling was a popular student and successful businessman.

explanation.text: The article traces how Roebling created the first American iron cable, used it to modernize suspension bridges, and designed increasingly ambitious structures. These achievements therefore establish him as an inspired engineer.

questions.alt: Question 30. What does the phrase “snatching up” (paragraph 3) suggest about John Roebling’s iron cables? A They sold quickly. B They were inexpensive. C They lasted a long time. D They pulled a lot of weight.

explanation.text: Once tests proved the cables thinner, stronger, and longer-lasting than rope, delighted business owners began buying them. “Snatching up” therefore suggests the cables sold quickly.

questions.alt: Question 31. Which paragraph does the photo of the Brooklyn Bridge best support? A paragraph 3 B paragraph 4 C paragraph 7 D paragraph 8

explanation.text: Paragraph 4 explains that modern suspension bridges use thick metal cables attached to strong towers to hold the roadway. The Brooklyn Bridge photograph clearly shows those cables and towers.

questions.alt: Question 32. Which sentence best describes how John Roebling influenced his son Washington? A Washington learned why it was important to use science to improve his military skills. B Washington applied what his father taught him about the different types of iron cables. C Washington learned the reasons suspension bridges needed to be improved. D Washington applied what his father taught him and built bridges when he was a soldier.

explanation.text: During the Civil War, Washington builds temporary suspension bridges using his father's ideas and later becomes his chief engineer. This demonstrates that he applied the bridge knowledge John had taught him.

questions.alt: Question 33. What do paragraphs 7 and 10 most contribute to the article? A They introduce different opinions about the bridge. B They outline the long process involved in planning, paying for, and constructing the bridge. C They highlight the concerns people had about the appearance of the bridge. D we describe the disagreements people had about where the bridge should be uilt.

explanation.text: Paragraph 7 contrasts New Yorkers who call the bridge wasteful with Brooklyn residents who favor it. Paragraph 10 therefore adds safety worries and the experts' approval, introducing more opinions about the project.

questions.alt: Question 34. What effect did the winter of 1866-67 have on the construction of the Brooklyn Bridge? A The weather caused people to go to Brooklyn because they thought it was safer there. B The weather caused ferry service to , making more people decide the bridge was a good idea. C The weather made more people go to New York to find work and to shop. D The weather made more people want to leave the area, making the mayor decide the bridge was necessary.

explanation.text: Ice on the East River stopped ferry service for days, showing how vulnerable travel between the cities was. That disruption persuaded Brooklyn's mayor and other leaders that a bridge was necessary.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which detail from the article would be most important to include in a summary? A John Roebling graduated from a technical school in Germany. B John Roebling bought a large plot of farm land in Pennsylvania. Cc John Roebling had a son who was promoted to colonel in the Civil War. D John Roebling made the first iron cable used in the United States.

explanation.text: Roebling's creation of the first iron wire cable in the United States made stronger modern suspension bridges possible and shaped his later work. This major innovation therefore belongs in a summary more than biographical details.

exams.title: New York Grade 6 ELA - 2018 Released Questions
exams.description: Practice the multiple-choice questions released from the 2018 New York State Grade 6 English Language Arts Test.
exams.sourceTitle: 2018 NYS Grade 6 English Language Arts Test Released Questions
