Official passage for Questions 29-35. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. What does paragraph 5 reveal about Paige? A _ She fears going out on the lake. B She wants to avoid her grandparents. Cc She prefers the outdoors to coming inside. D _ She wants to play with the neighborhood girls.

explanation.text: When Paige hears that her grandparents have arrived, she whispers "Rats," lowers her head, and reluctantly walks back. Her disappointment reveals that she had been trying to avoid spending time with them.

questions.alt: Question 2. How do paragraphs 8 through 10 develop the plot of the story? A They give background information about Paige. B_ They illustrate Paige's internal conflict. C They explain why Paige admires her Grandpa. D They show how Paige and her brothers are alike.

explanation.text: Grandpa invites Paige fishing, while she dreads the outing and uses the heat as an excuse instead of stating her true feelings. This exchange develops the plot by showing her internal conflict between what she wants and what will please him.

questions.alt: Question 3. Read the sentence from paragraph 14 below. Across from the kitchenette stood the bathroom, which contributed to the monster’s bad case of morning breath. What does the metaphor mean in the sentence? A __ The RV had a rotten smell. B People slept poorly inside the RV. C __ The RV wasa cramped place. D__ People made a mess inside the RV.

explanation.text: The narrator imagines the RV as a monster, so its "morning breath" refers to the odor coming from the dirty kitchenette and nearby bathroom. This metaphor means that the inside of the RV smells rotten.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which detail signals a change in the direction of the story? A __ Grandpa loans Paige a fishing pole. B_ Paige warns her family about the heat. Cc Grandpa and Grandma arrive in their RV. D Paige and the girls beat the boys in a water fight.

explanation.text: The story begins with Paige enjoying a neighborhood water fight, but her grandparents' RV brings the unwanted prospect of another fishing trip. Their arrival therefore shifts the story toward its central conflict.

questions.alt: Question 5. How does the author most develop Grandpa's point of view in the story? A __ by having the narrator describe Grandpa B__bysharing Grandpa's thoughts with the reader C by including dialogue between Grandpa and the kids D by showing how Grandpa acts with Paige’s brothers

explanation.text: Grandpa's dialogue shows that he thinks it is never too hot to fish, believes Paige loves the boat, and later calls the outing fun. His own words reveal his enthusiastic view of fishing more directly than the narrator's descriptions do.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A_ Paige loses a lot of fishing poles. B Grandpa owns many different hats. C Paige enjoys water-skiing and tubing. D Grandpa wants to take the kids fishing.

explanation.text: Grandpa's desire to take the children fishing causes Paige's main conflict and leads to the trip that occupies the rest of the story. That event belongs in a summary because it is central rather than a minor detail about poles, hats, or water sports.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which sentence best expresses the theme of the story? A People usually change as they grow older. B Sometimes people are embarrassed by family. Cc People often cherish their childhood memories. D__ Sometimes people make choices to please others.

explanation.text: Paige dreads fishing and tries to avoid the trip, yet she goes rather than openly disappoint her grandfather. Her reluctant choice supports the theme that people sometimes do things they do not want to do in order to please someone else.

questions.alt: Question 22. How do paragraphs 3 and 4 contribute to the story? A They help show Rakhee'’s kindness and ease around creatures in the outdoors. B They help the reader understand Rakhee’s concern of whether her grandmother will remember her. C they provide evidence for Rakhee’s love of singing Indian songs as a child. D They give the reader insight into Rakhee’s memories of her grandmother.

explanation.text: Rakhee recalls her grandmother singing an ant song, letting ants tickle her hand, and secretly releasing them later. These scenes provide insight because they show the warm but incomplete memories Rakhee has kept of her grandmother.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which important idea does the author develop in paragraphs 7 through 9? A __ Rakhee has lived a very exciting life. B Rakhee wishes she had grown up in India. Cc Rakhee has much to learn about her family’s culture. D _ Rakhee’s family is typical of Indian families.

explanation.text: Rakhee's mother must explain the extended family, Indian household customs, and why she changes into a sari and bindi before arriving home. These unfamiliar details show that Rakhee still has much to learn about her family's culture.

questions.alt: Question 24. Which statement best describes how the narrator reacts to the events in paragraphs 8 and 9? A __ She becomes more appreciative of her mother. B_ She is uneasy about the change in her mother. C She becomes confused by her mother. D She admires the change in her mother.

explanation.text: Rakhee says she loves her mother's transformation and describes the sari, loose hair, and younger, more natural appearance with admiration. Her sincere statement that her mother looks beautiful confirms that she admires the change.

questions.alt: Question 25. In paragraph 9, what is the meaning of the phrase “it streamed down her back in a lustrous river”? A her long hair was flowing and shiny B her long hair was damp from being in a bun Cc her long hair seemed heavier than normal D her long hair moved steadily in one direction

explanation.text: The image of a "lustrous river" compares the mother's released hair to shining water flowing down her back. The comparison therefore means that her long hair looks smooth, flowing, and bright.

questions.alt: Question 26. What does paragraph 9 most reveal about the narrator’s mother? A that she is more talkative when she is in India B that she follows customary traditions when she is in India Cc that she is more self-conscious when she is in India D that she visits many people when she is in India

explanation.text: Rakhee's mother changes from American clothes into a sari, adds a bindi, and releases her hair before meeting the family. Her explanation that she cannot arrive dressed like an American shows that she follows customary traditions in India.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which statement best states a theme of the story? A Families can be surprising. B Beauty can be found in most things. C Traveling to new places can be tiring. D___Newexperiences can change how we see the world.

explanation.text: India exposes Rakhee to unfamiliar family customs, a new side of her mother, and everyday sights such as cows and elephants in the road. Her changing impressions support the theme that new experiences can alter how people see others and the world.

questions.alt: Question 28. How does the author most develop Rakhee’s point of view? A__ bycomparing Rakhee’s experience to her mother’s B by having Rakhee describe her impressions of India C__ by showing Rakhee’s alarm during the scene with the elephant D by including Rakhee’s reaction to Amma’s changed appearance

explanation.text: As the first-person narrator, Rakhee repeatedly describes what she notices and feels about India's landscape, people, customs, and animals. This develops her point of view because readers experience the unfamiliar country through her personal impressions.

questions.alt: Question 29. How is the idea “Lightning begins inside thunderstorms” (paragraph 2) developed in the article? A __ bycomparing a lightning bolt to a small spark B by describing how lightning occurs in different types of weather C__byexplaining how air at different temperatures creates a charge in clouds D by providing examples of how much electricity is produced by electrons jumping through air

explanation.text: The article traces how warm updrafts lift raindrops while cold downdrafts lower ice particles, causing differently charged regions to form in a storm cloud. This explanation develops the idea by showing how changing air temperatures create the electrical charge that becomes lightning.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read this quotation from paragraph 6. Between 1,500 and 2,000 thunderstorms are crackling and booming around the world at this very moment... Why does the author use the words “crackling and booming” instead of “occurring”? A to help the reader experience the speed of lightning B_ to help the reader imagine the sounds of thunderstorms C tobe clear about the electrical nature of lightning D tobe accurate about how common thunderstorms are

explanation.text: The words "crackling" and "booming" imitate the sharp and deep noises associated with lightning and thunder. They are more effective because they help readers hear and imagine the storms in a way that the neutral word "occurring" cannot.

questions.alt: Question 31. How does the illustration support the author’s claims in the section “Lightning around the World”? A _ byhighlighting the size of lightning strikes in particular areas B by providing evidence of why lightning strikes in some areas YP 8 'Yy 48 8 C.__ byillustrating which areas are more affected by lightning strikes y 8 D __byshowing the limited areas in the world where lightning strikes occur y s 8) 8

explanation.text: The map uses darker shading to mark places that receive more lightning and highlights hotspots such as central Africa and Florida. This visual evidence illustrates which parts of the world are more affected by lightning strikes.

questions.alt: Question 32. How does the section “Lightning Safety” connect to the section “What is Lightning?” A “Lightning Safety” summarizes the effects of lightning described in “What is Lightning?” B “Lightning Safety” contrasts different types of lightning described in “What is Lightning?” Cc “Lightning Safety” elaborates on the cause of the powerful lightning described in “What is Lightning?” D “Lightning Safety” describes ways to avoid the powerful lightning explained in “What is Lightning?”

explanation.text: "What Is Lightning?" explains the enormous heat, energy, and electrical power of a strike. "Lightning Safety" connects to that information by giving practical ways to avoid or take shelter from the danger it creates.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which sentence best expresses a central idea in the article? A Lightning strikes are only about as thick as a pencil. B__Lightning strikes are uncommon far out over the ocean. C Lightning strikes are due to unstable weather conditions. D Lightning strikes are able to travel through pipes in a building.

explanation.text: The article explains that thunderstorms form electrical charges and that collisions between warm and cool air create lightning hotspots. Because this relationship appears across the explanation and world examples, unstable weather conditions causing lightning is a central idea.

questions.alt: Question 34. What does paragraph 9 suggest about the author's point of view in the article? A __ Theauthor has great respect for lightning. B The author has difficulty understanding lightning. C the author believes that lightning can be useful. D The author believes that it is impossible to avoid lightning.

explanation.text: After warning readers about lightning's danger, the author ends by inviting them to enjoy the "awesome beauty of Earth's electricity" from safety. That admiring language reveals respect and awe for lightning's power.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which idea would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A “The farther away lightning strikes, the deeper the sound of the thunder . .” (paragraph 5) B “Lightning zaps the remote mountain village of Kifuka, in central Africa, nearly every day.’ (paragraph 6) Cc “Because of its unpredictability and power, lightning can be extremely dangerous.” (paragraph 8) D “You're safe inside the car because electricity will travel over the metal surface instead of through the interior” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: The article explains lightning's immense power, unpredictable distribution, and the precautions people should take around it. The statement that those qualities make lightning extremely dangerous captures a major idea, while the other choices are narrower supporting facts.

Official passage for Questions 22-28. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 29-35. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 15. Which phrase best describes how the article develops the idea presented in paragraph 2? A __ by providing counterarguments B__by offering solutions to the problem C by discussing personal experiences D by introducing research results

explanation.text: Paragraph 2 introduces the idea that important brain development occurs while teenagers sleep. The article develops it through research results because studies and specialists explain sleep's effects on attention, mental health, immunity, and brain maturation.

questions.alt: Question 16. The use of quotation marks around the word “relaxing” in paragraph 4 suggests that A some activities hinder true relaxation B deep sleep helps one experience true relaxation C _ teens do not value activities that give true relaxation D blue light helps one experience true relaxation

explanation.text: The quotation marks signal that screen activities only seem relaxing; their blue light makes the brain respond as if it were daytime and delays sleep. This irony suggests that some supposed relaxation actually prevents true rest.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which statement best describes how the section “Hello, Night Owl!” supports a central idea of the article? A It describes an important cause and effect relationship. B It creates an effective comparison and contrast between ideas. C It provides evidence that disproves a popular theory. D It presents a counterargument to the initial claim.

explanation.text: The section explains that busy schedules, screen light, online emotions, caffeine, and a shifted circadian rhythm all delay teenagers' sleep. By linking those causes to later bedtimes and less sleep, it develops an important cause-and-effect relationship.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which idea would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A “Screens for television, games, computers, tablets, e-readers, and cell phones give off blue light.” (paragraph 4) B “Caffeine from sodas and energy drinks makes matters worse.” (paragraph 6) c “For one thing, lack of sleep makes it harder to pay attention.” (paragraph 10) D “One study found that the more sleep people got, the more likely people were to find them attractive.” (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: A major part of the article explains the broad consequences of insufficient sleep for teenagers' learning, health, and development. Difficulty paying attention belongs in a summary because it is one of those central effects, unlike the narrower supporting details in the other choices.

questions.alt: Question 19. Which claim from the article is most strongly supported with evidence? A “Even without modern technology, though, teens shift their circadian rhythm.’ (paragraph 7) B “The rest of the world doesn't shift, however.” (paragraph 9) C “Lack of sleep could hurt mental health” (paragraph 12) D “Lots of issues remain for sleep researchers to explore.” (paragraph 20)

explanation.text: The mental-health claim is followed by research showing an inverse relationship between sleep and depression or other mental illnesses. As teenagers' sleep decreased, those risks increased, which provides direct evidence for the claim.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which sentence from the article best shows the author's point of view? A “Blame your brain, at least in part.” (paragraph 1) B “And that’s a serious public health problem? (paragraph 9) C “Crankiness can result, especially if you don't feel well? (paragraph 14) D “And you can't easily change your body’s natural circadian rhythm.” (paragraph 21)

explanation.text: Calling teenage sleep loss "a serious public health problem" is an evaluation rather than a neutral fact. This wording reveals the author's view that the issue is important and harmful to society.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which paragraph best summarizes a central idea from the article? A paragraph 1 B_ sparagraph 3 C paragraph 20 D paragraph 21

explanation.text: Paragraph 20 acknowledges unanswered research questions but states the clear conclusion that teenagers' brains need sleep. It best summarizes the central idea because it gives the article's overall finding rather than one cause or recommendation.

questions.alt: Question 22. What does the phrase “collect his thoughts” tell the reader about Ohkwa’ri in paragraph 1? A He is memorizing a speech. B He is expecting an argument. Cc He is hesitant to express disagreement. D He is preparing to say something important.

explanation.text: Ohkwa'ri pauses while Big Tree gives him time to "collect his thoughts" before he makes his request about building a lodge. The phrase means he is organizing his ideas because he is preparing to say something important.

questions.alt: Question 23. How does the forest setting shape the actions that are described in paragraph 11? A The setting causes a person to move quickly. B The setting requires a person to be very careful. C The setting requires a person to be extremely quiet. D_ The setting causes a person to behave nervously.

explanation.text: At a forest stream, bending down to drink could let an enemy or dangerous animal approach unseen, while deeper water must be reached safely. These hazards require a person to stay watchful and carefully use a cup.

questions.alt: Question 24. Which important idea does the author develop in paragraph 13? A __ Respecting privacy is a problem in the community. B Sharing helps people in the community. C Acquiring wealth is valued in the community. D Hiding useful objects prevents loss for the community.

explanation.text: The sumac blowpipe is kept in plain sight because it can help more than one person, allowing anyone in the village to borrow and return it. This custom develops the idea that sharing useful objects benefits the whole community.

questions.alt: Question 25. Which quotation best supports a central idea of the story? A “He could help his uncle finish hollowing the bowl by blowing on the coal through the thin hollow branch of a sumac.” (paragraph 5) B “Now I only have to smooth the inside and this cup will be ready to use.” (paragraph 6) Cc “Tt was useful, but it was better to have something that was useful and beautiful” (paragraph 12) D “It would be a good way to learn, a good way to make myself tougher and stronger.’ (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: Ohkwa'ri says that building and sometimes sleeping in his own lodge would help him learn and become tougher and stronger. This goal supports the story's central idea about preparing for adulthood and greater responsibility.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A Ohkwarri helps his uncle Big Tree make a fine drinking cup. B — Ohkwarri keeps his personal things under his bed in a box. Cc Ohkwa’ri blows on the coal through a sumac branch. D — Ohkwa’ri knows how to safely obtain water from a river.

explanation.text: Most of the story first shows Ohkwa'ri helping Big Tree make and finish a carefully crafted drinking cup. That shared work is important to a summary because it establishes their relationship and the lesson about becoming capable and mature.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which paragraph best shows a change in the direction of the plot of the story? A paragraph 6 B paragraph 7 C paragraph 12 D paragraph 15

explanation.text: Before paragraph 15, the story focuses on making the cup and explaining community customs. The plot changes direction because Ohkwa'ri's announcement that he wants to build a lodge introduces his readiness to grow up and live independently.

questions.alt: Question 28. How does the author most develop Ohkwa’ri’s point of view in the story? A__ bycontrasting him with his uncle B_ by showing how he imitates his uncle C _byrevealing his thoughts about growing up D by describing his impatience to live on his own

explanation.text: The narration reveals Ohkwa'ri's private thoughts about the skills he lacks, his boy's cup, and his plan to prepare for living on his own. These thoughts develop his point of view by showing how strongly he wants to become capable and mature.

questions.alt: Question 29. Paragraph 2 mainly contributes to a central idea of the article because it A __ shows that Napoleon III was an unpopular leader B gives the exact number of purposes for the gift C describes the loyalty of the French people D _ explains both reasons for the gift

explanation.text: Paragraph 2 says the monument would strengthen the bond between France and America and declare France's devotion to liberty and equality after Napoleon III. It contributes to the central idea because it explains both purposes behind the proposed gift.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read this sentence from paragraph 3. Bartholdi wrote that the seed for the Statue of Liberty was sown at the party that night. The words “the seed for the Statue of Liberty was sown’ refer to the A timetable for building the statue B first ideas about the project C plan for funding the project D design for the statue

explanation.text: Sowing a seed is the beginning of later growth. The metaphor refers to the project's first ideas because the discussion at the party eventually developed into the Statue of Liberty plan.

questions.alt: Question 31. Read this sentence from paragraph 6. He hoped to drum up enthusiasm for the project as well as find an appealing location to display the work. The use of the phrase “drum up” shows that Bartholdi needed to A___ discover the best place for exhibiting the completed project B reduce the cost of the project C create a widespread public demand for the project D teach the public about the reason for the project

explanation.text: Bartholdi traveled to learn how Americans felt about the project and tried to increase their enthusiasm. Thus, "drum up" means creating broad public interest and demand for the proposed monument.

questions.alt: Question 32. Why was Bedloe’s Island selected for the site of the Statue of Liberty? A The local people already knew about the history of the island. B The island was close to a populated city. C The size of the island would make the statue stand out. D The island was located in a busy harbor.

explanation.text: Bedloe's Island stood in active New York Harbor, where the monument would attract attention and greet immigrants entering the country. Its busy harbor location therefore made it ideal for the statue's intended visibility and meaning.

questions.alt: Question 33. Which evidence from paragraph 11 best supports the author’s claim that the fund-raising efforts for the Statue of Liberty were “creative”? “Though the original goal of completing the statue for the hundredth A birthday... seemed unlikely, the group still did its best to meet that deadline.” B “Appeals for donations for the statue appeared in the French press...” C — “Banquets and balls were held in several French cities.” D “Bartholdi came up with just enough money to begin work on Lady Liberty”

explanation.text: Instead of relying only on ordinary donation appeals, the organizers held banquets and balls and donated the food and ballroom proceeds. These inventive fundraising events best support the description of their efforts as "creative."

questions.alt: Question 34. Which event showed a change in the attitude of Americans towards the construction of the Statue of Liberty? A President Ulysses S. Grant met with Bartholdi to discuss the project. B People from the United States agreed to support the Franco-American Union. Cc Americans wanted to hear more about the design of the statue from Bartholdi. D Americans learned that the statue was to be a symbol of freedom and democracy.

explanation.text: Americans were initially curious but unwilling to help finance the statue. The Franco-American Union's inclusion of supporters from both countries and its work collecting donations shows that American attitudes shifted toward active support.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which statement best describes a major contribution of Bartholdi toward making the Statue of Liberty a reality? A He met with some of the most famous people in America. B He planned for a monument that would be extremely large. C He remained committed to the project over a long period of time. D He drew sketches to show the way the monument would look when completed.

explanation.text: Bartholdi continued pursuing the monument through years of delay, war, weak American enthusiasm, and financial difficulty. His commitment was a major contribution because it kept the idea alive until the project could attract support and begin construction.

passage.alt: Official passage for Questions 1-7. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.
Official passage for Questions 8-14. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 15-21. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. What does paragraph 4 most reveal about the mother? A _ Sheis tireless. B She is responsible. C She has a hard time relaxing. D She has devotion for animals.

explanation.text: The mother searches for wandering turkeys even after Dad says they will return, and she says she takes care of whatever she has. Her principle and actions reveal that she feels responsible for what belongs to the family.

questions.alt: Question 2. The personification of the sky as “sulky” in paragraph 9 suggests the sky is A _ unpredictable B northern C snowy D gloomy

explanation.text: A sulky person appears dark, unhappy, or moody. Describing the cold-season sky as "sulky and dark" therefore suggests a gloomy appearance rather than snowiness or unpredictability.

questions.alt: Question 3. What does paragraph 9 mainly reveal about the narrator? A _ She thinks of nature as calming. B She is attached to familiar things and is close to her family. C She loves language and has a vivid imagination. D _ She pays attention to yearly patterns.

explanation.text: The narrator delights in the announcer's formal words and turns "spring wheat" into an image of frozen ground, raw air, and dark skies. This imaginative response reveals both her love of language and her vivid imagination.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which idea would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A “We had to sell our wheat this month and not hold it over; that is, we did if I was going to the university that fall” (paragraph 2) B “It might go higher along toward Christmas, but we couldn't wait for that.” (paragraph 2) Cc “She stood there untying her bandanna and I watched her as though I didn’t know her face better than my own.” (paragraph 14) D “I took off my apron and was running across to the barn for the pickup before Dad had taken his hat from behind the door” (paragraph 17)

explanation.text: The family must sell its wheat immediately at a sufficient price to pay for the narrator's university plans. This financial stake is essential to a summary because it explains why the broadcast and price increase matter so much.

questions.alt: Question 5. In paragraph 19, what does the phrase “like a monument” mainly suggest about the elevator? A __Itisa beautiful building. B It is a landmark. C It was designed by an architect. D _Itisan old building.

explanation.text: The elevator rises visibly from the crossroads and, with the railroad station, marks Gotham as a town. Comparing it to a monument suggests that it is a prominent, recognizable landmark.

questions.alt: Question 6. B What is the best definition of “spoiled” as used in paragraph 21? A decreased the value of B made greedy by giving too much C took away an opportunity D made unfit for use

explanation.text: Dad explains that the war limited his college education to one year, then promises that nothing will do the same to Ellen. In this context, "spoiled" means the war took away his opportunity to continue college.

questions.alt: Question 7. How does the author mainly develop the narrator’s point of view in the story? A __by exaggerating how harsh the winter months are B___ by using elements of humor C__byshowing how the narrator interacts with her family D _bysharing the narrator's thoughts

explanation.text: The first-person narration gives direct access to Ellen's reflections on the harvest, the broadcast's language, her parents, and her hopes for college. This develops her point of view because readers learn how she interprets events through her private thoughts.

questions.alt: Question 8. Read this sentence from paragraph 1. Mr. Baum’s stories were pure fantasy, so when he walked down the street in his finely tailored suit, children clamored in his wake. What is the effect of the author's word choices in this sentence? A ___Itindicates how much children admired Baum and his stories. B_ It hints that Baum was a serious person who told silly stories. C_ It suggests that children moved aside when Baum was near. D _Itshows how rarely Baum told his stories to others.

explanation.text: The children "clamored" around Baum and followed in his wake whenever he walked through town. Those energetic words indicate that they eagerly sought him out because they admired him and his fantasy stories.

questions.alt: Question 9. Read this sentence from paragraph 2. Unlike stories told by parents, Baum’s were not merely lectures in disguise. Based on this sentence, what is the author’s point of view about Baum’s stories? A They are good at providing a strong moral. B They are more entertaining than educational. C They are different from children’s stories of today. D They are enjoyed more by children than by adults.

explanation.text: The author contrasts disguised lectures with Baum's colorful fantasies in which everyday objects come alive. The stories seem more entertaining than educational because their purpose is imaginative delight rather than delivering a lesson.

questions.alt: Question 10. How does paragraph 3 develop a central idea in the article? A _bylisting characters that appeared in Baum’s books B_ by describing the work for which Baum is best known C _ byexplaining that other authors continued Baum’s work D __byemphasizing that many books Baum wrote were forgotten

explanation.text: Paragraph 3 centers on The Wonderful Wizard of Oz, Baum's thirteen later Oz books, their many characters, and the series' lasting appeal. It develops the central idea because those details describe the work for which Baum became best known.

questions.alt: Question 11. In paragraph 4, what inference can be drawn from the information about Baum’s early childhood? A __ Baum’s health problems affected how he felt about his family. B __Baum’s older siblings influenced his approach toward education. Cc Baum’s home education affected how he felt about other children. D___Baum’s constant reading of fairy tales influenced his later writing.

explanation.text: Because illness made Baum a sedentary and solitary child, he read fairy tales constantly while being educated at home. His later career creating fantasy worlds suggests that this early reading strongly influenced his writing.

questions.alt: Question 12. Read this sentence from paragraph 6. No road to success was ever more winding than Baum’s. How does this sentence contribute to the structure of the article? A __ It summarizes the first five paragraphs of the article. B__It contrasts the first half of the article with the second half. C__Itserves as the topic sentence for the remainder of the article. D__Itgives the cause for the events in the remainder of the article.

explanation.text: The "winding" road announces a pattern of changing jobs, setbacks, moves, failed businesses, and eventual success as an author. It functions as a topic sentence because the remaining paragraphs trace that indirect route.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which sentence states a central idea of the article? A ___ Baum was devoted to his wife and family. B Baum wrote a play and acted in the starring role. C Baum lived for several years in a small prairie town. D Baum had to overcome many difficulties throughout his life.

explanation.text: Baum faces childhood illness, uncertain acting work, lost family money, business failures, and years of professional dead ends before succeeding. Because obstacles recur throughout the biography, overcoming many difficulties is a central idea rather than one isolated fact.

questions.alt: Question 14. What benefit did Baum’s stories offer to listeners, readers, and to Baum himself? A They offered an entertaining distraction from the harsh realities of life. B They offered a chance to invent new and exciting characters and places. C They offered an exciting way to learn about the world around them. D They offered a new perspective on how adults and children should behave.

explanation.text: Prairie children use Baum's fantasies to escape lives of bleakness and hard work, while the broke Baum returns to fantasy after his store fails. For both creator and audience, the stories provide an entertaining refuge from harsh real-life conditions.

questions.alt: Question 15. What does paragraph 2 most reveal about Eliza? A _ She has a strong imagination. B_ She seldom misspells a word. Cc She thinks her father knows best. D She gets easily distracted.

explanation.text: Eliza experiences each letter as glowing color, shape, movement, warmth, or touch while watching it transform into the next letter. This elaborate inner imagery reveals that she has a powerful imagination.

questions.alt: Question 16. In paragraph 12, how does the description of the nightmare mainly affect the tone of the story? A __It suggests that Eliza doubts she will win the bee. B__It suggests that the bee seems unreal to Eliza. C It suggests that Eliza is unwilling to compete in the bee. D__It suggests that the bee is stressful for Eliza.

explanation.text: Eliza remembers expectant silence, the feeling of stopping time, and an endless walk to the microphone as parts of a nightmare. The tone becomes tense because these anxious images make the spelling bee feel intensely stressful to her.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which quotation best expresses a central idea of the story? “She wants to win with a word so difficult her father will have to admit that he was wrong, that the letters are already guiding her” (paragraph 1) B “Eliza finds herself bracing for the next blow, but none comes.” (paragraph 10) C “The judge's voice is ever neutral, revealing nothing” (paragraph 21) D ‘Eliza wants to see herself through the judge’s eyes.” (paragraph 22)

explanation.text: Eliza's desire to win a difficult word, prove her father wrong, and trust the guiding letters establishes the conflict that drives the scene. The quotation therefore expresses the central idea of her struggle to turn her unusual inner process into success.

questions.alt: Question 18. The ideas in paragraphs 22 and 26 mostly contribute to the reader’s understanding of Eliza by revealing A her inability to understand the judges B_ her discomfort standing in front of the audience C _ her concern about impressing her father D her difficulty with spelling the assigned word correctly

explanation.text: Eliza wonders how the judge sees her, struggles to clear distracting images from her mind, and must force the first letter to appear. These details reveal her difficulty using her method to spell the assigned word correctly.

questions.alt: Question 19. What does paragraph 31 mainly reveal about Eliza? A her patience B her intelligence Cc her confidence D _ her enthusiasm

explanation.text: Eliza interprets the long silence as proof that she is correct and already imagines the famous host and trophy as hers. Her certainty that she has succeeded reveals confidence just before the bell proves her wrong.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A _ Eliza knows that difficult words follow easy words at spelling bees. B_ Eliza gets a word that means a kind of fabric with a twill weave. C Eliza empties her mind and sees the letters come up as images. D Eliza is one of seventeen spellers in this round of the spelling bee.

explanation.text: Eliza's unusual method of emptying her mind and seeing letters transform is central to both her earlier success and her final spelling attempt. It belongs in a summary because it drives the main action and conflict, unlike details about the round or word definition.

questions.alt: Question 21. How does the point of view most influence the tone of the story? A The descriptions of the contestants competing builds suspense. B The references to unusual words add humor. C __ Eliza’s distant observations of her father develop conflict. D Eliza's interior monologue creates a sense of strangeness.

explanation.text: The narration stays close to Eliza as she watches contestants freeze, struggle, succeed, or leave and then anticipates her own turn. Her descriptions of the competition build suspense because every performance raises the stakes for what she must do next.

questions.alt: Question 7. In paragraph 3, when the author says that "Neil felt like the universe was calling him," 7 she is referring to how A he heard the booming voice inside the Hayden Planetarium on his first visit B he was delighted by seeing the stars inside the Hayden Planetarium C his experiences looking at the stars made him want to learn more about astronomy D he wanted to spend more time in the countryside because he could see more of the sky

explanation.text: Neil's visits to the planetarium and the dark Pennsylvania sky make him realize how many real stars exist. Saying the universe was "calling him" means these experiences inspired him to learn more about astronomy.

questions.alt: Question 8. Paragraph 6 of the passage supports paragraph 3 by showing that Neil 8 A saw the same things at home that he saw at the planetarium B wanted to return to Pennsylvania to use his new binoculars C continued his interest in learning about the universe D tried to share his interest in stars with his parents

explanation.text: Paragraph 3 says Neil wanted to know more about the stars after seeing them clearly. Paragraph 6 supports that idea because he uses binoculars, studies the moon's craters, and wants an even better view.

questions.alt: Question 9. g What do paragraphs 6 through 8 show about Neil's parents? A They work hard to give Neil everything he wants. B They are supportive of Neil's interests. C They set good examples for Neil to follow. D They want Neil to become a scientist.

explanation.text: Neil's parents buy his first telescope and later pay half the cost of a more powerful one after he saves the rest. These actions show that they support his interest in studying the sky.

questions.alt: Question 10. The information in the section "LEARNING ABOUT THE UNIVERSE" adds to the 10 information in the rest of the passage by showing how Neil A finally became a successful student B made new discoveries with his telescope C made choices that helped him become an astrophysicist D earned money to pay for his education as an astrophysicist

explanation.text: Neil takes astronomy courses, studies with his telescope, chooses a science high school, and attends space camp. These choices show how he turns an early interest in stars into the beginning of a career as an astrophysicist.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which sentence best supports the main idea of the passage? A "Not long after this, Neil and his family took a trip to Pennsylvania." (paragraph 3) B "Neil lived with his parents, his older brother, and his younger sister in a tall building called the Skyview Apartments." (paragraph 4) C "In fact, his third-grade teacher wrote a note on his report card." (paragraph 5) D "He often took his telescope to the roof of his apartment to study the night sky." (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The passage mainly traces Neil's lasting interest in the universe and the work he does to become an astrophysicist. Taking his telescope to the roof to study the night sky directly supports that main idea.

questions.alt: Question 12. 1 2 According to the passage, which sentence best describes Neil? A He is serious about reaching his goals. B He is tired from working different jobs. C He is proud of his success in school. D He is happy to help his family earn money.

explanation.text: Neil walks dogs and saves money for a stronger telescope, then continues taking courses and studying the sky. His repeated effort demonstrates that he is serious about reaching his goal of becoming an astrophysicist.

questions.alt: Question 19. g Which sentence best explains why Hayley is chosen to perform in the talent show? 1 A Other students think she is cool. B She plays the ukulele well in the tryouts. C Her act is more unusual than others'. D Her great-great aunt used to play music.

explanation.text: Hayley practices before the tryouts, and Mr. Y gives her a thumbs-up and tells her she is in the show. This evidence indicates that she is chosen because she plays the ukulele well enough to pass the tryout.

questions.alt: Question 20. Why are paragraphs 4 through 7 important to the story? A They show how accepting Mr. Y is. B They describe how confused Tilly is. C They describe how worried Hayley's dad is. D They show how supportive Hayley's family is.

explanation.text: Hayley's parents cheer when she makes the show, and her father confidently predicts that she will be a star. These reactions are important because they show that her family believes in and supports her.

questions.alt: Question 21. Which statement about the talent show is true? 21 A Most students like Hayley's talent show act the best. B The talent show has performers from different grades. C Some performers canceled their acts in the talent show. D Hayley knows all the other performers in the talent show.

explanation.text: The story mentions two fifth graders, some fourth graders, and a kindergartener among the acts. This evidence confirms that students from different grades perform in the talent show.

questions.alt: Question 22. 2 2 Which paragraph does the illustration best support? A paragraph 10 B paragraph 11 C paragraph 13 D paragraph 15

explanation.text: The illustration shows Hayley onstage playing her ukulele and making a big performing gesture before a cheering crowd. This scene matches paragraph 13, which describes her song and her guitar-hero arm swing.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which happens as a result of Hayley's performance in the talent show? 2 3 A Many students respect Hayley's musical ability. B Many students learn to play the ukulele. C Skeeter starts a band for ukulele players. D Hayley understands how to be a celebrity.

explanation.text: After Hayley performs, other students tell her she is cool and ask if they can join her band. Their response shows that her successful performance earns their respect for her musical ability.

questions.alt: Question 24. Which sentence best states a central message of the story? A Learning to do something new takes time. B Making mistakes is a part of growing up. C Conquering fear can lead to success. D Sharing with friends brings happiness.

explanation.text: Hayley first feels sick with fear and wants to escape, but she takes a breath, becomes confident, and performs. The applause that follows demonstrates the central message that overcoming fear can lead to success.

questions.alt: Question 25. 2 5 What is Kathryn's first clue that Nitro is blind? A Nitro seems to be afraid. B Nitro does not know where his food is. C Nitro runs into the fence. D Nitro does not look outside his cage.

explanation.text: Most animals look through the chain-link wall, but Nitro sits facing the concrete walls and follows voices instead of movements. His failure to look outside the cage is Kathryn's first clue because it suggests that he cannot see what is beyond it.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which quotation best states the main idea of the passage? A "'He would sit and stare at the concrete walls,' Kathryn said." (paragraph 3) B "That cage had been so small, Nitro had been able to memorize every inch." (paragraph 5) C "'We just don't know what caused his condition,' Kathryn said." (paragraph 7) D "They had to help a blind tiger find his way, without the use of his eyes." (paragraph 8)

explanation.text: The passage centers on the caretakers' challenge of teaching a blind tiger to move safely through a large new home. The statement in paragraph 8 best expresses that main idea because it names both Nitro's problem and the help he needs.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which detail from the passage best shows that Kathryn cares about Nitro? 2 7 A She puts Nitro into a new cage. B She does not want Nitro to hurt himself. C She thinks Nitro has a great attitude. D She does not understand why Nitro is blind.

explanation.text: Kathryn worries when Nitro walks into the fence and hurts his nose, then helps create scent markers to protect him. Her desire to keep him from injuring himself shows that she cares about his well-being.

questions.alt: Question 28. Which statement from the passage shows that the problem in paragraph 8 was solved? 2 8 A "Nitro walked through his big new home." (paragraph 9) B "He was trying to feel what was ahead of him." (paragraph 9) C "Once he learned where his fences were, the people at the Rescue put down sand pathways." (paragraph 13) D "In time, Nitro learned where every bump, every tree, and every food box was in his new cage." (paragraph 15)

explanation.text: The problem in paragraph 8 is that Nitro cannot use sight to find his way through the new cage. Learning where every bump, tree, and food box is shows that he has solved that problem and can navigate his home.

questions.alt: Question 29. In p aragraph 16, what does the word "mastered" suggest about Nitro? 29 A that he needed a larger space B that he felt comfortable in his space C that he liked people to visit his space D that he wanted to defend his space

explanation.text: By paragraph 16, Nitro knows the location of everything in his cage and can even locate people who stand still. This shows that he has "mastered" the space because he can move through it confidently and comfortably.

questions.alt: Question 30. What causes Nitro to chuffle? 30 A being aware of people B feeling afraid C wanting a treat D having a cough

explanation.text: Nitro knows when people are nearby even though he cannot see them. He chuffles toward them to make them answer, which lets him recognize who is there by the person's voice.

questions.alt: Question 31. 3 l What makes Nitro "a Rescue favorite,, (paragraph 18)? A his reaction to visitors B his response to peppermint C his unusual chuffle D his large paws

explanation.text: Visitors always stop at Nitro's cage, and he responds by coming to the fence and happily chuffling toward people. This friendly, dependable reaction to visitors explains why he has become a favorite at the Rescue.

questions.alt: Question 1. Which sentence from the article best explains why stunt performers are willing to do such a dangerous job? A “For the stunt performers, this is the day’s work.” (paragraph 4) B “But performers enjoy the thrill of their work and push themselves hard to do more spectacular stunts.” (paragraph 5) C “They work hard to make it look as if someone else is doing the stunt.” (paragraph 7) D “This means filming in remote places such as deserts, jungles, and mountains.” (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: Paragraph 5 acknowledges that stunt work is hard and dangerous, but it also says performers enjoy its thrill and push themselves toward harder stunts. Their enjoyment explains why they willingly accept the job's risks.

questions.alt: Question 2. Based on paragraphs 10 through 12, what must stunt actors do to train for their jobs? A They must work to get their bodies ready for action and in good shape. B They must live in far off places. C They must learn to be either a gymnast or a martial artist. D They must become specialists in horse riding, working with explosives, and scuba diving.

explanation.text: Paragraph 10 says stunt actors must be fit and strong and often use gymnastics or martial arts to develop flexibility and learn safe falls. These examples support the broader answer that they train their bodies for action and good physical condition.

questions.alt: Question 3. Based on the article, why do some stunt actors spend long periods of time away from home? A They need to live in different parts of the world to be able to help the actors. B They need to hike and climb in deserts, jungles, and mountains to help them stay in shape. C They need to travel to the different places where movies and television shows are filmed. D They need to go to different places to learn new skills from experts.

explanation.text: Stunt actors work wherever a movie or television program is being made, including remote deserts, jungles, and mountains. Because filming can continue far from home for weeks or months, the performers must travel and stay there too.

questions.alt: Question 4. _ Based on the article, music helps circus performers by A calming their fears when they are in front of an audience B reminding them of home when they are performing in new places C letting them know when to begin and end parts of their shows D allowing them to relax during difficult stunts

explanation.text: Paragraph 13 explains that circus acts are performed to music and that its rhythm gives performers cues for each section. Those cues let performers know when to begin and end the different parts of an act.

questions.alt: Question 5. How do paragraphs 15 through 19 support the author’s main points? A They show that stunt performing has too many dangers. B They give details about how stunt performers train their bodies. C They show how stunt performing is something everyone can do. D They give details about the types of danger stunt performers face.

explanation.text: Paragraphs 15 through 19 name falls, sports injuries, fires, explosions, and accidents as risks of stunt work. These examples support the author's points by giving specific details about the dangers performers face.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which paragraph best supports a main idea of the article? A paragraph 7 B paragraph 10 C paragraph 13 D paragraph 19

explanation.text: The article mainly explains the work and lives of stunt actors and circus performers. Paragraph 7 best supports that idea because it identifies both kinds of performers and describes the important difference between their roles.

questions.alt: Question 19. yg What does paragraph 3 reveal about Ms. Snoops? A She rarely gets great ideas. B She is an organized person. C She sometimes forgets things. D She wishes Ali were her daughter.

explanation.text: Ms. Snoops writes a large "M" as a reminder so that she will not lose her wonderful idea. Even with the note, she cannot remember what the letter means, which reveals that she sometimes forgets things.

questions.alt: Question 20. 9 Which detail best reveals what Ali wants to be when she grows up? A Ali thinks of things starting with “M” to try to help Ms. Snoops remember. B Ali collects old objects to show to Ms. Snoops. C Ms. Snoops explains to Ali that making things up is what writers do. D Ms. Snoops thanks Ali for bringing her treasures.

explanation.text: Ali later says that she plans to become an archaeologist, someone who studies objects from the past. Her habit of collecting old objects and asking about their histories best reveals that interest.

questions.alt: Question 21. According to the story, what does the phrase “fill in the holes myself” (paragraph 16) mean? A rely on memory to finish a true story B find evidence for the most likely explanation for a story C ask someone questions to figure out the whole story D create details to complete an unfinished story

explanation.text: Ms. Snoops says she would gather pieces of old stories and then "fill in the holes" herself. Her next comment that writers make things up shows that the phrase means creating details to complete an unfinished story.

questions.alt: Question 22. Which sentence expresses something important that Ali and Ms. Snoops have in common? A They both like old-fashioned fruit preserves. B The icy-blue stone is a mystery to both of them. C They both enjoy making up stories. D Shirley the doll means a lot to both of them.

explanation.text: Ms. Snoops says she enjoyed old stories and invented missing details, and Ali replies that she likes to do the same thing. Ali also admits that making things up can be fun, which shows that both characters enjoy creating stories.

questions.alt: Question 23. Which theme is best supported by paragraph 21? A Childhood toys should be kept forever. B Important lessons can be learned from the past. C Nothing brings more joy than to talk with a friend. D Something that seems worthless may be special to someone.

explanation.text: The rusty tin and broken doll head may look like discarded objects, but Ms. Snoops recognizes the doll as dear Shirley and reacts with delight. This contrast supports the theme that an object that seems worthless can be special to someone.

questions.alt: Question 24. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A “Ms. Snoops went to her desk and brought back a sheet of paper marked with a big handwritten ‘M.” (paragraph 3) B “Maybe you were thinking of baking your delicious orange muffins. You haven't made those in a while.” (paragraph 4) C “I would bet dollars to doughnuts this was one of Pug’s stones. He collected unusual ones.” (paragraph 12) D “All these treasures you've shown me have brought back my memories, and I am so grateful.” (paragraph 24)

explanation.text: Ali's treasures restore Ms. Snoops's memories and help her remember her plan to write memoirs about Orange Street. This detail belongs in a summary because it states the central result of their visit and resolves the forgotten-"M" mystery.

questions.alt: Question 25. In paragraph 2, the details about Larry Greene are important because they suggest why A coaches need running experience B his book is full of good ideas for running C young people need special training D his advice about training can be trusted

explanation.text: Larry Greene is an exercise science expert, a former professional distance runner and coach, and an author on training. These qualifications show that he has knowledge and experience, so readers can trust his running advice.

questions.alt: Question 26. Based on the article, what is the main reason it is helpful to join a team or a club? A You can have fun running with people. B You can improve by working with a coach. C You can learn to change your time goals. D You can learn to run far without getting tired.

explanation.text: A good coach can teach proper form, help prevent injuries, and correct a runner's pace. Joining a team or club is therefore helpful because it gives a runner access to coaching that is difficult to replace alone.

questions.alt: Question 27. Why is the text box “Fun Run” included in the article? A It provides ideas for training that add to suggestions given in the article. B It gives an opinion about training that is different from the rest of the article. C It suggests that a silly approach to running is better than the article’s serious approach. D It provides evidence to support the article’s claim that people can become better runners.

explanation.text: The "Fun Run" box suggests playful movements and new exercises to do after each lap. These are extra training ideas that add to the main article's advice about preparing and gradually building running ability.

questions.alt: Question 28. Read this sentence from paragraph 1. 28 A s your body becomes more conditioned to the exercise, you’ll get the endurance to go even farther and faster. Which paragraph best supports this claim? A paragraph 2 B paragraph 3 C paragraph 5 D paragraph 6

explanation.text: Paragraph 5 tells runners to begin with a short distance and slowly add more over several weeks. This gradual increase supports the claim because it gives the body time to adjust and build the endurance to run farther.

questions.alt: Question 29. B ased on paragraph 6, what does it mean to run “efficiently”? 29 A to stretch and cool down correctly B to use correct speed and form C to train with another person D to avoid any danger

explanation.text: Paragraph 6 advises a partner to check a runner's posture and running form, while the article also stresses proper pacing. In this context, running "efficiently" means using correct speed and form so effort is not wasted.

questions.alt: Question 30. Which sentence best expresses the main idea of the article? 30 A “If your school participates in the President’s Challenge, chances are you have taken the Physical Fitness Test.” (paragraph 1) B “ ‘But to perform your best in the mile run, and to feel good doing it, you really need to prepare properly,’ says Larry Greene.” (paragraph 2) C “ To train for a mile run, start by running a short distance, such as one-quarter mile.” (paragraph 5) D “ ‘Don’t push yourself when running becomes painful,’ Greene says.” (paragraph 6)

explanation.text: The article's sections explain how to prepare with coaching, gradual distance increases, and safe running habits. Greene's statement about preparing properly best expresses that overall idea because it includes both strong performance and feeling good.

questions.alt: Question 31. B ased on information in the text box “First Place Finish!” what can the reader 31 conclude about racing? A Running in races can encourage people to work hard. B C ompeting in races is something every runner must try. C T raining three days a week is necessary to win races. D W inning championship races requires joining a program.

explanation.text: Demian trains regularly, qualifies for a national event, and wins his age group's race after joining a running program. His progress and increased confidence demonstrate that racing goals can encourage a person to work hard.

questions.alt: Question 1. What do details in paragraphs 1 through 5 suggest about Amy and Todd? A Todd has different interests than Amy does. B_ Todd is more focused on school than Amy is. C Todd is more interested in watching TV than Amy is. D Todd has a different reaction to his mother’s voice than Amy does.

explanation.text: Todd enjoys groceries, math problems, and neat calculations, while Amy is absorbed in a book and barely notices her mother enter. These contrasting details show that the siblings have different interests.

questions.alt: Question 2. D What does the word “straggled” in paragraph 5 suggest about Todd, Amy, and their father? A They are trying to keep the mother from seeing the kitchen. B They are determined to finish the work in the kitchen. C They want to keep close together in the kitchen. D They want to avoid going into the kitchen.

explanation.text: The three move slowly into the kitchen, and the narrator imagines their tails tucked between their legs. That guilty, reluctant movement suggests they would rather avoid facing the kitchen and their mother.

questions.alt: Question 3. Why does Todd and Amy’s father refuse to look at the Sixty-Minute Gourmet cookbook? A He thinks that they do not have the skills to make the recipes. B He knows the family does not have the right ingredients. C He knows that the recipes will take too long to make. D He thinks that the mother will dislike the meal.

explanation.text: The family has only sixty minutes for cleaning and cooking, and their father even asks whether a thirty-minute recipe can be shortened to fifteen. Therefore, a sixty-minute recipe would take too long.

questions.alt: Question 4. The narrator's description of the conversation in paragraphs 11 through 13 shows that A the family has different ideas about a balanced diet B Amy knows which foods her mother likes best C Todd has experience with cooking D the father relies on his children’s knowledge

explanation.text: Their father asks how the food pyramid works, and Todd explains its food groups while Amy clarifies what their mother probably expects. This shows the father depends on his children's school knowledge to plan the meal.

questions.alt: Question 5. The illustration best supports the information provided in which paragraph? A paragraph 12 B_ paragraph 13 C paragraph 24 D paragraph 27

explanation.text: Paragraph 12 explains the food pyramid's wide grain base, small fats-and-sugars tip, and other food groups. The illustration visually organizes those same groups and proportions.

questions.alt: Question 6. How does paragraph 28 relate to paragraph 8? A Paragraph 28 provides further details about the problem. B Paragraph 28 shows the result of the mother’s instructions. C Paragraph 28 presents a summary of the tasks that are introduced. D Paragraph 28 explains how the meal is unlike the mother’s request.

explanation.text: Paragraph 8 gives the mother's instructions to clean, put away groceries, and prepare a balanced meal. Paragraph 28 shows the completed result when the father sends Amy to call her mother to dinner.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which theme is supported by the events in the story? A Solving problems becomes easier with daily practice. B_ Challenges can give people opportunities for learning. C Doing work can help people forget their problems. D Feelings of guilt may go away over time.

explanation.text: The mother's demanding task forces the family to solve problems, use the food pyramid, select a recipe, and prepare a full meal. Because of the challenge, they learn practical cooking and planning skills.

questions.alt: Question 8. g How do paragraphs 1 through 4 support a main idea of the article? A by describing how well Clara did at school B_ by showing how Clara’s parents made decisions C by showing Clara’s behavior around other people D by providing details about Clara’s cousins

explanation.text: The opening describes Clara as shy around strangers, unable to speak before a crowded classroom, and physically weakened by the experience. These behaviors therefore establish how strongly other people affected her.

questions.alt: Question 9. Why did Clara return from boarding school? A The people who cared for Clara were concerned about her health. B Clara was younger and smaller than most of the other students. C The teachers thought Clara could learn more at home. D Clara was unhappy because she missed her family.

explanation.text: Clara became pale and lost weight at school, so her parents, teachers, and doctor met to discuss her condition. Their concern for her health led them to decide she should return home.

questions.alt: Question 10. Read this sentence from paragraph 5. But it was a little late to rein Clara in now. What does the phrase “to rein Clara in” suggest? A Clara was too old to play with her cousins. B Clara was often outside after dark. C Clara was determined to learn new things in the country. D Clara was unable to ride horses.

explanation.text: Clara's parents try to restrict her adventurous behavior by forbidding ice skating, but she secretly persuades her cousins to teach her. In context, “rein Clara in” means to restrain her determination to explore and learn.

questions.alt: Question 11. What do paragraphs 5 and 6 show about Clara? A Clara is active and adventurous. B Clara is obedient and intelligent. C Clara is quiet and cooperative. D Clara is creative and serious.

explanation.text: Clara explores streams, climbs onto mill poles, secretly learns to skate despite repeated falls, and practices horseback riding. These energetic risks show that she is active and adventurous.

questions.alt: Question 12. Why is paragraph 9 important for the article? A It explains why Clara was a good caretaker to her brother. B It shows a result of Clara’s caretaking skills. C It suggests why Clara’s great aunt inspired her. D It connects Clara and her desire to care for people to her great aunt.

explanation.text: Paragraph 9 identifies Clara's great-aunt as a midwife who treated illnesses, then explains Clara's satisfaction from nursing David. This connects Clara's growing desire to care for others with a family example of service.

questions.alt: Question 13. How did Clara’s relationship with her brother David most affect her life? A By doing chores for David, Clara realized she enjoyed living at home. B By caring for David when he was injured, Clara developed a desire to help others. C By giving David his medicine, Clara learned about effective medical treatments. D By being home when David fell to the ground, Clara felt responsible for his injuries.

explanation.text: Clara spends two years nursing David and discovers how useful and satisfied the work makes her feel. Because of that experience, she later seeks meaningful ways to help sick and struggling families.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A Clara learned how to ride horses at a young age. B Clara had a great aunt who was a skilled midwife. C Clara was seriously hurt while ice skating with her cousins. D Clara remembered how good it felt to care for her brother.

explanation.text: Clara's lasting memory of how satisfying it felt to nurse David explains her later search for people who needed help. That motivation is central to a summary of her development into a caregiver.

questions.alt: Question 29. What does “take it up a notch” mean as it is used in paragraph 4? A to make more interesting B_ to become more forceful C to complete another level D to make another play

explanation.text: After calmly reviewing fundamentals, Gaters increases the intensity by sharply criticizing sloppy play, even during an easy win. Thus, “take it up a notch” means becoming more forceful.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read this sentence from paragraph 7. That candor might be hard for the members of the girls’ basketball team at John Marshall Metropolitan High School to hear. What idea does this sentence help to support? A that Gaters can be considered impolite B_ that Gaters speaks very softly at times C that Gaters demands a lot from her players D that Gaters is impatient with her players

explanation.text: Gaters openly tells players when their play is sloppy and insists on fundamentals even when they are winning. Such difficult honesty shows that she holds her team to demanding standards.

questions.alt: Question 31. Read this sentence from paragraph 7. They know this is a woman who can take them places. How does the author best support this point? A by explaining how Gaters helps many of her players attend college B_ by describing the long hours of practice Gaters makes her players perform C by providing examples of how Gaters helps players in need D by providing examples of the basketball tips Gaters teaches her players

explanation.text: The article says Pondexter starred at Rutgers and that Gaters helped many other players reach college, one of her proudest achievements. This evidence shows how Gaters can take players beyond high school.

questions.alt: Question 32. Read this sentence from paragraph 21. But though Gaters is tough, it is a tough love, her former players say. Which point in the article does this detail best support? A Gaters has a very strong desire to win so can get upset easily. B_ Gaters is unlikely to retire because she enjoys coaching. C Gaters cares about every aspect of her players’ lives. D Gaters is nice only after practice or games are finished.

explanation.text: Gaters checks grades, calls parents about skipped classes, buys needed clothing, and sometimes takes players out socially. These actions show that her tough love extends to every part of her players' lives.

questions.alt: Question 33. What does paragraph 26 suggest about Gaters? A Gaters is appreciated for her important contributions to girls basketball. B_ Gaters is considered the best high school basketball coach in the country. C Gaters has coached basketball almost as long as more famous coaches. D Gaters has been honored mainly for her work with students off the basketball court.

explanation.text: Photographs show Gaters honored at the White House, inducted into the Women's Basketball Hall of Fame, and recognized with a lifetime achievement award. These honors demonstrate appreciation for her contributions to girls basketball.

questions.alt: Question 34. How do paragraphs 27 through 29 contribute to the article? A by comparing Gaters’s work ethic with Howard’s B_ by showing an effect Gaters has had on Howard C by giving an example of Gaters’s desire to win D by suggesting how Gaters feels about her job

explanation.text: When Gaters mentions retirement, Howard playfully dismisses the idea and says the coach would continue forever if possible. That reaction suggests Gaters remains deeply devoted to and enthusiastic about her work.

questions.alt: Question 35. According to the article, why do so many people respect Gaters? A Gaters teaches her players about more than just basketball. B_ Gaters encourages her players to play college basketball. C Gaters teaches her players about the importance of playing basketball. D Gaters wants her players to concentrate on improving their basketball skills.

explanation.text: Gaters pushes players to finish school, support themselves and their families, attend college, and meet needs off the court. People respect her because she teaches responsibility and life success, not only basketball.

questions.alt: Question 36. What does the simile “like an earthquake” in paragraph 5 help the reader understand? A that volume can strongly affect parts of the ear B that hair cells are easily damaged C how our body is unable to re-grow hair cells D how much damage the ear can take

explanation.text: An earthquake shakes the ground with great force; similarly, loud volume violently shakes the membrane supporting the ear's hair cells. The simile shows how strongly sound can physically affect the ear.

questions.alt: Question 37. How do paragraphs 4 and 5 connect to paragraph 6? A Paragraphs 4 and 5 explain how hearing loss can occur, and paragraph 6 explains how to prevent it. B Paragraphs 4 and 5 show what damage can occur, and paragraph 6 shows how it affects people. C Paragraphs 4 and 5 describe how the ear can be damaged, and paragraph 6 describes how people react to the damage. D Paragraphs 4 and 5 explain why ears get damaged, and paragraph 6 explains why protecting hearing is important.

explanation.text: Paragraphs 4 and 5 explain how high-decibel sounds shake the ear's membrane and permanently break or uproot hair cells. Paragraph 6 then gives safe-volume rules that can prevent this damage.

questions.alt: Question 38. 3g Which evidence best supports a claim made by the author in paragraph 4? A “If others can hear your music, the volume is too high.” (paragraph 6) B “Yahaira admits that sometimes after listening to loud music, her ears make a ringing sound.” (paragraph 7) C “So we need to protect that very special sense.” (paragraph 8) D “Music companies have been deliberately turning up the volume.” (paragraph 9)

explanation.text: Paragraph 4 claims that high-volume MP3 use can damage the ear's delicate hair cells. Yahaira's ringing ears after listening to loud music provide a concrete symptom supporting that claim.

questions.alt: Question 39. According to paragraphs 9 through 12, how is the music business today different from the 39 a business in the 1980s or 1990s? A Today’s music companies control the volume of music more than in the past. B_ Today’s music companies compete against each other more than in the past. C Today’s listeners of music like their music louder than they did in the past. D Today’s sound engineers make music sound clearer than it did in the past.

explanation.text: Modern music companies use compression to raise recordings' overall volume, so a new song sounds louder without changing the player's setting. Older recordings from the 1980s and 1990s were not controlled this way.

questions.alt: Question 40. Which paragraph best explains how loud noises can damage our hearing? A paragraph 1 B_ paragraph 5 C paragraph 7 D paragraph 13

explanation.text: Paragraph 5 traces the damage directly: a loud sound shakes the membrane, then breaks or uproots hair cells that cannot regrow. This mechanism most clearly explains how noise harms hearing.

questions.alt: Question 41. Which information explained in paragraph 13 does the drawing help the reader understand? A the shape of the parts of the ear B the position of the parts of the ear C the order in which sound waves strike the parts of the ear D the size of sound waves when moving through the parts of the ear

explanation.text: Paragraph 13 describes sound moving from the auditory canal to the eardrum, ossicles, cochlea, auditory nerve, and finally the brain. The labeled drawing therefore helps readers follow that order through the ear.

questions.alt: Question 42. Which idea is most important to include in a summary of the article? A Music is not as powerful when played at quiet volumes. B Powerful instruments cause most hearing loss. C Choosing to play music quietly can protect hearing. D Music companies determine safe volume levels.

explanation.text: The article's main warning is that loud music can permanently destroy hair cells and cause hearing loss. Choosing a lower volume therefore directly addresses that danger and protects hearing.
