Official passage for Questions 36-42. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 8. I n line 3, what does the simile “like clouds across the sky” help the reader understand about the horses in Euphemia’s sketches? A They are drawn sloppily. B They look like they are in motion. C They are getting tangled up with each other. D They look like they are trotting through fog.

explanation.text: Clouds drift and flow across the sky, and Euphemia's horses have swirling manes and seem to run freely across the paper. The simile therefore makes the drawings appear full of motion.

questions.alt: Question 9. H ow do lines 14 through 16 contribute to the development of the plot? A They establish Marisa’s problem. B They emphasize Marisa’s hopefulness. C They contrast Marisa’s artistic abilities with Euphemia’s. D They illustrate Marisa’s determination to not let Luke bother her.

explanation.text: Marisa sharpens her pencil, uses a clean sheet, pictures a perfect horse, and feels certain she can finally draw it. These details emphasize her hope that this attempt will succeed.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which phrase best conveys the tone in lines 1 through 30? A “They’re always running wild and free. . .” (lines 2 and 3) B “He also calls me Messy.” (lines 8 and 9) C “Next I roughed in most of the body. . .” (lines 17 and 18) D “I scratched a big X through my earless, macaroni‑legged horse, . . .” (line 26)

explanation.text: Marisa's phrase mocks the horse's failed ears and noodlelike legs as she crosses out and hides the drawing. This self-critical description therefore conveys her frustrated, discouraged tone.

questions.alt: Question 11. Read this sentence from line 32. I brooded about it all through dinner. What effect does the word “brooded” have in the story? A It shows Marisa’s anxiety about her abilities. B It reveals Marisa’s motives for drawing. C It emphasizes how Marisa is growing as a character. D It indicates Marisa has a major decision to make.

explanation.text: Marisa continues dwelling on the failed drawing through dinner instead of letting it go. “Brooded” therefore emphasizes her worry and insecurity about whether she can draw well.

questions.alt: Question 12. H ow do lines 36 through 38 help convey the theme of the story? A They show that some situations take time to change. B They prove that practice can help natural talents to develop. C They suggest that inspiration may come in unexpected ways. D They demonstrate that new ideas will eventually be accepted.

explanation.text: The sunset makes even the familiar, scraggly horses look special, prompting Marisa to sit down and observe them closely. This unexpected beauty therefore becomes the inspiration for her successful drawing.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which sentence best explains why Marisa’s final horse drawing was different than her first tries? A “Everything looked special in that light, even the scraggly horses next door.” (lines 37 and 38) B “I noticed the curves of their muscles, the shadows on their faces, the shine along their backs.” (lines 42 through 44) C “An idea began to take shape in my mind, and just then the cinnamon horse turned its head toward me and nodded three times.” (lines 49 and 50) D “I choose a deep brown, pulling it across my paper in the shape of the chocolate horse.” (line 52)

explanation.text: Because Marisa carefully notices the real horses' curved muscles, facial shadows, and shining backs, she sees their forms accurately and makes the final drawing different.

questions.alt: Question 14. H ow does Marisa change while watching her neighbors’ horses? A She realizes that Euphemia’s horses do not look realistic, so she decides to try to draw better pictures than her friend. B She decides to try a different way of drawing and is proud of her work. C She realizes she can never be an artist like Euphemia but wants to draw like her anyway. D She finally learns that drawing horses is easier with proper lighting and art supplies.

explanation.text: At first Marisa copies Euphemia's imagined horse and repeatedly fails; then she observes the real horses and uses pastels. The new approach works, so she feels proud enough to sign her own name.

questions.alt: Question 15. Read line 15 of the story. “ You call this one only fair?” he asked. “It is a gem for the king.” What does this line suggest about the father? A He has not looked at the pearl as closely as Ramón has. B He does not think that Ramón is correct about the pearl. C He is testing Ramón’s confidence in judging the pearl’s value. D He is teaching Ramón about the pearl’s quality.

explanation.text: The father exaggerates the pearl's worth as a “gem for the king,” but Ramón confidently points out its hidden flaw. His later smile and praise show that he was testing Ramón's confidence and judgment.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which detail from the story best supports the idea that Ramón is becoming an expert at judging pearls? A “ ‘For this one,’ he said, ‘what is your notation?’ ” (lines 11 and 12) B “ ‘For a poor king,’ I said.” (line 16) C “ ‘With a little care the flaw can be peeled away,’ he said.” (lines 19 and 20) D “I would like to go today.” (line 29)

explanation.text: Calling it suitable only “for a poor king,” Ramón rejects his father's high valuation and then identifies the muddy flaw. This confident, accurate judgment shows that his expertise is developing.

questions.alt: Question 17. W hy is the father reluctant to bring Ramón on a pearl‑diving trip? A He is concerned for Ramón’s safety. B He needs Ramón to evaluate more pearls. C He thinks Ramón is still too young to sail. D He is unsure Ramón is ready to dive.

explanation.text: The father imagines storms drowning them both and warns Ramón about sharks, devilfish, and giant mantas. These dangers show that his reluctance comes from concern for Ramón's safety.

questions.alt: Question 18. I n line 55, why does Ramón suggest that he will “stay on deck while the others dive”? A His father needs him to help with other jobs on the boat. B He realizes that his father will never actually let him go. C His father has convinced him that it is too dangerous. D He is trying to gradually change his father’s mind.

explanation.text: Ramón changes his request from diving to performing the safer deck work of pulling baskets and tending ropes. This compromise is therefore a gradual attempt to overcome his father's objections and gain permission.

questions.alt: Question 19. How does line 57 best contribute to the development of the story? A by signaling a turning point B by providing a solution to the problem C by comparing the characters’ actions D by introducing a new conflict

explanation.text: After Ramón offers to remain on deck, he sees his father's face begin to soften. That visible shift therefore signals the turning point from firm refusal toward the final decision to let Ramón come.

questions.alt: Question 20. How does the father change during the story? A He becomes concerned about a diver’s health. B He begins to acknowledge Ramón’s maturity. C He becomes frustrated by Ramón’s persistence. D He stops worrying about his family business.

explanation.text: The father initially treats Ramón as vulnerable, but he praises Ramón's pearl judgment and softens when Ramón proposes useful deck work. By inviting him along, he therefore begins to acknowledge Ramón's maturity.

questions.alt: Question 21. T he author develops Ramón’s point of view in the story mostly by A describing Ramón’s fear of pearl diving B including Ramón’s analysis of the pearl C describing how Ramón feels about his father D including dialogue between Ramón and his father

explanation.text: Ramón's dialogue reveals his confidence about the flawed pearl, his desire to join the voyage, and each argument he uses to persuade his father. Their exchange most directly develops his point of view.

questions.alt: Question 29. I n lines 4 through 12, the author explains why scientists are trying to find a way to produce spider silk using goats by showing A possible uses for spider silk B the popularity of spider silk C how easy spider silk is to use D how quickly spider silk can be developed

explanation.text: The opening lists spider silk's strength, lightness, and flexibility, then describes possible medical, clothing, sports, building, and aerospace uses. These applications explain why scientists want a way to produce much more silk.

questions.alt: Question 30. Which statement b est explains an advantage of using goats rather than spiders for the production of silk? A Goats produce stronger silk than spiders do. B Scientists can insert genes into goats but not into spiders. C Spider proteins in goat milk can be spun into silk. D Goats are bigger than spiders and are much easier to raise.

explanation.text: Spiders need isolation and may eat one another, while goats are friendly, easy to manage, and large enough to outproduce many spiders. Their size and temperament therefore make goats easier to raise for silk production.

questions.alt: Question 31. W hat did Jeffrey Turner discover about using Nubian goats for possible silk production? A Nubian goats already make a similar substance. B Nubian goats have high amounts of protein in their milk. C Nubian goats and spiders both prefer living in large groups. D Nubian goats and spiders have body parts that are similar.

explanation.text: Turner noticed that the spider body parts that produce silk closely resemble the goat parts that produce milk. This structural similarity therefore led him to believe goats could make spider-silk proteins.

questions.alt: Question 32. I n the process described in lines 35 through 47, which step allows the threads to become strong enough for surgical procedures? A The silk proteins are turned into clumps. B The silk is stretched repeatedly. C Salt is added to the goat’s milk. D Water is added to thin the clumps.

explanation.text: The spinning machine repeatedly stretches the silk, causing its protein building blocks to line up and lock together. That repeated stretching gives the thread the strength required for delicate surgical uses.

questions.alt: Question 33. W hy are lines 55 through 59 important to the article? A They suggest that the goats are unusual. B They explain how the goats are kept busy. C They explain that the goats are treated well. D They suggest that the goats are like humans.

explanation.text: The goats live in a normal environment with ample room to roam and play, and they are described as healthy, curious, energetic, calm, and happy. These details therefore reassure readers that the animals are treated well.

questions.alt: Question 34. Which statement b est expresses a central idea of the article? A Nubian goats produce better quality silk than spiders. B Spider silk is a complex substance that takes effort to make. C Nubian goats have been genetically altered to produce spider silk. D Spider silk contains proteins that are similar to proteins in other living things.

explanation.text: Scientists place spider genes in Nubian goats, causing the goats' milk to contain spider-silk proteins that can be separated and spun. This genetic alteration and its result therefore form the article's central idea.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which detail is most important to include in a summary of the article? A Scientists have made an attempt to gather silk from spiders living on farms. B Spider silk has qualities that can be used in many products. C A scientist started a company to research goat silk. D Machines spin spider silk into thin threads.

explanation.text: Spider silk is stronger than steel yet light and flexible enough for products ranging from surgical stitches to bridges. Those unusual and useful qualities explain the entire effort to produce it in large quantities.

questions.alt: Question 36. How does Nina’s attitude toward Ms. Charlemagne change? A Nina becomes less interested after noticing Ms. Charlemagne’s bracelets. B Nina becomes more fascinated after learning Ms. Charlemagne’s name. C Nina becomes less surprised after hearing Ms. Charlemagne’s viewpoints. D Nina becomes more suspicious after hearing Ms. Charlemagne’s assignment.

explanation.text: Nina first stares at the substitute's unusual bracelets, but the name Charlemagne makes her imagine a connection to medieval royalty and call it thrilling. Learning the name therefore increases her fascination.

questions.alt: Question 37. H ow do lines 34 through 39 contribute to the development of the story? A by suggesting that Ms. Charlemagne is not qualified to teach art B by introducing Nina to a new way to think about art C by showing that Ms. Charlemagne does not understand how artists work D by describing the way Nina usually completes art assignments

explanation.text: Ms. Charlemagne asks students to let creativity guide them and discover what appears on the page. This therefore introduces Nina, who normally plans a finished image first, to a more spontaneous view of art.

questions.alt: Question 38. W hy does the author use the word “chirped” in line 43 of the story? A to reveal that Ms. Charlemagne has creative ideas B to imply that Ms. Charlemagne is new at teaching art C to demonstrate that Ms. Charlemagne has a cheerful outlook D to show that Ms. Charlemagne easily relates to the art students

explanation.text: “Chirped” suggests a bright, lively, upbeat voice. The word matches Ms. Charlemagne's enthusiastic invitation to let colors and pictures choose themselves, demonstrating her cheerful outlook.

questions.alt: Question 39. Read this sentence from line 54. I c ould change that number, at least here if not in real life. How does this sentence best contribute to the development of the story? A by signaling a change in Nina’s thinking B by emphasizing the importance of the setting C by revealing Nina’s strong feelings D by suggesting a new plot development

explanation.text: Nina has been staring helplessly at the low score, but she suddenly realizes she controls what appears in her drawing and can erase it. This insight therefore signals a change from anxious fixation to imaginative agency.

questions.alt: Question 40. Which quotation best supports a theme of the story? A “Still, I knew she liked the picture and felt proud to see it up on the wall.” (lines 8 and 9) B “I had never seen anything like them.” (line 18) C “Imagine being descended from a medieval French king!” (lines 30 and 31) D “I was the lord of my own picture, wasn’t I?” (line 56)

explanation.text: Nina realizes that within her artwork she is “the lord” and can transform the score that troubles her. This quotation supports the theme that creative expression can provide control and a new perspective.

questions.alt: Question 41. B ased on details in the story, what can readers conclude about Ms. Charlemagne? A She is a respected artist. B She has a famous relative. C She has a unique personality. D She is a popular substitute teacher.

explanation.text: Ms. Charlemagne has beaded braids, one feather earring, clattering bracelets, a gleeful manner, and an unconventional approach to art. This distinctive combination supports the conclusion that she has a unique personality.

questions.alt: Question 42. H ow do the details in the story help develop a theme? A Nina’s thoughts about her mother help develop the theme that being honest will make you feel better. B Nina’s interaction with Isobel helps develop the theme that experiencing a new situation is easier with a friend. C Nina’s drawing helps develop the theme that expressing yourself can help you work through your struggles. D Nina’s description of Ms. Charlemagne helps develop the theme that judging others by their appearance is not a good idea.

explanation.text: Nina draws the feared scene of her mother seeing the low score, then discovers she can erase and transform that score within the picture. Her art therefore helps her confront and work through her anxiety.

Official passage for Questions 36-42. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. According to the article, why do some parrotfishes make a cocoon? A to attract other fishes to their hiding place B_ to show other fishes they are asleep C to create a safe place for their eggs D to hide themselves from predators

explanation.text: Parrotfish secrete a sticky cocoon that seals their scent from hungry moray eels, and they flee if anything breaks into it. These details show that the cocoon conceals the fish from predators while it rests.

questions.alt: Question 2. W hy are some diurnal fishes harder to see in the evening than in the daytime? A The movement on the reef blurs their shapes. B Their predators can only detect outlines. C The light casts shadows that hide them. D Their skin color changes to blend in.

explanation.text: Some diurnal fish adjust their color cells so their bright daytime colors become dull, dark shades at twilight. This change helps them blend into the dim reef and makes them harder to see.

questions.alt: Question 3. What statement best summarizes the information in lines 1 through 9? A Daytime fish who live in the coral reef generally feeding at dusk. B Fish have the natural ability to become less visible at night in the coral reef. C Predatory fish hide in the coral reef so they can feed on the diurnal fish. D Fish in the coral reef have remarkably better vision during the day.

explanation.text: As daylight fades, fish move toward shelters and their bright colors fade or darken. Together, these details show the fishes' natural ways of becoming less visible at night.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which sentence from the article best explains why some fishes may be dangerous to humans? A “Though twilight predators are not very good at distinguishing colors, they can detect shape, outlines, and movement well.” (lines 25 and 26) B “Many predators that have been quietly waiting in the background all day become more active at dusk and dawn.” (lines 28 and 29) C “The crepuscular hunters have ingenious ways of picking off their prey.” (lines 29 and 30) D “By thrusting out its lower jaw, its mouth becomes big enough to swallow almost any prey.” (lines 31 and 32)

explanation.text: A grouper can thrust out its lower jaw until its mouth is large enough to swallow almost any prey. That physical ability explains why a large fish might pose a danger even to a human.

questions.alt: Question 5. W hat does the phrase “zero in on” in line 40 mean? A to locate B to look C to threaten D to smell

explanation.text: The passage lists sharks' smell, vibration sensing, electric-pulse detection, and low-light vision as ways they find prey. In that context, "zero in on" means to locate a target precisely.

questions.alt: Question 6. T he author develops a central idea about how fishes adapt to their environments by focusing mostly on the A light in the water B depth of the reef C currents in the water D shape of the reef

explanation.text: Changes in dusk light cause daytime fish to darken and retreat while predators with dim-light senses become active. This repeated focus shows how reef fish adapt their color, vision, and behavior to the available light.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which detail is most important to include in a summary of the article? A “Because fishes don’t have eyelids to close, it’s impossible to tell whether or not most fishes are really sleeping.” (lines 15 and 16) B “Many predators that have been quietly waiting in the background all day become more a ctive at dusk and dawn.” (lines 28 and 29) C “It has been rumored that giant groupers (which may weigh up to 1,000 pounds) have been known to swallow divers whole!” (lines 32 and 33) D “Many daytime fishes move into deeper water, rise to the surface, or spawn during outgoing tides . . .” (lines 49 and 50)

explanation.text: Predators that waited in the background all day become active hunters at dusk and dawn. This detail belongs in a summary because it captures a major shift in the reef as daytime activity gives way to nighttime life.

questions.alt: Question 8. H ow does the author support the claim that “gracious living had been a long time coming” (line 4)? A by describing the excitement caused by new improvements in refrigerators B by describing the menus recommended by refrigerator manufacturers C by tracing the development of various methods for keeping food cool D by explaining the relationship between temperature and food safety

explanation.text: The article moves from streams, cellars, snow, and iceboxes to early mechanical refrigerators and modern models. Tracing those methods over many years supports the claim that convenient, dependable cooling took a long time to develop.

questions.alt: Question 9. R ead this sentence about pond ice from lines 20 through 22. T hen it was hauled from home to home on a horse-drawn cart, packed in sawdust, and put in the family’s icehouse, where, she says, it lasted for months. Based on the information in lines 8 through 10, which was most likely the reason for packing pond ice in sawdust? A to keep the ice from chipping B to prevent the ice from melting C to keep the ice from becoming slimy D to prevent the ice from becoming polluted

explanation.text: Earlier, the author explains that sawdust served as insulation inside an icebox. Packing pond ice in sawdust therefore slowed the transfer of heat and prevented the ice from melting quickly.

questions.alt: Question 10. W hat is the meaning of the word “hoist” in line 26? A lift B sell C carve D locate

explanation.text: The iceman uses tongs to hoist a one-hundred-pound block before carrying it into the house. Because the block must be raised from the cart, "hoist" means lift.

questions.alt: Question 11. According to the article, what improvements were made to resolve a safety issue in the older refrigerators? A lining the cabinets with zinc B attaching an overflow pipe C using sawdust as insulation D changing the type of coolant

explanation.text: Early refrigerator coolants could leak, and some of the gases were deadly. Manufacturers addressed that safety problem by changing the coolant because a safer gas reduced the danger posed by leaks.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which quotation from the article best supports the conclusion that advances in refrigeration improved life for the average person? A “To stock the box, city people bought ice, and country dwellers harvested it.” (line 17) B “When commercial icehouses opened in the early 1800s, they were considered a business with a future.” (lines 26 and 27) C “All of a sudden, the middle class could have things that seemed high class a few years before.” (lines 47 and 48) D “Today, while the mechanics have remained much the same, the refrigerator has gotten ever fancier.” (lines 51 and 52)

explanation.text: The statement that the middle class could suddenly have things that had seemed high class directly concerns ordinary families. It supports the conclusion that improved refrigeration made previously exclusive foods and comforts available to the average person.

questions.alt: Question 13. How do lines 50 through 53 develop a central idea of the article? A by showing that some people considered refrigerators unnecessary B by explaining that refrigerators have improved very little over the years C by comparing refrigerators sold in the past with refrigerators sold today D by showing that refrigerators gained widespread acceptance over time

explanation.text: Only about two million households owned refrigerators in 1937, but more than eighty percent had one by the mid-1950s. That sharp rise over time demonstrates that refrigerators gained widespread acceptance.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A Refrigeration was essential for making jelled salads. B “Summer complaint” was another name for food poisoning. C Modern refrigerators include icemakers and defrost systems. D Refrigerators gained popularity after they became small and affordable.

explanation.text: The article's main history shows that bulky, costly, and dangerous early machines became smaller and safer before ownership spread rapidly. The fact that refrigerators gained popularity after becoming small and affordable therefore captures an essential development, not a minor detail.

questions.alt: Question 29. Read these sentences from lines 13 and 14. “ It has everything you need.” And except for the tools, rocks, plants, fish, accessories, electrical power to the site, and the hole itself, it did. Why does the author most likely include this description of the pond-building kit? A to maintain a humorous tone B to introduce the main conflict C to express the narrator’s disappointment D to demonstrate the parents’ support of their son

explanation.text: The kit is advertised as having everything needed, but the narrator immediately lists nearly every essential item it lacks. This exaggerated contradiction shows why the description is humorous and maintains the story's playful tone.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read this sentence from line 38. I approached the task like a starving man at a banquet. What is the main purpose of the comparison in this sentence? A to describe the narrator’s lack of attention to detail B to emphasize the depth of the narrator’s enthusiasm C to show that the narrator feels weak from excitement D to show that the narrator is overcome by the job at hand

explanation.text: A starving person would approach a banquet with intense eagerness, just as the narrator attacks the digging job. The comparison therefore emphasizes the depth of his enthusiasm for building the pond.

questions.alt: Question 31. Read line 46. A t this rate, I figured, I’ll be basking in tranquillity in no time at all. What meaning does the phrase “basking in tranquillity” convey to the reader? A The narrator expects positive recognition around town for his efforts. B The narrator believes the vision of peaceful relaxation shown in the video. C The narrator is unaccustomed to such difficult work and will soon need a rest. D The narrator is comparing himself to a groundhog that is sitting in the sunshine.

explanation.text: The instructional video repeatedly presents a water garden as a soothing, peaceful place. By imagining himself "basking in tranquillity," the narrator shows that he believes this vision of relaxed enjoyment will soon become real.

questions.alt: Question 32. Which lines best reveal an overall theme of the story? A lines 25 through 28 B lines 38 through 40 C lines 47 and 48 D lines 63 and 64

explanation.text: In lines 47 and 48, the narrator observes that few things happen as people think they will. That reflection reveals the story's broader theme because his ideal pond later becomes a muddy trap instead of the peaceful garden he imagined.

questions.alt: Question 33. I n lines 63 through 73, how does the narrator’s description of the location and history of Melville, Kansas, contribute to the plot? A It demonstrates that the new pond is better than other ponds. B It reveals the foolishness of the narrator’s attempt to create a new pond. C It emphasizes the importance of the new pond to the narrator. D It explains the town’s need for a new pond.

explanation.text: Melville is described as landlocked and dry, with only a brief period in its history when water surrounded it. This background explains why even a small new body of water feels especially important to the pond-obsessed narrator.

questions.alt: Question 34. W hich lines from the story reveal a change in the narrator’s point of view? A lines 49 through 53 B lines 54 through 57 C lines 59 through 62 D lines 74 through 77

explanation.text: In lines 74 through 77, the narrator stops seeing a sparkling model pond and recognizes a sticky, muddy trap. This contrast reveals his point of view changing from hopeful imagination to disappointed realism.

questions.alt: Question 35. H ow does the narrator’s reaction to his pond first filling with water differ from his outlook at the end of the story? A He is excited at first but then becomes disappointed. B He is worried at first but then feels satisfied. C He is scared at first and later becomes angry. D He is happy at first and later feels proud.

explanation.text: When rain first fills the hole, the narrator enthusiastically sees it as the beautiful pond from his plans. By the end, he sees only mud and complains that reality interferes with dreams, showing that excitement has become disappointment.

questions.alt: Question 36. Which words from lines 1 through 8 best help the reader understand the meaning of “suppressing” (line 5)? A “now understand the importance of fire” B “a lightning fire poses no danger” C “have not been allowed to burn” D “carefully planned burns”

explanation.text: The passage says that natural fires "have not been allowed to burn" after describing years of suppressing them. That wording indicates that "suppressing" means stopping or preventing the fires.

questions.alt: Question 37. Read this sentence from lines 6 and 7. T he best answer to these problems appears to be to fight fire with fire. Which evidence from the article best supports this claim? A “But prescribed burning is more likely to be planned for less extreme conditions so the fires can be better controlled.” (lines 10 and 11) B “A prescribed fire should burn away heavy undergrowth of brush to remove potential fuel for wildfire.” (lines 17 and 18) C “When the brush and deadwood on the forest floor burn, they release nutrients that can nourish the trees, grasses, and other forest plants.” (lines 22 and 23) D “In recent years, more and more people have moved into the countryside, often right on the borders of National Forest lands.” (lines 33 and 34)

explanation.text: A prescribed fire burns away heavy undergrowth that could otherwise fuel a destructive wildfire. This evidence supports "fight fire with fire" because a carefully controlled burn reduces the material that would make a later fire more dangerous.

questions.alt: Question 38. How do fires benefit wildlife? A Fires help remove older trees that crowd forests. B The possibility of fire limits where cattle are allowed to graze. C The possibility of fire limits how close people can live to forests. D Fires help create conditions that cause more food to become available.

explanation.text: Fire removes old shrub growth and encourages tender new shoots, causing available wildlife food to increase dramatically. These conditions show how a burn can create much more food for animals.

questions.alt: Question 39. W hat is the meaning of “prone” (line 44) as used in the article? A likely B useful C starting D existing

explanation.text: The passage describes forests that have gone too long without fire as especially vulnerable to extremely hot burns. In that context, "prone" means likely to experience such a fire.

questions.alt: Question 40. W hat have managers of public lands learned from Native Americans? A how fire helps maintain grassland areas B the importance of protecting natural habitats C how planned fires can be used to prevent large fires D the role of cattle management in protecting grassland areas

explanation.text: Native Americans deliberately used fire to keep grasslands from becoming overgrown with shrubs and trees. Their practice demonstrates to modern land managers how fire helps maintain healthy grassland areas.

questions.alt: Question 41. Which sentence best expresses the central idea of the article? A “Managers of some public lands now understand the importance of fire in the cycles of nature.” (lines 1 and 2) B “How do land managers decide where to burn?” (line 32) C “Unfortunately, many of the forests have gone so long without fire that even prescribed burning would be dangerous.” (lines 47 and 48) D “Not everyone is happy with prescribed burning.” (line 59)

explanation.text: The article explains several ways carefully managed fire restores habitats, releases nutrients, and reduces wildfire fuel. Those examples develop the central idea that public-land managers now recognize fire as an important part of nature's cycles.

questions.alt: Question 42. H ow do lines 59 through 69 contribute to the discussion of prescribed burns? A by describing how attitudes have changed with time B by showing that the benefits outweigh the costs C by proving that conflicting opinions are wrong D by exposing the self-interest of opponents

explanation.text: The final section acknowledges the expense and smoke of prescribed burns, then compares them with the greater costs and pollution of uncontrolled wildfires. It also notes ecological renewal, so the comparison shows that the benefits of planned burns outweigh their costs.

questions.alt: Question 1. Read these words from lines 18 and 19. M r. Scully’s shades were drawn; the house had a pinched look as though it felt the cold. The use of the words “pinched look” contributes to the tone of the story by making the house seem A tense B angry C uncertain D disappointed

explanation.text: The drawn shades and the image of a house tightened against the cold make the building seem strained and closed up. Describing it as having a "pinched look" therefore contributes a tense tone.

questions.alt: Question 2. L ines 20 through 24 contribute to the development of the plot by A showing that Ned and Mr. Scully are friends B describing the challenges of dealing with heavy snow C suggesting that Mr. Scully has been neglecting the cat D describing weather conditions that can be dangerous for the cat

explanation.text: Mr. Scully has brought in the cat's bowl because anything left outside will freeze, while eating snow or ice can make an animal sick. These weather hazards explain why Ned worries that the cat may be in danger and begins looking for it.

questions.alt: Question 3. I n lines 31 through 36, Ned keeps his distance from the cat because he A envisions the cat being sick from licking ice or snow B believes the cat will make him late to school C imagines the cat will become panicked D remembers the cat is deaf and unlikely to respond

explanation.text: Ned knows the cat's deaf ear will keep it from hearing him approach. He stays back because he imagines the animal suddenly exploding into fear once it finally notices him.

questions.alt: Question 4. L ines 37 through 42 in the story reveal that Ned feels A isolated B confused C relieved D confident

explanation.text: Ned sees that Billy, Evelyn, and Janet have passed without knowing he was nearby. His feeling that he is ghostly and alone on a silent white globe reveals a strong sense of isolation.

questions.alt: Question 5. Ned’s decision to leave the cat causes A the cat to become more afraid B Ned to be left behind by the other children C the cat to go hungry for the rest of the day D Ned to be distracted from his work during school

explanation.text: After leaving the motionless cat, Ned tries to focus on his lesson, but its image persists and the poem makes no sense to him. His decision therefore causes worry about the cat to distract him throughout school.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which quotation best supports a central theme of the story? A “He ate breakfast hastily, too preoccupied to read the story on the cereal box.” (line 11) B “Twice, his glance passed over the icebox.” (line 28) C “. . . but try as he might, the image of the unmoving animal on the ragged old quilt persisted.” (lines 55 and 56) D “Ned read the lines several times before copying them down in his copybook.” (line 61)

explanation.text: Even while Ned tries to concentrate, the image of the unmoving cat keeps returning to his mind. This persistence supports the theme that genuine concern for another living creature cannot simply be ignored.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which quotation from the story best shows how the cat has impacted Ned’s life? A “He was going to be late to school if he didn’t get a move on, but he kept looking hard all over the yard as though he could make the cat appear out of snow and gray sky.” (lines 26 through 28) B “The third time, he saw that the motionless mound on top of it was not only the quilt but the cat, joined into one shape by a dusting of snow.” (lines 29 and 30) C “It was odd to think that Billy, huffing and puffing, had gone past Mr. Scully’s place, thinking his own thoughts, while he, Ned, only a few yards away, had been searching for the cat.” (lines 39 through 41) D “It was this that had made his hours in school so hard ever since he and Mr. Scully had seen the cat last autumn, this drawing away of his attention from everything that was going on around him.” (lines 62 through 64)

explanation.text: The quotation directly states that concern for the cat has made Ned's school hours difficult by pulling his attention away from everything around him. It therefore shows a lasting impact on his daily life, beyond this single snowy morning.

questions.alt: Question 22. How do lines 1 through 7 mainly establish the tone of the article? A They create curiosity by inviting the reader to provide an opinion on the two condiments. B They create interest by describing loyal fans supporting their favorite condiment. C They create humor by personifying two condiments in an imagined contest. D They create tension by analyzing the popularity of two condiments.

explanation.text: The opening treats wasabi and chili peppers like aggressive boxers, complete with loyal fans, a formal welcome, and a "Match of the Moment." This playful personification contributes humor while introducing the comparison.

questions.alt: Question 23. Read lines 25 and 26 from the article. C hilies have since taken the culinary world by storm. Which detail best supports the author’s claim? A Chili peppers come in many varieties. B Chili peppers are used in many different countries. C Chili peppers are related to tomatoes and eggplants. D Chili peppers were mistakenly thought to resemble black pepper.

explanation.text: The article says chili peppers appear beside dishes from the Basque provinces and North Africa to India and Southeast Asia. Their use across many countries supports the claim that chilies have taken the culinary world by storm.

questions.alt: Question 24. R ead this sentence from lines 37 and 38 of the article. A s a consequence, the consumption of wasabi launches an airborne assault on the consumer’s sinuses. What does the phrase “airborne assault” add to the author’s description? A It explains the effect of experiencing the molecules in wasabi. B It suggests a painful experience that makes wasabi undesirable. C It warns that direct contact with wasabi causes injury. D It cautions that wasabi causes an intense repeated attack occurring over time.

explanation.text: Wasabi's lightweight molecules travel through the air into the nose and throat, dissolve there, and activate pain receptors. Calling this an "airborne assault" vividly explains the effect of those moving molecules on the consumer's sinuses.

questions.alt: Question 25. W hat is the role of the section “Powerful Punches” in the development of the article? A It describes the physical differences between wasabi and chili peppers. B It explains the best ways to experience the heat from wasabi and chili peppers. C It describes why wasabi and chili peppers are both enjoyable and painful to consume. D It provides a scientific explanation for the effects of consuming wasabi and chili peppers.

explanation.text: The section identifies allyl isothiocyanate and capsaicin, then traces how each chemical reaches and activates a different kind of pain receptor. These details provide a scientific explanation for the distinct physical effects of eating wasabi and chili peppers.

questions.alt: Question 26. T he Scoville scale determines the strength of the heat in chili peppers by A counting the number of sips of chili pepper juice a human subject can consume B recording the amount of capsaicin present in specific amounts of chili pepper juice C measuring how much chili pepper juice must be weakened for it to no longer cause pain D comparing descriptions of the heat a human subject feels while drinking chili pepper juice

explanation.text: Testers repeatedly dilute extracted chili juice until its heat can no longer be detected. The Scoville rating therefore measures how much the juice must be weakened before it stops causing pain.

questions.alt: Question 27. W hat is the result of being unable to use the Scoville scale to measure the heat strength of wasabi? A Chromatography is used to compare the heat strengths of wasabi and chili peppers. B Comparing the heat strengths of wasabi and chili peppers using a scientific method is impossible. C A new scale will be developed to compare the degree of pain caused by wasabi and chili peppers. D Experts now rely on a scale based on measuring consumer pain responses to wasabi and chili peppers.

explanation.text: The Scoville method works only for capsaicin, while chromatography cannot show whether capsaicin and wasabi's different chemical cause equal pain. Because the substances also activate different receptors, no objective scientific method described can compare their heat strengths directly.

questions.alt: Question 28. R ead this sentence from lines 71 and 72 of the article. W asabi and chili peppers are like pungent apples and oranges. Which evidence from the article best supports this statement? A “They get our attention by purposely causing us pain.” (lines 3 and 4) B “After separation, the amounts of each component are quantified.” (lines 62 and 63) C “If two chili peppers have the same amount of capsaicin, it can be assumed that those peppers are equally ‘hot.’ ” (lines 65 and 66) D “There’s no way to know if equal amounts of capsaicin and allyl isothiocyanate cause equal degrees of pain.” (lines 67 and 68)

explanation.text: The article states that equal amounts of capsaicin and allyl isothiocyanate may not cause equal degrees of pain. That uncertainty supports the apples-and-oranges comparison because the two condiments cannot be judged by the same chemical measure.

questions.alt: Question 29. W hich statement expresses a central idea of the article? A Very few people use echolocation in their daily lives. B Echolocation is a technique that can be utilized by humans. C Echolocation has been studied by scientists for many years. D Some animals are known for using echolocation to find food.

explanation.text: Ben Underwood and Daniel Kish both use returning clicks to identify objects, navigate, play sports, and explore outdoors. Their experiences, together with the brain research, develop the central idea that humans can utilize echolocation.

questions.alt: Question 30. H ow do lines 1–5 contribute to the understanding of the text? A by showing the widespread use of echolocation by animals B by giving examples to explain how echolocation works C by presenting the characteristics of animals that use echolocation D by describing how each species uses echolocation differently

explanation.text: The opening describes bats and dolphins sending out sounds and locating prey from the echoes that return. Those examples explain how echolocation uses reflected sound to identify an object.

questions.alt: Question 31. I n people who are blind, the visual cortex seems to help A activate the optic nerve where images are formed B increase the amount of light reaching the brain C create images in the brain based on sounds D make echoes of sounds from clicks

explanation.text: Research shows that a blind person's visual cortex becomes active when touch or hearing substitutes for sight. Since echolocation uses sound and "creates its own images," the cortex appears to form mental images from echoes.

questions.alt: Question 32. Read this sentence from lines 17 and 18. B en says every object in his life talks to him in ways that no one else can hear or understand. Which quotation best supports this claim? A “He can find the pole and the backboard on a basketball goal, and tell which is which by the distinctive echo each makes.” (lines 26 and 27) B “Even though he can’t see the goal he’s aiming for, he can sink a basket.” (lines 27 and 28) C “In school, Ben recognizes his classmates by their voices.” (line 41) D “With the help of Braille books and a talking laptop computer, Ben attends the same classes as sighted students.” (lines 41 through 43)

explanation.text: Ben can distinguish a basketball pole from its backboard by the distinctive echo each object returns. This example supports his claim because it shows that different objects communicate specific information to him through sounds others do not interpret that way.

questions.alt: Question 33. Read Daniel Kish’s claim from line 46. “ I have mental images that are very rich, very complex.” Which quotation from the article best supports this claim? A “. . . Kish of Long Beach, Calif., also uses echolocation, and has become an expert on it. . . .” (lines 19 and 20) B “He was raised to believe he could do pretty much anything. . . .” (lines 44 and 45) C “. . . Kish varies the pace and volume of his clicks as he walks along. . . .” (lines 48 and 49) D “. . . what he can tell you about an object’s qualities is sometimes astonishingly thorough.” (lines 49 and 50)

explanation.text: The description of Kish's astonishingly thorough knowledge of an object's qualities shows how much detail he gains from echoes. Such detailed awareness supports his claim that his nonvisual mental images are rich and complex.

questions.alt: Question 34. H ow do lines 51 through 53 develop a key concept of the article? A by using a comparison to suggest the echolocation potential of humans B by demonstrating that humans use echolocation more effectively than animals do C by describing why using echolocation benefits bats and dolphins in unique ways D by showing that scientists need more time to study echolocation techniques

explanation.text: After describing animals that detect tiny or distant targets, the author asks what Ben and Kish might ultimately be able to do. This comparison uses the abilities of bats and dolphins to suggest the still-unknown echolocation potential of humans.

questions.alt: Question 35. E cholocation used by humans is distinct from echolocation used by animals because animals can A create louder clicking noises B distinguish among more sounds C see objects that are farther away D locate objects that are smaller in size

explanation.text: Humans produce clicks at much slower rates and lower frequencies than bats and dolphins. As a result, people can picture only larger objects, which means the animals can locate objects that are smaller in size.

questions.alt: Question 36. Lines 1 through 11 b est support the idea that the author A is fearful the chicks will be vulnerable to predators B is unsure about what she can expect the chicks to understand C wants the chicks to explore the yard she has set up for their needs D has not planned how she will teach the chicks to adjust to a new environment

explanation.text: The author repeatedly wonders whether the young chicks will recognize their new home, return to the barn, or even avoid getting lost. These questions and her comparison to her own childhood confusion show that she is unsure what the chicks can understand.

questions.alt: Question 37. B ased on lines 12 through 18, which statement best describes the exchange between Gretchen and the author? A Gretchen proves a point, and the author feels embarrassed. B Gretchen gives the author advice, and the author learns from it. C Gretchen comforts the author, and the author feels more confident. D Gretchen shares her personal experiences, and the author criticizes them.

explanation.text: Gretchen advises keeping the chicks in their pen for twenty-four hours and predicts that they will return at dark. When the birds do exactly that, the author recognizes that Gretchen's advice was right and learns from it.

questions.alt: Question 38. W hat do lines 23 through 31 indicate about the developmental strategy of chickens? A Chickens are adapted to food availability and pressure from predators. B Chickens are born ready and require no further maturing. C Chickens have a faster growth rate than other birds. D Baby chickens spend no time with their parents.

explanation.text: Ground-nesting game birds face predators, so their chicks hatch mobile and leave quickly, already able to search for food. These traits indicate that the precocial strategy is an adaptation to both food availability and pressure from predators.

questions.alt: Question 39. L ines 49 through 59 develop the key idea that chickens raised by humans A are curious about the activities of their caregivers B become a nuisance to the other projects of their owners C grow to prefer the company of people over other chickens D develop their intelligence more than chickens raised by hens

explanation.text: The hens follow the author while she hangs laundry, weeds, and eats, and they closely inspect the family's tools and projects. This repeated involvement shows that human-raised chickens are curious about their caregivers' activities.

questions.alt: Question 40. Read lines 36 through 42 from the passage. I n scientific experiments, researchers have trained days-old chicks to find hidden food using both distant and nearby landmarks as cues. Italian researchers demonstrated that at the tender age of fifteen days, after just a week’s training to find hidden food in the middle of their cage, chicks can correctly calculate the center of a given environment—even in the absence of distinctive landmarks. Even more astonishing, they can do it in spaces they have never seen before, whether the area be circular, square, or triangular. How do these lines relate to lines 59 through 64? A Lines 36 through 42 express an opinion, and lines 59 through 64 provide support. B Lines 36 through 42 identify why something happens, and lines 59 through 64 describe what happens. C Lines 36 through 42 present facts, and lines 59 through 64 support the facts with a personal experience. D Lines 36 through 42 provide a comparison, and lines 59 through 64 provide evidence for the comparison.

explanation.text: The scientific study establishes that young chicks can use spatial cues and even calculate the center of unfamiliar spaces. The author's chickens then provide a personal experience that supports those facts: they recognize their home and property boundaries without anyone knowing how they do it.

questions.alt: Question 41. Which claim do lines 65 through 72 support? A The chickens stay where they do as a direct result of what the author has taught them. B The chickens do what they do because of their interactions with their environment. C The chickens stay where they do because they are unfamiliar with other areas. D The chickens do what they do as a result of trial and error.

explanation.text: The hens independently recognize property lines and remain within them even though they are physically free to travel farther. Their later change follows a change in social relationships rather than a lesson from the author, supporting the claim that interaction with their environment shapes what they do.

questions.alt: Question 42. H ow does the author’s attitude toward the chickens change from the beginning of the passage to the end? A It varies from fear for their safety to gratitude for winning over the author’s father-in-law. B It shifts from being uncertain about their abilities to being amazed at their complex ways. C As she observes the behavior of the chickens, she realizes their learning keeps pace with the risks they take. D As she gains confidence in her ability to raise her chickens, she comes to appreciate their self-sufficiency.

explanation.text: At first, the author doubts that the chicks can recognize their home or return to it after roaming. By the end, their spatial learning, curiosity, and sensitivity to social relationships have amazed her, showing a shift from uncertainty to wonder at their complexity.

questions.alt: Question 1. Read these sentences from paragraphs 13 and 17 of the story. She wrote: The Case of the Big Stink. Inez wrote: Plan 1: Sniff out the stink. Which best describes why these lines are important to the story? A They show how the girls record their information. B They provide proof that Inez has the best ideas. C They give a clue that the story is about a dog. D They show that Inez wants to be a writer.

explanation.text: Inez writes the case name and then writes the group’s first plan in her notebook. These entries show how the girls keep a record of their investigation as they gather clues.

questions.alt: Question 2. 3 In the story, which two items smell the same? A the curtains and the garbage can B the slippers and the counter C the table and the lamps D the sofa and the pillow

explanation.text: Ivy says the garbage can has a different smell, but when the girls sniff the sofa she says, “That’s the stink.” This shows that the sofa smells the same as Ivy’s pillow.

questions.alt: Question 3. ‘In paragraphs 27 and 28, why does Izzy think the clue is important? A It shows why the smelly things are in the house. B It shows a way to connect all of the smelly things. C It shows that the girls need to look for more things that smell the same. D It shows that the girls have become good at finding the things that smell.

explanation.text: The pillow, slippers, and sofa seem unrelated until Inez notices that each is soft and cushy. This clue is important because it connects all the smelly objects and eventually points the girls toward Baxter.

questions.alt: Question 4. Read the sentence from paragraph 45 of the story. “Check out the area behind these bushes,” said Izzy. Which best describes the meaning of “check out” as it is used in the sentence? A search B write on C sit next to D stay away from

explanation.text: Izzy tells the girls to “check out” the area because they are following the smell and looking for its source. In this context, the phrase means to search or investigate behind the bushes.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which best describes the main idea of the story? A Three girls write in their notebook about a smelly dog. B Three girls try to discover where a bad smell comes from. C Three girls discover that some parts of the house are smelly. D Three girls help look for things in the house that smell the same.

explanation.text: The girls investigate matching smells, connect the objects to Baxter, and follow the odor to rotting eggs under the bushes. Therefore, discovering where the bad smell comes from is the story’s main idea.

questions.alt: Question 6. Why do the girls most likely call themselves the “Private I's”? A because they want to keep a big secret B_ because they keep their information in a notebook C because they are all important and because of their ideas D because they are solving a mystery and because of their names

explanation.text: Izzy, Inez, and Ivy all have names beginning with I, and they investigate clues to solve the bad-smell mystery. This shows that “Private I’s” is a playful version of “private eyes,” meaning detectives.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A Mary Jane is bringing her art project to school that day. B Mary Jane notices that her art leftovers are gone from the table. C Mary Jane thanks her mother for cleaning the table. D Mary Jane plans to eat some eggs and bacon for breakfast.

explanation.text: Mary Jane discovers that the art scraps she left on the table have disappeared. Because this creates the mystery that drives the story, it is the most important detail for a summary.

questions.alt: Question 14. Why is paragraph 17 important to the rest of the story? A It introduces Smokey’s favorite piece of furniture. B It introduces a conflict between Josh and his mother. C It suggests that Josh knows where Smokey hides. D It gives the information that helps Mary Jane solve the problem.

explanation.text: Josh explains that Smokey used to hide stolen toys behind the large brown chair. This information helps Mary Jane solve the problem because she searches there and finds her missing art scraps.

questions.alt: Question 15. Read these sentences from paragraph 19. She hopped out of her seat and ran into the next room. With a grunt, she slowly pulled out the heavy chair. Which words give the best clue for why Mary Jane grunts? A “hopped out” B “ran into” C “she slowly” D “heavy chair”

explanation.text: Mary Jane grunts while slowly pulling the chair away from the wall. The words “heavy chair” explain why moving it requires enough effort to make her grunt.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which items suggest that Smokey has been taking things for a long period of time? A Mary Jane’s hair ribbons B bits of tissue paper C Josh’s piece of bacon D broken ice pop sticks

explanation.text: The tissue paper and broken sticks came from Mary Jane’s project the night before, but the hair ribbons had been missing already. This shows that Smokey has collected objects over a longer period of time.

questions.alt: Question 17. What does paragraph 22 mostly show about Mary Jane? A She is very creative. B She is an excellent student. C She enjoys art class. D She spends a lot of time on her assignments.

explanation.text: Mary Jane turns discarded ice-pop sticks into borders and tissue paper into the colored glass of a pretend stained-glass window. This inventive reuse of ordinary materials shows that she is creative.

questions.alt: Question 18. 1g What did Mary Jane make for her art project? A a sheet of glass with colored tissue paper B a fancy window with ice pop sticks and colored tissue paper C a tiny home with ice pop sticks and colored tissue paper D a bed frame with ice pop sticks

explanation.text: Mary Jane says the painted ice-pop sticks form the borders and the colored tissue paper represents glass. These details show that her project is a decorative stained-glass-style window made from both materials.

questions.alt: Question 25. In paragraph 4, the word “vehicle” means something that A leads to questions B takes years to create C moves people and goods D encourages people to invent

explanation.text: The inventors want something that can go faster and haul heavier loads than people or animals could. This context shows that a vehicle is something used to move people and goods.

questions.alt: Question 26. Why was Nicolas Joseph Cugnot’s invention able to move by itself? A It used steam. B It had wheels. C It was very large. D It was very strong.

explanation.text: Cugnot’s boiler heated water to make steam, and pressure from that steam forced the front wheel to turn. Because steam supplied the power, the automobile could move without an animal pulling it.

questions.alt: Question 27. Read this sentence from paragraph 11. Yet they had drawbacks, too. The word “drawbacks” shows that the electric car A was not quickly accepted by the public B changed the way that people traveled C did not provide a perfect solution D used batteries for power

explanation.text: After introducing “drawbacks,” the passage lists short battery range, scarce charging places, weak performance in mud, and slow speed. These limitations show that electric cars were not a perfect solution.

questions.alt: Question 28. 2g How do paragraphs 10 through 15 support a main idea of the passage? A by discussing how several inventors improved cars B by listing the names of countries to show how far apart they are C by describing the condition of the streets to show the need for better roads D by showing that drivers had to be stopped because their cars were going too fast

explanation.text: These paragraphs move from battery-powered cars to faster, longer-ranging gasoline cars and then to Ford’s less expensive Model T. This progression supports the main idea because it shows inventors improving cars in several ways.

questions.alt: Question 29. The first gas-powered cars were an improvement on the electric car because the gas-powered cars A did not cost much B did not smell bad C were able to travel farther D made less noise when driven

explanation.text: Electric cars could travel only about 20 to 40 miles before their batteries died. Gas-powered cars carried extra fuel in tanks, which means they could continue for longer distances.

questions.alt: Question 30. How are paragraphs 14 through 17 connected? A They show a problem that existed and explain how Henry Ford solved it. B They describe the order of steps to move a Model T from the factory to the roads. C They compare the number of parts in a Model T to the number of parts in other cars. D They explain the effect of typical earnings on the people who worked for Henry Ford.

explanation.text: Paragraph 14 explains that early gas cars cost more than most people could afford. Paragraphs 15 through 17 show how Ford’s fast assembly-line process solved that problem by producing a much cheaper car.

questions.alt: Question 31. The headings divide the passage by the A uses for automobiles B changes in automobiles C inventors of automobiles D popularity of automobiles

explanation.text: The headings move from the need for faster travel to steam, battery, and gasoline power. This organization shows different stages in how automobiles changed over time.

Official passage for Questions 25-31. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. In paragraph 1, what does Minna learn from watching Willie? A She sees that playing music makes a crowd gather. B She notices that the music makes others happy. C She realizes that she wants to have a vibrato. D She discovers that music makes her sad.

explanation.text: Minna listens to Willie’s rich playing and thinks, “Willie on the street corner has a vibrato. Where is mine?” This comparison shows that watching him makes her realize she wants a vibrato of her own.

questions.alt: Question 2. What new information about Willie does the reader learn in paragraph 12? A He is more than he seems. B He is a generous person. C He performs with a vibrato. D He plays with something in mind.

explanation.text: Minna knows Willie only as a street-corner player, but Porch reveals that Willie is a fine musician in a symphony chamber group. This new detail shows that Willie is more accomplished than he first appears.

questions.alt: Question 3. Read these sentences from paragraph 18 of the story. He peered at Minna. “It is like a light going on over your head.” What does the phrase “a light going on over your head” mean as it is used in paragraph 18? A a new way of understanding B something that is above you C a way someone helps you out D something that happens after hard work

explanation.text: Porch compares developing a vibrato to suddenly understanding a book for the first time. Therefore, “a light going on over your head” means gaining a new way of understanding something.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which statement best reflects a theme of the story? A Having something new can make things better. B With time and practice, a person can achieve a goal. C A teacher can help you figure out everything you need to know. D Playing music is fun even when you are still learning how to do it.

explanation.text: Porch explains that vibrato is not something that simply appears; Minna can work at it until understanding develops. This supports the theme because time and practice can help a person reach a goal.

questions.alt: Question 5. Read these sentences from the story. “Willie is a fine musician, Minna. And he was a fine musician before he got his vibrato.” (paragraph 12) “And we will play it wonderfully. In tune. With or without a vibrato.” (paragraph 26) How do these sentences develop the story overall? A They explain why Willie gives back Minna’s money. B They show Minna’s eagerness to play with a vibrato. C They show what Minna learns from Porch about Willie. D They suggest that Minna does not need a vibrato to play well.

explanation.text: Porch points out that Willie was already a fine musician before gaining vibrato, then says he and Minna can play wonderfully without it. Together, these statements show that vibrato is not required for Minna to play well.

questions.alt: Question 6. ‘eS Based on the story, which sentence best describes Minna? A She plays the cello because she enjoys it. B She complains about her music lessons. C She talks with Willie about music only. D She thinks Mozart is too difficult.

explanation.text: Although Minna grumbles and worries about her vibrato, the story explicitly says she plays the cello because she wants to. Her smiling and playing wonderfully with Porch further show that she enjoys it.

questions.alt: Question 13. Read these sentences from paragraph 1 of the article. A thousand years ago, chocolate was a big secret. Only a few people drank it and nobody ate it. What is the main reason that the author begins with these sentences? A to explain that chocolate comes from the jungle B to explain how chocolate was discovered C to show that chocolate used to be rare D to show how old chocolate is

explanation.text: The opening contrasts the modern world, where chocolate is widely eaten, with a time when only a few people drank it and no one ate it. This shows readers how rare chocolate once was.

questions.alt: Question 14. What is the main reason the author includes the story about monkeys at the beginning of the article? A to show that animals like chocolate B to describe the trees where the cacao pods came from C to explain why the farmers first tasted the cacao pods D to show how the beans inside cacao pods are eaten

explanation.text: The monkeys break open cacao pods and suck out the pulp, and a farmer later copies exactly what they do. This sequence explains that watching the monkeys is why farmers first tasted cacao pods.

questions.alt: Question 15. Read the sentence from paragraph 8 of the article. But then, some villagers noticed a delicious smell, drifting up from a pile of rotting beans. Why did the author include the phrases “delicious smell” and “rotting beans” in the same sentence? A to show an unexpected result B to show what people had planned C to describe a process as it happens D to describe a solution to a problem

explanation.text: Rotting food would normally be expected to smell unpleasant, yet these rotting cacao beans produce a delicious smell. Placing the phrases together therefore emphasizes an unexpected result.

questions.alt: Question 16. How did the invention of the steam engine mostly affect the making of chocolate? A It improved the taste of the cacao bean. B It added different ingredients to chocolate. C It changed the way cacao trees were grown. D It provided an easier way to make chocolate.

explanation.text: After the steam engine was invented, factories could produce many goods more easily, including chocolate. Because production no longer depended on slow hand processing, chocolate became available beyond rich consumers.

questions.alt: Question 17. Which sentence from the article best shows how the Mayans felt about chocolate? A “But then, some villagers noticed a delicious smell, drifting up from a pile of rotting beans.” (paragraph 8) B “They let the beans rot for a few days under banana leaves .. . then put them out to dry in the hot sun.” (paragraph 10) C “To avoid hiking into the jungle for pods, the farmers planted cacao trees in their own fields.” (paragraph 12) D “When the Mayans first caught a whiff of rotting cacao beans, they knew they had found something exciting.” (paragraph 23)

explanation.text: The final paragraph says the Mayans knew they had found “something exciting” when they smelled the cacao beans. This direct statement best reveals their enthusiasm about the discovery.

questions.alt: Question 18. 1g How does the author organize this article? A by showing the effects of chocolate on people around the world B by comparing and contrasting different ways of eating chocolate C by describing events connected to chocolate in the order they happened D by presenting the problem with making chocolate and then giving the solution

explanation.text: The article moves from the first Central American farmers to the Mayans, Aztecs, Spanish explorers, European factories, and chocolate today. This sequence shows that events are presented in the order they happened.

questions.alt: Question 25. How does paragraph 5 relate to paragraph 4? A Paragraph 5 explains why Sarah feels helpless in paragraph 4. B Paragraph 5 shows how Sarah is brave even though she is afraid in paragraph 4. C Paragraph 5 explains why the grandfather is concerned about Sarah in paragraph 4. D Paragraph 5 reveals how the grandfather keeps Sarah out of the danger described in paragraph 4.

explanation.text: Paragraph 4 details Sarah’s fear of the shark, but paragraph 5 says she is “scared but determined” and begins preparing to enter the water. This shows bravery because she chooses to act despite being afraid.

questions.alt: Question 26. paragraph 7, the word “croaked” shows that Sarah A does not want the shark to notice her B does not want to scare the shark away C has difficulty getting the words out D wants to hide her feelings

explanation.text: Sarah is excited and frightened, with a queasy stomach, shaking body, and weak limbs. Her answer comes out as a croak because fear makes it difficult for her to speak normally.

questions.alt: Question 27. How is Sarah helped by her previous swimming experience? A She knows how to get in the water without disturbing the shark. B She knows which supplies to bring with her on the boat. C It helps her to pretend that she is the shark. D It helps her to feel calm in the water.

explanation.text: The story says Sarah’s experience swimming in the bay “paid off” when she slipped into the water and hardly made a ripple. This shows she knows how to enter quietly without disturbing the shark.

questions.alt: Question 28. 2g Which detail best shows that the stories about sharks being killers are wrong? A “But he was no fool and knew there were always risks when you were near any wild animal.” (paragraph 2) B “If the shark comes toward us, just stay calm and don’t wave your hands or feet in front of him.” (paragraph 6) C “Now she knew a shark was below her.” (paragraph 8) D “The tiger shark rested on the bottom not ten feet from them.” (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: The tiger shark rests on the bottom only ten feet from Sarah and her grandfather without attacking them. This calm behavior directly contradicts the idea that sharks are killers that eat anything nearby.

questions.alt: Question 29. How does paragraph 12 contribute to the rest of the story? A It shows the reason for the change in a character. B It gives important details about the story's setting. C It gives new details about the shark’s condition. D It shows why people are able to get close to sharks.

explanation.text: Sarah imagines the shark’s senses and perspective, which helps her replace frightening legends with an understanding of its actual behavior. As a result, the next paragraphs show that she is no longer scared and follows fearlessly.

questions.alt: Question 30. Which sentence describes how Sarah’s grandfather feels about sharks in the ocean? A He thinks sharks will never harm humans who swim with them. B He respects sharks as wild animals and is careful around them. C He knows sharks are smarter than humans and can sense fear. D He admires sharks’ ability to eat almost anything they find.

explanation.text: Sarah’s grandfather has safely worked with sharks for years, but he still recognizes the risks of any wild animal and gives careful instructions. This shows that he respects sharks rather than treating them as harmless.

questions.alt: Question 31. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A Sharks can eat boards and even nails. B Sharks depend on their strong sense of smell. C Sarah notices that the shark is bigger than average. D Sarah is nervous but she is prepared to swim with the shark.

explanation.text: The central action follows Sarah as she feels nervous, prepares carefully, and finally enters the water beside the shark. Therefore, her fear and readiness are essential to a summary of the story.
