questions.alt: Question 1. Why did Wiesner become interested in art? A He enjoyed watching family members draw. B He experimented with the birthday gifts he received. C He found fun pictures in comic books and magazines. D He browsed the supplies at the housepaint and wallpaper store.

explanation.text: Wiesner says his artistic older siblings fascinated him when he watched them draw, and their supplies captivated him. This shows that observing family members first sparked his interest in art.

questions.alt: Question 2. Which quote best expresses the main idea of paragraphs 1 and 2? A “They had many different art supplies around the house.” (paragraph 1) B “There was, and still is, something very appealing about art materials . . .” (paragraph 1) C ‘TI loved looking at all the exotic things they had for sale.” (paragraph 2) D “... paint that seemed so much more grown up than the kind we used at school.” (paragraph 2)

explanation.text: Both paragraphs linger on the colors, textures, smells, tools, and unusual supplies that Wiesner loved. The statement that art materials were deeply appealing therefore best captures the shared main idea.

questions.alt: Question 3. 2 Read this sentence from paragraph 7. Well, it sounded like a good idea. What does the sentence suggest? A The teacher did not approve of Wiesner’s future painting plans. B_ The class was curious about the paintings Wiesner hoped to create. C Wiesner and his friends liked painting a canvas with squirt guns. D Painting with squirt guns did not turn out the way Wiesner expected.

explanation.text: Wiesner says he actually tried shooting colored paint with friends, then qualifies the memory with “it sounded like a good idea.” This wording suggests that the experiment did not work as well as he imagined.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which statement describes how Wiesner’s parents felt about his college and career plans? A His parents encouraged him to go to art school, yet they worried about his future career choices. B_ His parents allowed him to choose his career, and they were glad he decided to go to art school. C His parents knew he would rather do art than school work, and they accepted his choices. D His parents raised him to be an artist, so they expected him to choose the right school.

explanation.text: Wiesner never describes his parents resisting his decision; instead, he says they were excited about his choice. This shows that they let him pursue art and were glad when he chose art school.

questions.alt: Question 5. _ Read this sentence from paragraph 10. As I looked into art schools, I felt like doors were being thrown wide open. What does the phrase “doors were being thrown wide open” suggest? A Wiesner had always known that he would go to art school. B_ Wiesner’s parents decided to allow him to attend art school. C Wiesner found that art school offered many possibilities. D Wiesner was invited by a large number of art schools.

explanation.text: Before art school, Wiesner’s creativity was private and he expected pressure to choose a different career. Finding a place full of other artists revealed many possibilities, which explains the image of doors opening wide.

questions.alt: Question 6. _ Paragraphs 10 and 3 are connected because A paragraph 10 confirms how surprised Wiesner felt about the label he was given as a child B paragraph 10 shows how Wiesner’s friends responded to his childhood interest C paragraph 10 tells how Wiesner finally found others who shared his creativity D paragraph 10 describes how Wiesner’s image had changed

explanation.text: As a child, Wiesner stood out as “the kid who could draw,” unlike most classmates. At art school everyone shared that identity, which shows that he finally found a community of similarly creative people.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which statement is most important to include in a summary of the passage? A Wiesner was always interested in drawing and painting pictures. B Wiesner was entertained by the process of drawing with black ink on white paper. C Wiesner was always pleased to receive art-related gifts for his birthday. D Wiesner was disappointed because no speaker came to talk about art-related fields.

explanation.text: The passage traces Wiesner’s constant drawing from early childhood through his eventual choice of art school and an artistic career. Therefore, his lifelong interest in drawing and painting is essential to a summary.

questions.alt: Question 8. g ‘In paragraph 3, what does the phrase “dragged her backpack along” suggest about Yasmeen? A She is nervous about the upcoming holiday. B She treats her possessions carelessly. C She is in an unhappy mood. D She carries heavy books.

explanation.text: Yasmeen “dragged” her backpack while worrying about being the only Indian student and what classmates would think. This slow, heavy action shows that she is unhappy rather than simply carrying many books.

questions.alt: Question 9. g__ As her family prepares for the holiday Eid-ul-Fitr, how is Yasmeen’s experience this year different from other years? A She feels proud of her family this year. B She feels worried about school this year. C She thinks about getting dressed up this year. D She wants to spend time with the other ladies this year.

explanation.text: Yasmeen usually feels excited when relatives gather for Eid-ul-Fitr, but this year she shrugs and worries about what to present. This contrast shows that the school assignment is troubling her during the celebration.

questions.alt: Question 10. Based on paragraphs 10 and 11, what does Yasmeen understand about her father? A He prefers old books over new books. B_ The Koran is very precious to him. C Reading is one of his favorite activities. D He thinks the Koran is too delicate to take to school.

explanation.text: Yasmeen handles the worn, leather-bound Koran carefully and knows her father would never allow her to take it to school. These details show that the book is precious to him.

questions.alt: Question 11. What is the most important way that paragraphs 13 and 14 develop the story? A They show the process of making henna paste. B They provide additional details about the setting. C They present a hint about how the problem will be solved. D They provide information about the characters’ appearance.

explanation.text: Yasmeen is still searching for a custom when she watches Amma prepare henna paste and fill the cones. This scene hints at the solution because mehndi made from that paste becomes Yasmeen’s presentation.

questions.alt: Question 12. [m paragraphs 12 and 19, what does the reader learn about how Yasmeen’s family celebrates Eid-ul-Fitr? A Jewelry is exchanged at the celebration. B Hand painting is done first at the celebration. C Herbs are used for cooking during the celebration. D Dressing in fine clothing is part of the celebration.

explanation.text: Paragraph 12 describes Amma’s ornate salwar kameez for the party, and paragraph 19 shows all the women and girls gathered in glittering dresses. Together these details show that fine clothing is part of the celebration.

questions.alt: Question 13. ‘[m paragraph 25, the narrator explains that Yasmeen “felt herself gasp” to show that Yasmeen feels A surprised by the teacher’s response B_ nervous about what will happen next C confused by her classmates’ requests D afraid to tell her parents about what happened

explanation.text: Yasmeen expects uncertainty about her tradition, but Mrs. Cross asks for a mehndi design and classmates eagerly join in. Her gasp therefore reveals surprise at their enthusiastic response.

questions.alt: Question 14. What is the most important lesson that Yasmeen learns in the story? A Family belongings should be treated with respect. B Sharing family traditions can be rewarding. C Sharing holidays with family is important. D Family traditions change over time.

explanation.text: Yasmeen initially fears classmates will laugh at her customs, yet they respond eagerly to her mehndi presentation and she rushes home happily. This outcome shows that sharing a family tradition can be rewarding.

questions.alt: Question 15. How are paragraphs 2 through 6 important to the structure of the story? A They describe the setting and the main characters. B_ They describe an event that is later repeated. C They explain why the fox is in the field. D They introduce a problem into the plot.

explanation.text: These paragraphs establish the field and grove where Tom is writing, then introduce Tom and the black fox as they approach one another. This shows how the section sets the scene and presents the main characters.

questions.alt: Question 16. Read this phrase from paragraph 4. ... my heart seemed to have moved up in my body and got stuck in my throat. What does this phrase suggest about Tom? A He is having trouble standing still. B He is having difficulty speaking. C He is experiencing a thrill. D He is feeling affection.

explanation.text: Tom’s paper shakes, his heart seems caught in his throat, and he says he has never been so excited. These physical reactions show that seeing the fox gives him an intense thrill.

questions.alt: Question 17. How are the events described in paragraphs 6 and 7 different from earlier events? A The fox is moving through an open area. B_ The fox is acting with less certainty. C The fox is moving at a faster pace. D The fox is aware of Tom.

explanation.text: At first the fox moves toward Tom because she has not seen him. In paragraphs 6 and 7, the wind carries his scent and she looks directly at him, which shows that she has become aware of Tom.

questions.alt: Question 18. 1g [How is Tom’s experience in the field different from his experience in zoos? A He is able to observe a black fox in the wild. B He is able to observe the way a black fox moves at the zoo. C He spends time observing a black fox at the zoo. D He is frightened to see a black fox in the wild.

explanation.text: At zoos, Tom hurried past foxes displayed before artificial scenery and never truly observed them. The field experience is different because he watches a rare black fox move naturally and freely in the wild.

questions.alt: Question 19. [n paragraph 12, why does ‘Tom hesitate over his letter? A He does not want other people finding out about the fox. B He is not sure his friend will believe him about the fox. C He is not sure how to capture his experience in words. D He does not have time to explain what he witnessed.

explanation.text: Tom thinks the sentence “Today I saw a black fox” makes the most exciting event of his life sound like nothing. He hesitates because he cannot find words powerful enough to capture the experience.

questions.alt: Question 20. Based on paragraphs 13 and 14, how are the words “WAR!” and “FOX!” similar? A They inspire a powerful response in Tom. B_ They provide Tom with a physical reminder. C They help promote Tom’s interest in writing. D They create connections within Tom’s family.

explanation.text: The huge word “WAR!” on the old newspaper leaves Tom unable to speak, and he imagines “FOX!” in the same bold form after his encounter. This comparison shows that both words inspire an overwhelming response in him.

questions.alt: Question 21. oN Which statement best summarizes how the fox affects Tom? A Tom regrets not being able to share his experience. B Tom feels as though he is in a movie. C Tom is deeply moved by the event. D Tom can no longer concentrate.

explanation.text: Tom remains still after the fox leaves, cannot believe what happened, and finds ordinary words unable to express it. These reactions show that the rare encounter moves him deeply.

questions.alt: Question 36. Which evidence best supports the claims the author makes in paragraph 4? A “They may become grumpy, angry, or depressed.” (paragraph 9) B “Sure, Kenny missed his friends, and he was sad at times.” (paragraph 16) C “Kenny’s mom helped him with homework for the first time in two years.” (paragraph 17) D “I had a wonderful experience.” (paragraph 18)

explanation.text: Paragraph 4 warns that constant texting can replace face-to-face time and hurt family relationships. Kenny’s mother helping him for the first time in two years supports that claim because phone-free time renewed their interaction.

questions.alt: Question 37. What does Dr. Elizabeth Dowdell suggest when she says “people expect to be able to access anyone or to be accessed by anyone at any time”? (paragraph 6) A Teens need to stay available by phone at all times. B Many teens want to own a phone that receives texts. C Constant phone use is a behavior of teens that cannot be changed. D Teens are so used to having phones that nonstop texting has become a habit.

explanation.text: Dr. Dowdell describes an expectation of being reachable every moment and explains that this leaves no downtime. This shows that constant access and nonstop texting have become habits for many teens.

questions.alt: Question 38. 3g How does the author organize paragraphs 15 through 20? A by describing the events of the experiment in the order that they happened B_ by explaining the goals and directions of the experiment C by showing the reasons for doing the experiment with the two teens D by comparing the effects that the experiment had on the two teens

explanation.text: The results section alternates between Kenny’s reduced stress, sleep, schoolwork, and family time and Franchesca’s exercise, friends, and better sleep. This organization is a comparison because it shows each teen’s effects side by side.

questions.alt: Question 39. What can the reader infer from paragraphs 17 through 20? A Asking teens to live without their phones will help them become smarter. B Teens may not realize how different their lives can be without their phones. C Teens around the world will enjoy experimenting with their phone use. D Expecting teens to give up their phones is not realistic.

explanation.text: Both teens begin uncertain about losing their phones but discover better sleep, exercise, schoolwork, relationships, or reduced stress. These unexpected benefits show that teens may not realize how different phone-free life can be.

questions.alt: Question 40. How were Kenny’s and Franchesca’s reactions to the 48-hour challenge different? A Only Franchesca benefitted from better sleep with no texting interruptions. B Only Franchesca fully appreciated the freedom of having no phone. C Only Kenny participated in physical activities instead of constant texting. D Only Kenny spent time talking with people after giving up the phone.

explanation.text: Kenny calls the challenge torture and does not plan to repeat it, despite recognizing benefits. Franchesca says she loved the experience and continues it, which shows that only she fully appreciates the phone-free freedom.

questions.alt: Question 41. Which statement best expresses a main idea of the article? A “Some experts are worried about how all that texting is affecting teenagers’ lives.” (paragraph 3) B “If they regularly lose sleep, she adds, teens may start having trouble in school.” (paragraph 9) C “Kenny and Franchesca handed their phones to their mothers for safekeeping.” (paragraph 14) D “Franchesca decided to continue the experiment for a while.” (paragraph 19)

explanation.text: The article explains concerns about texting’s effects on sleep, school, health, and relationships, then tests those concerns with two teens. Therefore, experts’ worry about how texting affects teenagers best expresses the main idea.

questions.alt: Question 42. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A Kenny admits to sleeping with his phone under his pillow. B_ Franchesca claims to exchange a thousand texts per day. C The teens engage in gossip and drama without their phones. D The teens have more free time when they give up texting.

explanation.text: Without constant texting, the teens use their time for sleep, exercise, schoolwork, family, and in-person friends. This broader change is important to a summary because it captures the experiment’s main result.

questions.alt: Question 1. Read this sentence from lines 10 and 11 of the story. I kicked and soared and pranced and whirled, stepped and twirled and spun and flew, tripped and skipped and skimmed and sailed, all over that stage. The author most likely includes this description of Kathleen’s dancing to A emphasize that the dance ends quickly B demonstrate Kathleen's desire for recognition C provide an image of a spirited performance D demonstrate Kathleen’s skills as a dancer

explanation.text: The rapid list of movements—kicking, soaring, whirling, and flying—creates a vivid picture of Kathleen bursting with energy across the stage. This spirited imagery supports choice C rather than focusing on how quickly the dance ends or whether she receives recognition.

questions.alt: Question 2. Read this sentence from lines 11 through 14 of the story. I hardly knew where I was, and I was completely oblivious to the audience, the strange hall, even the adjudicator, though I knew she must be out there somewhere in the blur of humanity beyond the stage, watching carefully and taking notes. What is the meaning of the phrase “oblivious to” as it is used in this sentence? A unaware of B confused by C scornful of D afraid of

explanation.text: Kathleen hardly knows where she is and barely notices the audience, the hall, or even the judge while she dances. Because those surroundings have faded from her attention, oblivious to means unaware of.

questions.alt: Question 3. What do lines 10 through 17 mostly show about Kathleen? A Her confidence in her abilities helps her to remember her routine. B Her delight in dancing allows her to briefly forget her concerns. C She tries to ignore the unfamiliar setting and the audience. D She avoids people who are critical about her appearance.

explanation.text: Kathleen becomes absorbed in the joy of the dance and wishes the music would never stop. This delight makes the audience, unfamiliar hall, judge, and Tess O’Hara disappear from her thoughts for a while, which supports choice B.

questions.alt: Question 4. _ Lines 36 through 42 mainly develop a central idea of the story by revealing A the importance of being able to communicate effectively B_ that Kathleen has lost interest in the conversation because of her success C that Kathleen’s success is meaningful only if she can share it with her family D the significance of the support of family members even when they are absent

explanation.text: Even after hearing the audience praise her, Kathleen loses interest and searches for Polly. She wants Polly to describe the performance to her mother, which shows that her success matters most when she can share it with her family.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which detail from the story best shows why Polly is important to Kathleen? A Polly can best describe Kathleen’s dance to her mam. B_ Polly allows Kathleen to receive praise from her teacher. C Polly made Kathleen’s dress using her own curtain fabric. D Polly inspired Kathleen to participate in the dance recital.

explanation.text: Kathleen says that neither she nor the other girls can adequately describe the performance to her mother. She believes Polly will be able to tell it with panache, which directly explains why Polly’s presence is so important to her.

questions.alt: Question 6. _ Read this sentence from lines 49 through 51 of the story. I kept on smiling, and at the same time I was trying to look around Mrs. Maguire’s bulk to see if I could catch a glimpse of Polly’s flying figure and wide smile. This sentence suggests that Kathleen A is surprised by her teacher’s presence B_ hopes to avoid chatting after her performance C is trying to be polite even though she is distracted D feels uncomfortable due to her teacher’s compliments

explanation.text: Kathleen keeps smiling at Mrs. Maguire, so she continues to treat her teacher politely. At the same time, she looks around the teacher for Polly, which shows that her attention is elsewhere even as she maintains the conversation.

questions.alt: Question 7. How does the author establish the point of view differently in lines 1 through 32 as compared to lines 33 through 61? A by portraying the narrator’s actions rather than sharing only the narrator’s feelings B_ by describing the narrator’s inner thoughts rather than sharing the inner thoughts of all the characters C by revealing the narrator’s intentions through description instead of using description to share the opinions of all the characters D by revealing the narrator’s thoughts only through description instead of through both description and dialogue

explanation.text: In lines 1–32, Kathleen’s feelings and reactions are conveyed through her first-person narration and descriptions of the dance. Lines 33–61 add spoken comments and conversations with Mrs. Maguire and Polly, so her thoughts are then revealed through both description and dialogue.

questions.alt: Question 22. What does most of the poem describe? A the sounds of evening B animals preparing for nightfall C creatures hunting for food at night D the way the weather changes at sunset

explanation.text: Most stanzas show creatures responding to the fading day: birds seek nests, flowers close, cattle return home, and deer settle to sleep. These repeated details support the idea that the poem mainly describes animals preparing for nightfall.

questions.alt: Question 23. Read lines 1 through 3 of the poem. Oh, pleasant eventide! Clouds on the western side Grow grey and greyer hiding the warm sun: Which words best describe the tone that the poet creates at the beginning of the poem? A concern for warmth B_ surprised alarm at day’s end C enthusiasm for the time of day D disapproval of the weather pattern

explanation.text: The opening exclamation calls evening pleasant even as the clouds hide the sun. That approving, energetic greeting reveals enthusiasm for this time of day rather than alarm or disapproval.

questions.alt: Question 24. Read lines 6 and 7 of the poem. Then read this dictionary entry for “screen.” screen v. 1, to provide with a means to exclude insects 2. to shelter or conceal from view 3. to separate or sort 4. to use a method to determine suitability for a task Which definition best matches the meaning of “screened” as it is used in lines 6 and 7? A definition 1 B definition 2 C definition 3 D definition 4

explanation.text: The stock-doves sit within the leafy wood, where the leaves hide and protect them from view. This context shows that screened means sheltered or concealed, matching definition 2.

questions.alt: Question 25. Which lines provide the best evidence that events in nature are repeated over many years? A “The very squirrel leaps from bough to bough But lazily; pauses; and settles now Where once he stored his food.” (lines 8 through 10) B “Nibbling his fill he stops from time to time And listens where he sits.” (lines 19 and 20) C “Hark! that’s the nightingale, Telling the selfsame tale Her song told when this ancient earth was young:” (lines 31 through 33) D “Through all the hours of night until the dawn They sleep, forgetting fear.” (lines 44 and 45)

explanation.text: The nightingale tells the selfsame tale that it sang when the ancient earth was young. By connecting its present song to the distant past, these lines provide evidence that the event in nature has repeated for many years.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which lines of the poem best demonstrate a gradual change that occurs during the evening? A lines 11 through 13 B lines 16 through 20 C lines 44 and 45 D lines 54 and 55

explanation.text: Lines 11–13 say that the flowers close one by one, with the lily and rose gradually shutting their petals. The phrase one by one demonstrates a change unfolding step by step during the evening.

questions.alt: Question 27. Which word best describes most of the evening activity mentioned in the poem? A busy B lonely C strained D cooperative

explanation.text: Throughout the evening, creatures nest, leap, eat, return home, emerge, hunt, and sing. Although the scene feels calm, this variety of activity shows that busy best describes what most of the animals are doing.

questions.alt: Question 28. 2g Which lines best show that the night is different from the evening? A lines 1 through 5 B lines 11 through 15 C lines 26 through 30 D lines 56 through 60

explanation.text: Lines 56–60 explicitly announce that evening is done and night has come. The statement that the great calm has ceased shows that night is a new stage, clearly distinguishing it from the evening described before.

questions.alt: Question 29. Lines 6 through 14 are important to the article because they A show the potential effects of reading over time B highlight the value of scientific research C emphasize the challenges beginning readers face D provide examples of the problems scientists face

explanation.text: Lines 6–14 explain that reading builds attention, sequential thinking, and the ability to connect causes with effects. The discussion of childhood brain flexibility and improvement through practice shows how reading can affect the mind over time.

questions.alt: Question 30. Why is it important for parents to encourage reading at a young age? A Children are more likely than adults to follow stories all the way through. B Children are more likely than adults to use technology for information. C Children have longer attention spans than college students and older adults. D Children can more easily grasp new ideas and abilities than adults.

explanation.text: Greenfield says that children’s brains have more plasticity, or ability to grow and change. Because young children can form new thinking skills more readily, parents should encourage reading while new ideas and abilities are easier to grasp.

questions.alt: Question 31. What is the meaning of the word “simulate” as it is used in line 34 of the article? A to expect B to illustrate C to remember D to imitate

explanation.text: The article says that readers’ brains activate as though they were actually experiencing the described sights, sounds, smells, and tastes. In this context, simulate means to imitate or re-create a real experience.

questions.alt: Question 32. How do lines 45 through 53 illustrate possible ways to prevent brain disease? A by including the positive effects of certain activities on the brain B_ by describing how harmful substances form in the brain C by emphasizing the importance of brain scans for older adults D by proving that brain-based activities should be enjoyed by adults

explanation.text: The study found low levels of disease-related plaques in older adults who had read, played chess, or written letters since childhood. This evidence illustrates how brain-stimulating activities may have a protective effect against brain disease.

questions.alt: Question 33. How does the author support John Stein’s claim that “Reading exercises the whole brain” (line 26)? A by describing how other physical functions in the body work B_ by explaining which parts of the brain connect to senses C by explaining how functions of the brain are triggered D by describing a new method of creating pathways

explanation.text: The MRI study found that imagining a book’s landscapes, sounds, smells, and tastes activates the same brain regions used for those real experiences. This evidence supports Stein’s claim by showing that reading triggers many different brain functions.

questions.alt: Question 34. Which statement best conveys the central idea of the article? A ‘Tm a novelist—I’ve written five books for teenagers—and it’s obviously in my interest to encourage people to read.” (lines 1 and 2) B “But there’s increasing evidence that reading for pleasure isn’t just another leisure pursuit, or merely a way of improving literacy skills and factual knowledge.” (lines 3 and 4) C “When we ‘get lost’ in a good book, we're doing more than simply following a story.” (line 28) D “As the old saying goes: ‘You're never alone with a book.” (lines 36 and 37)

explanation.text: The article develops evidence that reading improves attention and empathy, reduces stress, and may protect the brain. The statement in choice B introduces this broad idea by saying that reading offers benefits beyond pleasure, literacy, and factual knowledge.

questions.alt: Question 35. What is the author’s main purpose for writing the article? A to encourage teens to read books that she has written B_ to convince people that reading is a worthwhile activity C to demonstrate why reading is a good form of entertainment D to show the negative effects of playing games instead of reading

explanation.text: The author presents studies and expert testimony about reading’s mental, social, and physical benefits. This collection of evidence is meant to convince readers that reading is worthwhile, not merely to advertise her books or compare forms of entertainment.

questions.alt: Question 36. How do lines 18 through 21 build on the idea introduced in lines 10 through 13? A by showing a relationship between DNA and plant growth B_ by showing how changes in DNA can help weeds as well as crops C by showing that weeds continue to resist farmers’ attempts to kill them D by showing how glyphosate is a long-term solution to the problem of weeds

explanation.text: Lines 10–13 describe scientists changing crop DNA so the crops survive glyphosate. Lines 18–21 extend that idea by explaining that natural DNA changes can also let weeds survive the chemical, which shows that genetic changes can benefit both crops and weeds.

questions.alt: Question 37. What is the meaning of the phrase “impossibly improbable” as it is used in lines 21 through 23? A usually certain B highly unlikely C extremely slow D rarely noteworthy

explanation.text: The author says that the chance of a helpful DNA change in any one weed is very, very small. That context indicates that impossibly improbable means highly unlikely.

questions.alt: Question 38. Which conclusion is best supported by lines 27 through 32? A The DNA of weeds evolves rapidly. B Changes to the DNA of plants can occur by chance. C Greater quantities of glyphosate are required to kill weeds. D Plants evolve more quickly in some parts of the world than in others.

explanation.text: The lottery comparison explains that any individual weed has only a tiny chance of developing resistance, but billions of repeated chances eventually produce a winner. This evidence supports the conclusion that useful DNA changes can occur by chance.

questions.alt: Question 39. Which statement best describes the main idea of the section titled “Winning the lottery”? A Farmers used glyphosate for many years on millions of hectares of crops. B_ The odds of any one person winning the lottery are tiny. C Many people expected glyphosate to work forever. D Several kinds of weeds developed a resistance to glyphosate.

explanation.text: The section explains how repeated exposure made rare resistant weeds increasingly likely and then names rigid ryegrass, horseweed, and at least twenty-one resistant species. Those examples support the main idea that several kinds of weeds developed resistance to glyphosate.

questions.alt: Question 40. __ The section titled “Copy that” relates to the section titled “Winning the lottery” by A providing evidence to support an opinion B_ introducing new ways to solve a problem C providing an example to explain a main idea D describing events in the order that they take place

explanation.text: Winning the lottery introduces the general idea that rare DNA changes eventually produce glyphosate-resistant weeds. Copy that then focuses on palmer pigweed and explains how it develops and spreads resistance, providing a specific example of that main idea.

questions.alt: Question 41. _ Lines 49 through 53 support which of the following claims? A The use of glyphosate should be stopped because the herbicide has proven to be a failure. B_ The only way to prevent weeds from resisting herbicides is to change the DNA of weeds. C Blocking sunlight from reaching the soil is the best way to prevent weed seeds from sprouting. D Fighting weeds is especially difficult because of their ability to evolve to resist threats.

explanation.text: Resistant pigweed carries extra copies of the gene that makes glyphosate’s target enzyme. Because the plant produces more enzyme than the chemical can block, these lines demonstrate how weeds can evolve around a threat and why fighting them is difficult.

questions.alt: Question 42. What is the most likely reason the author describes the seed destructor in lines 64 through 67? A to support the idea that farmers need to use a variety of methods to fight weeds B_ to show how technology is the solution to uncontrollable weed growth C to explain why mechanical methods to fight weeds are better than herbicides D to describe how scientists have found the best way to protect desirable crops

explanation.text: The seed destructor is described alongside herbicide mixtures, flattened rye, and plowing seeds deeper underground. Adding this different mechanical approach supports the author’s point that farmers need a variety of methods because no single solution protects every crop.

questions.alt: Question 1. ‘Why are lines 14 through 18 important to the article? A They explain how old maps were inaccurate. B They emphasize the limited knowledge of geography at the time. C They emphasize Great Britain’s knowledge of sea exploration. D They explain Great Britain’s choice for using James Cook to explore unknown lands.

explanation.text: Cartographers could only draw the southern regions vaguely and labeled them Unknown Southern Land because they did not know what was there. This uncertainty emphasizes how limited geographic knowledge was when Great Britain chose Cook to explore.

questions.alt: Question 2. Read this sentence from lines 25 and 26. Cook was ambitious and quickly learned that his ability to read set him apart from other sailors, even ones much older than he was. Which evidence from the article best supports the author's claim in these lines? A “While the merchant ships he worked on carried cargo from port to port, James Cook assumed more and more responsibility as a navigator and learned the ways of the sea.” (lines 30 through 32) B “After ten years on the collier, Cook decided to join the Royal Navy.” (line 34) C “Its officers were recognized not only as outstanding sailors but also as ‘gentlemen.” (lines 35 and 36) D “During the trip, he also explored New Zealand and the east coast of Australia, which had never been visited by a European.” (lines 53 and 54)

explanation.text: Cook used his reading to study navigation and then took on increasing responsibility as a navigator aboard merchant ships. That advancement supports the claim that his literacy and ambition distinguished him from older sailors.

questions.alt: Question 3. According to the article, why were cartographers so valued during Cook’s lifetime? A They provided the information ships required to stay on course while at sea. B_ They were responsible for informing the public of the names of unfamiliar regions. C They prevented sailors from visiting exotic lands with unknown dangers. D They were assigned the duty of teaching other sailors to read and draw maps.

explanation.text: The article explains that even a small map error could send a ship hundreds of miles off course and delay its search for food or water. Cartographers were therefore valued because their accurate information helped ships navigate safely.

questions.alt: Question 4. What idea about Cook is demonstrated by lines 46 through 53? A his impressive experience with the collier Endeavour B the necessity of sending him to North America C the praise he deserved for expanding Great Britain’s territory D his effectiveness in drawing charts

explanation.text: Cook’s calculations were so accurate that his reputation grew, and he successfully charted the Canadian border. His later selection to lead the South Seas expedition further demonstrates his effectiveness at drawing reliable charts.

questions.alt: Question 5. What connection does the article make between astronomy and navigation? A Explorers traveled the seas to try to discover new planets and stars. B_ Explorers used maps to determine the positions of planets and stars. C Traveling on the sea was difficult without a reference to the position of the sun and stars. D Traveling on the sea required a constant view of the sun and stars.

explanation.text: Before electronic navigation, sailors used instruments to determine a ship’s position relative to the sun and stars. This explains why astronomy provided essential reference points for traveling accurately at sea.

questions.alt: Question 6. How do lines 51 through 58 develop a central idea of the article? A by naming previously unknown lands B_ by emphasizing Cook’s value to the navy C by describing Cook’s contributions to exploration D by explaining improvements in the study of geography

explanation.text: Cook successfully completed the Venus expedition, explored New Zealand and eastern Australia, and drew accurate maps of the region. These achievements develop the article’s central idea by showing his important contributions to exploration.

questions.alt: Question 7. How does the author organize the ideas in the article? A by explaining how James Cook encouraged Great Britain to seek out new lands B_ by showing how the lack of reliable maps caused dangers in early ocean expeditions C by relating events sequentially to elaborate on James Cook’s accomplishments D by describing ship instruments to show the development of sea navigation

explanation.text: The article moves in time from Cook’s childhood and apprenticeship through his naval advancement and major expeditions. This chronological sequence shows how his education, discipline, and mapping skills led to increasingly significant accomplishments.

questions.alt: Question 8. g The author’s purpose in lines 4 through 7 is to A give examples of the variety of sports available B_ emphasize the benefits of participating in sports C provide reasons for letting her family play sports D show a personal connection to the topic of sports

explanation.text: The author mentions playing basketball and volleyball herself and describes her son’s and daughter’s sports activities. These family experiences show her personal connection to youth sports before she discusses their risks.

questions.alt: Question 9. g _ According to the article, why have injuries to veterans influenced studies of athletes with concussions? A Thousands of veterans have been diagnosed with traumatic brain injuries. B_ Repeated head injuries are potentially damaging to the brain or spinal cord. C Repeated head injuries sustained in sports and combat have similar outcomes. D Public awareness of the symptoms and seriousness of head injuries has been growing.

explanation.text: The article acknowledges that combat and sports are very different situations, but says repeated head injuries in both can have similar consequences. That shared outcome explains why evidence from injured veterans has informed the study of athletes with concussions.

questions.alt: Question 10. Read this sentence from lines 22 and 23. No longer can a soccer player or cyclist just “walk off” a bump to the head. What does the phrase “walk off” suggest? A The injury is made worse by moving around. B_ The person needs to leave the playing area. C The person is not seriously injured. D The injury does not affect the legs.

explanation.text: To walk off an injury means to keep moving and treat it as a minor problem that will quickly pass. The author says players can no longer do this because even an ordinary-looking bump may cause serious brain damage.

questions.alt: Question 11. Lines 26 through 32 support the central idea that A progress is being made to make sports safer for kids B resistance to changes in youth sports is being overcome C children count on parents and coaches to keep them safe D a lack of training in treating brain injuries has inspired legislation

explanation.text: Minnesota has enacted education requirements about head injuries and requires medical clearance before a student returns to play. These concrete reforms support the central idea that progress is being made to make youth sports safer.

questions.alt: Question 12. Read these sentences from lines 44 through 47. Sports-related brain injuries are also receiving more public attention because of the willingness of professional athletes to speak out. For example, more than 2,000 retired NFL players filed a lawsuit against football helmet maker Riddell’ and the NFL™ for hiding information about dangers of concussions and the consequences of hits to the head. Which inference do these sentences best support? A Older helmets do not protect athletes from head injuries. B_ The problem of head injuries is not confined to student athletes. C Professional athletes are reluctant to report head injuries during their careers. D Legal action is an effective way to discover information about the dangers of concussions.

explanation.text: The example involves more than two thousand retired professional football players, not children or school teams. Their lawsuit over concussion dangers supports the inference that head injuries affect professional as well as student athletes.

questions.alt: Question 13. Read this sentence from lines 50 through 52. By taking what we already know, we can create opportunities for our children to participate in sports while keeping them healthy and safe. Which lines best support the author’s claim in this sentence? PP A “We are already learning a lot about traumatic brain injuries from our returning Afghanistan and Iraq veterans. An estimated 40,000 men and women veterans have been diagnosed with traumatic brain injuries from multiple concussions in combat.” (lines 15 through 17) B “According to the American Association of Neurological Surgeons (AANS), roughly 446,788 sports-related head injuries were treated at U.S. emergency rooms in 2009.” (lines 20 and 21) C ‘Recently, the largest youth football league, Pop Warner, announced it will limit the amount of contact and collisions in practice to protect its 285,000 football players (ages 5-15) from potential repetitive brain trauma. This move responded to a recent study of second-grade football players that showed the average player suffers more than 100 head impacts during 5 games and 10 practices.” (lines 33 through 37) D “Sports-related brain injuries are also receiving more public attention because of the willingness of professional athletes to speak out. For example, more than 2,000 retired NFL players filed a lawsuit against football helmet maker Riddell’ and the NFL™ for hiding information about dangers of concussions and the consequences of hits to the head.” (lines 44 through 48)

explanation.text: A study documented more than one hundred head impacts for an average second-grade player, and Pop Warner responded by limiting contact during practice. This example directly supports the claim because it shows an organization using existing knowledge to let children play more safely.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which claim can be supported by evidence from the article? A Participating in youth sports can expose children to risks. B Limiting physical contact during practice keeps players safer during games. C The age that a child begins playing football is related to the incidence of head injuries. D Poorly made sports equipment accounts for increases in head injuries among professional athletes.

explanation.text: The article reports hundreds of thousands of sports-related head injuries and describes young football players experiencing impacts comparable to car accidents. This evidence supports the claim that participation in youth sports can expose children to real risks.

questions.alt: Question 15. Read lines 10 and 11. He could sit and watch the tube and munch on some junk, or he could go to bed because it was getting late, or .. . These lines suggest that Terry A prefers to relax in the evening B_ disapproves of snacking in front of the television C wants to do something different from his usual activities D wastes time deciding whether to watch television or sleep

explanation.text: Terry considers his familiar choices of television, snacks, or bed, yet he is already moving toward the garage. His unfinished thought and movement toward the kit car show that he wants to do something different from those usual activities.

questions.alt: Question 16. How do lines 16 through 23 mostly contribute to the story? A by describing the types of tools made for fixing cars B by revealing details about the previous owner of the tools C by suggesting that Terry has had previous experience fixing cars D by showing that Terry has all the necessary tools for the work he plans to do

explanation.text: The passage inventories Terry’s complete set of sockets, wrenches, gauges, and specialized tools, all kept in excellent condition. These details show that he already has the equipment needed to begin working on the kit car.

questions.alt: Question 17. Based on lines 24 through 29, readers can conclude that Terry A rarely works in the garage B feels proud of his possessions C is worried about losing his tools D is still becoming familiar with his tools

explanation.text: Terry carefully wipes every tool before returning it and checks that every item is in its proper drawer. This consistent care reveals pride in his possessions rather than unfamiliarity or fear that they are missing.

questions.alt: Question 18. 1g What is the main significance of the cat clock in the story? A Details about the clock help the reader to better understand the characters. B_ Descriptions of the clock provide clues about upcoming conflicts. C Descriptions of the clock help the reader to understand the theme. D Details about the clock add suspense to the story.

explanation.text: Terry bought the clock as a child in an unsuccessful attempt to get his mother’s attention, which reveals something about their relationship and his emotional history. His later shock at the clock’s time also shows how completely he becomes absorbed in the car, so the clock deepens the reader’s understanding of him.

questions.alt: Question 19. /n lines 55 through 59, what does “Time seemed to ” suggest about Terry? A Terry works very quickly. B_ Terry is absorbed in his task. C Terry forgets to eat his dinner. D Terry is ignoring the cat clock.

explanation.text: Terry organizes parts for hours without noticing his hunger or the passing time, then is stunned to see that it is three in the morning. This shows that time seems to stop because he is deeply absorbed in the task.

questions.alt: Question 20. 29 Which lines best reveal the change in Terry’s attitude toward the task he has chosen? A “Let's see what we've got... , Terry said under his breath and started taking the boxes off, setting them around the garage on the floor, looking in each one as he did so.” (lines 39 and 40) B “All of that he knew, could understand, but there were numbered bags and boxes with just bolts and parts, and many of them made no sense to him, and he despaired of ever understanding it all when in the bottom of one of the boxes he found the instructions.” (lines 43 through 46) C “A monkey could do this, he said, sitting on the frame, going page by page. “You don’t have to know anything about cars at all. It’s beautiful . . 2” (lines 50 and 51) D “Not only were the instructions complete but they explained what was in each numbered box or bag—what each set of bolts was for—and he set about organizing all of them to get ready for starting work on the car.” (lines 52 through 54)

explanation.text: Before finding the instructions, Terry despairs of ever understanding the many unfamiliar parts. His excited claim that even a monkey could follow the detailed guide shows his change from discouragement to confidence and enthusiasm.

questions.alt: Question 21. How does the setting affect the plot of the story? A The pile of boxes and car parts motivate him to clean up the garage. B_ The instructions and set of tools inspire Terry to work on the car. C The car parts and tools in the garage bring up memories Terry has long forgotten. D The time of night gives Terry time to do something interesting.

explanation.text: The garage contains the unfinished kit car, Terry’s complete tool set, and the detailed instructions hidden among the parts. This shows how the setting makes the project seem possible and inspires him to organize the pieces and begin work.

questions.alt: Question 36. Read these sentences from lines 13 through 16. A cramped public-school test kitchen might seem an unlikely outpost for a food revolution. But Collazo and scores of others across the country—celebrity chefs and lunch ladies, district superintendents and politicians—say they’re determined to improve what kids eat in school. Which central idea is supported by these sentences? A It is not easy to make changes in school lunch programs. B Public schools have become test kitchens for improving the American diet. C Many people have been seeking to improve the nutritional value of school lunches. D Educating students about nutrition can improve their health for the rest of their lives.

explanation.text: The sentences identify chefs, cafeteria workers, administrators, politicians, and others across the country who are determined to change school food. This broad coalition supports the central idea that many people are seeking to improve the nutritional value of school lunches.

questions.alt: Question 37. Ag. Read this sentence from lines 16 through 18. Most school cafeterias are staffed by poorly trained, badly equipped workers who churn out 4.8 billion hot lunches a year. What does the phrase “churn out” suggest about the school lunches? A They are mass-produced without careful planning. B_ They are easily prepared using modern kitchens. C They are economically made and include nutritious ingredients. D They are thoughtfully created and include wide-ranging menus.

explanation.text: Poorly trained workers with inadequate equipment produce billions of inexpensive lunches, often repeating breaded meat, fries, and overcooked vegetables. In this context, churn out suggests mass production with little careful planning rather than thoughtful preparation.

questions.alt: Question 38. 3g Read this sentence from lines 24 and 25. As rates of childhood obesity and diabetes skyrocket, public-health officials say schools need to change the way kids eat. Why should this information be included in a summary of the article? A It predicts the consequences of current eating habits for students. B_ It emphasizes the importance of healthy meal options for students. C It suggests that schools are responsible for diseases related to eating. D It highlights the role school administrators have in teaching healthy habits.

explanation.text: Rising childhood obesity and diabetes establish the serious health problem that the proposed lunch reforms are meant to address. This information belongs in a summary because it explains why providing students with healthier meal options is important.

questions.alt: Question 39. Based on lines 24 through 30 of the article, what is an obstacle to improving nutrition outside of school? A Families do not make time to cook meals at home. B_ Kids enjoy watching ads for fast food. C Health officials hinder meal planning by parents. D Kids do not know how to grow fresh produce.

explanation.text: The article says that home cooking is becoming a forgotten art while children are surrounded by fast-food advertising. This indicates that many families are not preparing meals at home, creating an obstacle to better nutrition outside school.

questions.alt: Question 40. 490 How do lines 36 through 46 mostly contribute to the development of ideas in the article? A They show how restaurateurs can get involved to improve school lunches. B_ They show how easily students accepted healthy changes to school food. C They use the Santa Fe school district as an example of how change happens gradually. D They explain why a Santa Fe school district did not serve fruits and vegetables.

explanation.text: In Santa Fe, volunteers first taught a cooking class, then struggled with kitchens designed only to reheat food, and finally obtained basic tools through a grant. This sequence uses one district to demonstrate that improving school food requires gradual steps through practical obstacles.

questions.alt: Question 41. _ Based on lines 43 through 46, which statement about school kitchens is most likely true? A They do not have professional cooks. B_ They have too little time to prepare healthy foods. C They are not equipped to serve fresh foods. D They contain older equipment that should be replaced.

explanation.text: The school kitchens could reheat prepared food but could not prepare fresh ingredients, and the cooks needed grant-funded knives just to peel and chop produce. These details support the inference that the kitchens were not equipped to serve fresh foods.

questions.alt: Question 42. According to lines 47 through 56, why is cost a factor in changing school food? A Schools receive income from the sale of popular but unhealthy vending machine snacks. B_ Schools across the country have to buy new vending machines to sell healthy snacks. C Students have less money to buy healthy snacks from vending machines. D New vending machines stock healthy but expensive snacks.

explanation.text: Schools can earn as much as seventy-five thousand dollars each year from soda and candy machines. Because students spend less on healthier replacements, changing the food means giving up income from popular unhealthy snacks.

questions.alt: Question 1. According to the narrator, what makes playing basketball appealing? A She likes the feeling of knowing exactly what to do when she plays basketball. B She feels the skills needed for playing basketball are simple to master. C She thinks that when she plays basketball she is likely to succeed. D She gets to be like her brothers when she plays basketball.

explanation.text: The narrator contrasts the clear rules and specific jobs in basketball with the uncertainty she feels in school and life. Knowing exactly what to do makes basketball appealing because it gives her confidence and direction.

questions.alt: Question 2. According to the narrator, why is basketball “as good as Shakespeare” (lines 16 and 17)? A It connects her to important people. B It helps her to understand her life. C It has the power to strongly affect her. D It helps her deal with disappointments in life.

explanation.text: The narrator says basketball has force and power, much like Shakespeare or poetry, and that it makes her come alive. This shows that basketball is "as good as Shakespeare" because it can affect her deeply and intensely.

questions.alt: Question 3. 2 Based on lines 20 through 29, what can readers conclude about the narrator's father? A He thinks boys and girls should pursue different activities. B He believes that siblings should support one another. C He values sports more than he values education. D He wishes that all of his children were boys.

explanation.text: Her father says that boys may play sports and attend college, while girls should stay home, work, and marry. His contrast indicates that he believes boys and girls should pursue different activities.

questions.alt: Question 4. What does the narrator discover in lines 30 through 36 that affects the plot of the story? A Watching basketball gives her the same feeling as studying. B She wants other people to pay more attention to her. C She is jealous of the opportunity her brother has. D Her passion for basketball is overwhelming.

explanation.text: As she watches her brother's games, the narrator imagines herself making every move and says her desire becomes bigger than either parent. These details reveal that her passion for basketball is overwhelming and will drive the story's conflict.

questions.alt: Question 5. How does the incident described in lines 53 through 59 affect the story? A It shows how the narrator and her family attempt to compromise. B It escalates the conflict so that the father is forced to make a choice. C It reveals the influence the mother and brother have on the father’s behavior. D It demonstrates the father’s beliefs and his realization that his beliefs are wrong.

explanation.text: After her father bans her from games, the narrator locks herself in a closet, cries, and refuses food. Her extreme response escalates the conflict because it forces her father to choose between maintaining the ban and protecting the daughter he loves.

questions.alt: Question 6. How does the structure of the story contribute to its meaning? A The narrator describes how she differs from her brothers, which leads to a conflict with her father. B_ The narrator expresses her point of view, which provides background for the conflict with her father. C The narrator describes her frustration with life at home and school, which leads to problems with her father. D The narrator begins with a reflection on rules, which leads to an improvement in her relationship with her father.

explanation.text: The first-person opening explains how basketball gives the narrator certainty, purpose, and a feeling of being alive. This background makes the later conflict understandable because readers know why her father's opposition threatens something essential to her.

questions.alt: Question 7. How does the basketball game setting relate to a theme of the story? A by showing where the narrator feels the most alive B_ by showing how the narrator utilizes her knowledge C by showing how the narrator differs from other girls D by showing why the narrator supports her brother

explanation.text: At basketball games, the narrator feels alert, certain, and fully engaged as she imagines herself on the court. This setting supports the theme because the gym is where she feels most alive and discovers a sense of purpose.

questions.alt: Question 22. Read these sentences from line 3. My friends were fond of practical jokes. I do not care to know people who are not. These statements impact the story by explaining A why the narrator laughs so hard at dinner B_ the narrator’s presence at the dinner C the narrator’s reason for suspicion D why the guests embraced him

explanation.text: The opening identifies the narrator's friends as people who enjoy practical jokes, and their exaggerated welcome and laughter seem suspicious to him. That knowledge gives him a reason to expect a trick and drives his wary actions throughout the story.

questions.alt: Question 23. What does the phrase “as a dog scents game” in line 14 of the story suggest about the narrator’s actions? A He is enjoying the laughter of the other guests. B He feels on the verge of finding the prankster. C He feels left out of the dinner conversation. D He is alert to some hidden plan.

explanation.text: A dog scenting game becomes intensely alert while tracking something it cannot yet see. The comparison therefore shows that the narrator is watching closely for evidence of a hidden plan.

questions.alt: Question 24. Read lines 14 and 15 of the story. I did not let a word, or a meaning, or a gesture escape me. This sentence suggests that the narrator A feels anxious about his current situation Bis highly critical of his friends’ interactions C feels defenseless against the coming events D is too distracted to join his friends’ conversation

explanation.text: The narrator studies every word, look, and gesture because he expects his friends to deceive him. This restless monitoring reveals that he feels anxious rather than relaxed among them.

questions.alt: Question 25. How do lines 25 through 33 build suspense? A They suggest that someone is looking in the window. B_ They extend the narrator’s search for deceit. C They indicate unusual details about the room. D They reveal the narrator’s fear of the dark.

explanation.text: The narrator lights candles and searches the room, the bed, the curtains, and the shutters without finding a trap. This extended search builds suspense because each new inspection prolongs the uncertainty about what his friends may have planned.

questions.alt: Question 26. Which words reveal the irony of the narrator's situation? A “I thought: ‘Here are people who have more than their share of amusement, and apparently without reason. They must have planned some good joke.” (lines 9 through 11) B “The hour struck for retiring; and the whole household came to escort me to my room.” (line 17) C “However, the night was advancing; and I ended by coming to the conclusion that I was foolish.” (lines 35 and 36) D “The precautions I had taken in closing the shutters and going to sleep in the middle of the room had only brought about the practical joke I had been trying to avoid.” (lines 68 and 69)

explanation.text: The narrator moves the mattress to avoid a trick, but that precaution places it where the valet trips and spills the tea. The outcome is ironic because his attempt to prevent a practical joke creates the very comic accident he feared.

questions.alt: Question 27. What effect does the point of view of the story have on the reader? A The point of view increases suspense by limiting the reader to the narrator’s understanding of events. B The point of view creates humor by showing how the narrator's friends misunderstand him. C The point of view raises the reader’s sympathy for the questions the narrator faces. D The point of view adds to the reader’s mistrust of what the narrator reports.

explanation.text: The first-person narration gives readers only the narrator's suspicions and observations, not his friends' intentions. Because readers cannot know whether a trap actually exists, they share his uncertainty and feel greater suspense.

questions.alt: Question 28. 2g Which detail best expresses a theme of the story? A “One autumn I went to spend the hunting season with some friends . . .” (line 1) B “Assuredly I am to be the victim...” (line 11) C ‘I heard persons moving about outside my door.” (line 23) D “For at least another hour I remained awake, starting at the slightest sound.” (line 51)

explanation.text: The narrator's certainty that he will be a victim makes him search the room and move the mattress into the valet's path. His actions demonstrate the theme that fearful expectations can help bring about the very outcome a person hopes to avoid.

questions.alt: Question 29. Which words from the article provide the best clue to the meaning of the word “tedious” in line 3? A “Confession Time” (line 1) B “head flat on desk” (lines 1 and 2) C “takes an eternity to tick by” (line 4) D “You begin to wish you had your cell phone” (lines 4 and 5)

explanation.text: Saying that the minute hand takes "an eternity" to move makes a brief period seem painfully long. This clue indicates that "tedious" describes something slow, tiresome, and boring.

questions.alt: Question 30. Read this sentence from line 6 of the article. Boredom is the enemy of fun. Which quotation best helps the reader understand this sentence? A “If fun is the big drop of a roller coaster, boredom is waiting in line.” (line 7) B “If we want to figure out the future of fun, we have to first figure out how to end boredom.” (line 8) C “Imagine that: a world where each second is interesting and nothing is dull.” (line 9) D “That sounds like science fiction, but new psychological research hints that being bored—even in class—may one day be a thing of the past.” (lines 9 through 11)

explanation.text: The thrilling drop of a roller coaster is fun, while waiting in its long line feels dull and frustrating. Placing these opposite experiences together explains why the author calls boredom the enemy of fun.

questions.alt: Question 31. Lines 19 through 26 connect modern entertainment to boredom by suggesting that it causes us to A want more challenging games to play B_ misunderstand the role of fun in our lives C develop expectations for high levels of excitement D avoid adapting to new technological innovations

explanation.text: Modern entertainment supplies a constant stream of fast, intense stimulation. The article explains that people become accustomed to that high level of excitement, so ordinary situations can then seem boring by comparison.

questions.alt: Question 32. How does Professor Eastwood’s work differ from previous research? A by attempting to create a new definition for boredom B by failing to look into a connection to brain activity C by focusing on the human attention system D by using data from earlier studies on boredom

explanation.text: Eastwood considers earlier research but proposes that boredom comes from the human attention system and its search for an interesting target. His explanation differs because it focuses specifically on how attention works rather than on the broader ideas summarized before it.

questions.alt: Question 33. Read lines 9 through 11 of the article. That sounds like science fiction, but new psychological research hints that being bored—even in class—may one day be a thing of the past. Which quotation provides the best evidence to support this claim? A “... less than one in 20 adults were bored at work.” (lines 15 and 16) B “... our brains adapt really quickly to certain levels of stimulation . . .” (lines 23 and 24) C “... anything the attention system in your brain locks onto will be automatically sucked up into your conscious awareness . . .” (lines 43 and 44) D “Each of us has the ability to make every situation interesting; all we need to do is change the way we look at it...” (lines 61 and 62)

explanation.text: The article says boredom may exist inside the brain and that people can make a situation interesting by changing their outlook. This shows that boredom may end because a person can deliberately redirect attention instead of waiting for the situation to change.

questions.alt: Question 34. ©The final sentence in lines 68 and 69 best contributes to the article by A suggesting that the author often fell asleep in classes B connecting the information to the incident described at the beginning C encouraging students to stay awake and avoid boredom in class D emphasizing that teenagers are often more bored than other groups

explanation.text: The article opens with the author falling asleep during a boring class. The final wish that he had known the research earlier connects back to that incident and frames the article as the lesson he lacked at the beginning.

questions.alt: Question 35. Which sentence states a central idea that is supported throughout the article? A Modern entertainment decreases boredom. B_ Boredom is present in teenagers more than in adults. C Everyone experiences boredom occasionally. D People have the ability to control their boredom.

explanation.text: The author explains that boredom depends on where people direct their attention and how they view a situation. Therefore, people can exercise some control over boredom by changing their focus and finding an interesting target.

questions.alt: Question 36. How was CATIA essential to the success of Gehry’s plan for the museum? A It helped him find less expensive materials. B It allowed him to work earlier ideas into his design. C It suggested unique materials to use for construction. D It allowed him to add less-common shapes into his design.

explanation.text: CATIA translated Gehry's unusual three-dimensional models into precise working drawings and construction dimensions. This capability made the museum's sweeping curves possible because builders could produce the uncommon shapes accurately and at a manageable cost.

questions.alt: Question 37. What do lines 25 through 30 contribute to the development of ideas in the article? A They explain a way of thinking that allowed an obstacle to be overcome. B They show how preferences in the shapes of buildings have changed. C They describe a step in the building process. D They illustrate how different artists work.

explanation.text: Gehry explains that technology and cost usually limit what architects can build, then says the goal is to make technology work for the design. This way of thinking helped overcome the obstacle because CATIA turned his radical shapes into practical plans.

questions.alt: Question 38. 3g The phrase “waved a magic wand” (line 40) suggests that the A museum fulfilled the city’s wishes B project’s success amazed many people C project succeeded in part because of luck D museum resembled a creation from a fairy tale

explanation.text: Titanium initially cost too much for the museum, but a Russian surplus unexpectedly caused its price to fall. The change seems like a "magic wand" because this lucky event suddenly made Gehry's preferred material affordable.

questions.alt: Question 39. According to the article, what quality is the author referring to by comparing the Bilbao museum to the Eiffel Tower and the Pyramids (lines 67 through 69)? A the impact of its height B the ability to generate revenue C the effort needed to construct it D the widespread amazement felt about it

explanation.text: The Eiffel Tower and the Pyramids are famous structures that inspire wonder far beyond their home cities. Comparing the museum with them indicates that Gehry's building produced the same kind of widespread amazement.

questions.alt: Question 40. 49 Which phrase has the strongest connection to the photo? A “... someone must have waved a magic wand.” (line 40) B “... blossoming like silver petals . . .” (line 48) C “... connecting the urban fabric of a fairly dense city . . .” (lines 52 and 53) D ‘Tm trying to get more liquid . . .” (line 83)

explanation.text: The phrase "blossoming like silver petals" creates a clear visual image of curved, overlapping titanium forms. Because it describes both the museum's shape and its metallic appearance, it best supports the photograph.

questions.alt: Question 41. Which sentence best supports the central idea of the article? A “Three architects from Austria, Japan, and the United States were invited to submit proposals.” (lines 12 and 13) B “Gehry already had used CATIA for parts of several other buildings, including Fred and Ginger in Prague.” (lines 22 and 23) C “Instead more than a million came to marvel at the titanium-clad miracle.” (lines 64 and 65) D “However, he admits feeling pleased when people on the streets of Bilbao come up and give him a hug.” (lines 79 and 80)

explanation.text: The report that a million people came to marvel at the titanium museum proves that the building became an international attraction. This evidence directly supports the central idea that Gehry's remarkable design transformed Bilbao and helped rescue the city.

questions.alt: Question 42. Which information would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A “Shipyards sat idle; steel mills, silent.” (lines 3 and 4) B “Their master plan included a first-class museum, so original that it would attract worldwide attention and rescue their town from its slow decline.” (lines 6 through 8) C “You find his fish motif appearing here and there throughout the museum.” (lines 55 and 56) D “At seventy Gehry finds his creativity is speeding up.” (line 81)

explanation.text: The city planned an original museum to draw worldwide attention and reverse Bilbao's decline. This information belongs in a summary because it states the central problem and goal that explain the design process and the museum's eventual success.
