questions.alt: Question 1. Read paragraphs 3 and 4 from the story. “Wear it around your neck,” he whispered, “and you’ll understand everything that animals say. But if you reveal your secret, the charm won’t work anymore.” “I won’t tell anyone,” Niel assured him. Which word from paragraph 3 or 4 best helps the reader understand the meaning of the word “reveal”? A whispered B understand C tell D assured

explanation.text: The snake warns that the charm will stop working if Niel reveals his secret. Niel answers, “I won’t tell anyone,” which shows that reveal means to tell or make something known.

questions.alt: Question 2. Why does Niel clasp his charm in paragraph 8? A He was afraid he would lose the charm. B He could understand what the mouse was saying. C He wanted to make sure the charm was still working. D He could understand how the snake felt about secrets.

explanation.text: Niel clasps the charm just after hearing the mice and realizing that he understands their squeaking. This shows that the charm has given him the promised ability to understand animals.

questions.alt: Question 3. How does Niel add to the problem in this story? A He invites his aunt to come visit. B He laughs at what the animals say. C He gives the snake tooth to his wife. D He helps eat all the food in the house.

explanation.text: Niel repeatedly laughs at the animals’ comments but refuses to explain why. Because his behavior makes his wife increasingly suspicious and angry, it creates the story’s main conflict between them.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which paragraph best explains the powers the charm has? A paragraph 3 B paragraph 5 C paragraph 28 D paragraph 30

explanation.text: Paragraph 3 states that wearing the charm lets Niel understand everything animals say. Because it also explains that revealing the secret ends that power, this paragraph gives both the ability and its limit.

questions.alt: Question 5. Read paragraph 31 from the story. The snake rattled his tail and laughed. “I told you that secrets are hard to keep, my friend. We’ll see how long you last this time.” Why does the snake say this to Niel? A The snake thinks the secret is funny. B The snake thinks Niel will tell someone the secret. C The snake thinks the wife will figure out the secret. D The snake thinks his friendship with Niel is more valuable than secrets.

explanation.text: Niel has already promised once to keep the secret and then told his wife. The snake’s challenge, “We’ll see how long you last,” therefore shows that he expects Niel to reveal the new secret too.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which statement best describes a lesson learned from this story? A Secrets can often be entertaining. B Secrets are not very useful. C Rewards should not be taken for granted. D Rewards can be more trouble than they are worth.

explanation.text: The charm seems like a wonderful reward, but Niel’s unexplained laughter nearly drives his wife away, and telling her makes the charm fail. Therefore, the reward creates more trouble than enjoyment.

questions.alt: Question 7. Based on paragraphs 2 and 7, a “paleontologist” is a person who mainly A digs deep in the ground B studies ancient animal bones C leads a big team D finds complete animal skeletons

explanation.text: Dave explains that paleontologists and fossil hunters may search for weeks and rarely find a whole skeleton. Because the dig focuses on locating and carefully uncovering dinosaur fossils, a paleontologist studies ancient animal bones.

questions.alt: Question 8. Read this sentence from paragraph 7. He added, “You know, Ben, if you’re tired of searching, you can always help someone else on the crew.” What does this show about Dave? A He is concerned that Ben may be feeling unhappy. B He hopes that Ben will work harder than he has been. C He needs Ben to work with some of the others in the group. D He is worried that Ben will become careless with the tools.

explanation.text: Dave notices that Benny may be tired of the slow search and offers him a different job with the crew. This shows that Dave is concerned Benny may be unhappy or discouraged.

questions.alt: Question 9. Paragraphs 9 through 11 show that Benny A knows about changes that took place over time B prefers working in cooler weather C enjoys thinking out loud D continues to work hard

explanation.text: Benny decides, “I’ll keep looking,” even though another job might be more fun. He then carefully picks, chips, brushes, and sweeps in the growing heat, which shows that he continues to work hard.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which paragraph shows a change in Benny’s feelings? A paragraph 4 B paragraph 8 C paragraph 9 D paragraph 12

explanation.text: Before paragraph 12, Benny is bored, hot, and becoming drowsy. He then discovers the tooth and becomes surprised and excited. This shows why paragraph 12 marks the change in his feelings.

questions.alt: Question 11. How is the dig different from what Benny thought it would be? A Benny uses brushes rather than shovels. B Benny did not expect the land to be so hilly. C Benny did not expect to find so many workers. D Benny has to help the assistants.

explanation.text: Benny expected a dig like the pictures, but Dave forbids shovels because they could break fossils. Instead, the crew uses ice picks and paintbrushes to remove dirt slowly and carefully.

questions.alt: Question 12. Based on the article, what do all the young animals have in common? A They play in groups with other animals. B They need to prepare for when they are older. C They are able to locate items that interest them. D They let other animals know that they want to play.

explanation.text: Each young animal practices through play for skills it will need as an adult, such as escaping predators, hunting, communicating, or keeping its footing. This shows that they are preparing for later life.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which definition matches the meaning of “frolic” as it is used in paragraph 3? A to run around B to find protection C to learn by watching D to walk next to its mother

explanation.text: The fawn “leap[s] and frolic[s]” in a twisting, twirling dance while strengthening its legs. This shows that frolic means to run or move around playfully.

questions.alt: Question 14. The word “stalks” in paragraph 4 shows that the lion cub A follows silently B moves quickly C looks around D bends down

explanation.text: The cub slinks on crouched legs before it pounces on another cub. This shows that “stalks” means it follows or approaches silently so it can surprise its target.

questions.alt: Question 15. Paragraphs 3 through 5 support a main idea because they show A how animals are different B how animals escape trouble C what animals need to learn D that animals help each other

explanation.text: The fawn practices escaping predators, while the lion cubs practice stalking, pouncing, and hunting. These examples support the main idea because they show what young animals must learn to survive as adults.

questions.alt: Question 16. Before wolf pups can hunt together, they must first A discover new games B learn to use signals C practice with toys D know how to race

explanation.text: Wolves must communicate before they can hunt and protect one another as a pack. The pups practice gestures such as bowing, wagging, and holding their mouths open, which shows that they first learn to use signals.

questions.alt: Question 17. Read this sentence from paragraph 9. If one youngster plays too rough, the others will let him know they are unhappy with his behavior. What does this sentence show about the animals? A The older animals introduce the games to the younger ones. B The larger animals are prevented from wrestling with the smaller ones. C The young animals enforce limits so that their games are safe for all. D The adult animals are nearby to make sure that no one gets hurt.

explanation.text: When one youngster becomes too rough, the other young animals communicate that they are unhappy. This shows that the animals set limits on one another’s behavior so play remains safe for everyone.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which sentence best states a main idea of the entire article? A “For animals in the wild, play is important to their very survival.” (paragraph 2) B “Much like human children, young dolphins love to play with toys.” (paragraph 8) C “When they play, the young animals learn to keep their footing no matter what might happen.” (paragraph 10) D “Playing makes them strong and confident.” (paragraph 12)

explanation.text: The article’s many examples show play building the strength, hunting, communication, and safety skills wild animals need. Therefore, the statement that play is important to their survival best covers the entire article.

questions.alt: Question 1. The details about the setting are important to the story because they A explain why the Overtons arrive so quickly B explain why the bat is able to enter the house C show why the family is looking for excitement D show why Betsy sees the bat before anyone else

explanation.text: The evening is so hot that no one notices the back door has been left ajar, and its stubborn catch was not pulled shut. Because the door remains open, the bat is able to enter the house.

questions.alt: Question 2. The phrase “couldn’t get a word in edgewise” in paragraph 10 shows that A Andrew was taking a long time to finish talking B Andrew’s family doubted what he was saying C Andrew’s family did not know he had joined them D Andrew was not given the opportunity to talk

explanation.text: Everyone is talking, worrying, crying, or whimpering at once while Andrew wants to reassure them. He “couldn’t get a word in edgewise” because the commotion gives him no opportunity to speak.

questions.alt: Question 3. How do paragraphs 14 through 16 show a change in the story? A The characters become calmer. B The action centers around the bat. C The action moves inside the house. D The characters admit they are panicking.

explanation.text: Andrew asks the adults to wait and hear his idea, and they loosen their grips on the weapons. Mr. Overton even expresses relief, which shows that the characters have become calmer.

questions.alt: Question 4. In paragraph 17, Andrew turns the lights off in the house because he A knows the bat is used to being in the dark B is worried that the bat may be dangerous C does not want the men to attack the bat D does not want his family to see the bat

explanation.text: Because Andrew knows that bats are nocturnal, darkness inside will feel familiar rather than frightening. He leaves a bright light outside to attract insects and guide the bat toward the open door.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which phrase from paragraph 17 helps readers understand the meaning of “illuminated” in paragraph 19? A “hear the flapping” B “unfamiliar surroundings” C “turned on the floodlight” D “in the backyard”

explanation.text: Andrew “turned on the floodlight” beside the open back door. Because a floodlight makes an area bright, that phrase explains that the later “illuminated doorway” is a lighted doorway.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which sentence would be best to include in a summary of the story? A Mr. Overton and Mr. Halvorsen have a plan, but Andrew objects. B Because of Andrew, the bat is able to fly off into the night. C Mr. Overton praises Andrew for his smart thinking. D Andrew asks everyone to wait quietly.

explanation.text: Andrew prevents the adults from killing the bat and creates conditions that lead it safely through the open door. Therefore, the bat’s escape because of Andrew captures the story’s main problem and resolution.

questions.alt: Question 7. According to the authors, why should readers who want to mountain bike ask questions? A to figure out if they can do mountain biking tricks B to figure out if they are ready for mountain biking C to learn where they are allowed to ride their bikes D to make sure they choose the right type of bike

explanation.text: The article says off-road riding requires a bike that can handle rough treatment, then advises asking experienced friends and bike stores what works best. These questions help because riders need to choose a suitable bike.

questions.alt: Question 8. How does the information in paragraphs 4 and 5 support a main idea of the article? A by explaining how to prepare for mountain biking B by giving details about the difficulty of mountain biking C by showing how mountain biking can be painful D by describing what muscles are used in mountain biking

explanation.text: Paragraphs 4 and 5 explain how to select a sturdy bike, strengthen the needed muscles, warm up, cool down, and stretch. These details support the main idea because they describe preparation for mountain biking.

questions.alt: Question 9. Read this sentence from paragraph 6. Broadslides, bunny-hops, and jumps will also be a part of your arsenal as you attack a biking trail. What does “arsenal” refer to in this sentence? A a type of event B a type of brake C a collection of skills D a place to mountain bike

explanation.text: The sentence lists broadslides, bunny-hops, and jumps as techniques used to overcome trail obstacles. Calling these techniques an “arsenal” therefore means a collection of useful biking skills.

questions.alt: Question 10. According to the “Competition” section of the article, why do some mountain biking events need to be watched closely? A to keep track of riders’ times B to keep track of riders’ mistakes C to make sure riders wear helmets D to make sure riders are staying safe

explanation.text: During Trials, judges observe riders and penalize them for putting a foot down, stopping, or falling. The event must be watched closely because officials need to notice and record those mistakes.

questions.alt: Question 11. According to the authors, how will training, learning about bike equipment, and wearing helmets and pads help riders? A Riders will be prepared to safely enjoy mountain biking. B Riders will win mountain biking competitions. C Riders will not get stiff and sore after biking. D Riders will not get lost while biking.

explanation.text: Training prepares a rider’s body, equipment knowledge helps the rider choose and repair a bike, and protective gear reduces danger. Together these steps support safely enjoying the sport because they address both skill and safety.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which sentence from the text best summarizes a main idea of the article? A “Check with friends who already take part in the sport.” (paragraph 4) B “Ask questions at bike stores, but remember, they want to sell you a bike, so think carefully about what they tell you.” (paragraph 5) C “Maneuvering your bike on off-road trails calls for skills not usually used around your neighborhood.” (paragraph 6) D “The winner is the first to make it through a tough course filled with obstacles like sharp turns, logs, rocks, streams, and jumps.” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: The article repeatedly explains the special equipment, techniques, repairs, competitions, and safety measures needed off-road. Therefore, the statement that trail riding requires skills beyond neighborhood biking best summarizes its main idea.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which information most c ontributes to the organization of paragraph 1? A the comparison to the toys of today B the explanation of the problem of transportation C the mention of the cause for only a few trips to town D the use of words and numbers that indicate periods of time

explanation.text: Paragraph 1 begins by asking readers to imagine life without modern entertainment, then contrasts today’s battery-powered toys with pioneer toys. This shows why comparison to the present organizes and introduces the historical topic.

questions.alt: Question 14. Which sentence gives the best evidence that readers can relate the article to their own lives? A “Town stores featured candy, jump ropes, marbles, books that were designed to teach children good behavior, china and paper dolls.” (paragraph 2) B “Without store-bought toys, pioneer children made their own fun out of what they had.” (paragraph 3) C “Some of the games they played have been memorized and handed down from generation to generation and are still played today, like hopscotch, jump rope, hide and seek, and ‘Mother, May I?’ ” (paragraph 4) D “Step back in time and try your hand at making these toys from over 100 years ago.” (paragraph 5)

explanation.text: The article names hopscotch, jump rope, hide-and-seek, and “Mother, May I?” as pioneer games still played today. This shows that readers can connect familiar games from their own lives to pioneer children.

questions.alt: Question 15. Which sentence best supports a main idea of the article? A “For pioneer children growing up in the 1800s, the nearest town could be days away by horseback or covered wagon.” (paragraph 1) B “Most pioneer families had little money for fun and games.” (paragraph 2) C “Without store-bought toys, pioneer children made their own fun out of what they had.” (paragraph 3) D “Today, pioneer crafts and games are just as much fun.” (paragraph 5)

explanation.text: The article centers on how pioneer children created entertainment despite having little money and few supplies. The sentence about making their own fun from available materials supports this idea because the examples develop it throughout.

questions.alt: Question 16. In the “Whirligig” instructions, which step relates to positioning an object? A step 1 B step 2 C step 3 D step 4

explanation.text: Step 2 tells the maker to hold each end of the string so the button sits in the middle. This instruction concerns positioning because it specifies exactly where the button should be before it is swung.

questions.alt: Question 17. Step 1 for making a tin can lantern is helpful because A nails will not puncture the hard surface created by the ice inside B it allows the lantern maker to avoid smashing in the side of the can C a hard surface will allow the lantern maker to draw detailed patterns D it allows the lantern maker to avoid scratching the surface of the can

explanation.text: Freezing water inside the can creates a hard support behind its thin metal wall. This support is helpful because it keeps the side of the can from being crushed inward while the holes are hammered.

questions.alt: Question 18. Which definition of “secure” best matches its use in step 5 of “Tin Can Lantern”? A to remove from danger B to get for oneself C to protect an area D to attach firmly

explanation.text: Step 5 directs the maker to wrap the wire ends around the holes several times to “secure the handle.” That action shows that secure means to attach the handle firmly to the can.

questions.alt: Question 19. Which object was most likely useful for the entire family? A dolls B whirligigs C tin can lanterns D clay marbles and beads

explanation.text: The tin can lantern provided light inside dark cabins and barns while protecting the candle flame. Because lighting was useful for household work and safety, the lantern could benefit the entire family rather than only entertain children.

Official passage for Questions 8–13. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

Official passage for Questions 14–20. The complete released passage is shown with its original title, byline, illustrations, line or paragraph numbers, and source credits; PDF page breaks are removed.

questions.alt: Question 1. 00 What does the word “terrain” mean as it is used in paragraph 5? A bodies of water B plant life C surface features of the land D area with small amounts of rain

explanation.text: Paragraph 5 describes the island’s terrain as being covered in snow, referring to the ground and landscape. This context shows that terrain means the surface features of the land.

questions.alt: Question 2. 00 Which detail best reflects the main goal of the Haughton Mars Project? A “Now I have some idea of what it’s like to be on the Red Planet.” (paragraph 1) B “That means people can work there only during the summer months . . .” (paragraph 5) C “Others test how well their robot rovers collect rock and soil samples.” (paragraph 6) D “Recent robot missions there found possible signs of frozen water.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The project uses Devon Island’s Mars-like conditions so people can experience and study an environment similar to the Red Planet. Therefore, gaining an idea of what Mars is like best reflects the project’s central goal.

questions.alt: Question 3. 00 Which sentence best supports the main idea of paragraphs 3 through 5? A “It is about 900 miles from the North Pole.” (paragraph 1) B “It lets them figure out what they would need to make human explorations of Mars successful and safe.” (paragraph 8) C “Scientists have been studying Mars for decades.” (paragraph 10) D “NASA officials have said that they hope to put astronauts on the Red Planet by 2030.” (paragraph 11)

explanation.text: Paragraphs 3 through 5 explain which Devon Island conditions resemble Mars and which do not. The later statement about making Mars exploration successful and safe supports this idea because it explains why scientists study those conditions.

questions.alt: Question 4. Which evidence best supports the point the author makes in paragraph 11? A Scientists are practicing tasks they might perform on Mars. B Scientists work on the island during the summer months. C Scientists have gathered on the island since 1997. D Scientists are limiting phone and Internet use.

explanation.text: Paragraph 11 says the Haughton scientists are working to ensure humans are ready for a Mars mission. Their practice with spacesuits, rovers, samples, and a greenhouse supports that point because those are tasks astronauts may perform.

questions.alt: Question 5. The main reason no one lives on Devon Island is because the island A is difficult to reach B has no Internet service C is cold most of the year D has continuous sunlight all summer

explanation.text: Devon Island is a frigid desert covered in snow for most of the year, and even its summer average is only about 34°F. These details show that year-round cold makes the island unsuitable for permanent residents.

questions.alt: Question 6. How have recent robot missions to Mars affected future exploration by humans? A The missions determined when humans can safely travel to Mars. B The missions suggested that humans will be able to survive on Mars. C The missions discovered proof of life on Mars that only humans can confirm. D The missions raised new questions about life on Mars that humans may answer.

explanation.text: Robot missions found possible frozen water, suggesting that tiny life may have existed on Mars or may remain there. This shows why the missions raised questions that future human exploration could help answer.

questions.alt: Question 7. Why does the author quote Dr. Pascal Lee throughout the article? A Dr. Lee is the scientist on the island who has the most information about Mars. B Dr. Lee was hired by NASA to be the next scientist to explore Mars. C Dr. Lee started the Haughton Mars Project and has remained involved since its beginning. D Dr. Lee lives at the research site on Devon Island and conducts experiments throughout the year.

explanation.text: Pascal Lee founded the Haughton Mars Project in 1997 and has returned every summer since. Because he directs and has long participated in the work, his quotations provide knowledgeable explanations throughout the article.

questions.alt: Question 8. How does paragraph 14 relate to paragraph 4? A Paragraph 14 explains the effect of the event in paragraph 4. B Paragraph 14 gives a solution to the problem introduced in paragraph 4. C Paragraph 14 provides further details that summarize the event in paragraph 4. D Paragraph 14 provides further details that contrast the information in paragraph 4.

explanation.text: Paragraph 4 introduces the letter offering Pappa work in Iowa, the event that starts the move. Paragraph 14 explains its effect because Seema’s four-person household feels broken away from the larger family.

questions.alt: Question 9. Which sentence from the story best shows how Seema feels about going to America without Raju? A “I stood near the acacia tree growing at the edge of an abandoned lot and watched Raju’s back as the dust rising from his shoes covered my white blouse and my beige pinafore.” (paragraph 2) B “Raju was my cousin, and I wanted to tell him that everything would be fine—but how could I?” (paragraph 3) C “Why had Kaka and Kaki told Uma and Raju, and why had Pappa told me?” (paragraph 21) D “Raju and I went to school together and were in the same class.” (paragraph 22)

explanation.text: Seema wants to reassure Raju but immediately wonders how she possibly can. This shows her uncertainty and sadness about leaving him, rather than merely stating facts about their school routine.

questions.alt: Question 10. What does paragraph 23 of the story suggest about Seema? A She does not want to spoil her father’s mood. B She does not have to convince her mother to stay. C She is upset that her father’s laboratory job keeps him busy. D She is scared to be away from her mother.

explanation.text: Seema is frightened about the move, but she does not tell Pappa because he is so happy about going to America. This shows that she holds back her fears to avoid spoiling his excited mood.

questions.alt: Question 11. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A “School was over, and I would never wear this uniform again.” (paragraph 2) B “I never could understand how Pappa could forget his lunch while working with tiny bugs that he could only see under a microscope.” (paragraph 5) C “When I went to bed, I wondered how I could leave the rest of my family and go to America.” (paragraph 22) D “The next morning I was tired and groggy.” (paragraph 24)

explanation.text: The story’s central conflict is Seema’s fear and sadness about moving to America away from her close extended family. Her wondering how she can leave them therefore belongs in a summary of the main events.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which detail from the story best demonstrates the relationship among all the members of Seema’s family? A Seema goes to the same school as her cousin. B Seema spends most of her time with her cousin. C The members of Seema’s family visit each other. D The members of Seema’s family live in the same house.

explanation.text: Seema explains that the grandparents, parents, aunt, uncle, and cousins all live in one house and eat from one kitchen. This shared home best demonstrates how closely all members of the extended family live together.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which sentence best expresses a theme of the story? A Families often work together to accept change. B Change provides new opportunities. C Change can be difficult to accept. D It is impossible to avoid change.

explanation.text: Raju says he hates the year of the move, while Seema worries about leaving, cannot discuss her fears, and sleeps poorly. These reactions show that major change can be difficult to accept.

questions.alt: Question 14. Why do scientists want to create robots that imitate human facial expressions? A to encourage people to buy robots to do their tasks B to encourage people to pay more attention to robotic work C to help create a better connection between robots and people D to help establish a reason for robots and people to work together

explanation.text: People use facial expressions to communicate, and the researcher says such expressions develop trust and understanding. Therefore, giving robots humanlike expressions can create a better connection with the people beside them.

questions.alt: Question 15. Which evidence best supports the idea that robots could be in every household by the year 2025? A Robots can perform daily tasks. B Robots can work in many places. C Robots can show human emotions. D Robots can replace human workers.

explanation.text: The article describes robots vacuuming, mowing lawns, cleaning toys, checking email, and performing other routine chores. Because robots can handle useful daily tasks, households have practical reasons to adopt them widely.

questions.alt: Question 16. Which evidence best supports the idea that Nao will be a popular invention? A Nao is able to talk and walk. B Nao has a number of different uses. C Nao is the first robot to pick up toys. D Nao has been praised by robotic experts.

explanation.text: Nao can clean toys, play games, talk, walk, recognize people and places, and assist with tasks such as checking email. This variety of uses supports its likely popularity because it can meet many household needs.

questions.alt: Question 17. Based on the article, which statement best explains what Nexi, Nao, and RIBA have in common? A They interact and communicate with humans. B They have many possible military applications. C They can perform medical services that pose difficulties to people. D They are designed to imitate human emotions and facial expressions.

explanation.text: Nexi communicates with speech and expressions, Nao talks and remembers faces and voices, and RIBA recognizes people and responds to commands. These shared abilities show that all three interact and communicate with humans.

questions.alt: Question 18. What does the expression “making a splash” (paragraph 10) mean? A getting soaked B swimming along C creating excitement D cleaning up

explanation.text: The phrase introduces innovative water robots that resemble fish, clams, rays, lobsters, and penguins and perform unusual jobs. Because these inventions are noteworthy, “making a splash” means creating excitement or attracting attention.

questions.alt: Question 19. Which detail best shows that robots can perform tasks that are difficult for humans? A “They’re in more places than you might know.” (paragraph 1) B “Some homes have robots that vacuum floors or mow the lawn.” (paragraph 1) C “You might be living or working with one of them one day!” (paragraph 2) D “It can also locate submerged ships or oil and gas pipelines.” (paragraph 10)

explanation.text: The robotic fish can search beneath the water for submerged ships and oil or gas pipelines. This shows a robot performing deep underwater work that would be difficult or dangerous for a human to carry out directly.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A A robotic penguin was designed by engineers in Germany. B Because Nexi can raise its eyebrows, it can express feelings and emotions. C Nao is a two-foot-tall robot that can be programmed to perform several tasks. D Because some new robots act like humans in many ways, they will work well with people.

explanation.text: Across its examples, the article emphasizes robots that communicate, recognize people, respond to commands, and assist with useful work. Therefore, the broad idea that humanlike robots will work well with people is most important for a summary.

questions.alt: Question 1. How do lines 1 through 3 most contribute to the article? A by demonstrating that the school day starts too early for many students B by describing a common and familiar experience for readers to relate to C by showing the technology around sleep issues for teenagers and adults D by asking readers to compare their morning routine to the one described in the article

explanation.text: The alarm, a parent's call, the bus ride, and the school start recreate a morning many students know. This familiar routine gives readers an experience they can connect to before the article explains teen sleep loss.

questions.alt: Question 2. The authors use the phrase “impaired concentration” in line 35 to mean that drivers are A irritated B injured C unconcerned D unfocused

explanation.text: The sentence says that even an awake but tired driver has impaired concentration and coordination, making crashes more likely. In this context, impaired concentration means an inability to focus well.

questions.alt: Question 3. The information presented by Jodi Mindell in lines 16 through 21 and lines 37 through 45 best supports the claim that A a lack of sleep affects the body and the mind B the body does most of its growing during sleep C a lack of sleep can lead to more arguments between people D researchers hope to understand the sleep habits of other species

explanation.text: Mindell calls sleep a basic biological need and explains that too little sleep weakens immune function and disrupts hormones. She also says tired people cannot regulate their emotions, showing effects on both body and mind.

questions.alt: Question 4. How does the section “Taking a Toll” relate to the section “So Why Don’t Teens Sleep More”? A Both sections show how a situation has changed over time. B Both sections compare problems at home and school. C The first section describes effects, while the second section describes causes. D The first section describes a problem, while the second section describes solutions.

explanation.text: “Taking a Toll” describes what sleep deprivation does, including poor grades, crashes, illness, and unstable moods. “So Why Don't Teens Sleep More?” then explains causes such as electronics, caffeine, and teens' natural sleep patterns.

questions.alt: Question 5. Which lines best support the authors’ idea that people do not take sleep deprivation seriously? A “On average, American teens get two hours less than the average 9.2 hours of sleep they need each night to function at their best.” (lines 8 and 9) B “You never brag about how much sleep you got. You only brag if you didn’t get very much.” (lines 14 and 15) C “So we kind of look at it [the need for sleep] in a backwards way.” (line 21) D “Many school districts are starting to pay attention to sleep researchers and are shifting their start times to later morning for middle school and high school students.” (lines 65 and 66)

explanation.text: The speaker observes that people brag about getting very little sleep but never brag about sleeping enough. Treating sleep loss as an achievement shows that people do not recognize its seriousness.

questions.alt: Question 6. Which evidence best supports the idea that teenagers could be happier and more successful in school if they got more sleep? A “Did you know that if you need an alarm clock to wake up in the morning, you’re probably sleep-deprived? Most teens don’t get enough sleep, according to Cornell University psychologist Dr. James B. Maas. ‘Almost all teenagers, as they approach puberty, become walking zombies because they are getting far too little sleep,’ Maas says.” (lines 4 through 7) B “Wolfson’s research into high school students and sleep revealed that kids who received C, D, and F grades in school usually slept 25 minutes less and went to bed 40 minutes later than kids who received A’s and B’s. Lack of sleep also makes people more likely to feel down or depressed.” (lines 28 through 31) C “They naturally fall asleep later at night than younger children, while needing to wake up earlier in the morning for school. When you go to bed late and wake up early, there just isn’t enough time for sleep.” (lines 62 through 64) D “Those extra two hours of sleep on Saturday and Sunday mornings can really help.” (lines 71 and 72)

explanation.text: Wolfson's research links less sleep with C, D, and F grades rather than A's and B's. The same evidence says sleep loss makes people more likely to feel depressed, directly connecting more sleep with school success and happiness.

questions.alt: Question 7. Which evidence from the article supports the “Tips for a Good Night’s Sleep” section? A Amy Wolfson’s research into high school students in lines 28 through 31 B Mark Mahowald’s comments about safety in lines 33 and 34 C the National Sleep Foundation’s survey for teenagers in lines 51 through 59 D the authors’ remarks about sleep on the weekends in lines 71 through 78

explanation.text: The survey connects heavy electronics use and daily caffeine consumption with insufficient sleep. Those findings support the tips to limit electronics before bed and avoid caffeine after lunchtime.

questions.alt: Question 8. The author demonstrates that Jim Simmons is qualified for Project Sea Hunt by A describing his previous work with animals B giving examples of his training techniques C explaining his choice of birds for the program D establishing his relationship with the government

explanation.text: Before Project Sea Hunt, Jim had trained dolphins, whales, and gooney birds and had studied how animals behave and learn. That previous animal work demonstrates that he had relevant experience for leading the pigeon program.

questions.alt: Question 9. The author uses the phrase “nose to beak” in line 24 to show that pigeons are A replacing people on rescue missions B using their senses to find people C working alongside people D being compared to people

explanation.text: The phrase appears while the pigeons' detection performance is measured against a human's performance. “Nose to beak” playfully parallels “head to head,” showing that the birds are being compared directly with people.

questions.alt: Question 10. Which evidence best shows that teaching animals is challenging? A Project Sea Hunt was halted after six years. B Most pigeons were not able to complete the training. C The trained birds did not find any missing people. D Jim Simmons had to switch from mammals to birds.

explanation.text: The program began with a large number of pigeons, yet only about twelve to fourteen completed ten months of training. That low completion rate shows how difficult it was to teach the required behavior reliably.

questions.alt: Question 11. Jim Simmons used the pigeons in several official search and rescue missions because he A needed to determine the limits of their endurance B wanted to test their ability to work with humans C wanted to assess what they had learned in their training D planned to save many people with their skills

explanation.text: After training the pigeons to recognize rescue colors and press an alert button, Jim put them on official missions “to put their skills to the test.” The missions therefore assessed whether they could apply what they had learned.

questions.alt: Question 12. Which detail best suggests that Project Sea Hunt was a partial success? A The pigeons were selected for their good health and quick learning ability. B The project made scientists aware of the need for search and rescue missions. C The project helped allow helicopter pilots to concentrate on flying while searching. D The pigeons found objects that matched the colors they had been trained to identify.

explanation.text: The pigeons never located a missing person, but they did spot small objects in the exact colors they had learned to identify. That result shows the training worked in part even though the larger rescue goal was not achieved.

questions.alt: Question 13. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? A “He once trained bottlenose dolphins, pilot whales, and gooney birds.” (lines 7 and 8) B “These objects are often used by people to get help when they are stranded in the ocean.” (lines 14 and 15) C “. . . the pigeons could see objects from as far away as 2000 feet.” (lines 25 and 26) D “. . . the pilot was notified in the cockpit by a flashing light and could begin a closer search.” (lines 32 and 33)

explanation.text: The pigeons' ability to see objects from nearly seven football fields away explains why they were valuable searchers. That central ability is more important to a summary than details about Jim's earlier animals or the cockpit signal.

questions.alt: Question 14. The author conveys the purpose of the article by A describing pigeons’ searching abilities B indicating how few pigeons graduate training C telling how long the training takes D showing the success of the missions

explanation.text: The article's central focus is the surprising ability of trained pigeons to scan the ocean quickly, for long periods, and at great distance. Describing those searching abilities explains why pigeons could serve as rescue workers.

questions.alt: Question 15. How does the arrival of electricity propel the main events of the plot? A The arrival of electricity brings electrical poles to Grimes Lake Road. B The arrival of electricity makes it cheaper for Ruby to clothe her growing family. C The arrival of electricity forces Ruby to update the technology she uses for sewing. D The arrival of electricity causes Ruby to consider buying an electric sewing machine.

explanation.text: Because electricity has reached the farm, Ruby immediately asks Howard about getting an electric sewing machine. That decision launches the machine's decades of sewing and the later search for a replacement.

questions.alt: Question 16. How do lines 11 through 18 help develop the plot of the passage? A They illustrate Ruby’s passion for her hobby. B They emphasize Ruby’s generosity toward children. C They prove the economic value of an electric sewing machine. D They demonstrate the benefits of an electric sewing machine.

explanation.text: These lines compress years of Ruby's sewing into a long list of clothes, quilts, and special dresses for family and neighbors. The sheer amount and variety of her work demonstrate her lasting passion for sewing.

questions.alt: Question 17. The author most likely includes the event described in lines 23 through 34 to show that A metal sewing machines are still in use but no longer practical B the value of an object is not always related to its price C any object can be purchased for the right price D metal sewing machines can still be located

explanation.text: The working 1948 Singer is only a display and costs the narrator just twenty-five dollars, yet it restores the activity that gives Ruby purpose. This contrast shows that an object's importance can far exceed its price.

questions.alt: Question 18. Read lines 31 through 33. I told him about Ruby—how she lives by herself and sews to keep busy, how she charges only six dollars to make a dress because the people she sews for don’t have a lot of money, how a lot of times she doesn’t charge a dime, how sewing is her ministry. The word “ministry” is connected to A Ruby’s purpose in life B what people expect of Ruby C why Ruby chooses to treat her customers fairly D Ruby’s desire to share the profits from her sewing

explanation.text: Ruby sews to stay busy and serves people who have little money, often charging almost nothing. Calling sewing her “ministry” therefore means it is a central purpose through which she helps others.

questions.alt: Question 19. Which statement best shows how the author reveals the narrator’s opinion of Ruby? A by telling about the purchase of the first sewing machine in lines 8 through 10 B by including the explanation given to the man in lines 31 through 33 C by quoting Ruby’s words when she sees the replacement machine in line 39 D by describing Ruby’s reaction to the sewing machine in line 41

explanation.text: The narrator tells the shopkeeper that Ruby sews for poor neighbors, charges very little, and often works for free because sewing is her ministry. Choosing these generous details reveals his deep admiration for her.

questions.alt: Question 20. Which detail shows the main reward Ruby receives for her efforts? A “Her family would fall asleep under Ruby-made quilts, lulled to sleep by the Singer hum.” (lines 15 and 16) B “The Singer stitched maternity clothes, baby dresses, baptismal gowns and quilts for the cribs.” (lines 17 and 18) C “When Ruby heard the hum, she clapped her hands.” (line 41) D “Rachael called to say ‘Thank you’ and ‘I love you’ and ‘When can I see you again?’ ” (lines 48 and 49)

explanation.text: After Ruby makes matching dresses, Rachael responds with thanks, love, and a wish to see her again. Because that affection directly answers Ruby's care, it is the meaningful reward for her sewing.

questions.alt: Question 21. Read this sentence from lines 54 and 55. I’m grateful, too, for a woman who sews way into the night, who dispenses love one stitch at a time. The phrase “dispenses love one stitch at a time” mainly suggests that Ruby A is obsessed with her interest in sewing B approaches her sewing in a tender and patient way C shows her kindness for others through her sewing D divides her attention between sewing and her family

explanation.text: Ruby makes clothing and quilts for family, neighbors, brides, and children, often for little or no money. Each stitch becomes an act of care, so the phrase means she expresses kindness through what she sews.
